topics.title: Introduction to Basic Concepts and Recent Developments: Collaborative Recommendation - User-based Nearest Neighbour Recommendations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT741 - RECOMMENDATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Basic Concepts and Recent Developments: Collaborative Recommendation - User-based Nearest Neighbour Recommendations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Recommendation System?

A **Recommendation System (RecSys)** is an information filtering and decision-support engine that learns from historical interactions (ratings, clicks, purchases, dwell-time) between **users** and **items** to predict the *utility* (preference) of items that a user has **not yet experienced**, and surface a personalized, ranked list of suggestions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** A Recommendation System is a subclass of information filtering system that seeks to predict the rating or preference a user would give to an item. It is the central machinery behind personalization in e-commerce, OTT platforms, music streaming, news feeds, and social networks.

The general predictive task is to estimate an unknown entry $r_{u,i}$ in a sparse **user–item rating matrix** $R \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$:

$$r_{u,i} \approx \hat{r}_{u,i} = f(u, i, \theta)$$

where $m$ is the number of users, $n$ the number of items, and $\theta$ the model parameters learnt from observed entries.

## 1.2 Taxonomy of Recommendation Approaches

Recommendation algorithms are broadly partitioned into five families. KTU 2024 Scheme insists on knowing this taxonomy before diving into any one technique.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five Paradigms of Recommendation (KTU 2024 – Module 1 highlight)**
> 1. **Content-Based Filtering (CBF)** – recommends items similar in *feature-space* to those a user previously liked.
> 2. **Collaborative Filtering (CF)** – recommends items that *similar users* have liked (this entire topic).
> 3. **Knowledge-Based Filtering** – uses explicit domain knowledge/rules (e.g., "if budget < 30k, exclude flagship phones").
> 4. **Demographic Filtering** – matches users by demographic attributes (age, region, occupation).
> 5. **Hybrid Systems** – combine two or more of the above to overcome individual weaknesses.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Book Club"

Imagine a college book club with 100 readers. You are new and have read only 5 books. You loved *Sapiens*, hated *Twilight*, and felt neutral about *Atomic Habits*. The club librarian has *no idea what you like* in detail, but she notices that two other members — **Riya** and **Arjun** — have rated *Sapiens* 5/5, *Twilight* 1/5, and *Atomic Habits* 3/5 — *almost exactly your pattern*. The librarian, therefore, trusts their future ratings as proxies for yours.

- *If Riya loved a book you haven't read, you'll probably love it too.*
- *If Arjun hated a book you haven't read, you'll probably hate it too.*

This is the **essence of User-Based Collaborative Filtering (User-Based CF)**: *find people who behave like you, and trust their judgments for the things you haven't decided on yet.*

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Neighbour" in Nearest Neighbour** refers to the **K most similar users** in the rating space — the closest "neighbours" of the target user in a high-dimensional preference space. The algorithm is called **memory-based** (or **lazy-learning**) because it does not build a compact model during training; it stores the entire rating matrix and performs computation at query time.

## 1.4 Definition: User-Based Nearest Neighbour Recommendation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (Exam-Ready):**
> **User-Based Nearest Neighbour Collaborative Filtering (User-Based KNN-CF)** is a *memory-based* collaborative filtering technique that predicts the unknown rating $\hat{r}_{u,i}$ of user $u$ for item $i$ by computing a **weighted aggregate of the actual ratings** given to item $i$ by the **K users most similar to $u$** in the rating space, where the weights are the pairwise **similarity coefficients** between $u$ and each neighbour.

The two key sub-problems are therefore:
1. **Similarity computation** — How do we measure "closeness" between two users in rating space?
2. **Rating prediction / aggregation** — How do we combine neighbours' ratings into a single prediction?

Both are addressed rigorously in the next two sections.

## 1.5 Recent Developments (Context for KTU 2024)

While classical **User-Based KNN-CF** (Resnick et al., 1994; Breese et al., 1998; Herlocker et al., 1999) is the focus of this topic, KTU 2024 requires the student to position it within the evolution of the field:

> [!NOTE]
> **Recent Developments You MUST mention in the exam**
> - **Matrix Factorization (MF)** — Koren et al. (2009) Netflix Prize; latent factor models such as SVD, NMF, ALS, BPR-MF.
> - **Deep Learning CF** — He et al. (2017) *Neural Collaborative Filtering*; Autoencoders (AutoRec, EASE); VAE-based (Mult-VAE).
> - **Graph-Based** — LightGCN, NGCF; random-walk methods (PageRank-style).
> - **Sequential / Session-Based** — GRU4Rec, SASRec, BERT4Rec (Transformer-based).
> - **LLM-Based & Conversational RecSys** — ChatRec, RecLLM, prompt-tuned recommenders.
> - **Self-Supervised & Contrastive** — SGL, NCL, SimGCL.
> - **Privacy-Preserving** — Federated RecSys, on-device personalization.
> - **Multi-Modal & Context-Aware** — incorporating text, image, audio, location, time, weather.

User-Based KNN-CF is *not obsolete* — it remains a **strong, interpretable baseline**, often used inside ensembles and as a teacher model in knowledge distillation pipelines.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Pipeline of User-Based KNN-CF (Step-by-Step)

The complete operational flow consists of **six logical stages**. Memorize this pipeline — it is the skeleton of almost every Part-B 14-mark answer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Six-Stage Pipeline of User-Based KNN-CF**
> 1. **Construct** the user–item rating matrix $R$ from explicit/implicit feedback.
> 2. **Select** the target user $u$ and the target item $i$ (the cell $r_{u,i}$ to be predicted).
> 3. **Identify** the set of users $N_u$ who have rated item $i$ (the candidate neighbour pool).
> 4. **Compute** the similarity $\text{sim}(u, v)$ between $u$ and every $v \in N_u$, using a chosen metric.
> 5. **Rank** candidates by similarity and retain the **top-K nearest neighbours** $N_u^K$.
> 6. **Aggregate** the neighbours' ratings on item $i$ using a similarity-weighted scheme to produce $\hat{r}_{u,i}$.

## 2.2 Similarity Metrics (the "Why" behind each formula)

Let $I_{uv} = I_u \cap I_v$ be the set of items rated by **both** users $u$ and $v$. All similarity metrics operate *only* on this co-rated subset, which is what makes CF vulnerable to **data sparsity**.

### 2.2.1 Cosine Similarity
Treats each user as a vector in $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ and measures the angle between them. **Insensitive to magnitude**, only direction.

> [!NOTE]
> - **Intuition:** Two users are similar if their *pattern* of high/low ratings aligns, regardless of whether one is a "5-stars-everywhere" user and the other is a "3-stars-everywhere" user.
> - **Best for:** Implicit feedback, sparse data, text/embedding-based vectors.

### 2.2.2 Pearson Correlation Coefficient
Pearson captures **linear correlation** between two rating vectors after mean-centering each user. It corrects the magnitude inflation problem of raw cosine.

> [!NOTE]
> - **Intuition:** Two users are similar if, *when one rates higher than their average, the other also rates higher than theirs*.
> - **Best for:** Explicit ratings on a fixed scale (1–5 stars), where users have personal rating biases.
> - **Case variants:** Pearson I (uses all co-rated items), Pearson II (shrinkage/regularized form, Herlocker et al. 1999).

### 2.2.3 Adjusted Cosine Similarity
Centers each *item* (not user) before computing cosine. Useful when comparing items, but for user-user it degenerates to a form of Pearson.

### 2.2.4 Spearman Rank Correlation
Replaces raw ratings with their **ranks** and then applies Pearson. Robust to outliers, but loses the magnitude information.

### 2.2.5 Jaccard & Constrained Metrics
For binary (like/dislike) feedback. Not the focus of this topic but worth naming.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Table — use this verbatim in your exam revision**

| # | Metric / Quantity | Formula | Notation | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cosine Similarity** | $\text{sim}_{cos}(u,v)=\dfrac{\sum_{j \in I_{uv}} r_{u,j}\, r_{v,j}}{\sqrt{\sum_{j \in I_{uv}} r_{u,j}^{2}}\;\sqrt{\sum_{j \in I_{uv}} r_{v,j}^{2}}}$ | $I_{uv}$: co-rated items | Implicit, sparse data |
| 2 | **Pearson Correlation** | $\text{sim}_{pear}(u,v)=\dfrac{\sum_{j \in I_{uv}}(r_{u,j}-\bar r_{u})(r_{v,j}-\bar r_{v})}{\sqrt{\sum_{j \in I_{uv}}(r_{u,j}-\bar r_{u})^{2}}\;\sqrt{\sum_{j \in I_{uv}}(r_{v,j}-\bar r_{v})^{2}}}$ | $\bar r_{u}=\dfrac{1}{\vert I_{u}\vert}\sum_{j \in I_{u}} r_{u,j}$ | Explicit 1–5 star ratings |
| 3 | **Spearman Rank** | $\text{sim}_{sp}(u,v)=1-\dfrac{6 \sum_{j \in I_{uv}} d_{j}^{2}}{k(k^{2}-1)}$, $\;k=\vert I_{uv}\vert$ | $d_j$: rank difference | Ordinal / outlier-robust |
| 4 | **Mean-Centred Weighted Prediction** | $\hat r_{u,i}=\bar r_{u}+\dfrac{\sum_{v \in N_u^K}\text{sim}(u,v)\,(r_{v,i}-\bar r_{v})}{\sum_{v \in N_u^K}\vert \text{sim}(u,v)\vert}$ | $N_u^K$: top-K neighbours | Default KNN-CF predictor |
| 5 | **Raw Weighted Average** | $\hat r_{u,i}=\dfrac{\sum_{v \in N_u^K}\text{sim}(u,v)\, r_{v,i}}{\sum_{v \in N_u^K}\vert \text{sim}(u,v)\vert}$ | — | Cosine-based pipelines |
| 6 | **Significance Weighting** | $\text{sim}'(u,v)=\text{sim}(u,v)\cdot \dfrac{\min(\vert I_{uv}\vert, \gamma)}{\gamma}$ | $\gamma \in [25, 50]$ | Penalises overlap too small |
| 7 | **Shrinkage / Damping** | $\text{sim}''(u,v)=\dfrac{\vert I_{uv}\vert}{\vert I_{uv}\vert+\lambda}\text{sim}(u,v)$ | $\lambda$: regulariser | Bayesian-style damping |
| 8 | **Top-K Cutoff** | $N_u^K=\text{TopK}_{v \in N_u}\;\text{sim}(u,v)$ | $K$: hyper-parameter | $K \in [20, 50]$ typical |
| 9 | **Evaluation: MAE** | $\text{MAE}=\dfrac{1}{\vert T\vert}\sum_{(u,i)\in T}\vert r_{u,i}-\hat r_{u,i}\vert$ | $T$: test set | Regression accuracy |
| 10 | **Evaluation: RMSE** | $\text{RMSE}=\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\vert T\vert}\sum_{(u,i)\in T}(r_{u,i}-\hat r_{u,i})^{2}}$ | — | Penalises large errors |
| 11 | **Precision@N** | $\text{Prec@N}=\dfrac{\vert \text{Recommended}_N \cap \text{Relevant}\vert}{N}$ | — | Top-N ranking quality |
| 12 | **Recall@N** | $\text{Rec@N}=\dfrac{\vert \text{Recommended}_N \cap \text{Relevant}\vert}{\vert \text{Relevant}\vert}$ | — | Coverage of relevant items |
| 13 | **Sparsity Level** | $\text{Sparsity}=1-\dfrac{\text{\# ratings observed}}{m \times n}$ | $m,n$: users, items | Usually $0.95$–$0.99$ |
| 14 | **Coverage** | $\text{Cov}=\dfrac{\vert \text{Items ever recommended}\vert}{n}$ | — | Catalogue exposure |

> [!NOTE]
> All vertical bars $\vert \cdot \vert$ above denote *cardinality* (set size) or *absolute value* of a scalar, **not** matrix determinant or matrix entry delimiters. They never appear inside the LaTeX cells in a way that conflicts with markdown table syntax.

## 2.4 Why Mean-Centering? (The Hidden Bias Problem)

Some users are *generous raters* (always give 4–5), others are *tough* (always give 1–3). Without correction, two such users are considered "dissimilar" by raw cosine even if they have identical *relative preferences*. The mean-centred form:

$$\tilde{r}_{u,j}=r_{u,j}-\bar r_{u}$$

subtracts the personal baseline, isolating **preference pattern** from **rating scale**. This is precisely what Pearson does — and it is why Pearson is the **default** for 1–5 star rating data.

## 2.5 The Choice of K — Why Not Use All Users?

Using *all* users (instead of top-K) hurts because:
- Weakly correlated users inject noise.
- Computational cost grows with $m$.
- Top-K is a form of **regularization by neighbourhood selection**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Empirical Rule (Herlocker et al., 1999):** A neighbourhood size of $K \in [20, 50]$ combined with **significance weighting** (formula 6 in the cheat-sheet) typically yields near-optimal accuracy on the MovieLens-1M and MovieLens-10M datasets.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Use-Case | Why User-Based KNN-CF Works |
|---|---|---|
| E-Commerce (Amazon, Flipkart) | "Customers like you bought…" | Repeated purchase patterns cluster by user |
| OTT (Netflix, Hotstar) | "Because you watched X" | Tastes evolve slowly; nearest neighbours stable |
| Music (Spotify, JioSaavn) | Daily Mix, Discover Weekly | Genre/listening-hour clusters are dense |
| News (Google News, InShorts) | Personalized feed | Reading behaviour has high user-user correlation |
| Jobs / Matrimonial | Match suggestions | Demographic + preference overlap is strong |

## 2.7 Limitations (Critical for Exam)

1. **Sparsity** — When $I_{uv}$ is small, similarity estimates are noisy.
2. **Cold Start** — New users (no ratings) have no neighbours; new items (no ratings) cannot be recommended by CF at all.
3. **Scalability** — $O(m^2 n)$ similarity matrix; online queries $O(m)$.
4. **Popularity Bias** — Tends to over-recommend blockbuster items.
5. **Shilling / Profile Injection** — Adversaries can create fake users to push an item.
6. **Gray Sheep** — Users with eclectic taste have no reliable neighbours.

These limitations are the **bridge** to recent developments (MF, DL, GNN, LLM-based).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Numerical Example (Exam-Grade).** A small rating matrix is used so every algebraic step is verifiable by hand. **Memorize the structure of this worked example** — it is the most-tested problem type in the KTU Module-1 question bank.

## 3.1 Problem Statement

Consider a movie-recommendation scenario with **4 users** ($U_1, U_2, U_3, U_4$) and **4 movies** ($I_1, I_2, I_3, I_4$), rated on a **1–5 scale**. The "?" indicates the rating to be predicted.

| User \\ Item | $I_1$ | $I_2$ | $I_3$ | $I_4$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **$U_1$** (target) | 5 | 3 | 4 | **?** |
| $U_2$ | 3 | 1 | 2 | 5 |
| $U_3$ | 4 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
| $U_4$ | 3 | 3 | 1 | 4 |

**Task:** Predict $\hat r_{U_1, I_4}$ using **Pearson correlation similarity** with **$K = 3$** (all three other users) and the **mean-centred weighted prediction formula**.

## 3.2 Step 1 — Compute Each User's Mean Rating

The mean rating of user $u$ over all items they have rated is:

$$\bar r_{u}=\frac{1}{|I_{u}|}\sum_{j \in I_{u}} r_{u,j}$$

Apply this to each user:

$$\bar r_{U_1}=\frac{5+3+4}{3}=\frac{12}{3}=4.000$$

$$\bar r_{U_2}=\frac{3+1+2+5}{4}=\frac{11}{4}=2.750$$

$$\bar r_{U_3}=\frac{4+3+4+5}{4}=\frac{16}{4}=4.000$$

$$\bar r_{U_4}=\frac{3+3+1+4}{4}=\frac{11}{4}=2.750$$

> [!NOTE]
> The "co-rated set" $I_{uv}$ for similarity between $U_1$ and any other user is $\{I_1, I_2, I_3\}$ — these are the only items rated by **all** four users.

## 3.3 Step 2 — Mean-Centred Ratings (Deviations)

Build the deviation table restricted to the co-rated set $\{I_1, I_2, I_3\}$:

| User \\ Item | $I_1$ | $I_2$ | $I_3$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $U_1$ | $5-4=+1$ | $3-4=-1$ | $4-4=0$ |
| $U_2$ | $3-2.75=+0.25$ | $1-2.75=-1.75$ | $2-2.75=-0.75$ |
| $U_3$ | $4-4=0$ | $3-4=-1$ | $4-4=0$ |
| $U_4$ | $3-2.75=+0.25$ | $3-2.75=+0.25$ | $1-2.75=-1.75$ |

## 3.4 Step 3 — Pearson Similarity of $U_1$ with Each Neighbour

The general Pearson formula restricted to the co-rated set is:

$$\text{sim}_{pear}(U_1,U)=\frac{\sum_{j \in I_{U_1 U}}(r_{U_1,j}-\bar r_{U_1})(r_{U,j}-\bar r_{U})}{\sqrt{\sum_{j \in I_{U_1 U}}(r_{U_1,j}-\bar r_{U_1})^{2}}\;\sqrt{\sum_{j \in I_{U_1 U}}(r_{U,j}-\bar r_{U})^{2}}}$$

### 3.4.1 $\text{sim}(U_1, U_2)$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator } N_{12} &= (+1)(+0.25) + (-1)(-1.75) + (0)(-0.75) \\
&= 0.25 + 1.75 + 0 \\
&= 2.00 \\[4pt]
\text{Denominator term for } U_1 &= \sqrt{1^{2}+(-1)^{2}+0^{2}} = \sqrt{2} \\
\text{Denominator term for } U_2 &= \sqrt{0.25^{2}+(-1.75)^{2}+(-0.75)^{2}} = \sqrt{0.0625+3.0625+0.5625} = \sqrt{3.6875} \\
\text{sim}(U_1,U_2) &= \frac{2.00}{\sqrt{2}\cdot\sqrt{3.6875}} = \frac{2.00}{\sqrt{7.375}} = \frac{2.00}{2.7157} \approx \mathbf{0.7364}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.4.2 $\text{sim}(U_1, U_3)$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator } N_{13} &= (+1)(0) + (-1)(-1) + (0)(0) \\
&= 0 + 1 + 0 \\
&= 1.00 \\[4pt]
\text{Denominator term for } U_1 &= \sqrt{2} \;\;( \text{same as before}) \\
\text{Denominator term for } U_3 &= \sqrt{0^{2}+(-1)^{2}+0^{2}} = \sqrt{1} = 1.000 \\
\text{sim}(U_1,U_3) &= \frac{1.00}{\sqrt{2}\cdot 1} = \frac{1.00}{1.4142} \approx \mathbf{0.7071}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.4.3 $\text{sim}(U_1, U_4)$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator } N_{14} &= (+1)(+0.25) + (-1)(+0.25) + (0)(-1.75) \\
&= 0.25 - 0.25 + 0 \\
&= 0.00 \\[4pt]
\text{sim}(U_1,U_4) &= \frac{0.00}{\sqrt{2}\cdot\sqrt{3.6875}} = \mathbf{0.0000}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A zero similarity means the two users have **no linear correlation** in their co-rated preferences. Such a user contributes nothing to the prediction (in fact, the prediction formula divides by $\sum |\text{sim}|$, so a zero sim just lowers the contribution of others).

## 3.5 Step 4 — Compile the Neighbour Table

| Neighbour $v$ | $\text{sim}(U_1,v)$ | $r_{v,I_4}$ (actual) | $\bar r_{v}$ (mean) | Deviation $r_{v,I_4}-\bar r_{v}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $U_2$ | 0.7364 | 5 | 2.750 | 2.250 |
| $U_3$ | 0.7071 | 5 | 4.000 | 1.000 |
| $U_4$ | 0.0000 | 4 | 2.750 | 1.250 |

## 3.6 Step 5 — Apply the Mean-Centred Weighted Prediction Formula

$$\hat r_{U_1,I_4}=\bar r_{U_1}+\frac{\sum_{v \in N_{U_1}^{K}}\text{sim}(U_1,v)\,(r_{v,I_4}-\bar r_{v})}{\sum_{v \in N_{U_1}^{K}}\big|\text{sim}(U_1,v)\big|}$$

### 3.6.1 Numerator

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Num} &= (0.7364)(2.250) + (0.7071)(1.000) + (0.0000)(1.250) \\
&= 1.6569 + 0.7071 + 0.0000 \\
&= 2.3640
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.6.2 Denominator

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Den} &= |0.7364| + |0.7071| + |0.0000| \\
&= 0.7364 + 0.7071 + 0.0000 \\
&= 1.4435
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.6.3 Final Predicted Rating

$$\begin{aligned}
\hat r_{U_1,I_4} &= 4.000 + \frac{2.3640}{1.4435} \\
&= 4.000 + 1.6378 \\
&\approx \mathbf{5.64}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Clamping Convention (write this in the exam!):** Ratings are bounded to $[1, 5]$ on a 1–5 scale. Therefore the *clamped* prediction is $\hat r_{U_1, I_4}=\min(5,5.64)=\mathbf{5.00}$ stars. The unclamped value (5.64) tells you the user would have rated it even higher than the maximum allowed, which is a strong recommendation signal.

## 3.7 Analytical Decision Matrix (When to Use What)

| Scenario | Recommended Metric | Recommended Predictor | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Explicit 1–5 star ratings, dense | **Pearson** | Mean-centred weighted | Corrects user bias |
| Implicit feedback (clicks, plays) | **Cosine** | Raw weighted | No negative ratings, no bias |
| Small co-rated set ($\vert I_{uv}\vert < 5$) | **Cosine with significance weighting** | Raw weighted | Pearson is unstable on tiny samples |
| Outlier-heavy data | **Spearman** | Mean-centred weighted | Robust to rating extremes |
| Latent-feature taste | **Matrix Factorization** | Dot product of embeddings | Captures hidden factors |
| Sequential / time-aware | **GRU / Transformer** | Sequence model | Order matters |

## 3.8 Worked Example — Cosine Variant (Cross-Verification)

Using the **same matrix** and **cosine similarity** restricted to $I_{U_1 U} = \{I_1,I_2,I_3\}$:

$$\text{sim}_{cos}(U_1,U)=\frac{\vec U_1 \cdot \vec U}{\|\vec U_1\|_2 \cdot \|\vec U\|_2}$$

- $\|\vec U_1\|=\sqrt{5^{2}+3^{2}+4^{2}}=\sqrt{50}\approx 7.0711$
- $\|\vec U_2\|=\sqrt{3^{2}+1^{2}+2^{2}}=\sqrt{14}\approx 3.7417$
- $\vec U_1 \cdot \vec U_2 = 15+3+8=26$

$$\text{sim}_{cos}(U_1,U_2)=\frac{26}{7.0711 \times 3.7417}=\frac{26}{26.458}\approx 0.9827$$

Similarly, $\text{sim}_{cos}(U_1,U_3)\approx 0.9940$ and $\text{sim}_{cos}(U_1,U_4)\approx 0.9089$.

With the **raw weighted average** (no mean-centering):

$$\hat r_{U_1,I_4}=\frac{0.9827(5)+0.9940(5)+0.9089(4)}{0.9827+0.9940+0.9089}=\frac{4.9135+4.9700+3.6356}{2.8856}=\frac{13.5191}{2.8856}\approx \mathbf{4.685}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison Insight:** Pearson + mean-centring gives a *stronger* (and more bias-corrected) prediction, while raw cosine gives a *smoother* prediction. The two answers (5.00 clamped vs 4.685) are both valid recommendations; Pearson is preferred for explicit ratings per the literature.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams use **Mermaid** syntax with **alphanumeric node IDs prefixed by letters**, **plain uppercase labels inside double-quotes**, and **no markdown formatting** inside the labels (per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 mermaid safeguards).

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — High-Level Recommendation System Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[USER POOL] --> C[COLLABORATIVE FILTERING ENGINE]
    B[ITEM CATALOG] --> C
    R[RATING DATABASE] --> C
    C --> D[USER USER SIMILARITY MATRIX]
    D --> E[K NEAREST NEIGHBOUR SELECTOR]
    E --> F[MEAN CENTRED PREDICTOR]
    F --> G[TOP N RECOMMENDATION LIST]
    G --> H[END USER INTERFACE]
    I[OFFLINE EVALUATION MODULE] --> C
    J[RECENT DEVELOPMENTS MF DEEP LEARNING GNN] -.->|EVOLVED FROM| C
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Detailed Process Flow of User-Based KNN-CF

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[START USER QUERY TARGET USER U ITEM I] --> stepB[LOAD USER ITEM RATING MATRIX R]
    stepB --> stepC[COMPUTE MEAN RATING OF EACH USER]
    stepC --> stepD[FOR EACH OTHER USER V WITH RATING ON I]
    stepD --> stepE[COMPUTE PEARSON OR COSINE SIMILARITY BETWEEN U AND V]
    stepE --> stepF[STORE SIM U V]
    stepF --> stepD
    stepD --> stepG[COLLECT ALL SIMILARITY VALUES]
    stepG --> stepH[SORT DESCENDING AND PICK TOP K NEIGHBOURS]
    stepH --> stepI[FETCH EACH NEIGHBOUR V RATING ON ITEM I]
    stepI --> stepJ[COMPUTE WEIGHTED DEVIATION SUM]
    stepJ --> stepK[COMPUTE DENOMINATOR SUM OF ABS SIM]
    stepK --> stepL[APPLY MEAN CENTRED PREDICTION FORMULA]
    stepL --> stepM[CLAMP PREDICTION TO VALID RATING RANGE]
    stepM --> stepN[RETURN PREDICTED RATING]
    stepN --> stepO[END]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Functional Block Architecture of the User-Based KNN Pipeline

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph INPUT
        inp1[EXPLICIT RATINGS STARS NUMERIC]
        inp2[IMPLICIT FEEDBACK CLICKS WATCH TIME]
    end
    subgraph PREPROCESS
        pre1[SPARSE MATRIX CONSTRUCTION]
        pre2[NORMALIZATION AND MEAN CENTERING]
    end
    subgraph SIMILARITY
        sim1[PEARSON CORRELATION ENGINE]
        sim2[COSINE SIMILARITY ENGINE]
        sim3[SPEARMAN RANK ENGINE]
    end
    subgraph NEIGHBOURHOOD
        nb1[TOP K SELECTION MODULE]
        nb2[SIGNIFICANCE WEIGHTING MODULE]
    end
    subgraph PREDICTION
        pr1[MEAN CENTRED WEIGHTED AGGREGATOR]
        pr2[RAW WEIGHTED AGGREGATOR]
        pr3[RATING CLAMPER]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT
        op1[PREDICTED RATING VECTOR]
        op2[TOP N RECOMMENDATION LIST]
    end
    inp1 --> pre1
    inp2 --> pre1
    pre1 --> pre2
    pre2 --> sim1
    pre2 --> sim2
    pre2 --> sim3
    sim1 --> nb1
    sim2 --> nb1
    sim3 --> nb1
    nb1 --> nb2
    nb2 --> pr1
    nb2 --> pr2
    pr1 --> pr3
    pr2 --> pr3
    pr3 --> op1
    op1 --> op2
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution as per KTU 2024 Scheme (PEC Elective, Continuous + End-Sem Mix):** The questions below are written strictly in the KTU ESE (End-Semester Examination) style — Part A carries **3 marks each** (short, definition-based) and Part B carries **14 marks** (deep, sub-divided) with **internal choice** between two full-length alternatives.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define User-Based Collaborative Filtering. List any two similarity metrics used in it.** **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
User-Based Collaborative Filtering is a memory-based recommendation technique that predicts the unknown rating of a target user on an item by aggregating the actual ratings given to that item by a set of users who are *most similar* to the target user, where similarity is computed in the historical rating space. The two most widely used similarity metrics are:
1. **Cosine Similarity** — measures the angle between two user-rating vectors.
2. **Pearson Correlation Coefficient** — measures the linear correlation between two mean-centered rating vectors and corrects for personal rating bias. **(1 + 1.5 = 3 marks)**

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by "Mean-Centring" in user-based collaborative filtering? Why is it necessary?** **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
**Mean-Centring** is the process of subtracting each user's average rating $\bar r_u$ from their individual ratings $r_{u,j}$ to obtain a *deviation score* $\tilde r_{u,j}=r_{u,j}-\bar r_u$. It is necessary because raw ratings carry two pieces of information — a user's *personal rating bias* (e.g., "generous" vs. "tough" rater) and their *relative preference pattern*. Without mean-centring, raw cosine conflates these two, falsely declaring users with different rating scales as dissimilar. Pearson correlation uses mean-centring to isolate the pure preference pattern. **(1.5 + 1.5 = 3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B always carries an **internal choice** — the student answers **EITHER** Question A (with sub-parts a and b) **OR** Question B (with sub-parts a and b). Each sub-part is worth 7 marks.

---

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Explain in detail the architecture and working of a User-Based Nearest Neighbour Recommendation System with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of similarity computation, neighbourhood selection, and rating aggregation.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):** User-Based KNN-CF is a *memory-based* (lazy) recommendation algorithm. It does not learn a parametric model during training; instead, it stores the entire user–item rating matrix $R$ and performs all computation at query time.

**2. Architecture — Block Diagram (2 marks):**

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| **Rating Database** | Stores $R \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ |
| **Mean Centring Module** | Computes $\bar r_u$ and the deviation vector for each user |
| **Similarity Engine** | Computes $\text{sim}(u,v)$ for all user pairs using Pearson/Cosine |
| **Neighbour Selector** | Picks the Top-K most similar users to the target user |
| **Prediction Aggregator** | Computes the weighted rating estimate $\hat r_{u,i}$ |
| **Output** | Top-N ranked recommendation list |

**3. Six-Step Working Pipeline (3 marks):**
1. **Construct** the rating matrix $R$ from explicit or implicit feedback.
2. **Identify** the target user $u$ and the unrated item $i$.
3. **Compute pairwise similarity** $\text{sim}(u,v)$ over the co-rated set $I_{uv}$ using Pearson:

$$\text{sim}_{pear}(u,v)=\frac{\sum_{j \in I_{uv}}(r_{u,j}-\bar r_{u})(r_{v,j}-\bar r_{v})}{\sqrt{\sum_{j \in I_{uv}}(r_{u,j}-\bar r_{u})^{2}}\;\sqrt{\sum_{j \in I_{uv}}(r_{v,j}-\bar r_{v})^{2}}}$$

4. **Rank & select** the Top-K neighbours $N_u^K$ (typically $K \in [20,50]$).
5. **Aggregate** the neighbours' ratings on item $i$:

$$\hat r_{u,i}=\bar r_{u}+\frac{\sum_{v \in N_u^K}\text{sim}(u,v)\,(r_{v,i}-\bar r_{v})}{\sum_{v \in N_u^K}|\text{sim}(u,v)|}$$

6. **Clamp & rank** the final score, returning the Top-N items.

**4. Role of Each Stage (1 mark):** Similarity computation identifies who behaves like whom; neighbourhood selection regularizes by ignoring weak correlations; rating aggregation fuses the trustworthy neighbours' opinions into a single prediction.

**(Distribution: 1 + 2 + 3 + 1 = 7 marks)**

---

**(b) Given the following user–item rating matrix (1 = worst, 5 = best), predict the rating of user $U_1$ for item $I_4$ using **Pearson correlation similarity** and the **mean-centred weighted prediction** formula. Use $K = 3$ (all three other users).** **(7 Marks)**

| User | $I_1$ | $I_2$ | $I_3$ | $I_4$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $U_1$ | 5 | 3 | 4 | **?** |
| $U_2$ | 3 | 1 | 2 | 5 |
| $U_3$ | 4 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
| $U_4$ | 3 | 3 | 1 | 4 |

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — User Means (1 mark):**
$\bar r_{U_1}=4.0$, $\bar r_{U_2}=2.75$, $\bar r_{U_3}=4.0$, $\bar r_{U_4}=2.75$.

**Step 2 — Deviation Table on Co-Rated Set $\{I_1,I_2,I_3\}$ (1 mark):**
- $U_1: (+1, -1, 0)$
- $U_2: (+0.25, -1.75, -0.75)$
- $U_3: (0, -1, 0)$
- $U_4: (+0.25, +0.25, -1.75)$

**Step 3 — Pearson Similarities (2 marks):**

$$\text{sim}(U_1,U_2)=\frac{2.00}{\sqrt{2}\sqrt{3.6875}}=\frac{2.00}{2.7157}\approx 0.7364$$

$$\text{sim}(U_1,U_3)=\frac{1.00}{\sqrt{2}\cdot 1}\approx 0.7071$$

$$\text{sim}(U_1,U_4)=\frac{0.00}{\sqrt{2}\sqrt{3.6875}}=0.0000$$

**Step 4 — Weighted Aggregates (2 marks):**

$$\text{Num}=0.7364(2.25)+0.7071(1.00)+0.0000(1.25)=1.6569+0.7071+0.0000=2.3640$$

$$\text{Den}=0.7364+0.7071+0.0000=1.4435$$

**Step 5 — Final Prediction (1 mark):**

$$\hat r_{U_1,I_4}=4.0+\frac{2.3640}{1.4435}=4.0+1.6378\approx 5.64 \xrightarrow{\text{clamp}}\boxed{\mathbf{5.00}}$$

**(Distribution: 1 + 1 + 2 + 2 + 1 = 7 marks)**

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice). `[KTU University Exam – May 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Compare and contrast the various similarity measures used in user-based collaborative filtering — Cosine, Pearson, Adjusted Cosine, and Spearman Rank — with mathematical formulas, advantages, and limitations.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Cosine Similarity (1.5 marks):**
$$\text{sim}_{cos}(u,v)=\frac{\sum_{j \in I_{uv}}r_{u,j}\,r_{v,j}}{\sqrt{\sum_{j \in I_{uv}}r_{u,j}^{2}}\;\sqrt{\sum_{j \in I_{uv}}r_{v,j}^{2}}}$$
- ✅ Simple, fast, works for sparse / implicit data.
- ❌ Ignores rating magnitude; cannot distinguish a "5-everywhere" user from a "selective 5" user.

**2. Pearson Correlation (1.5 marks):**
$$\text{sim}_{pear}(u,v)=\frac{\sum_{j \in I_{uv}}(r_{u,j}-\bar r_{u})(r_{v,j}-\bar r_{v})}{\sqrt{\sum_{j \in I_{uv}}(r_{u,j}-\bar r_{u})^{2}}\;\sqrt{\sum_{j \in I_{uv}}(r_{v,j}-\bar r_{v})^{2}}}$$
- ✅ Corrects personal rating bias; the **default** for 1–5 star explicit ratings.
- ❌ Unstable when $\vert I_{uv}\vert$ is very small; affected by outliers.

**3. Adjusted Cosine (1.5 marks):**
$$\text{sim}_{adj}(u,v)=\frac{\sum_{j \in I_{uv}}(r_{u,j}-\bar r_{j})(r_{v,j}-\bar r_{j})}{\sqrt{\sum_{j \in I_{uv}}(r_{u,j}-\bar r_{j})^{2}}\;\sqrt{\sum_{j \in I_{uv}}(r_{v,j}-\bar r_{j})^{2}}}$$
Subtracts the **item mean** $\bar r_j$ (not the user mean) — useful when items have global popularity effects.
- ✅ Compensates for items that are universally liked/disliked.
- ❌ Less commonly used for user-based CF; mostly an item-based CF tool.

**4. Spearman Rank (1.5 marks):**
$$\text{sim}_{sp}(u,v)=1-\frac{6\sum_{j \in I_{uv}}d_{j}^{2}}{k(k^{2}-1)},\quad k=|I_{uv}|$$
Replaces raw ratings with ranks, then computes Pearson.
- ✅ Robust to outliers; ordinal-data-friendly.
- ❌ Loses magnitude information; computationally heavier due to ranking.

**5. Comparative Summary Table (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Cosine | Pearson | Adj-Cosine | Spearman |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Centring | None | User mean | Item mean | Rank |
| Outlier Robust | No | No | No | Yes |
| Bias Correction | None | User bias | Item bias | None |
| Best for | Implicit | Explicit | Item-based | Ordinal |

**(Distribution: 1.5×4 + 1 = 7 marks)**

---

**(b) Discuss the major limitations of classical user-based collaborative filtering and explain how recent developments — Matrix Factorization, Neural Collaborative Filtering, and Graph-Based methods — address them.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Limitations of Classical User-Based KNN-CF (2.5 marks):**
- **Sparsity:** Real rating matrices are 95–99% empty. Small $I_{uv}$ makes similarity estimates noisy and unreliable.
- **Cold Start:** A new user with no ratings has no neighbours; a new item with no ratings can never be recommended by CF.
- **Scalability:** $O(m^2 n)$ similarity computation; online queries $O(m)$. Unsuitable for platforms with millions of users.
- **Popularity Bias:** Tends to over-recommend popular items; **gray-sheep** users (eclectic tastes) get poor recommendations.
- **Interpretability vs Accuracy Trade-off:** KNN is interpretable but less accurate than latent-factor models.

**2. Matrix Factorization (MF) — Koren et al. 2009 (1.5 marks):**
Each user $u$ and item $i$ is mapped to a $d$-dimensional latent vector $p_u, q_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$. The predicted rating is the dot product:
$$\hat r_{u,i}=p_u^{T}q_i=\sum_{f=1}^{d}p_{u,f}\,q_{i,f}$$
Trained by SGD or ALS on the observed ratings. *Addresses:* Sparsity (latent vectors are learnt from global co-occurrence), Scalability (constant-time prediction), and improves accuracy substantially.

**3. Neural Collaborative Filtering (NCF) — He et al. 2017 (1.5 marks):**
Replaces the inner product with a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) over concatenated user and item embeddings:
$$\hat r_{u,i}=f(\text{MLP}([p_u \oplus q_i]))$$
*Addresses:* Non-linear user-item interactions (NCF models arbitrary interaction functions), Cold Start (side-information fusion via deep features), and enables end-to-end multi-modal learning.

**4. Graph-Based Methods — LightGCN, NGCF (1.5 marks):**
Models the user–item bipartite graph and propagates information through multiple message-passing layers:
$$p_u^{(k+1)}=\sum_{i \in N_u}\frac{1}{\sqrt{|N_u||N_i|}}q_i^{(k)}$$
*Addresses:* High-order connectivity (captures users connected via paths longer than 1 hop), structural information, and provides strong performance on sparse, large-scale graphs.

**(Distribution: 2.5 + 1.5 + 1.5 + 1.5 = 7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read this twice before submitting your answer sheet.**
> 1. **Don't forget to clamp the predicted rating to the valid scale** (e.g., [1, 5]). Examiners explicitly test whether you report $5.64$ as the *raw prediction* and $5.00$ as the *clamped* answer. Forgetting clamping costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Don't use the raw rating $r_{u,j}$ where the deviation $r_{u,j}-\bar r_u$ is required** for Pearson. Mixing the two is the most common 2-mark loss in numericals.
> 3. **Always specify the co-rated set $I_{uv}$** before plugging into the similarity formula. Examiners deduct marks when similarity is computed on the *full* rating vector instead of the *intersection*.
> 4. **In Part B (a) theory answers, always pair each formula with a one-line intuition.** A formula without explanation is treated as half-marks.
> 5. **Mention the choice of $K$ and its impact** ($K$ too small → overfit/noise; $K$ too large → bias). This 1-mark observation is often the difference between a 13 and a 14.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — Print this and keep it for the night before the exam.**

- **Recommendation System** = filter that predicts unknown user–item preferences from observed interaction data.
- **Five paradigms:** Content-Based, Collaborative, Knowledge-Based, Demographic, Hybrid. (CF is the focus.)
- **User-Based KNN-CF** is a **memory-based, lazy-learning** algorithm — no training phase, all computation at query time.
- **Two sub-problems:** (1) similarity computation, (2) weighted rating aggregation.
- **Cosine Similarity** = angle between rating vectors; **does not** correct rating bias.
- **Pearson Correlation** = cosine on **mean-centered** vectors; **corrects user bias**; default for explicit 1–5 star ratings.
- **Adjusted Cosine** = cosine on **item-mean-centered** vectors; primarily used in item-based CF.
- **Spearman** = Pearson on **ranks**; robust to outliers but loses magnitude.
- **Mean-centred weighted prediction formula (must memorize):**
  $\hat r_{u,i}=\bar r_{u}+\dfrac{\sum_{v \in N_u^K}\text{sim}(u,v)(r_{v,i}-\bar r_{v})}{\sum_{v \in N_u^K}|\text{sim}(u,v)|}$
- **Significance weighting** dampens similarities computed on very small $I_{uv}$ (e.g., $\vert I_{uv}\vert < 25$).
- **Typical $K$ range:** 20 – 50 neighbours.
- **Always clamp** the final prediction to the rating scale.
- **Five major limitations:** Sparsity, Cold Start (user + item), Scalability, Popularity Bias, Shilling Attacks.
- **Recent developments in one line each:**
  - MF (latent dot product, ALS/SGD)
  - NCF (MLP over embeddings)
  - LightGCN / NGCF (graph message passing)
  - AutoRec / EASE (autoencoder, closed-form)
  - SASRec / BERT4Rec (Transformer-based sequential)
  - LLM-based RecSys (prompt tuning, ChatRec)
  - Federated RecSys (on-device privacy)
- **Metrics to know:** MAE, RMSE (regression); Precision@N, Recall@N, MAP, NDCG (ranking).
- **Co-rated set $I_{uv}$** is the intersection of items rated by *both* users $u$ and $v$ — never the union, never the full set.
- **User-mean $\bar r_u$** is computed over **all items rated by $u$**, not just the co-rated items.
- **One-liner to remember KNN-CF's spirit:** *"Tell me who your friends are, and I'll tell you what you'll love."*
- **Recent developments one-liner for viva:** *"Classical CF used handcrafted similarity; modern CF learns the similarity itself through latent factors, deep networks, and graph propagation."*

> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — User-Based Nearest Neighbour Recommendations (PECDT741)**

topics.title: Item-based Nearest Neighbour Recommendation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT741 - RECOMMENDATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Item-based Nearest Neighbour Recommendation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Standard Definition

**Item-based Nearest Neighbour (Item-based kNN) Recommendation** is a *model-based*, neighbourhood-style Collaborative Filtering (CF) technique that generates personalised recommendations by **computing pairwise similarity between items** (rather than between users) and then, for a target user *u* and an unrated item *i*, aggregating the ratings that *u* has given to the *k* items most similar to *i*. The item-item similarity matrix is pre-computed **offline**, which decouples the expensive similarity step from the **online prediction** step, making it highly scalable for production recommender systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Module 1, PECDT741):** Item-based CF is a *latent* neighbourhood method in which the predicted rating $\hat{r}_{u,i}$ is a weighted aggregation over the items rated by user $u$ that are nearest to item $i$ in the item-item similarity space.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Friends Who Buy Together"**
> Imagine you walk into a bookshop and buy *“Introduction to Machine Learning”*. The shopkeeper does not know you personally, but she has **decades of receipts**. She observes that *almost every customer who bought that book also bought “Pattern Recognition and Machine Learning”*. She therefore *places the second book on the counter* before you even ask. She never looked at *who you are* — she only looked at *what travels with what*. This is exactly the philosophy of **item-based kNN**: **items are the citizens; users are merely witnesses**.

Formally, if two items $i$ and $j$ have been co-rated by many users in a *similar pattern*, their similarity $\text{sim}(i,j)$ will be high, and they belong to the same *neighbourhood*.

## 1.3 Why Item-based over User-based?

| Aspect | User-based CF | Item-based CF |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Similarity computed between | Users | Items |
| Stability over time | Low (user tastes drift) | **High** (item relationships are stable) |
| Offline cost | Heavy (re-compute on every new user action) | **Light** (item matrix rarely changes) |
| Online cost | Heavy | **Light** (lookup + dot product) |
| Sparsity handling | Poor | **Better** (item-item matrix is denser) |
| Scalability | Limited | **Excellent** (used by Amazon, Netflix) |

## 1.4 Standard Symbols Used in KTU 2024 Notation

- $U$ — set of all users; $\vert U \vert = m$
- $I$ — set of all items; $\vert I \vert = n$
- $R$ — the $m \times n$ user–item rating matrix
- $r_{u,i}$ — actual rating of user $u$ for item $i$
- $\hat{r}_{u,i}$ — **predicted** rating
- $\bar{r}_{u}$ — mean rating of user $u$
- $\bar{r}_{i}$ — mean rating of item $i$
- $S(i; k)$ — set of **top-k items most similar to $i$** that user $u$ has actually rated
- $U_{i,j}$ — set of users who have rated **both** items $i$ and $j$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline (Structured Logic Steps)

The item-based kNN algorithm follows a strict, two-phase execution model:

**Phase A — Offline (Item-Item Similarity Computation)**
1. Construct the user–item rating matrix $R \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$.
2. For every item pair $(i, j)$, identify the co-rated user set $U_{i,j}$.
3. Compute a similarity score $\text{sim}(i,j)$ using one of the standard metrics (Cosine, Adjusted Cosine, Pearson).
4. Store the **top-k neighbours** of each item in a lookup table (often $k = 20$ to $50$ is optimal in practice).

**Phase B — Online (Prediction & Top-N)**
1. Receive an active user $u$ and target item $i$.
2. Retrieve the neighbourhood $S(i;k)$ intersected with items rated by $u$.
3. Compute the weighted-sum prediction $\hat{r}_{u,i}$.
4. Sort all unseen items by $\hat{r}_{u,i}$ and return the **Top-N** recommendations.

## 2.2 Similarity Metrics (Detailed)

### (a) Cosine Similarity

Treats the **column vectors** of $R$ (item rating vectors) as geometric vectors. Item $i$ is represented by the vector $\mathbf{r}_{*,i}$.

$$\text{sim}_{\cos}(i,j) = \frac{\mathbf{r}_{*,i} \cdot \mathbf{r}_{*,j}}{\lVert \mathbf{r}_{*,i} \rVert_2 \, \lVert \mathbf{r}_{*,j} \rVert_2}$$

- **Why it works:** Two items are similar if their rating vectors point in the same direction.
- **Limitation:** It does *not* correct for users who are systematically lenient or harsh.

### (b) Adjusted Cosine Similarity (Most common in item-based CF)

Subtracts each user's mean rating $\bar{r}_{u}$ from their ratings, eliminating **user-rating bias**.

$$\text{sim}_{adj}(i,j) = \frac{\sum_{u \in U_{i,j}} (r_{u,i} - \bar{r}_{u})(r_{u,j} - \bar{r}_{u})}{\sqrt{\sum_{u \in U_{i,j}} (r_{u,i} - \bar{r}_{u})^{2}} \, \sqrt{\sum_{u \in U_{i,j}} (r_{u,j} - \bar{r}_{u})^{2}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Favourite:** Adjusted Cosine is the **default** choice for item-based CF in almost every KTU textbook reference (Sarwar et al., 2001). The denominator normalises by the variance contributed by users who co-rated $i$ and $j$.

### (c) Pearson Correlation on Items

$$\text{sim}_{pearson}(i,j) = \frac{\sum_{u \in U_{i,j}} (r_{u,i} - \bar{r}_{i})(r_{u,j} - \bar{r}_{j})}{\sqrt{\sum_{u \in U_{i,j}} (r_{u,i} - \bar{r}_{i})^{2}} \, \sqrt{\sum_{u \in U_{i,j}} (r_{u,j} - \bar{r}_{j})^{2}}}$$

where $\bar{r}_{i}$ is the mean rating of item $i$ across all users who rated it.

## 2.3 The Weighted-Sum Prediction Formula

For predicting the rating of user $u$ for item $i$:

$$\boxed{\hat{r}_{u,i} \;=\; \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{j \,\in\, S(i;k) \,\cap\, \text{Rated}(u)} \text{sim}(i,j) \,\cdot\, r_{u,j}}{\displaystyle\sum_{j \,\in\, S(i;k) \,\cap\, \text{Rated}(u)} \bigl\vert \text{sim}(i,j) \bigr\vert}}$$

A **bias-aware** variant normalises by the user mean:

$$\hat{r}_{u,i} \;=\; \bar{r}_{u} \;+\; \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{j} \text{sim}(i,j) \cdot (r_{u,j} - \bar{r}_{u})}{\displaystyle\sum_{j} \bigl\vert \text{sim}(i,j) \bigr\vert}$$

## 2.4 Top-N Recommendation

After computing $\hat{r}_{u,i}$ for all unseen items $i$, the system outputs:

$$\text{TopN}(u) \;=\; \underset{i \,\notin\, \text{Rated}(u)}{\arg\!\max}_{N} \; \hat{r}_{u,i}$$

## 2.5 KTU 2024 Formula Cheat-Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **high-yield KTU formula sheet** for Module-1 item-based kNN. Memorise every cell. Use `\vert` (not `\vert`-piped) characters only when they appear *inside* math — table cell text uses plain vertical rules for column separation, but values are written using LaTeX `\,` spacing only.

| # | Symbol / Formula | Mathematical Form | Role in Item-based kNN |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | User mean | $\bar{r}_{u} = \frac{1}{\vert I_{u} \vert} \sum_{i \in I_{u}} r_{u,i}$ | Required for Adjusted Cosine & bias-aware prediction |
| 2 | Item mean | $\bar{r}_{i} = \frac{1}{\vert U_{i} \vert} \sum_{u \in U_{i}} r_{u,i}$ | Required for item-Pearson |
| 3 | Cosine similarity | $\text{sim}_{\cos}(i,j) = \frac{\mathbf{r}_{*,i} \cdot \mathbf{r}_{*,j}}{\lVert \mathbf{r}_{*,i} \rVert \, \lVert \mathbf{r}_{*,j} \rVert}$ | Basic item-similarity, no bias correction |
| 4 | Adjusted cosine | $\text{sim}_{adj}(i,j) = \frac{\sum_{u}(r_{u,i}-\bar{r}_{u})(r_{u,j}-\bar{r}_{u})}{\sqrt{\sum_{u}(r_{u,i}-\bar{r}_{u})^{2}} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{u}(r_{u,j}-\bar{r}_{u})^{2}}}$ | **Recommended** — corrects user-bias |
| 5 | Item-Pearson | $\text{sim}_{p}(i,j) = \frac{\sum_{u}(r_{u,i}-\bar{r}_{i})(r_{u,j}-\bar{r}_{j})}{\sqrt{\sum_{u}(r_{u,i}-\bar{r}_{i})^{2}} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{u}(r_{u,j}-\bar{r}_{j})^{2}}}$ | Item-side correlation |
| 6 | Weighted-sum prediction | $\hat{r}_{u,i} = \frac{\sum_{j} \text{sim}(i,j) \cdot r_{u,j}}{\sum_{j} \vert \text{sim}(i,j) \vert}$ | Online score aggregation |
| 7 | Bias-aware prediction | $\hat{r}_{u,i} = \bar{r}_{u} + \frac{\sum_{j} \text{sim}(i,j) \cdot (r_{u,j} - \bar{r}_{u})}{\sum_{j} \vert \text{sim}(i,j) \vert}$ | Higher-quality estimate |
| 8 | Top-N output | $\text{TopN}(u) = \arg\!\max_{N} \hat{r}_{u,i}$ | Final recommendation list |
| 9 | Common-user set | $U_{i,j} = \{u \in U : r_{u,i} \neq \emptyset \,\wedge\, r_{u,j} \neq \emptyset\}$ | Domain of every sum |
| 10 | Neighbourhood | $S(i;k) = \{j \in I : \text{sim}(i,j) \text{ is in top-}k\}$ | Reduces online cost |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Item-based kNN powers or has powered systems at **Amazon (item-to-item CF, Linden et al. 2003)**, **Netflix (pre-deep-learning era)**, and **Google News**. Its core appeal is **interpretability** — "we recommend *Interstellar* because you watched *Inception*" — and its **offline/online decoupling**, which lets the heavy lifting happen during nightly batch jobs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Worked Example

## 3.1 The User-Item Rating Matrix

We use a $5 \times 4$ rating matrix (5 users, 4 items), with rating scale $1\text{–}5$ and "?" denoting unrated entries. The task is to **predict user $U_{1}$'s rating for item $I_{3}$**.

| | $I_{1}$ | $I_{2}$ | $I_{3}$ | $I_{4}$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $U_{1}$ | 5 | 4 | **?** | 1 |
| $U_{2}$ | 4 | 3 | 3 | 2 |
| $U_{3}$ | 2 | 1 | 1 | 3 |
| $U_{4}$ | 1 | 2 | 2 | 4 |
| $U_{5}$ | 3 | ? | 4 | ? |

## 3.2 Step 1 — Compute User Means $\bar{r}_{u}$

Each mean uses **only the items the user has actually rated**.

$$\bar{r}_{U_{1}} = \frac{5 + 4 + 1}{3} = \frac{10}{3} \approx 3.333$$

$$\bar{r}_{U_{2}} = \frac{4 + 3 + 3 + 2}{4} = \frac{12}{4} = 3.000$$

$$\bar{r}_{U_{3}} = \frac{2 + 1 + 1 + 3}{4} = \frac{7}{4} = 1.750$$

$$\bar{r}_{U_{4}} = \frac{1 + 2 + 2 + 4}{4} = \frac{9}{4} = 2.250$$

$$\bar{r}_{U_{5}} = \frac{3 + 4}{2} = \frac{7}{2} = 3.500$$

## 3.3 Step 2 — Compute the Deviation Matrix $d_{u,i} = r_{u,i} - \bar{r}_{u}$

| | $I_{1}$ | $I_{2}$ | $I_{3}$ | $I_{4}$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $U_{1}$ | $1.667$ | $0.667$ | — | $-2.333$ |
| $U_{2}$ | $1.000$ | $0.000$ | $0.000$ | $-1.000$ |
| $U_{3}$ | $0.250$ | $-0.750$ | $-0.750$ | $1.250$ |
| $U_{4}$ | $-1.250$ | $-0.250$ | $-0.250$ | $1.750$ |
| $U_{5}$ | $-0.500$ | — | $0.500$ | — |

> Each cell is the user's **deviation from their own mean** — this is the "adjusted" part of adjusted cosine.

## 3.4 Step 3 — Compute $\text{sim}(I_{3}, I_{1})$ (Adjusted Cosine)

The co-rated user set is $U_{I_{3}, I_{1}} = \{U_{2}, U_{3}, U_{4}, U_{5}\}$ (i.e., all users who rated **both** items).

**Numerator** (dot product of deviations on common users):

$$\text{Num} = d_{U_{2},I_{3}} \cdot d_{U_{2},I_{1}} + d_{U_{3},I_{3}} \cdot d_{U_{3},I_{1}} + d_{U_{4},I_{3}} \cdot d_{U_{4},I_{1}} + d_{U_{5},I_{3}} \cdot d_{U_{5},I_{1}}$$

Substituting the deviation values:

$$\text{Num} = (0.000)(1.000) + (-0.750)(0.250) + (-0.250)(-1.250) + (0.500)(-0.500)$$

$$\text{Num} = 0.000 + (-0.1875) + (0.3125) + (-0.2500) = -0.1250$$

**Denominator** (product of $\ell_{2}$-norms of the deviation sub-vectors):

$$\text{Den}_{I_{3}} = \sqrt{0.000^{2} + (-0.750)^{2} + (-0.250)^{2} + (0.500)^{2}} = \sqrt{0.000 + 0.5625 + 0.0625 + 0.2500} = \sqrt{0.875}$$

$$\text{Den}_{I_{1}} = \sqrt{1.000^{2} + 0.250^{2} + (-1.250)^{2} + (-0.500)^{2}} = \sqrt{1.000 + 0.0625 + 1.5625 + 0.2500} = \sqrt{2.875}$$

$$\text{Den} = \sqrt{0.875} \cdot \sqrt{2.875} = 0.9354 \cdot 1.6956 = 1.5865$$

**Result:**

$$\text{sim}(I_{3}, I_{1}) = \frac{-0.1250}{1.5865} \approx -0.0788$$

## 3.5 Step 4 — Compute $\text{sim}(I_{3}, I_{2})$

Common users $U_{I_{3}, I_{2}} = \{U_{2}, U_{3}, U_{4}\}$.

**Numerator:**

$$\text{Num} = (0.000)(0.000) + (-0.750)(-0.750) + (-0.250)(-0.250) = 0.000 + 0.5625 + 0.0625 = 0.6250$$

**Denominator:**

$$\text{Den}_{I_{3}} = \sqrt{0.000^{2} + (-0.750)^{2} + (-0.250)^{2}} = \sqrt{0.6250} = 0.7906$$

$$\text{Den}_{I_{2}} = \sqrt{0.000^{2} + (-0.750)^{2} + (-0.250)^{2}} = \sqrt{0.6250} = 0.7906$$

$$\text{Den} = 0.7906 \times 0.7906 = 0.6250$$

**Result:**

$$\text{sim}(I_{3}, I_{2}) = \frac{0.6250}{0.6250} = 1.000 \quad \text{(perfectly similar on the adjusted scale)}$$

## 3.6 Step 5 — Compute $\text{sim}(I_{3}, I_{4})$

Common users $U_{I_{3}, I_{4}} = \{U_{2}, U_{3}, U_{4}\}$.

**Numerator:**

$$\text{Num} = (0.000)(-1.000) + (-0.750)(1.250) + (-0.250)(1.750)$$

$$\text{Num} = 0.000 + (-0.9375) + (-0.4375) = -1.3750$$

**Denominator:**

$$\text{Den}_{I_{3}} = \sqrt{0.6250} = 0.7906$$

$$\text{Den}_{I_{4}} = \sqrt{1.000^{2} + 1.250^{2} + 1.750^{2}} = \sqrt{1.000 + 1.5625 + 3.0625} = \sqrt{5.6250} = 2.3717$$

$$\text{Den} = 0.7906 \times 2.3717 = 1.8750$$

**Result:**

$$\text{sim}(I_{3}, I_{4}) = \frac{-1.3750}{1.8750} \approx -0.7333$$

## 3.7 Step 6 — Summary of Item-Item Similarity with $I_{3}$

| Item $j$ | $\text{sim}(I_{3}, j)$ |
| :--- | :---: |
| $I_{1}$ | $-0.0788$ |
| $I_{2}$ | $+1.0000$ |
| $I_{4}$ | $-0.7333$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the result:** $I_{2}$ is the *only* positively similar item to $I_{3}$ in this matrix. The negative similarity with $I_{4}$ indicates that whenever users rated $I_{3}$ above their personal mean, they rated $I_{4}$ below their personal mean — a **disagreement pattern**.

## 3.8 Step 7 — Predict $\hat{r}_{U_{1}, I_{3}}$ (Weighted-Sum Formula)

User $U_{1}$ has rated items $\{I_{1}, I_{2}, I_{4}\}$. All three are candidates (we are not enforcing top-k truncation here; for an exam, simply list $S = \{I_{1}, I_{2}, I_{4}\}$).

**Numerator** (sum of similarity-weighted ratings):

$$\text{Num}_{\text{pred}} = \text{sim}(I_{3},I_{1}) \cdot r_{U_{1},I_{1}} + \text{sim}(I_{3},I_{2}) \cdot r_{U_{1},I_{2}} + \text{sim}(I_{3},I_{4}) \cdot r_{U_{1},I_{4}}$$

$$\text{Num}_{\text{pred}} = (-0.0788)(5) + (1.0000)(4) + (-0.7333)(1)$$

$$\text{Num}_{\text{pred}} = -0.3940 + 4.0000 - 0.7333 = 2.8727$$

**Denominator** (sum of absolute similarities):

$$\text{Den}_{\text{pred}} = \lvert -0.0788 \rvert + \lvert 1.0000 \rvert + \lvert -0.7333 \rvert = 0.0788 + 1.0000 + 0.7333 = 1.8121$$

**Final Prediction:**

$$\hat{r}_{U_{1}, I_{3}} = \frac{2.8727}{1.8121} \approx 1.585$$

Since ratings are bounded in $[1, 5]$, the result is already valid.

## 3.9 Step 8 — Bias-Aware Alternative Prediction

For comparison, applying the **bias-aware** formula $\hat{r}_{u,i} = \bar{r}_{u} + \frac{\sum \text{sim}(i,j) \cdot (r_{u,j} - \bar{r}_{u})}{\sum \lvert \text{sim}(i,j) \rvert}$:

$$\sum \text{sim}(i,j) \cdot (r_{u,j} - \bar{r}_{u}) = (-0.0788)(1.667) + (1.0000)(0.667) + (-0.7333)(-2.333)$$

$$= -0.1314 + 0.6670 + 1.7111 = 2.2467$$

$$\hat{r}_{U_{1}, I_{3}} = 3.333 + \frac{2.2467}{1.8121} = 3.333 + 1.240 = 4.573$$

> The bias-aware variant yields a more *intuitive* prediction (User 1 is generally lenient, so adding the personalised mean lifts the score into a more reasonable range).

## 3.10 Analytical Summary Table (Examination Ready)

| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| $\text{sim}(I_{3}, I_{1})$ | $-0.0788$ |
| $\text{sim}(I_{3}, I_{2})$ | $+1.0000$ |
| $\text{sim}(I_{3}, I_{4})$ | $-0.7333$ |
| Weighted-sum prediction $\hat{r}_{U_{1}, I_{3}}$ | $1.585$ |
| Bias-aware prediction $\hat{r}_{U_{1}, I_{3}}$ | $4.573$ |
| Most influential item | $I_{2}$ (largest $\lvert \text{sim} \rvert$) |
| Interpretation | $U_{1}$ likely rates $I_{3}$ low-to-moderate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Item-based kNN Pipeline (Mermaid Flow)

The diagram below captures the **offline/online decoupling** that makes item-based CF production-ready. Every node ID is purely alphanumeric to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([User requests recommendation]) --> stepB[Fetch active user u history]
    stepB --> stepC[Lookup item-item similarity table]
    stepC --> stepD[Retrieve top-k neighbours of target item i]
    stepD --> stepE[Filter neighbours to those rated by u]
    stepE --> stepF[Apply weighted-sum prediction formula]
    stepF --> stepG[Sort unseen items by predicted score]
    stepG --> stepH[Return Top-N items to user]
    stepH --> endI([End of recommendation request])

    subgraph offline_block[OFFLINE PHASE - BATCH JOB]
        dirA[Load user-item rating matrix R] --> dirB[Compute user means r-bar u]
        dirB --> dirC[Build deviation matrix d-u-i]
        dirC --> dirD[For every item pair i j compute sim i j]
        dirD --> dirE[Store top-k neighbours per item]
    end

    offline_block -.Pre-computed lookup.-> stepC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The `offline_block` subgraph runs nightly; the `startA → endI` chain runs at request time. The dotted arrow between them is the only coupling — the pre-computed similarity table.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Alternative Schematic)

When a physical / architectural diagram is needed (e.g., for a 14-mark design question), the system decomposes into five functional blocks:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inpA[(User-Item Rating Matrix R)] --> blk1[Block 1: Data Ingestion and Preprocessing]
    blk1 --> blk2[Block 2: User Mean Computation]
    blk2 --> blk3[Block 3: Pairwise Adjusted Cosine Engine]
    blk3 --> blk4[Block 4: Neighbourhood Selection and Storage]
    blk4 --> blk5[Block 5: Online Prediction and Ranking Service]
    inpB[(Active User Request)] --> blk5
    blk5 --> outC[(Top-N Recommendation List)]
```

| Block | Function | Cardinality | Cost |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Cleanse matrix, drop sparse users/items | $O(mn)$ | One-time |
| 2 | Compute $\bar{r}_{u}$ per user | $O(mn)$ | One-time |
| 3 | Adjusted-cosine engine | $O(n^{2} \cdot \bar{\vert U_{i,j} \vert})$ | Offline |
| 4 | Top-k pruning and indexing | $O(n \cdot k \log n)$ | Offline |
| 5 | Online aggregation | $O(k)$ per query | Real-time |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**"Define item-based collaborative filtering. How does it differ from user-based collaborative filtering in terms of similarity computation?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Item-based collaborative filtering is a recommendation technique that predicts a user's interest in an item based on the ratings the user has given to **items similar to it**. Similarity is computed between **pairs of items** using rating data, not between users.
- **Similarity computation (1 mark):** In user-based CF, similarity is computed between user vectors $\mathbf{r}_{u,*}$ and $\mathbf{r}_{v,*}$. In item-based CF, similarity is computed between item vectors $\mathbf{r}_{*,i}$ and $\mathbf{r}_{*,j}$, using metrics like cosine, adjusted cosine, or item-Pearson.
- **Key difference (1 mark):** Item-item similarity is more *stable* over time (item relationships rarely change), enables *offline pre-computation*, and yields a *denser* co-rating matrix, making it more scalable and accurate than user-based CF in sparse datasets.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**"Explain the significance of the 'adjusted cosine similarity' in item-based recommendation. Why is plain cosine insufficient?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Adjusted cosine formula (1 mark):** $\text{sim}_{adj}(i,j) = \frac{\sum_{u \in U_{i,j}} (r_{u,i} - \bar{r}_{u})(r_{u,j} - \bar{r}_{u})}{\sqrt{\sum_{u}(r_{u,i}-\bar{r}_{u})^{2}} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{u}(r_{u,j}-\bar{r}_{u})^{2}}}$
- **Why plain cosine is insufficient (1 mark):** Plain cosine treats raw rating magnitudes, so a user who gives only 4s and 5s will appear to "disagree" with a user who gives 1s and 2s even when their *relative* preferences are identical.
- **Significance of adjustment (1 mark):** By subtracting each user's mean $\bar{r}_{u}$, adjusted cosine removes the **user-bias effect**, capturing *relative* preference co-movement between items — which is the true signal of substitutability.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay / Numerical (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In item-based CF numericals, students frequently **mix up user means with item means**, or **forget to subtract** them in the deviation step, leading to wrong similarity signs. Always *write down the deviation matrix explicitly* before plugging into the numerator. Also remember to **truncate neighbours to top-k when specified** — do not include weakly similar items that destroy the prediction.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]`

**(a)** *With a neat block diagram, describe the **two-phase architecture** of an item-based nearest neighbour recommender system. Clearly differentiate the offline and online phases, listing the key computations in each. **(7 marks)***

**(b)** *Given the user–item rating matrix below (scale 1–5, "?" = unrated), predict $\hat{r}_{U_{1}, I_{3}}$ using the **item-based weighted-sum prediction** with **adjusted cosine similarity**. Show every deviation step. **(7 marks)***

| | $I_{1}$ | $I_{2}$ | $I_{3}$ | $I_{4}$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $U_{1}$ | 5 | 4 | ? | 1 |
| $U_{2}$ | 4 | 3 | 3 | 2 |
| $U_{3}$ | 2 | 1 | 1 | 3 |
| $U_{4}$ | 1 | 2 | 2 | 4 |
| $U_{5}$ | 3 | ? | 4 | ? |

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Two-phase architecture (7 marks):**
- **Phase 1 – Offline (similarity build):** Ingest matrix → compute $\bar{r}_{u}$ → for each item pair $(i,j)$ compute $\text{sim}_{adj}(i,j)$ → prune to top-k → store in a lookup table. Cost is $O(n^{2})$ but amortised; runs once per day. **(3.5 marks)**
- **Phase 2 – Online (prediction):** Receive $(u, i)$ → fetch top-k neighbours of $i$ → filter to those rated by $u$ → apply $\hat{r}_{u,i} = \frac{\sum_{j} \text{sim}(i,j) \cdot r_{u,j}}{\sum_{j} \vert \text{sim}(i,j) \vert}$ → return Top-N. Cost is $O(k)$ per request. **(2.5 marks)**
- **Block diagram (1 mark):** As shown in Section 4.2.

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**
1. User means: $\bar{r}_{U_{1}} = 3.33$, $\bar{r}_{U_{2}} = 3.00$, $\bar{r}_{U_{3}} = 1.75$, $\bar{r}_{U_{4}} = 2.25$, $\bar{r}_{U_{5}} = 3.50$. **(1 mark)**
2. Deviation matrix (as derived in §3.3). **(1 mark)**
3. $\text{sim}(I_{3}, I_{1}) = -0.0788$, $\text{sim}(I_{3}, I_{2}) = +1.0000$, $\text{sim}(I_{3}, I_{4}) = -0.7333$. **(3 marks — 1 each)**
4. Weighted-sum: $\hat{r}_{U_{1}, I_{3}} = \frac{(-0.0788)(5) + (1.0)(4) + (-0.7333)(1)}{0.0788 + 1.0 + 0.7333} = \frac{2.8727}{1.8121} \approx 1.585$. **(2 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]`

**(a)** *Explain the three commonly used item-similarity metrics — **Cosine**, **Adjusted Cosine**, and **Pearson Correlation** — with their respective formulas. Discuss when each is most appropriate. **(7 marks)***

**(b)** *For the rating matrix in Question A, recompute the prediction $\hat{r}_{U_{1}, I_{3}}$ using the **bias-aware** formula. Compare the two predictions and justify the observed difference. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three similarity metrics (7 marks):**
- **Cosine (2 marks):** $\text{sim}_{\cos}(i,j) = \frac{\mathbf{r}_{*,i} \cdot \mathbf{r}_{*,j}}{\lVert \mathbf{r}_{*,i} \rVert \lVert \mathbf{r}_{*,j} \rVert}$. Treats items as vectors; angle-only metric. Best when rating scale is consistent across users.
- **Adjusted Cosine (2 marks):** Same as cosine but on **deviation** $(r_{u,i} - \bar{r}_{u})$ rather than raw rating. Best when users have differing rating scales (most real-world datasets).
- **Pearson on items (2 marks):** Correlates deviations from **item means** $\bar{r}_{i}$. Useful when item-level bias (some items are universally liked) dominates.
- **One-line comparative remark (1 mark).**

**(b) Bias-aware recomputation (7 marks):**
1. Already have $\bar{r}_{U_{1}} = 3.33$, $\bar{r}_{U_{2}} = 3.00$, $\bar{r}_{U_{3}} = 1.75$, $\bar{r}_{U_{4}} = 2.25$, $\bar{r}_{U_{5}} = 3.50$. **(1 mark)**
2. Use the same three similarity values from §3.4–§3.6. **(1 mark)**
3. Compute $\sum \text{sim}(i,j) \cdot (r_{u,j} - \bar{r}_{u})$:
   - $(-0.0788)(5 - 3.33) + (1.0)(4 - 3.33) + (-0.7333)(1 - 3.33) = -0.1314 + 0.6670 + 1.7111 = 2.2467$. **(2.5 marks)**
4. $\hat{r}_{U_{1}, I_{3}} = 3.33 + \frac{2.2467}{1.8121} \approx 4.573$. **(1.5 marks)**
5. **Comparison & justification (1 mark):** The bias-aware prediction (≈4.57) is higher than the raw weighted sum (≈1.59) because $U_{1}$ is a *lenient rater* ($\bar{r}_{u} = 3.33$), and the bias-aware formula anchors the prediction around the user's own mean, giving a more personalised, scale-aware estimate.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Item-based kNN (Module 1, PECDT741)**

- **Core idea:** Predict $\hat{r}_{u,i}$ by looking at items $j$ **similar to $i$** that user $u$ has already rated.
- **Similarity target:** Items (columns of $R$), not users.
- **Best metric for KTU 2024:** **Adjusted Cosine** (subtracts $\bar{r}_{u}$).
- **Numerator trap:** Forgetting to subtract user mean → spurious similarities.
- **Denominator trap:** Using raw ratings' norm instead of deviation norms.
- **Domain of summation:** Always restricted to $U_{i,j}$ (co-rated users).
- **Online cost:** $O(k)$ — pre-computation is what makes it scalable.
- **Stability:** Item-item matrix changes only when new items arrive; user-user matrix churns on every rating.
- **Formula to memorise:** $\hat{r}_{u,i} = \frac{\sum \text{sim}(i,j) \cdot r_{u,j}}{\sum \vert \text{sim}(i,j) \vert}$ and its bias-aware variant with $\bar{r}_{u}$.
- **Top-N output:** Sort all unseen items by $\hat{r}_{u,i}$ and pick the top *N* (typically $N = 5, 10, 20$).
- **Real-world precedent:** Amazon's item-to-item CF (Linden, Smith & York, 2003).
- **Limitation:** Suffers from the *cold-start* problem for newly added items with no ratings.
- **Sparsity remedy:** Dimensionality reduction (SVD) is often applied **after** item-based kNN as a hybrid (covered later in the course).
- **Numeric sanity check:** If $\hat{r}_{u,i} < 1$ or $> 5$, **clamp** to the rating range; document this in your answer for full marks.
- **Neighbourhood size:** $k = 20$–$50$ typically balances accuracy and noise; never use the entire item set.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Collaborative Recommendation Ratings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT741 - RECOMMENDATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Collaborative Recommendation Ratings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Collaborative Recommendation Ratings** refer to the structured, quantitative evaluations (e.g., numerical scores on a 1–5 scale, binary like/disklike signals, or implicit behavioral signals) that a **user** assigns to an **item** within a recommender system. These ratings are organized into a **User–Item Rating Matrix** denoted $R \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$, where $m$ users and $n$ items are represented as rows and columns respectively. Each cell $r_{u,i}$ stores the rating provided by user $u$ for item $i$, with $r_{u,i} = \emptyset$ when the user has not yet rated the item.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> Collaborative Filtering (CF) is the *process of predicting the utility (rating) of an item for a particular user based on the historical ratings of other users who are behaviorally similar*. The "rating" is therefore the atomic data element that powers every CF algorithm.

The fundamental operation of collaborative recommendation is **rating prediction**, expressed formally as:

$$\hat{r}_{u,i} = \text{predict}(u, i \mid R)$$

where the predictor $\text{predict}(\cdot)$ leverages either **neighborhood-based** (memory-based) or **model-based** (latent-factor) learning over the observed entries of $R$.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World)

Imagine a circle of friends where each person watches movies and rates them on **IMDb (1 to 10)**. You haven't yet seen the sci-fi film *Interstellar*, but two of your friends — whom you trust because they consistently rate movies the same way you do — both gave it a **9/10**. You are therefore highly likely to enjoy the movie too.

In this analogy:
- The **friends** represent *neighboring users* with high *similarity* to you.
- The **IMDb score** represents the *rating value* $r_{u,i}$.
- The act of trusting their opinion represents the *weighted aggregation* of neighborhood ratings.
- The **sparse ledger of scores** maintained on a shared notepad represents the *User–Item Rating Matrix* $R$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The power of collaborative recommendation comes **not** from analyzing the *content* of movies (genre, cast, plot), but purely from the *collective behavioral patterns* of users. This is why it is called "**collaborative**" — users "collaborate" implicitly through their ratings.

## 1.3 Core Vocabulary (KTU Glossary)

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| User | $u, v \in U$ | An individual whose preferences the system models |
| Item | $i, j \in I$ | A product, movie, song, or article to be recommended |
| Rating | $r_{u,i}$ | Numerical preference score from user $u$ for item $i$ |
| Rating Matrix | $R$ | The $m \times n$ matrix of all observed ratings |
| Sparsity | $1 - \frac{\vert\text{observed}\vert}{m \cdot n}$ | Fraction of *missing* entries in $R$ |
| Mean Rating | $\bar{r}_u$ | Average of all items rated by user $u$ |
| Neighborhood | $N(u)$ | Set of $k$ users most similar to $u$ |

## 1.4 Types of Ratings

1. **Explicit Ratings** — Directly provided by the user (e.g., 1–5 stars, thumbs up/down). They are *high-quality* but *scarce*.
2. **Implicit Ratings** — Inferred from behavior (clicks, purchases, dwell time). They are *abundant* but *noisy*.
3. **Binary Ratings** — $r_{u,i} \in \{0, 1\}$ (e.g., purchased or not).
4. **Unary Ratings** — Only positive feedback is observed ($r_{u,i} = 1$ if interacted, else unknown). No negative signal exists.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sparsity** is the **single biggest challenge** in collaborative recommendation. On Amazon, the rating matrix is over **99.9% sparse** — most users have rated only a handful of items.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline of Collaborative Rating Prediction

The collaborative recommendation process follows a **five-stage pipeline**:

1. **Stage 1 — Matrix Construction:** Build $R$ from explicit or implicit feedback.
2. **Stage 2 — Similarity Computation:** Quantify pairwise closeness between users (or items) using a *kernel function* (Pearson, Cosine, Jaccard, Spearman).
3. **Stage 3 — Neighborhood Selection:** Retain the **top-$k$ most similar** users (or items) to form $N(u)$.
4. **Stage 4 — Weighted Aggregation:** Combine the ratings of $N(u)$ using similarity-weighted averaging (with mean-centering to remove user bias).
5. **Stage 5 — Rating Prediction:** Produce $\hat{r}_{u,i}$ — the predicted score for the target user–item pair.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Stage

- **Why mean-center ratings?** User $A$ might rate everything a "3" while user $B$ rates everything a "5". Absolute scores are *misleading*; we need **relative preferences**. Subtracting $\bar{r}_u$ normalizes for personal bias.
- **Why use a neighborhood?** CF assumes the *homophily principle* — *similar users exhibit similar preferences*. Aggregating the top-$k$ neighbors gives a robust estimate.
- **Why weighted?** Not all neighbors are equally informative. Users with high similarity contribute more; dissimilar (or anti-correlated) users are down-weighted or excluded.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Notation | When Used |
|---|---------|----------|-----------|
| 1 | $\bar{r}_u = \frac{1}{\vert I_u \vert}\sum_{i \in I_u} r_{u,i}$ | $I_u$ = items rated by $u$ | Mean centering |
| 2 | $\text{sim}_{\cos}(u,v) = \frac{\vec{r}_u \cdot \vec{r}_v}{\Vert \vec{r}_u \Vert \cdot \Vert \vec{r}_v \Vert} = \frac{\sum_{i \in I_{uv}} r_{u,i} \cdot r_{v,i}}{\sqrt{\sum_{i \in I_u} r_{u,i}^{2}} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i \in I_v} r_{v,i}^{2}}}$ | $I_{uv}$ = co-rated items | Cosine similarity (raw) |
| 3 | $\text{sim}_{\text{pearson}}(u,v) = \frac{\sum_{i \in I_{uv}} (r_{u,i} - \bar{r}_u)(r_{v,i} - \bar{r}_v)}{\sqrt{\sum_{i \in I_{uv}} (r_{u,i} - \bar{r}_u)^{2}} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i \in I_{uv}} (r_{v,i} - \bar{r}_v)^{2}}}$ | Co-rated only | Pearson correlation |
| 4 | $\text{sim}_{\text{adj-cos}}(i,j) = \frac{\sum_{u \in U_{ij}} (r_{u,i} - \bar{r}_u)(r_{u,j} - \bar{r}_u)}{\sqrt{\sum_{u \in U_{ij}} (r_{u,i} - \bar{r}_u)^{2}} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{u \in U_{ij}} (r_{u,j} - \bar{r}_u)^{2}}}$ | $U_{ij}$ = users who rated both | Adjusted Cosine (item-based) |
| 5 | $\hat{r}_{u,i} = \bar{r}_u + \frac{\sum_{v \in N(u)} \text{sim}(u,v) \cdot (r_{v,i} - \bar{r}_v)}{\sum_{v \in N(u)} \vert \text{sim}(u,v) \vert}$ | $N(u)$ = neighbors of $u$ | User-based CF prediction |
| 6 | $\hat{r}_{u,i} = \frac{\sum_{j \in N(i)} \text{sim}(i,j) \cdot r_{u,j}}{\sum_{j \in N(i)} \vert \text{sim}(i,j) \vert}$ | $N(i)$ = similar items | Item-based CF prediction |
| 7 | $\text{Sparsity} = 1 - \frac{\text{\# observed ratings}}{m \times n}$ | $m$ users, $n$ items | Data-scarcity metric |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the backbone of production systems at **Netflix, Spotify, and Amazon**. Netflix's *Cinematch* (precursor to the famous **Netflix Prize**) was built on a weighted blend of user-based and item-based collaborative filtering using variations of the formulas above.

## 2.4 Comparison of Similarity Measures

| Property | Cosine | Pearson | Adjusted Cosine | Jaccard |
|----------|--------|---------|-----------------|---------|
| Handles rating bias | ❌ (raw magnitudes) | ✅ (mean-centered) | ✅ (mean-centered) | ❌ |
| Best suited for | Sparse data | User-user CF | Item-item CF | Binary ratings |
| Co-rating requirement | All co-rated | Co-rated | Co-rated | Intersection / Union |
| Range | $[-1, 1]$ | $[-1, 1]$ | $[-1, 1]$ | $[0, 1]$ |

# 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Mean-Centering in User-Based CF

The intuition behind mean-centering is that **two users may have radically different scoring habits**. We strip the user-specific bias before computing similarity.

**Step 1 — Define Mean Rating of User $u$:**

The mean rating of user $u$ across all items they have rated is given by the arithmetic average:

$$\bar{r}_u = \frac{1}{\vert I_u \vert} \sum_{i \in I_u} r_{u,i}$$

where $I_u = \{ i \in I : r_{u,i} \neq \emptyset \}$ is the set of items rated by user $u$, and $\vert I_u \vert$ is the cardinality (count) of that set.

**Step 2 — Subtract the Mean to Get Centered Vector:**

The *centered rating* is defined as:

$$r'_{u,i} = r_{u,i} - \bar{r}_u$$

This new quantity represents the *deviation* of user $u$'s rating from their personal average. A positive $r'_{u,i}$ means the user *liked the item more than usual*; a negative value means the user *disliked it more than usual*.

**Step 3 — Plug into Cosine Similarity:**

Replacing the raw ratings with the centered ratings in the cosine formula gives us the **Pearson Correlation** formula. We compute the dot product of centered vectors divided by their norms:

$$\text{sim}_{\text{pearson}}(u,v) = \frac{\sum_{i \in I_{uv}} (r_{u,i} - \bar{r}_u)(r_{v,i} - \bar{r}_v)}{\sqrt{\sum_{i \in I_{uv}} (r_{u,i} - \bar{r}_u)^{2}} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i \in I_{uv}} (r_{v,i} - \bar{r}_v)^{2}}}$$

This is *mathematically equivalent* to the cosine similarity applied to **mean-centered** rating vectors, restricted to the *co-rated* items $I_{uv}$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Rating Prediction Formula

We want to predict $\hat{r}_{u,i}$ — the rating that user $u$ *would* give to item $i$.

**Step 1 — Start with Naive Average (baseline):**

A first approximation is simply the mean rating of user $u$:

$$\hat{r}_{u,i} \approx \bar{r}_u$$

This captures the user's *baseline* tendency but ignores information from peers.

**Step 2 — Incorporate Neighbor Deviation:**

For each neighbor $v \in N(u)$ who has rated item $i$, compute the *deviation* from $v$'s mean:

$$\Delta_{v,i} = r_{v,i} - \bar{r}_v$$

This deviation tells us whether $v$ liked $i$ more or less than $v$'s typical item.

**Step 3 — Weight by Similarity:**

Not all neighbors are equally trustworthy. Weight each deviation by the similarity $\text{sim}(u,v)$:

$$\text{Weighted Deviation} = \sum_{v \in N(u)} \text{sim}(u,v) \cdot (r_{v,i} - \bar{r}_v)$$

**Step 4 — Normalize the Weights:**

Divide by the sum of absolute similarities to obtain a *weighted average deviation* (a value bounded roughly within $[-5, +5]$ for a 1–5 scale):

$$\text{Weighted Mean Deviation} = \frac{\sum_{v \in N(u)} \text{sim}(u,v) \cdot (r_{v,i} - \bar{r}_v)}{\sum_{v \in N(u)} \vert \text{sim}(u,v) \vert}$$

**Step 5 — Add the User's Mean (Shift Back):**

To recover the predicted rating on the original scale, add the user's mean back:

$$\hat{r}_{u,i} = \bar{r}_u + \frac{\sum_{v \in N(u)} \text{sim}(u,v) \cdot (r_{v,i} - \bar{r}_v)}{\sum_{v \in N(u)} \vert \text{sim}(u,v) \vert}$$

This is the **canonical user-based CF prediction formula** evaluated in the KTU syllabus.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Hand-Computing Pearson + Prediction)

Consider the following **4-user, 4-item** rating matrix. The `?` denotes an unrated entry that we want to predict.

| User | Item 1 | Item 2 | Item 3 | Item 4 |
|------|:------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|
| **U1** | 5 | 3 | 4 | 4 |
| **U2** | 3 | 1 | 2 | **?** |
| **U3** | 4 | 2 | 3 | 5 |
| **U4** | 2 | 4 | 1 | 3 |

**Goal:** Predict $\hat{r}_{U2, I4}$ using the top-$k=2$ most similar neighbors to $U2$.

### Step A — Compute Means

For user $U2$, the mean rating over the three observed items $\{I1, I2, I3\}$ is:

$$\bar{r}_{U2} = \frac{3 + 1 + 2}{3} = \frac{6}{3} = 2.0$$

For the candidate neighbors $U1, U3, U4$ (using their three observed items $\{I1, I2, I3\}$):

$$\bar{r}_{U1} = \frac{5+3+4}{3} = \frac{12}{3} = 4.0$$

$$\bar{r}_{U3} = \frac{4+2+3}{3} = \frac{9}{3} = 3.0$$

$$\bar{r}_{U4} = \frac{2+4+1}{3} = \frac{7}{3} \approx 2.333$$

### Step B — Compute Pearson Similarity (U2 vs U1)

Common items co-rated by $U2$ and $U1$: $I_{U2,U1} = \{I1, I2, I3\}$.

Numerator (sum of cross-products of deviations):

$$N_{21} = (3-2)(5-4) + (1-2)(3-4) + (2-2)(4-4) = (1)(1) + (-1)(-1) + (0)(0) = 1 + 1 + 0 = 2$$

Denominator (product of norms of deviations):

$$D_{21} = \sqrt{1^{2}+(-1)^{2}+0^{2}} \cdot \sqrt{1^{2}+(-1)^{2}+0^{2}} = \sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{2} = 2$$

Pearson similarity:

$$\text{sim}(U2, U1) = \frac{2}{2} = 1.0$$

### Step C — Compute Pearson Similarity (U2 vs U3)

$$N_{23} = (3-2)(4-3) + (1-2)(2-3) + (2-2)(3-3) = (1)(1) + (-1)(-1) + 0 = 2$$

$$D_{23} = \sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{2} = 2$$

$$\text{sim}(U2, U3) = \frac{2}{2} = 1.0$$

### Step D — Compute Pearson Similarity (U2 vs U4)

$$N_{24} = (3-2)\left(2 - \tfrac{7}{3}\right) + (1-2)\left(4 - \tfrac{7}{3}\right) + (2-2)\left(1 - \tfrac{7}{3}\right)$$

$$= (1)\left(-\tfrac{1}{3}\right) + (-1)\left(\tfrac{5}{3}\right) + 0 = -\tfrac{1}{3} - \tfrac{5}{3} = -2$$

$$D_{24} = \sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{\left(-\tfrac{1}{3}\right)^{2} + \left(\tfrac{5}{3}\right)^{2} + 0} = \sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{\tfrac{26}{9}} = \frac{\sqrt{52}}{3} \approx 2.404$$

$$\text{sim}(U2, U4) = \frac{-2}{2.404} \approx -0.832$$

### Step E — Select Top-$k$ Neighbors

We pick the **top-2** with highest similarity: $N(U2) = \{U1, U3\}$ (both with $\text{sim} = 1.0$). $U4$ is excluded due to its *negative* correlation (anti-similar user).

### Step F — Predict $\hat{r}_{U2, I4}$

Compute the weighted mean deviation:

$$\text{Numerator} = 1.0 \cdot (r_{U1, I4} - \bar{r}_{U1}) + 1.0 \cdot (r_{U3, I4} - \bar{r}_{U3})$$

$$= 1.0 \cdot (4 - 4) + 1.0 \cdot (5 - 3) = 0 + 2 = 2$$

$$\text{Denominator} = \vert 1.0 \vert + \vert 1.0 \vert = 2$$

Apply the prediction formula:

$$\hat{r}_{U2, I4} = \bar{r}_{U2} + \frac{2}{2} = 2 + 1 = \boxed{3.0}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The system predicts that user $U2$ would rate *Item 4* a **3 out of 5**, derived by leveraging the rating behavior of two *positively correlated* users ($U1$ and $U3$) and adding that to $U2$'s baseline average. This is the *classical KTU-evaluable* workflow.

### Step G — Error Analysis (Optional, for KTU depth)

If the true rating were known to be $r_{U2, I4}^{\text{true}} = 2$, the **Mean Absolute Error (MAE)** contribution of this prediction would be:

$$\text{MAE}_i = \vert 2 - 3 \vert = 1$$
