# 🗺️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Collaborative Filtering — End-to-End Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Collect User-Item Ratings] --> B[Construct Rating Matrix R]
    B --> C{Choose CF Strategy}
    C -->|User-Based| D[Compute User-User Similarity]
    C -->|Item-Based| E[Compute Item-Item Similarity]
    D --> F[Select Top-k Neighbors N_u]
    E --> G[Select Top-k Similar Items N_i]
    F --> H[Weighted Aggregation of Ratings]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Predict Rating r_hat]
    I --> J{R_hat exceeds threshold?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Generate Top-N Recommendation]
    J -->|No| L[Defer / Do Not Recommend]
```

## 4.2 Rating Matrix Schematic (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph R[User-Item Rating Matrix R]
        direction LR
        R1[U1: 5, 3, 4, 4] --- R2[U2: 3, 1, 2, ?]
        R2 --- R3[U3: 4, 2, 3, 5]
        R3 --- R4[U4: 2, 4, 1, 3]
    end
    TARGET[Target Cell: U2, I4 = ?]
    R2 -.-> TARGET
    PEER1[Neighbor: U1 sim=1.0] -.-> TARGET
    PEER2[Neighbor: U3 sim=1.0] -.-> TARGET
```

## 4.3 Similarity → Neighborhood → Prediction (Data-Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Stage]
        U[User u]
        I[Item i]
    end
    subgraph SIM[Similarity Stage]
        S1[Pearson sim]
        S2[Cosine sim]
        S3[Adj-Cosine sim]
    end
    subgraph NBR[Neighborhood Stage]
        TOPK[Top-k users in N_u]
    end
    subgraph PRED[Prediction Stage]
        AGG[Weighted Aggregation]
        OUT[Predicted r_hat_u_i]
    end
    U --> S1
    U --> S2
    I --> S3
    S1 --> TOPK
    S2 --> TOPK
    TOPK --> AGG
    AGG --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the diagrams:** The first flowchart shows the *full end-to-end pipeline* of a CF system. The second shows the *target cell* (U2, I4) being filled by leveraging peer cells. The third depicts the *mathematical workflow* from raw inputs to final prediction.

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between explicit and implicit ratings with two real-world examples for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Explicit Rating | Implicit Rating |
|--------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Definition | User *directly states* a numerical preference | System *infers* preference from user actions |
| Accuracy | High (subjective but intentional) | Lower (proxy signals) |
| Volume | Scarce | Abundant |
| Example 1 | A 5-star movie review on Netflix | A user *finishes watching* a TV series |
| Example 2 | A "thumbs-up" on Spotify | A user *adds a song to a playlist* |
| Example 3 | A 1–10 review on Amazon | A user *re-purchases* a product |

> **Valuation Tip:** Award **1.5 marks** for definition + difference, **1 mark** for examples, **0.5 mark** for example quality (e.g., 2 each, clearly classified).

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State and explain the formula for Pearson Correlation-based user similarity in collaborative filtering.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Pearson Correlation similarity between two users $u$ and $v$ is defined as:

$$\text{sim}_{\text{pearson}}(u,v) = \frac{\sum_{i \in I_{uv}} (r_{u,i} - \bar{r}_u)(r_{v,i} - \bar{r}_v)}{\sqrt{\sum_{i \in I_{uv}} (r_{u,i} - \bar{r}_u)^{2}} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i \in I_{uv}} (r_{v,i} - \bar{r}_v)^{2}}}$$

**Key elements:**
- **Numerator:** Sum of cross-products of *mean-centered* deviations over co-rated items.
- **Denominator:** Product of Euclidean norms of the deviation vectors.
- **Range:** $[-1, +1]$, where $+1$ = perfectly aligned tastes, $-1$ = opposite tastes, $0$ = no correlation.
- **Advantage:** *Robust to user-specific rating bias* (e.g., a generous raters and a strict raters can still be detected as similar).

> **Valuation Tip:** Award **1.5 marks** for formula, **1 mark** for the co-rated item notation, **0.5 mark** for the range and advantage.

---

## 📚 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** *Explain the different types of rating scales used in collaborative filtering. Discuss the impact of rating scale granularity on recommendation quality.*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Types of Rating Scales (3.5 marks):**

- **Binary Ratings:** Two values only — $r_{u,i} \in \{0, 1\}$. Examples: "Liked / Not Liked" on YouTube, "Purchased / Not Purchased" on Amazon. *Simple but uninformative.*
- **Unary Ratings:** Only the positive class is observed ($r_{u,i} = 1$ if interaction occurred; nothing if not). Examples: Page views, song plays. *Cannot distinguish dislike from indifference.*
- **Multi-level Ordinal Ratings:** Discrete ordered levels, e.g., 1–5 stars. Examples: Amazon (1–5), Netflix (1–5), IMDb (1–10). *Captures intensity of preference.*
- **Continuous Ratings:** Real-valued, e.g., a slider on a 0.0–10.0 scale. *More granular but cognitively taxing for users.*
- **Likert-Scale Ratings:** 5- or 7-point agreement scale ("Strongly Disagree → Strongly Agree"). Common in survey-driven recommenders.

**2. Impact of Granularity on Recommendation Quality (3.5 marks):**

| Granularity | Pros | Cons |
|-------------|------|------|
| **Too Coarse** (binary) | Easy for users; less fatigue | Cannot distinguish "loved" from "liked"; weak similarity signal |
| **Moderate** (1–5 stars) | Best trade-off; widely used (Netflix) | Users interpret scales subjectively (a "4" means different things to different users) |
| **Too Fine** (1–100) | Captures nuance; better for power users | High cognitive load → many users skip rating; sparsity increases |

> **Key Insight:** Empirically, the **1–5 star scale** offers the best trade-off between *information density* and *user cognitive effort*. Granularity beyond 10 points typically yields diminishing returns and increases sparsity.

---

**(b) [7 marks]** *Given the following user–item rating matrix (entries on a 1–5 scale), predict the missing rating $r_{U3, I4}$ using **User-based Collaborative Filtering** with **Pearson similarity** and top-$k=2$ neighbors. Show all steps.*

| User | Item 1 | Item 2 | Item 3 | Item 4 |
|------|:------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|
| U1 | 5 | 2 | 4 | 3 |
| U2 | 4 | 3 | 5 | 2 |
| U3 | 3 | 4 | 2 | **?** |
| U4 | 2 | 5 | 3 | 4 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute User Means (using items I1, I2, I3):**

$$\bar{r}_{U1} = \frac{5+2+4}{3} = \frac{11}{3} \approx 3.667$$

$$\bar{r}_{U2} = \frac{4+3+5}{3} = 4.0$$

$$\bar{r}_{U3} = \frac{3+4+2}{3} = 3.0$$

$$\bar{r}_{U4} = \frac{2+5+3}{3} = \frac{10}{3} \approx 3.333$$

**Step 2 — Compute Pearson Sim(U3, U1) on $I_{U3,U1} = \{I1, I2, I3\}$:**

$$N = (3-3)(5-3.667) + (4-3)(2-3.667) + (2-3)(4-3.667)$$
$$= 0 + (1)(-1.667) + (-1)(0.333) = -1.667 - 0.333 = -2.0$$

$$D = \sqrt{0 + 1 + 1} \cdot \sqrt{1.667^{2} + 1.667^{2} + 0.333^{2}} = \sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{2.778 + 2.778 + 0.111}$$

$$= \sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{5.667} \approx 1.414 \cdot 2.380 \approx 3.367$$

$$\text{sim}(U3, U1) = \frac{-2}{3.367} \approx -0.594$$

**Step 3 — Compute Pearson Sim(U3, U2) on $I_{U3,U2} = \{I1, I2, I3\}$:**

$$N = (3-3)(4-4) + (4-3)(3-4) + (2-3)(5-4) = 0 + (-1) + (-1) = -2$$

$$D = \sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{0+1+1} = 2$$

$$\text{sim}(U3, U2) = \frac{-2}{2} = -1.0$$

**Step 4 — Compute Pearson Sim(U3, U4) on $I_{U3,U4} = \{I1, I2, I3\}$:**

$$N = (3-3)(2-3.333) + (4-3)(5-3.333) + (2-3)(3-3.333)$$
$$= 0 + (1)(1.667) + (-1)(-0.333) = 1.667 + 0.333 = 2.0$$

$$D = \sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{1.333 + 2.778 + 0.111} = \sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{4.222} \approx 1.414 \cdot 2.055 \approx 2.906$$

$$\text{sim}(U3, U4) = \frac{2}{2.906} \approx 0.688$$

**Step 5 — Select Top-$k=2$ Neighbors:**

Similarities: $U1 = -0.594$, $U2 = -1.0$, $U4 = 0.688$.

$N(U3) = \{U4, U1\}$ — top-2 by similarity. (If we restrict to **positive** similarities, then $N(U3) = \{U4\}$ with $k=1$.)

> **Valuation Note:** Award full credit if student takes top-2 *by absolute value* (i.e., includes $U1$) OR top-2 *by positive similarity* (uses only $U4$). Both are valid KTU-accepted approaches.

**Step 6 (Approach A: Top-2 by absolute value, $N = \{U4, U1\}$):**

$$\hat{r}_{U3, I4} = 3 + \frac{0.688 \cdot (4 - 3.333) + (-0.594) \cdot (3 - 3.667)}{0.688 + 0.594}$$

$$= 3 + \frac{0.688 \cdot 0.667 + (-0.594) \cdot (-0.667)}{1.282}$$

$$= 3 + \frac{0.459 + 0.396}{1.282} = 3 + \frac{0.855}{1.282} \approx 3 + 0.667 = \boxed{3.67}$$

**Step 6 (Approach B: Top-1 positive, $N = \{U4\}$):**

$$\hat{r}_{U3, I4} = 3 + \frac{0.688 \cdot 0.667}{0.688} = 3 + 0.667 = \boxed{3.67}$$

> **Final Answer:** The predicted rating $\hat{r}_{U3, I4} \approx 3.67$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to **mean-center** before applying Pearson — results in *raw-cosine* values that are not bounded to $[-1, 1]$.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Including the target user ($U3$) in their *own* neighborhood. Always exclude self-similarity.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Dividing by *zero* in the denominator when a user has rated only one item, or when two users have *no co-rated items*. State this edge case explicitly.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting absolute values in the denominator's weight sum — similarity is *signed* and the denominator normalizes by $\sum \vert \text{sim} \vert$, not $\sum \text{sim}$.
> - **Mark Loss:** Typically **2–3 marks** lost per question for skipping steps. KTU expects each numerical transition to be shown.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** *Derive the **rating prediction formula** for user-based collaborative filtering. Explain each term clearly and discuss the role of mean-centering in removing user bias.*

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

We seek to predict $\hat{r}_{u,i}$ — the rating that user $u$ would assign to item $i$, given historical ratings from similar users.

**Step 1:** A trivial baseline prediction is the mean of user $u$'s known ratings:

$$\hat{r}_{u,i} \approx \bar{r}_u$$

**Step 2:** For each neighbor $v \in N(u)$ who has rated item $i$, define the **deviation** from $v$'s mean:

$$\Delta_{v,i} = r_{v,i} - \bar{r}_v$$

**Step 3:** Weight each deviation by the similarity $\text{sim}(u,v)$ between $u$ and $v$:

$$\text{Weighted Sum} = \sum_{v \in N(u)} \text{sim}(u,v) \cdot (r_{v,i} - \bar{r}_v)$$

**Step 4:** Normalize by the sum of absolute similarities:

$$\text{Normalized Deviation} = \frac{\sum_{v \in N(u)} \text{sim}(u,v) \cdot (r_{v,i} - \bar{r}_v)}{\sum_{v \in N(u)} \vert \text{sim}(u,v) \vert}$$

**Step 5:** Add the user's mean back to recover a rating on the original scale:

$$\hat{r}_{u,i} = \bar{r}_u + \frac{\sum_{v \in N(u)} \text{sim}(u,v) \cdot (r_{v,i} - \bar{r}_v)}{\sum_{v \in N(u)} \vert \text{sim}(u,v) \vert}$$

**Term-by-Term Explanation (2 marks):**

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| $\hat{r}_{u,i}$ | Predicted rating of user $u$ for item $i$ |
| $\bar{r}_u$ | Mean rating of user $u$ (their personal baseline) |
| $r_{v,i}$ | Observed rating of neighbor $v$ for item $i$ |
| $\bar{r}_v$ | Mean rating of neighbor $v$ (normalization for $v$'s bias) |
| $\text{sim}(u,v)$ | Pearson (or cosine) similarity between $u$ and $v$ |
| $N(u)$ | Set of $k$ most similar neighbors to $u$ |

**Role of Mean-Centering (1 mark):**
Mean-centering removes **user-specific rating bias**. Consider: User $A$ gives an average rating of 4.5 (a "generous" rater), while User $B$ averages 2.0 (a "strict" rater). Without mean-centering, the raw-rating similarity would incorrectly classify them as different. By subtracting the mean, we compare *relative preferences* (e.g., both loved Action but disliked Romance) rather than absolute scores. This dramatically improves the accuracy of the nearest-neighbor selection and the final prediction.

---

**(b) [7 marks]** *For the following user–item matrix, compute the **cosine similarity** between users $U1$ and $U2$, and predict $\hat{r}_{U1, I3}$ using all users as neighbors.*

| User | Item 1 | Item 2 | Item 3 | Item 4 |
|------|:------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|
| U1 | 4 | 5 | **?** | 3 |
| U2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 2 |
| U3 | 5 | 5 | 4 | 4 |
| U4 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 1 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Cosine Similarity between U1 and U2 (on co-rated items I1, I2, I4):**

$$\vec{r}_{U1} = (4, 5, 3), \quad \vec{r}_{U2} = (3, 4, 2)$$

Dot product:

$$\vec{r}_{U1} \cdot \vec{r}_{U2} = (4)(3) + (5)(4) + (3)(2) = 12 + 20 + 6 = 38$$

Norms:

$$\Vert \vec{r}_{U1} \Vert = \sqrt{4^{2}+5^{2}+3^{2}} = \sqrt{16+25+9} = \sqrt{50} = 5\sqrt{2} \approx 7.071$$

$$\Vert \vec{r}_{U2} \Vert = \sqrt{3^{2}+4^{2}+2^{2}} = \sqrt{9+16+4} = \sqrt{29} \approx 5.385$$

Cosine similarity:

$$\text{sim}(U1, U2) = \frac{38}{5\sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{29}} = \frac{38}{\sqrt{1450}} \approx \frac{38}{38.08} \approx 0.998$$

**Step 2 — Compute All Cosine Similarities (treating unrated as 0 for I3) for Prediction:**

Note: For I3 prediction, we need sim using all items. We use the *full* 4-D vectors.

| Pair | Dot Product | $\Vert U_a \Vert$ | $\Vert U_b \Vert$ | Similarity |
|------|-------------|-------------------|-------------------|------------|
| U1–U2 | 38 + (0)(5) = 38 | $\sqrt{50} \approx 7.071$ | $\sqrt{9+16+25+4} = \sqrt{54} \approx 7.348$ | $38 / 51.96 \approx 0.731$ |
| U1–U3 | $4 \cdot 5 + 5 \cdot 5 + 0 \cdot 4 + 3 \cdot 4 = 20+25+0+12 = 57$ | 7.071 | $\sqrt{25+25+16+16} = \sqrt{82} \approx 9.055$ | $57 / 64.02 \approx 0.890$ |
| U1–U4 | $4 \cdot 2 + 5 \cdot 3 + 0 \cdot 4 + 3 \cdot 1 = 8+15+0+3 = 26$ | 7.071 | $\sqrt{4+9+16+1} = \sqrt{30} \approx 5.477$ | $26 / 38.73 \approx 0.671$ |

**Step 3 — Item-Based Form of Prediction:**

Using the item-based formula (since we have ratings for $I3$ from U2, U3, U4):

$$\hat{r}_{U1, I3} = \frac{\sum_{v \in \{U2, U3, U4\}} \text{sim}(U1, v) \cdot r_{v, I3}}{\sum_{v \in \{U2, U3, U4\}} \vert \text{sim}(U1, v) \vert}$$

Numerator:

$$0.731 \cdot 5 + 0.890 \cdot 4 + 0.671 \cdot 4 = 3.655 + 3.560 + 2.684 = 9.899$$

Denominator:

$$0.731 + 0.890 + 0.671 = 2.292$$

Final prediction:

$$\hat{r}_{U1, I3} = \frac{9.899}{2.292} \approx \boxed{4.32}$$

> **Interpretation:** The system predicts that user $U1$ would rate *Item 3* approximately **4.32** out of 5, blending the opinions of three peers weighted by their cosine-similarity to $U1$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Using **Pearson** when the question specifies **cosine** — students often mix the two formulas. Always read the similarity method asked.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing **user-based** with **item-based** prediction. The two have *different* aggregation formulas (item-based has no mean-centering).
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Treating *unrated* ($r = 0$ or null) as a **real rating of zero**. It is *missing data*, not a dislike. The correct approach is to either (a) use only co-rated items, or (b) impute the user's mean.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting to **clip** the prediction to the valid range $[1, 5]$ if it falls outside (e.g., a cosine of 0.99 with all neighbors rating 5 could push the prediction above 5).
> - **Mark Loss:** ~**2 marks** for misidentifying the formula, ~**1 mark** for not clipping the output range.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Rating Matrix $R$** is the foundational data structure — rows = users, columns = items, cells = ratings (or null).
- [x] **Sparsity** is the dominant challenge in real-world CF systems (often > 99%).
- [x] **Three core similarity measures:** Cosine (raw magnitudes), Pearson (mean-centered, co-rated), Adjusted Cosine (item-based, mean-centered over users).
- [x] **Pearson similarity** is bounded in $[-1, +1]$ and handles user rating bias.
- [x] **User-based prediction** uses mean-centering and similarity-weighted deviations:

$$\hat{r}_{u,i} = \bar{r}_u + \frac{\sum_{v \in N(u)} \text{sim}(u,v) \cdot (r_{v,i} - \bar{r}_v)}{\sum_{v \in N(u)} \vert \text{sim}(u,v) \vert}$$

- [x] **Item-based prediction** uses raw-rating weighted aggregation (no mean-centering across users):

$$\hat{r}_{u,i} = \frac{\sum_{j \in N(i)} \text{sim}(i,j) \cdot r_{u,j}}{\sum_{j \in N(i)} \vert \text{sim}(i,j) \vert}$$

- [x] **Neighborhood size $k$** is a hyperparameter: small $k$ → noisy, large $k$ → biased. Typical values: $k \in [20, 50]$.
- [x] **Cold-start problem:** New users or new items have *no ratings* → cannot compute similarity. Mitigated by hybrid or content-based approaches.
- [x] **Implicit vs Explicit** ratings trade *volume* for *quality* — modern systems (e.g., YouTube, Spotify) lean heavily on implicit signals.
- [x] **Mean-centering** removes user bias; without it, "generous" and "strict" raters appear dissimilar.
- [x] **KTU Exam Pattern:** Expect a 14-mark question with **(a)** a 7-mark conceptual/theoretical sub-question and **(b)** a 7-mark numerical sub-question (hand-compute similarity + prediction from a 4×4 or 5×5 matrix).
- [x] **Final numerical answer must lie within the valid rating range** (typically $[1, 5]$). Clip if necessary.
- [x] **Key hyperparameters to remember:** neighborhood size $k$, similarity threshold $\tau$, shrinkage regularization $\lambda$ (used in advanced variants).
- [x] **Real-world deployment:** Netflix uses item-based CF, Spotify uses a blend of CF + matrix factorization, Amazon uses item-to-item CF for scalability.
- [x] **Pitfall:** Never include the *target user* in their own neighborhood. Never treat unrated entries as a rating of 0 unless explicitly stated.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Model-based and Pre-processing-based Approaches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT741 - RECOMMENDER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Model-based and Pre-processing-based Approaches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the taxonomy of recommender systems, **Collaborative Filtering (CF)** is broadly classified into two principal paradigms: *memory-based* (neighborhood) approaches and *model-based* approaches. **Model-based approaches** construct a compact predictive model from the user–item interaction matrix using statistical, machine-learning, or probabilistic techniques, and then use that compact representation to make rapid predictions for unseen user–item pairs. **Pre-processing-based approaches** are the auxiliary data-conditioning techniques (normalization, dimensionality reduction, denoising, sampling, discretization) that are applied to the raw interaction matrix *before* feeding it to a model-based learner to enhance accuracy, scalability, and robustness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> *Model-based approaches use a learned parametric or probabilistic model to predict user preferences, while pre-processing approaches transform the raw user-item matrix to improve the quality of subsequent modelling.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **massive library** (the rating matrix) where every reader has rated only a handful of the million books. A *memory-based* recommender walks over to the readers with similar tastes to yours and asks them what they liked. A *model-based* recommender instead *reads every book once*, builds an internal **map of "topic dimensions"** (e.g., sci-fi, romance, philosophy), places each reader and book as a point in this abstract map, and then simply **measures geometric distances** to suggest books that lie close to your point. The "topic dimensions" are the *latent factors* — the model never tells you the names of these dimensions, but they capture taste patterns.

*Pre-processing* in this analogy is the work of the librarian **before** the map is built: removing duplicate records, shelving damaged books, translating foreign titles, and grouping books by their raw themes so the map-builder can do a clean job.

### 1.3 Key Metrics and Standard Quantities

The fundamental quantities used throughout this module are:

- **m** — number of users
- **n** — number of items
- **k** — number of latent factors (typically $k \ll \min(m,n)$)
- **$\lambda$** — L2 regularization constant, $\lambda > 0$
- **$\gamma$ (or $\eta$)** — learning rate, typically $\eta \in [0.001, 0.1]$
- **$r_{ui}$** — observed rating of user *u* for item *i*
- **$\hat{r}_{ui}$** — predicted rating
- **$p_u \in \mathbb{R}^k$** — user latent factor vector
- **$q_i \in \mathbb{R}^k$** — item latent factor vector

> [!NOTE]
> The cardinal goal of all model-based recommenders is to **minimize the regularized prediction error** on observed entries of the rating matrix while **generalising** to unseen entries.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Model-based Approaches

Model-based collaborative filtering is sub-divided into six principal families, each distinguished by its mathematical foundation:

1. **Matrix Factorization (MF) Family** — SVD, PMF, NMF, SVD++, FunkSVD, BiasedMF
2. **Probabilistic Latent Topic Models** — PLSA, LDA
3. **Bayesian and Probabilistic Networks** — Bayesian CF, Restricted Boltzmann Machines
4. **Regression / Classification Models** — Slope-One, logistic regression
5. **Neural / Deep Models** — Autoencoders (AutoRec), Neural Collaborative Filtering
6. **Association Rule Mining** — Apriori, FP-Growth for basket-style recommendations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination), the **Matrix Factorization family** and **PLSA/LDA** carry the highest weightage and are almost guaranteed to appear in Part B.

### 2.2 Matrix Factorization — The Central Idea

The user–item rating matrix $\mathbf{R} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is decomposed into two low-rank matrices:

$$\mathbf{R} \approx \mathbf{P} \, \mathbf{Q}^{\top}$$

where $\mathbf{P} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times k}$ is the *user-factor* matrix and $\mathbf{Q} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$ is the *item-factor* matrix. The prediction for any user–item pair is the dot product of their latent vectors:

$$\hat{r}_{ui} = p_u^{\top} q_i = \sum_{f=1}^{k} p_{uf} \, q_{if}$$

The training objective minimises the **regularized squared error** only on observed ratings $\kappa$:

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{(u,i) \in \kappa} \left(r_{ui} - p_u^{\top} q_i\right)^2 + \lambda \left(\|p_u\|^2 + \|q_i\|^2\right)$$

The **Why** behind each term:
- The **first term** enforces that predictions are close to true ratings.
- The **$\lambda$ term (Tikhonov regularizer)** prevents the latent vectors from blowing up in magnitude and overfitting to sparse observations.

### 2.3 Probabilistic Matrix Factorization (PMF)

PMF (Mnih & Salakhutdinov, 2007) places Gaussian priors over both $\mathbf{P}$ and $\mathbf{Q}$. Assuming:

$$p(\mathbf{R} \vert \mathbf{P}, \mathbf{Q}, \sigma^2) = \prod_{(u,i) \in \kappa} \mathcal{N}\left(r_{ui} \mid p_u^{\top} q_i, \sigma^2\right)$$

$$p(\mathbf{P}) = \prod_u \mathcal{N}(p_u \mid 0, \sigma_P^2 \mathbf{I}), \quad p(\mathbf{Q}) = \prod_i \mathcal{N}(q_i \mid 0, \sigma_Q^2 \mathbf{I})$$

Taking the log-posterior yields **exactly the same regularized objective** as in section 2.2, providing a Bayesian interpretation for the L2 regularizer.

### 2.4 SVD++ — Incorporating Implicit Feedback

Standard MF uses only *explicit* ratings. **SVD++** augments the prediction by adding a term built from the set of items $\mathcal{N}(u)$ rated (or interacted with) by user *u*:

$$\hat{r}_{ui} = \mu + b_u + b_i + q_i^{\top} \left(p_u + \vert \mathcal{N}(u) \vert^{-1/2} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(u)} y_j\right)$$

Here $\mu$ is the global mean, $b_u$ and $b_i$ are user and item biases, and $y_j$ are implicit-feedback factor vectors.

### 2.5 Latent Topic Models — PLSA / LDA

Both treat users as mixtures of latent topics (preferences) and items as mixtures of latent topics. The generative process for a rating $r_{ui}$:

- Draw a topic $z \sim \text{Multinomial}(\theta_u)$ from the user-specific topic distribution.
- Draw an item $i \sim \text{Multinomial}(\phi_z)$ from the topic-specific item distribution.

The predicted probability of a user interacting with an item is:

$$\Pr(i \mid u) = \sum_{z=1}^{k} \Pr(z \mid u) \, \Pr(i \mid z)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **LDA vs PLSA — Examiner's Hot Question:** LDA places Dirichlet priors $\text{Dir}(\alpha)$ on $\theta_u$ and $\text{Dir}(\beta)$ on $\phi_z$, making it a fully Bayesian generative model. PLSA estimates $\theta_u$ and $\phi_z$ via maximum likelihood (EM), with no prior — hence prone to overfitting on small data.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Mathematical Form | Purpose / Unit |
|---|---------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | MF Prediction | $\hat{r}_{ui} = p_u^{\top} q_i$ | Score estimate, scalar |
| 2 | MF Loss | $\sum_{(u,i)\in\kappa}(r_{ui} - p_u^{\top} q_i)^2 + \lambda(\lVert p_u\rVert^2 + \lVert q_i\rVert^2)$ | Minimisation objective |
| 3 | Gradient w.r.t. $p_u$ | $-2(r_{ui} - \hat{r}_{ui})q_i + 2\lambda p_u$ | SGD update direction |
| 4 | Gradient w.r.t. $q_i$ | $-2(r_{ui} - \hat{r}_{ui})p_u + 2\lambda q_i$ | SGD update direction |
| 5 | SGD Update Rule | $p_u \leftarrow p_u + \eta\bigl[(r_{ui} - \hat{r}_{ui})q_i - \lambda p_u\bigr]$ | Iterative learning |
| 6 | SVD Decomposition | $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{\top}$ | Full-rank factorisation |
| 7 | Truncated SVD (pre-proc.) | $\mathbf{R}_k = \mathbf{U}_k\mathbf{\Sigma}_k\mathbf{V}_k^{\top}$ | Noise + dim. reduction |
| 8 | Mean-Centering | $\tilde{r}_{ui} = r_{ui} - \mu_u$ | Removes user bias |
| 9 | Z-Score Normalization | $\tilde{r}_{ui} = (r_{ui} - \mu_u)/\sigma_u$ | Standardises rating scale |
| 10 | Cosine Similarity | $\text{sim}(u,v) = \frac{p_u \cdot p_v}{\lVert p_u\rVert \, \lVert p_v\rVert}$ | Nearest-neighbour in latent space |
| 11 | PMF Variance | $\sigma^2$ — observation noise | Gaussian likelihood |
| 12 | LDA Priors | $\theta_u \sim \text{Dir}(\alpha),\; \phi_z \sim \text{Dir}(\beta)$ | Bayesian smoothing |
| 13 | PLSA Likelihood | $\mathcal{L} = \sum_{u,i} c_{ui} \log \sum_z \Pr(z \mid u) \Pr(i \mid z)$ | Maximum-likelihood topic model |
| 14 | RMSE Metric | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\vert\kappa\vert}\sum_{(u,i)\in\kappa}(r_{ui}-\hat{r}_{ui})^2}$ | Accuracy in rating units |
| 15 | MAE Metric | $\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{\vert\kappa\vert}\sum \vert r_{ui} - \hat{r}_{ui}\vert$ | Mean absolute error |

### 2.7 Engineering Utility

Model-based and pre-processing approaches are foundational to every commercial-grade recommender in production today: Netflix (matrix factorisation, SVD++ on implicit feedback), Spotify (PLSA/LDA on audio features + co-listen matrix), Amazon (item-to-item MF + association rules), YouTube (deep neural collaborative filtering with extensive pre-processing). The pre-processing step alone is often responsible for **20–40 % of the final accuracy gain**, as documented in the Netflix Prize and RecSys industry literature.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the MF Objective (Memory-based → Model-based)

**Step 1 — Begin with the loss function over the observed rating set $\kappa$:**

$$\mathcal{L}_0 = \sum_{(u,i) \in \kappa} (r_{ui} - \hat{r}_{ui})^2$$

*Reasoning:* We want predictions $\hat{r}_{ui}$ to deviate as little as possible from the observed ratings $r_{ui}$ in squared-error sense (penalising large errors more harshly).

**Step 2 — Substitute the factorised predictor $\hat{r}_{ui} = p_u^{\top} q_i$:**

$$\mathcal{L}_0 = \sum_{(u,i) \in \kappa} \left(r_{ui} - p_u^{\top} q_i\right)^2$$

*Reasoning:* The dot-product form forces the model to compress each user and item into a $k$-dimensional latent space.

**Step 3 — Observe the overfitting problem on sparse data.** When $|\kappa| \ll mn$, the system is underdetermined and we may obtain arbitrarily small training error with degenerate latent vectors. Add Tikhonov regularisation:

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{(u,i) \in \kappa} \left(r_{ui} - p_u^{\top} q_i\right)^2 + \lambda\left(\|p_u\|_F^2 + \|q_i\|_F^2\right)$$

*Reasoning:* The penalty term constrains the magnitude of the latent vectors, ensuring a unique, well-conditioned solution that generalises.

**Step 4 — Derive the partial derivative w.r.t. $p_u$:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial p_u} = -2 \sum_{i \,:\, (u,i) \in \kappa} \left(r_{ui} - p_u^{\top} q_i\right) q_i + 2\lambda p_u$$

*Reasoning:* By the chain rule applied to $(r - p^{\top}q)^2$ and to $\|p\|^2$.

**Step 5 — Derive the partial derivative w.r.t. $q_i$:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = -2 \sum_{u \,:\, (u,i) \in \kappa} \left(r_{ui} - p_u^{\top} q_i\right) p_u + 2\lambda q_i$$

**Step 6 — Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) update for a single sample $(u,i)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{ui} &\triangleq r_{ui} - p_u^{\top} q_i \\
p_u &\leftarrow p_u + \eta \left(e_{ui} \, q_i - \lambda \, p_u\right) \\
q_i &\leftarrow q_i + \eta \left(e_{ui} \, p_u - \lambda \, q_i\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Reasoning:* $e_{ui}$ is the *prediction error*; we move $p_u$ and $q_i$ in directions that reduce this error, with a shrinkage term to enforce the prior.

**Step 7 — Convergence criterion:** Stop when

$$\frac{1}{\vert\kappa\vert}\sum_{(u,i)\in\kappa} e_{ui}^2 \le \epsilon \quad \text{(typical } \epsilon = 10^{-4}\text{)}$$

or after a fixed number of epochs (e.g., 20–100).

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Biased MF on a Toy Matrix

Let the rating matrix be $\mathbf{R} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 3 & ? \\ 4 & ? & 2 \\ ? & 1 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$ with $k=2$, $\lambda=0.02$, $\eta=0.01$, and let $\mu = 3.0$ be the global mean of observed entries.

**Step 1 — Compute user and item biases:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_u &= \frac{1}{|I_u|}\sum_{i \in I_u} (r_{ui} - \mu) \\
\mu_{u1} &= \tfrac{1}{2}[(5-3) + (3-3)] = 1.0 \\
\mu_{u2} &= \tfrac{1}{2}[(4-3) + (2-3)] = 0.0 \\
\mu_{u3} &= \tfrac{1}{1}[(1-3)] = -2.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute item biases:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_{i1} &= \tfrac{1}{2}[(5-3-1.0) + (4-3-0.0)] = 1.0 \\
b_{i2} &= \tfrac{1}{3}[(3-3-1.0) + (1-3+2.0) + (3-3-1.0)] \;\; \text{(after one pass)} \\
b_{i3} &= \tfrac{1}{2}[(2-3-0.0) + (4-3+2.0)] = 1.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Initialise latent vectors randomly:**

$$p_1 = \begin{bmatrix}0.1\\0.2\end{bmatrix},\; p_2 = \begin{bmatrix}0.3\\0.1\end{bmatrix},\; p_3 = \begin{bmatrix}0.2\\0.3\end{bmatrix}, \quad q_1 = \begin{bmatrix}0.2\\0.1\end{bmatrix},\; q_2 = \begin{bmatrix}0.1\\0.3\end{bmatrix},\; q_3 = \begin{bmatrix}0.2\\0.2\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Biased MF prediction rule:**

$$\hat{r}_{ui} = \mu + b_u + b_i + p_u^{\top} q_i$$

For the missing entry $r_{1,3}$:

$$\hat{r}_{1,3} = 3.0 + 1.0 + 1.0 + (0.1)(0.2) + (0.2)(0.2) = 5.06$$

**Step 5 — Run SGD for a few iterations on the missing target. After training, the predicted value will converge close to the true hidden rating, demonstrating that the factor model can recover missing entries from very few observations.**

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Memory-based CF | Model-based CF | Pre-processing |
|--------|----------------|----------------|----------------|
| Computation at *query* time | High (scan neighbourhood) | **Very low (single dot product)** | — |
| Computation at *training* time | Low | High (iterative) | Low–Medium |
| Storage | Full rating matrix | **Only $\mathbf{P}, \mathbf{Q}$** | Reduced matrix |
| Scalability with $m, n$ | Poor | **Good** | Good |
| Handles sparsity | Poor | **Good** | **Excellent** |
| Interpretability | High | **Low (latent factors)** | High |
| Cold-start resilience | Medium | Low | **Medium (uses side info)** |
| Typical accuracy (RMSE) | 0.95–1.10 | **0.85–0.95** | — (improves others) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — End-to-End Recommendation Pipeline with Pre-processing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw User Item Matrix R] --> B[Missing Value Handling]
    B --> C[Normalisation Mean Centering]
    C --> D[Noise Filtering]
    D --> E[Dimensionality Reduction Truncated SVD]
    E --> F[Cleaned Matrix R clean]
    F --> G[Model based Learner MF PMF SVDpp]
    G --> H[Latent Factors P and Q]
    H --> I[Prediction Engine dot product]
    I --> J[Top N Recommendations]
    H --> K[Offline Evaluation RMSE MAE]
    K --> L[Hyperparameter Tuning eta lambda k]
    L --> G
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Internal Mechanism of Stochastic Gradient Descent for MF

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Initialise P and Q randomly] --> S2[Shuffle observed pairs u i]
    S2 --> S3[Pick one pair u i]
    S3 --> S4[Compute prediction r hat = p u transpose q i]
    S4 --> S5[Compute error e = r - r hat]
    S5 --> S6[Update p u = p u + eta times e times q i - lambda p u]
    S5 --> S7[Update q i = q i + eta times e times p u - lambda q i]
    S6 --> S8[More pairs remaining]
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S3
    S8 --> S9[Epoch complete]
    S9 --> S10[Compute RMSE on validation set]
    S10 --> S11[Converged]
    S11 --> S12[Output P and Q]
    S10 --> S13[Not converged]
    S13 --> S2
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Latent Topic Model Generative Pipeline (PLSA / LDA)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[Dirichlet Prior alpha on user topic distribution theta] --> T2[Draw theta u for each user u]
    T2 --> T3[For each user item pair]
    T3 --> T4[Sample latent topic z from Multinomial theta u]
    T4 --> T5[Sample item i from topic specific Multinomial phi z]
    T1b[Dirichlet Prior beta on topic item distribution phi] --> T6[Draw phi z for each topic z]
    T6 --> T5
    T5 --> T7[Observed interaction matrix R]
    T7 --> T8[Infer posterior on theta and phi]
    T8 --> T9[Predicted probability user u likes item i = sum over z Pr z given u times Pr i given z]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Comparative Architecture of Pre-processing Operations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Raw Ratings] --> P2[Mean Centering per user]
    P2 --> P3[Variance Normalisation per user]
    P3 --> P4[Z Score Matrix]
    P4 --> P5[Truncated SVD keeping top k singular values]
    P5 --> P6[Reduced Latent Representations]
    P1 --> P7[Binary Conversion implicit feedback]
    P7 --> P8[Negative Sampling for unobserved items]
    P8 --> P9[Sampling Balanced Training Set]
    P9 --> P10[Weighted Matrix for Implicit MF]
    P6 --> P11[Cleaned Input to Model]
    P10 --> P11
```

> [!NOTE]
> The mermaid diagrams above follow the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** convention since the actual physical structures (sparse matrices, embedding clouds) cannot be drawn textually. Each block maps a precise operation in the pre-processing + model-based pipeline.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Compare memory-based and model-based collaborative filtering with respect to scalability, accuracy and interpretability.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — allocate ~1 mark per point):**

| Dimension | Memory-based CF | Model-based CF |
|-----------|-----------------|----------------|
| Scalability | Poor — query-time cost grows with users and items | Excellent — only $\mathbf{P}, \mathbf{Q}$ stored; prediction is a dot product |
| Accuracy | Moderate; degrades severely on sparse data | Higher; latent factors capture hidden preference structure |
| Interpretability | High — explicit neighbourhood reasoning | Lower — latent factors are not human-readable |

Model-based CF trains a compact parametric model $\hat{r}_{ui} = p_u^{\top} q_i$ from the observed ratings, then performs rapid inference; this is why it scales to industrial datasets such as Netflix (~100 M ratings).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Why is pre-processing of the user–item matrix essential before applying a model-based recommender? List any three techniques.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The raw user–item matrix is **sparse** (often >95 % entries missing), **noisy** (outliers and adversarial clicks), and **biased** (some users systematically rate high, some low). Without pre-processing, the factorisation model will overfit to noise and bias. Three essential techniques are:

1. **Mean-Centering** — $\tilde{r}_{ui} = r_{ui} - \mu_u$ removes user-specific rating bias.
2. **Z-Score Normalisation** — $\tilde{r}_{ui} = (r_{ui} - \mu_u)/\sigma_u$ standardises the rating scale.
3. **Dimensionality Reduction (Truncated SVD)** — $\mathbf{R}_k = \mathbf{U}_k \mathbf{\Sigma}_k \mathbf{V}_k^{\top}$ projects the matrix onto a low-rank latent space, denoising and compressing the data simultaneously.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students must answer **one full question from each module**, and each 14-mark question carries two sub-parts of 7 marks each. The two alternative questions below are mutually exclusive — answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the regularised matrix factorisation objective from first principles. State the gradient update equations for Stochastic Gradient Descent.

**Model Solution:**

We start with the sum of squared errors over the observed rating set $\kappa$:

$$\mathcal{L}_0 = \sum_{(u,i) \in \kappa} \left(r_{ui} - \hat{r}_{ui}\right)^2$$

Substituting $\hat{r}_{ui} = p_u^{\top} q_i$:

$$\mathcal{L}_0 = \sum_{(u,i) \in \kappa} \left(r_{ui} - p_u^{\top} q_i\right)^2$$

Because $|\kappa| \ll mn$ the system is underdetermined; we add the L2 penalty:

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{(u,i) \in \kappa} \left(r_{ui} - p_u^{\top} q_i\right)^2 + \lambda \left(\lVert p_u\rVert^2 + \lVert q_i\rVert^2\right)$$

Taking the partial derivatives and setting the error $e_{ui} = r_{ui} - p_u^{\top} q_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial p_u} &= -2 e_{ui} q_i + 2 \lambda p_u \\
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} &= -2 e_{ui} p_u + 2 \lambda q_i
\end{aligned}
$$

The SGD update rules (using learning rate $\eta$) are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_u &\leftarrow p_u + \eta \left( e_{ui} \, q_i - \lambda \, p_u \right) \\
q_i &\leftarrow q_i + \eta \left( e_{ui} \, p_u - \lambda \, q_i \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

These updates are repeated for every observed pair until the RMSE on a validation set converges below a threshold $\epsilon$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the differences between Probabilistic Matrix Factorisation (PMF) and SVD++. Why is SVD++ preferred when implicit feedback is available?

**Model Solution:**

**PMF** assumes Gaussian noise on the observed ratings and Gaussian priors on the latent factors:

$$p(\mathbf{R} \vert \mathbf{P}, \mathbf{Q}, \sigma^2) = \prod_{(u,i) \in \kappa} \mathcal{N}\left(r_{ui} \mid p_u^{\top} q_i, \sigma^2\right)$$

PMF is purely a model of *explicit* ratings; if a user has only clicked/watched an item but never rated it, PMF ignores that signal.

**SVD++** extends the prediction rule to:

$$\hat{r}_{ui} = \mu + b_u + b_i + q_i^{\top} \left(p_u + \vert \mathcal{N}(u)\vert^{-1/2}\sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(u)} y_j\right)$$

Here $\mathcal{N}(u)$ is the set of items the user has interacted with (clicks, views, purchases) — even without a numerical rating — and $y_j$ is the implicit-feedback factor for item $j$. The summation captures *what the user has done*, not just *what they have rated*.

**Why SVD++ is preferred when implicit feedback is available:** Explicit ratings are sparse (a typical user rates < 1 % of items), but implicit interactions (views, clicks, watches) are orders of magnitude denser. SVD++ exploits this rich auxiliary signal to refine the user representation, yielding significantly lower RMSE and better top-N recommendation quality. The implicit feedback regularises the user vector and compensates for the sparsity of explicit ratings.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the probabilistic generative process of a Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) model for recommendation. Contrast it with PLSA.

**Model Solution:**

LDA is a three-level Bayesian generative model. For a corpus of $m$ users and $n$ items with $k$ latent topics, the generative process is:

1. For each topic $z \in \{1, \dots, k\}$: sample item-distribution $\phi_z \sim \text{Dir}(\beta)$.
2. For each user $u \in \{1, \dots, m\}$: sample topic-mixture $\theta_u \sim \text{Dir}(\alpha)$.
3. For each user–item interaction $(u, i)$:
   - Sample a topic $z \sim \text{Multinomial}(\theta_u)$.
   - Sample an item $i \sim \text{Multinomial}(\phi_z)$.

The resulting posterior over the latent variables is intractable; inference is performed via **variational inference** or **collapsed Gibbs sampling**.

**Predicted preference** of user $u$ for item $i$:

$$\Pr(i \mid u) = \sum_{z=1}^{k} \Pr(z \mid u) \, \Pr(i \mid z) = \sum_{z=1}^{k} \theta_{uz} \, \phi_{zi}$$

**Contrast with PLSA:**

| Aspect | PLSA | LDA |
|--------|------|-----|
| Priors on $\theta_u, \phi_z$ | **None** (point estimates) | **Dirichlet** (full Bayesian) |
| Parameter count | $mk + kn$ (grows with corpus) | $k(\alpha + \beta) + mk + kn$ (fixed topic priors) |
| Generalisation to new users | **Poor** — must re-train | **Good** — only sample $\theta_{u^\text{new}}$ |
| Overfitting | High on small data | **Low** (priors regularise) |
| Inference | EM algorithm | Variational / Gibbs sampling |

LDA is the preferred choice in modern systems because the Dirichlet priors provide principled smoothing, prevent overfitting, and enable inductive generalisation to new users and items.

**(b) [7 Marks]** List and explain any four pre-processing techniques used in recommender systems. Show the mathematical formulation of each.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Mean-Centering (per user)**
Removes user-specific bias:

$$\tilde{r}_{ui} = r_{ui} - \mu_u, \quad \text{where} \quad \mu_u = \frac{1}{|\mathcal{I}_u|}\sum_{i \in \mathcal{I}_u} r_{ui}$$

**2. Z-Score Normalisation**
Standardises the rating distribution per user:

$$\tilde{r}_{ui} = \frac{r_{ui} - \mu_u}{\sigma_u + \epsilon}$$

A small $\epsilon$ prevents division by zero for users with only one rating.

**3. Dimensionality Reduction via Truncated SVD**
Decompose $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{U} \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{V}^{\top}$ and keep the top $k$ singular values:

$$\mathbf{R}_k = \mathbf{U}_k \mathbf{\Sigma}_k \mathbf{V}_k^{\top}$$

This compresses the data, denoises it, and exposes a low-rank latent structure that downstream models can exploit efficiently.

**4. Negative Sampling (for implicit feedback)**
For each positive interaction, randomly sample a small set of unobserved items as "negatives":

$$\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}} = \{(u, i, y=1) \mid i \in \mathcal{N}(u)\} \cup \{(u, j, y=0) \mid j \sim \text{Uniform}(\mathcal{I} \setminus \mathcal{N}(u))\}$$

This converts an implicit feedback matrix into a balanced binary classification dataset suitable for models such as **Weighted MF (WMF)** or **Bayesian Personalised Ranking (BPR)**.

These four techniques are often applied in **cascade**: mean-centring first, then Z-score normalisation, then truncated SVD, and finally negative sampling before model training.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing SVD and Truncated SVD** — SVD is exact decomposition; truncated SVD *approximates* the matrix by retaining only the top $k$ singular values. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting the regularisation term** — Writing the MF objective *without* the $\lambda$ term is a **2-mark penalty** in 14-mark questions.
> 3. **Mixing PLSA and LDA** — Saying "LDA uses EM" is incorrect; EM is for PLSA. LDA requires variational inference or Gibbs sampling.
> 4. **Confusing explicit and implicit feedback** — SVD++ is *not* a deep-learning model; it is an extension of matrix factorisation that incorporates implicit feedback signals.
> 5. **Skipping derivation steps** — In 7-mark sub-parts, examiners award marks for *each logical transition*. Always show the chain rule, the error definition $e_{ui}$, and the final update equation separately.
> 6. **Missing the global mean $\mu$** — In Biased MF / SVD++, $\mu + b_u + b_i$ is a *mandatory* baseline. Omitting it costs a full mark.
> 7. **Wrong dimensionality of $p_u$ and $q_i$** — Both are column vectors of length $k$, not scalars. The dot product $p_u^{\top} q_i$ is a scalar.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Model-based CF** compresses the sparse rating matrix $\mathbf{R} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ into two low-rank matrices $\mathbf{P} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times k}$ and $\mathbf{Q} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$ with $k \ll \min(m, n)$.
- The **canonical MF objective** is $\min \sum (r_{ui} - p_u^{\top} q_i)^2 + \lambda (\lVert p_u\rVert^2 + \lVert q_i\rVert^2)$ — this is **the single most-tested formula** in Module 1.
- **SGD updates** move $p_u$ and $q_i$ in the direction of the prediction error $e_{ui} = r_{ui} - \hat{r}_{ui}$, with a shrinkage pull toward zero.
- **PMF** = Bayesian justification of the L2-regularised MF objective using Gaussian likelihood and Gaussian priors.
- **SVD++** = MF + user & item biases ($\mu, b_u, b_i$) + implicit feedback term involving $y_j$ for items in $\mathcal{N}(u)$.
- **NMF** constrains $p_u, q_i \ge 0$ — gives additive, parts-based interpretations but cannot capture subtractive patterns.
- **PLSA** estimates topic distributions by maximum likelihood (EM); **LDA** places Dirichlet priors and requires variational/Gibbs inference — LDA generalises better to unseen users.
- **Pre-processing essentials:** mean-centring, Z-score normalisation, truncated SVD, denoising, negative sampling, discretisation.
- **Mean-centring** removes *user bias*; **item-mean centring** removes *item popularity bias*; both are often applied.
- **Truncated SVD** with rank $k$ yields a denoised low-rank matrix — also a *form* of model-based CF (Latent Semantic Indexing).
- **Cosine similarity in latent space** $\text{sim}(u,v) = \frac{p_u \cdot p_v}{\lVert p_u\rVert \lVert p_v\rVert}$ is the standard neighbourhood metric for model-based recommender post-processing.
- **Evaluation metrics:** RMSE (regression), MAE (regression), Precision@K, Recall@K, NDCG@K, MAP, Hit-Rate (top-N ranking).
- **Cold-start limitation:** Pure model-based CF fails on new users/items with no interaction history — sidestepped using hybrid or content-based extensions.
- **Regularisation $\lambda$** is *essential*, not optional — without it, the model overfits the sparse rating matrix.
- **Hyperparameters to tune:** number of latent factors $k$, regularisation strength $\lambda$, learning rate $\eta$, number of SGD epochs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recent Practical Approaches and Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT741 - RECOMMENDATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Recent Practical Approaches and Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recent Practical Approaches and Systems in Recommendation** refer to the modern class of learning algorithms that leverage **Deep Neural Architectures, Sequential Models, Graph Neural Networks, Knowledge Graphs, and Reinforcement Learning** to overcome the inherent limitations of classical collaborative filtering and matrix factorization techniques. They aim to capture **non-linear, high-order, contextual, and temporal interactions** between users, items, and auxiliary information sources.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** framework, this topic directly maps to:
- **CO1:** *Understand and analyse modern architectures used in industrial-scale recommender systems.*
- **CO2:** *Apply sequential, graph-based, and contextual models to real recommendation problems.*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Library of Tomorrow"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine a massive library with millions of books. A *traditional* recommendation system behaves like a librarian who only remembers *"people who borrowed X also borrowed Y"* (collaborative filtering). A *recent practical system* behaves like a **digital reading companion** — it understands the *narrative* of what you read, the *mood* you were in, the *concepts* in the books, your *friends' reading lists*, and even the *time of day*. It combines all of this with deep neural reasoning to suggest not just a book, but the **right book at the right moment in your reading journey**.

---

### 1.3 Why Did Industry Move Beyond Classical Methods?

Classical **Matrix Factorization (MF)** and **neighbourhood-based CF** suffer from key industrial pain points:

| Limitation of Classical RS | Industrial Consequence |
|---|---|
| Linear interaction ($p_u^T q_i$) | Cannot model complex non-linear user–item affinity |
| Single feature source (ID only) | Cannot exploit side information (text, image, KG) |
| Static prediction | Cannot model temporal/session-based intent |
| Cannot capture high-order connectivity | Misses deeper user–item–user relationships |
| Cold-start issues persist | New items/users not handled well |

Recent practical systems were engineered to address these gaps using deep learning as the core representation engine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics Used to Evaluate These Systems:**
> **HR@K (Hit Rate)**, **NDCG@K (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain)**, **MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rank)**, **Recall@K**, **Precision@K**, and **AUC (Area Under ROC Curve)**.

---

### 1.4 Taxonomy of Recent Practical Approaches

The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies them into **six major families**:

1. **Neural Collaborative Filtering (NCF, NeuMF)** — non-linear user–item interaction.
2. **Wide & Deep / DeepFM** — hybrid memorization + generalization.
3. **Sequential / Session-Based Models (GRU4Rec, SASRec, BERT4Rec)** — temporal intent.
4. **Graph Neural Networks (PinSage, NGCF, LightGCN)** — high-order connectivity.
5. **Knowledge-Graph Based (RippleNet, KGAT)** — semantic side information.
6. **Reinforcement Learning & Bandits (LinUCB, DQN-Rec)** — interactive / exploration–exploitation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Family 1 — Neural Collaborative Filtering (NCF / NeuMF)

**Operational Logic:**
- Replaces the **inner product** of classical MF with a **Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP)** that can learn an *arbitrary* function $f(\cdot)$ from data.
- Introduces two parallel towers: **Generalized Matrix Factorization (GMF)** and **MLP**, fused into **NeuMF**.

**Why it works:** GMF preserves the *linear signal* (memorization), while MLP captures *non-linear interactions* (generalization). Concatenating them gives a richer representation.

**Real-world Utility:** Deployed by major OTT and e-commerce platforms where interaction patterns are deeply non-linear.

---

### 2.2 Family 2 — Wide & Deep and DeepFM

**Wide & Deep Operational Logic:**
- **Wide path:** Generalized linear model $w^T [x, \phi(x)]$ — strong **memorization** of co-occurrences.
- **Deep path:** DNN on dense embeddings — strong **generalization** via dense features.
- Output: $P(Y=1 \mid x) = \sigma(\text{Wide} + \text{Deep} + b)$.

**DeepFM Operational Logic:**
- **FM component:** Captures both 1st-order and 2nd-order feature interactions explicitly.
- **Deep component:** Captures *high-order* interactions via DNN.
- **Key advantage over Wide & Deep:** No manual feature engineering required for the FM side.

**Real-world Utility:** Google Play app recommendation (Wide & Deep); click-through-rate prediction in advertising stacks (DeepFM).

---

### 2.3 Family 3 — Sequential / Session-Based Models

**Operational Logic:**
- User behaviour is treated as a **time-ordered sequence** $S_u = (i_1, i_2, \ldots, i_t)$.
- Goal: predict the next item $i_{t+1}$.

**Model Evolution:**
- **GRU4Rec (2016):** Uses Gated Recurrent Units for session modelling.
- **SASRec (2018):** Uses **self-attention** to identify which past items matter most — outperforming RNNs in long sequences.
- **BERT4Rec (2019):** Uses **bidirectional Transformers** with Cloze (masked-item) objective for richer sequence encoding.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why self-attention dominates RNNs in modern RS:** Self-attention has $O(1)$ path length between any two positions (vs. $O(n)$ in RNNs), allowing the model to capture **long-range dependencies** in user histories efficiently.

---

### 2.4 Family 4 — Graph Neural Networks (PinSage, NGCF, LightGCN)

**Operational Logic:**
- The **user–item bipartite graph** is treated as the core structure.
- **Message passing** aggregates neighbour information across $K$ hops, capturing **high-order connectivity** $u \leftarrow i_1 \leftarrow u_2 \leftarrow i_2 \ldots$
- **LightGCN** simplifies GCN by removing non-linearities and weight matrices — yielding a pure linear propagation that often *outperforms* more complex GNNs.

**Real-world Utility:** Pinterest (PinSage), Yelp, Amazon, TikTok — graph signals dominate because user–item relationships are inherently graph-structured.

---

### 2.5 Family 5 — Knowledge-Graph Based Recommenders

**Operational Logic:**
- Side information (item attributes, relationships) is encoded as a **Knowledge Graph (KG)** with triples $(h, r, t)$.
- **RippleNet:** Propagates user preferences along KG *ripples* (analogous to water waves).
- **KGAT:** Combines CF signal with KG signal via graph attention.

**Real-world Utility:** Movie/news recommendation where item attributes (genre, director, actor, entity) matter.

---

### 2.6 Family 6 — Reinforcement Learning & Bandit-Based

**Operational Logic:**
- Recommender is modelled as an **agent** in an **MDP (Markov Decision Process)**: $\langle S, A, P, R, \gamma \rangle$.
- **Contextual Bandits (LinUCB):** Solves the *exploration–exploitation* dilemma — should we show the best-known item (exploit) or try a less-known one to learn (explore)?
- **DQN-based Rec:** Uses Deep Q-Networks where state = user history, action = item to recommend, reward = click/engagement.

**Real-world Utility:** News recommendation (Yahoo, LinkedIn feed), online advertising.

---

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers 80% of the numerical/derivation questions asked in the KTU University Exam for this module.**

| **Model Family** | **Key Equation** | **Cognitive Function** | **Typical Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|
| GMF | $\phi^{GMF}_{out} = p_u \odot q_i$ | Element-wise product (linear) | Non-linear MF baseline |
| NeuMF | $\hat{y}_{ui} = \sigma\bigl(h^{T} [\phi^{GMF} \Vert \phi^{MLP}]\bigr)$ | Fused non-linear prediction | General-purpose ranking |
| Wide & Deep | $P(Y\!\!=\!\!1 \mid x) = \sigma\bigl(w_{wide}^{T} [x,\phi(x)] + w_{deep}^{T} a^{(l_f)} + b\bigr)$ | Memorization + Generalization | App install prediction |
| DeepFM | $\hat{y} = \sigma(y_{FM} + y_{DNN})$ | 1st + 2nd + high-order | CTR prediction |
| SASRec Self-Attention | $\text{Attn}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\bigl(\tfrac{QK^{T}}{\sqrt{d}}\bigr)V$ | Weighted context aggregation | Sequential recommendation |
| LightGCN Propagation | $E^{(k+1)} = (D^{-1/2} A D^{-1/2}) E^{(k)}$ | Graph neighbour aggregation | High-order CF |
| LightGCN Final Embedding | $E = \alpha_{0} E^{(0)} + \alpha_{1} E^{(1)} + \cdots + \alpha_{K} E^{(K)}$ | Layer combination | Final user/item vectors |
| PinSage Sampling | $\mathcal{N}(u) = $ random walk neighbours | Efficient large-graph training | Industrial-scale graph RS |
| LinUCB Score | $\hat{r}_{t,a} = x_{t,a}^{T}\theta_{a}^{*} + \alpha\sqrt{x_{t,a}^{T} A_{a}^{-1} x_{t,a}}$ | UCB confidence bound | News / feed recommendation |
| DQN-Rec | $Q(s,a;\theta) = r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a';\theta^{-})$ | Bellman optimality | Sequential interactive RS |

**Boundary conditions / Notation conventions used by KTU examiners:**
- $p_u, q_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ — user/item latent embeddings.
- $A$ — adjacency matrix of the user–item bipartite graph.
- $D$ — degree matrix of $A$ (diagonal).
- $x_{t,a}$ — context feature vector at time $t$ for arm $a$.
- $\alpha$ — UCB exploration hyperparameter.
- $\gamma$ — discount factor in RL.
- $\sigma(\cdot)$ — sigmoid activation.

---

### 2.8 Why This Matters in Engineering

These systems power:
- **OTT platforms** (Netflix, Hotstar) → Sequential + Contextual.
- **E-commerce** (Amazon, Flipkart) → Wide & Deep / DeepFM.
- **Social media feeds** (TikTok, Instagram) → GNN + RL.
- **News portals** (Yahoo, TOI) → LinUCB / Bandit.
- **Music streaming** (Spotify) → Sequential + KG-based.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The derivations below are exhaustive. No step is skipped. Each transition is justified in plain English so that you can reproduce it in the KTU answer script.

---

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — NeuMF (Neural Matrix Factorization) Output

**Step 1: Define embeddings.**
Let $p_u \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ and $q_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ be the latent embeddings of user $u$ and item $i$, learned during training.

**Step 2: GMF tower (Generalized MF).**
The GMF tower performs an **element-wise (Hadamard) product** of user and item embeddings, then projects via a linear layer:

$$
\phi^{GMF}(p_u, q_i) = a_{out}\!\bigl( h^{GMF\,T} (p_u \odot q_i) \bigr)
$$

Here $\odot$ denotes element-wise multiplication, $h^{GMF}$ is the output projection, and $a_{out}$ is a non-linear activation (typically ReLU or linear).

**Step 3: MLP tower.**
The MLP tower first *concatenates* the raw embeddings, then passes them through $L$ dense layers:

$$
z_1 = \phi_1(p_u, q_i) = 
\begin{bmatrix} p_u \\ q_i \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\phi^{MLP}(p_u, q_i) = a_{L}\!\bigl( W_{L}^{T} (a_{L-1}(\cdots a_1(W_1^{T} z_1 + b_1)\cdots)) + b_{L} \bigr)
$$

**Step 4: Fusion (concatenation).**
The outputs of both towers are concatenated into a single vector:

$$
z = \begin{bmatrix} \phi^{GMF}(p_u, q_i) \\ \phi^{MLP}(p_u, q_i) \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5: Final prediction layer.**
A single fully-connected layer with sigmoid activation maps $z$ to a probability:

$$
\hat{y}_{ui} = \sigma\!\bigl( h^{T} z \bigr) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-h^{T} z}}
$$

This $\hat{y}_{ui} \in (0,1)$ is the predicted probability that user $u$ will interact with item $i$.

**Inference:** The dual-tower design is the key. GMF keeps the *expressive multiplicative interaction*, while MLP introduces *non-linear expressivity*. Fusing them is provably more general than either alone.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — SASRec Self-Attention Score

**Step 1: Embedding the sequence.**
Given a sequence $S_u = (i_1, i_2, \ldots, i_t)$, each item $i_k$ is mapped to a learnable embedding $E_{i_k} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$. A positional embedding $P_k \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is added so that order information is preserved:

$$
\hat{E}_{k} = E_{i_k} + P_k
$$

**Step 2: Project into Query, Key, Value.**
Three learnable projection matrices $W^{Q}, W^{K}, W^{V} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$ produce:

$$
Q = \hat{E} W^{Q}, \quad K = \hat{E} W^{K}, \quad V = \hat{E} W^{V}
$$

**Step 3: Compute raw attention scores.**
For a query position $k$ attending to all positions $j$:

$$
\text{score}(k,j) = \frac{Q_{k} \cdot K_{j}^{T}}{\sqrt{d}}
$$

The division by $\sqrt{d}$ prevents the dot products from growing too large, which would push the softmax into saturated regions with vanishing gradients.

**Step 4: Causal masking.**
To prevent position $k$ from attending to future positions $j > k$, mask those entries to $-\infty$:

$$
\text{score}(k,j) = 
\begin{cases}
\tfrac{Q_k K_j^{T}}{\sqrt{d}}, & j \le k \\
-\infty, & j > k
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 5: Softmax normalization.**

$$
\alpha_{k,j} = \frac{\exp(\text{score}(k,j))}{\sum_{j'=1}^{k} \exp(\text{score}(k,j'))}
$$

**Step 6: Weighted aggregation.**

$$
\text{head}_k = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \alpha_{k,j} \, V_{j}
$$

**Step 7: Multi-head output.**
Multiple parallel heads are concatenated and projected:

$$
\text{SelfAttn}(\hat{E}) = [\text{head}_1 \Vert \text{head}_2 \Vert \cdots \Vert \text{head}_h] W^{O}
$$

**Inference:** The resulting contextual embedding at each position encodes *which past items are most relevant* for predicting the next one.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — LightGCN Embedding Propagation

**Step 1: Build the bipartite graph.**
Construct the adjacency matrix $A$ of the user–item bipartite graph, where rows = users + items, columns = users + items. Normalize it symmetrically:

$$
\tilde{A} = D^{-1/2} A D^{-1/2}
$$

where $D$ is the diagonal degree matrix, with $D_{ii} = \sum_j A_{ij}$.

**Step 2: Initialize embeddings.**
Let $E^{(0)} \in \mathbb{R}^{(N+M) \times d}$ be the trainable embedding matrix for all $N$ users and $M$ items.

**Step 3: Propagate $K$ hops.**
For each propagation step $k = 0, 1, \ldots, K-1$:

$$
E^{(k+1)} = \tilde{A} \, E^{(k)}
$$

**Step 4: Aggregate across all layers.**
LightGCN combines embeddings from all propagation depths with equal weights $\alpha_k = \tfrac{1}{K+1}$:

$$
E = \frac{1}{K+1} \sum_{k=0}^{K} E^{(k)}
$$

**Step 5: Compute user–item interaction score.**

$$
\hat{y}_{ui} = e_u^{T} e_i
$$

where $e_u, e_i$ are the final embeddings of user $u$ and item $i$ extracted from $E$.

**Inference:** LightGCN's removal of non-linearities and weight matrices (a deliberate simplification over NGCF) is *not* a regression — it acts as a regularizer and often improves both accuracy and efficiency. The intuition is that the *graph structure itself* already provides the regularization needed.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — LinUCB Score (Contextual Bandit)

**Step 1: Assume linear reward.**
The expected reward of arm $a$ in context $x_{t,a}$ is:

$$
\mathbb{E}[r_{t,a} \mid x_{t,a}] = x_{t,a}^{T} \theta_a^{*}
$$

**Step 2: Ridge regression on observed data.**
After $t$ rounds, the arm's coefficient estimate is:

$$
\hat{\theta}_a = A_a^{-1} b_a
$$

where $A_a = \lambda I + \sum x_{t,a} x_{t,a}^{T}$ and $b_a = \sum x_{t,a} r_{t,a}$.

**Step 3: Construct UCB.**
With probability $1 - \delta$, the true parameter lies within a confidence ellipsoid of radius proportional to $\sqrt{x_{t,a}^{T} A_a^{-1} x_{t,a}}$. Hence the optimistic score is:

$$
\hat{r}_{t,a} = x_{t,a}^{T} \hat{\theta}_a + \alpha \sqrt{x_{t,a}^{T} A_a^{-1} x_{t,a}}
$$

The first term is the *exploitation* component, the second is the *exploration bonus* controlled by $\alpha$.

**Step 4: Select the arm with the highest UCB.**

$$
a_{t}^{*} = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}_{t}} \hat{r}_{t,a}
$$

**Inference:** LinUCB is the *de facto* bandit algorithm in news/feed recommendation. It guarantees sublinear regret of order $O(\sqrt{T \log T})$.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Criterion** | **NCF / NeuMF** | **Wide & Deep** | **SASRec** | **LightGCN** | **LinUCB** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Captures non-linearity | ✅ Strong | ✅ Strong | ✅ Via attention | ❌ Linear propagation | ❌ Linear |
| Handles sequence/order | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Strong | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| High-order graph signal | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ Very strong | ❌ |
| Side information | ⚠ Limited | ✅ Strong | ⚠ Limited | ❌ | ✅ Context vectors |
| Exploration–exploitation | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ Strong |
| Cold-start tolerance | ⚠ Moderate | ✅ Good | ⚠ Moderate | ⚠ Moderate | ✅ Good |
| Industrial scale feasibility | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| KTU exam weightage | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram — Evolution Timeline of Recommender Systems

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Classical CF 1992] --> A2[Matrix Factorization 2006]
    A2 --> A3[BPR MF 2009]
    A3 --> A4[NCF and NeuMF 2017]
    A4 --> A5[Wide and Deep 2016]
    A4 --> A6[DeepFM 2017]
    A4 --> A7[Sequential Models 2018]
    A4 --> A8[Graph Neural RS 2020]
    A4 --> A9[RL and Bandit RS 2018]
    A7 --> A10[SASRec 2018]
    A7 --> A11[BERT4Rec 2019]
    A8 --> A12[PinSage 2018]
    A8 --> A13[LightGCN 2020]
```

---

### 4.2 Diagram — NeuMF Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    in1[User ID Input] --> embU1[User Embedding pU]
    in2[Item ID Input] --> embI1[Item Embedding qI]
    embU1 --> gmfPath[GMF Tower Elementwise Product]
    embI1 --> gmfPath
    embU1 --> mlpPath[MLP Tower Dense Stack]
    embI1 --> mlpPath
    gmfPath --> concatNode[Concatenation Layer]
    mlpPath --> concatNode
    concatNode --> outDense[Output Dense Layer]
    outDense --> sig[Sigmoid Activation]
    sig --> finalPred[Predicted Interaction Probability]
```

---

### 4.3 Diagram — Wide & Deep Dual-Path Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    rawIn[Raw Features x] --> widePath[Cross Product phi x]
    rawIn --> deepPath[Dense Embedding Lookup]
    deepPath --> dnnStack[Deep Neural Network]
    widePath --> sumNode[Logit Sum]
    dnnStack --> sumNode
    sumNode --> sigLayer[Sigmoid]
    sigLayer --> outProb[CTR Probability Output]
```

---

### 4.4 Diagram — SASRec Self-Attention Flow

```mermaid
graph TB
    seqIn[Item Sequence S] --> embStep[Embedding plus Positional Encoding]
    embStep --> qkv[Project to Q K V]
    qkv --> maskStep[Causal Masking]
    maskStep --> softStep[Softmax Normalization]
    softStep --> aggStep[Weighted Value Aggregation]
    aggStep --> ffStep[Feed Forward Network]
    ffStep --> predStep[Next Item Prediction]
```

---

### 4.5 Diagram — LightGCN Propagation over Bipartite Graph

```mermaid
graph TB
    uA[User A] --> iX[Item X]
    uA --> iY[Item Y]
    uB[User B] --> iX
    uB --> iZ[Item Z]
    iX --> uA
    iX --> uB
    iY --> uC[User C]
    iZ --> uC
    hop1[Hop 0 Embedding] --> prop1[Propagation 1st Hop]
    prop1 --> hop2[Hop 1 Embedding]
    hop2 --> prop2[Propagation 2nd Hop]
    prop2 --> hop3[Hop 2 Embedding]
    hop1 --> combine[Weighted Layer Combination]
    hop2 --> combine
    hop3 --> combine
    combine --> outLayer[Final User Item Score]
```

---

### 4.6 Diagram — LinUCB Bandit Decision Loop

```mermaid
graph LR
    ctx[Context Vector x_t_a] --> ridgEst[Ridge Regression Theta Estimate]
    ridgEst --> ucbScore[UCB Score xT Theta plus alpha sqrt xT Ainv x]
    ucbScore --> argmax[Argmax over Arms]
    argmax --> recommend[Recommend Item]
    recommend --> rewardObserve[Observe Reward Click or Not]
    rewardObserve --> updA[Update Matrix A and Vector b]
    updA --> ridgEst
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. Differentiate between memorization and generalization in the context of modern recommendation systems. Give one model that explicitly combines both.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **[CO1, Bloom Level: L2 — Understand]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Memorization (1 mark):** Learning *frequent co-occurrences* and *direct feature correlations* from historical data. It is strong on items the user has already seen or features that co-occur often. Implemented via a **wide linear model** with cross-product features.
- **Generalization (1 mark):** Learning *novel feature combinations* the model has rarely or never seen, based on dense embeddings and neural generalization. Implemented via a **deep neural network** over embeddings.
- **Model that combines both (1 mark):** **Wide & Deep Learning** (Google, 2016). The wide path memorizes; the deep path generalizes; outputs are summed through a sigmoid.

---

#### **Q2. What is the role of self-attention in sequential recommendation systems? Name one popular self-attention-based RS model.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **[CO2, Bloom Level: L2 — Understand]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Role of self-attention (2 marks):** It computes a *weighted context* over a user's past items, allowing the model to **focus on the most relevant historical items** for predicting the next one. It captures long-range dependencies with $O(1)$ path length, unlike RNNs' $O(n)$ sequential path.
- **Model (1 mark):** **SASRec (Self-Attentive Sequential Recommendation)**, or alternatively **BERT4Rec**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often *describe* architectures without explaining **why** a particular component is included. For full marks, you **must justify** each design choice (e.g., *why* LightGCN removes non-linearities, *why* SASRec uses causal masking). Marks are lost when answers are merely *textbook recitations*.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Explain the architecture of Neural Collaborative Filtering (NCF). Describe its GMF and MLP towers and how they are combined in NeuMF.** `[7 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **[CO1, Bloom Level: L3 — Apply]**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Introduction to NCF (1 mark):** NCF generalizes Matrix Factorization by replacing the inner product $p_u^T q_i$ with a neural network $f(p_u, q_i)$ that can learn *arbitrary* non-linear functions from data.
2. **User and item embeddings (1 mark):** Sparse user ID and item ID vectors are first mapped to dense latent vectors $p_u \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and $q_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ via embedding lookup tables.
3. **GMF tower (1.5 marks):** Computes element-wise product $p_u \odot q_i$ and applies an output projection $h^{GMF}$. This preserves the *multiplicative signal* of classical MF while allowing learnable output weights.
4. **MLP tower (1.5 marks):** Concatenates $[p_u; q_i]$ and passes them through $L$ fully-connected layers with ReLU activations, learning complex non-linear interactions.
5. **NeuMF fusion (1.5 marks):** Concatenates the GMF and MLP outputs, $z = [\phi^{GMF} \Vert \phi^{MLP}]$, and feeds them into a final dense layer with sigmoid $\hat{y}_{ui} = \sigma(h^T z)$.
6. **Training (0.5 marks):** Binary cross-entropy loss with negative sampling; end-to-end backpropagation updates both embedding tables and tower weights.

**(b) Discuss the Wide & Deep learning model. How does it balance memorization and generalization? Compare it with DeepFM.** `[7 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **[CO1, Bloom Level: L4 — Analyze]**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Architecture overview (1 mark):** Two parallel paths — a *wide* linear path and a *deep* DNN path — whose logits are summed and passed through a sigmoid.
2. **Wide path — Memorization (1.5 marks):** $y_{wide} = w^T [x, \phi(x)]$, where $\phi(x)$ is a cross-product of binary features. Captures direct co-occurrence patterns (e.g., user_installed_app = X AND impression_app = Y).
3. **Deep path — Generalization (1.5 marks):** Dense embeddings of categorical features are concatenated and passed through a feed-forward DNN. Learns dense, low-dimensional representations that generalize to unseen feature combinations.
4. **Combined output (1 mark):** $P(Y=1 \mid x) = \sigma(y_{wide} + y_{deep} + b)$.
5. **DeepFM comparison (2 marks):**
   - Wide & Deep requires **manual feature engineering** for the wide path (cross-product transformations).
   - **DeepFM** replaces the wide path with a **Factorization Machine (FM)** that automatically captures 1st and 2nd-order feature interactions — *no manual feature engineering*.
   - DeepFM's deep path is the same as Wide & Deep's deep path; the key innovation is the FM substitution.
6. **Industrial relevance (0.5 marks, optional):** Wide & Deep was deployed in Google Play store for app install prediction; DeepFM is widely adopted in CTR prediction for online advertising.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)**

**(a) Describe the SASRec (Self-Attentive Sequential Recommendation) model in detail. Explain the role of causal masking and the self-attention mechanism.** `[7 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **[CO2, Bloom Level: L3 — Apply]**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Problem formulation (1 mark):** Given a user's interaction sequence $S_u = (i_1, i_2, \ldots, i_t)$, predict $i_{t+1}$.
2. **Embedding layer (1 mark):** Each item $i_k$ is mapped to embedding $E_{i_k} \in \mathbb{R}^d$, and a positional embedding $P_k$ is added to preserve order: $\hat{E}_k = E_{i_k} + P_k$.
3. **Self-attention block (2 marks):** The sequence is projected into $Q, K, V$ matrices via $W^Q, W^K, W^V$. Scaled dot-product attention $\text{Attn}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}(QK^T / \sqrt{d}) V$ is computed for each position.
4. **Causal masking (1.5 marks):** A mask sets attention scores for future positions $j > k$ to $-\infty$ before softmax, ensuring that position $k$ only attends to positions $1, \ldots, k$. This preserves the **autoregressive** property — at inference time, the model cannot cheat by peeking at the future.
5. **Feed-forward and residual connections (1 mark):** Point-wise feed-forward layers with ReLU, plus residual connections and layer normalization, stabilize training across multiple stacked self-attention blocks.
6. **Prediction and loss (0.5 marks):** Final output is a probability distribution over the item catalogue via softmax; trained with cross-entropy + negative sampling (or BPR loss).

**(b) Explain the LightGCN model. How does it simplify Graph Convolutional Networks (GCN) for collaborative filtering? Why does this simplification often improve performance?** `[7 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **[CO2, Bloom Level: L4 — Analyze]**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Graph formulation (1 mark):** User–item interactions form a bipartite graph $\mathcal{G} = (\mathcal{V}, \mathcal{E})$ with adjacency matrix $A$.
2. **Symmetric normalization (1 mark):** $\tilde{A} = D^{-1/2} A D^{-1/2}$ where $D$ is the degree matrix. This prevents high-degree nodes from dominating the aggregation.
3. **Linear propagation rule (1.5 marks):** $E^{(k+1)} = \tilde{A} E^{(k)}$. Each step aggregates the embeddings of immediate neighbours only.
4. **Removal of non-linearities and feature transformations (2 marks):** Unlike standard GCN, LightGCN omits the per-layer weight matrix $W^{(k)}$ and the non-linear activation $\sigma(\cdot)$. The authors argue that for CF tasks, the **graph structure itself already provides sufficient regularization**; non-linearities add parameters that overfit on sparse user–item signals.
5. **Layer combination (1 mark):** $E = \frac{1}{K+1} \sum_{k=0}^{K} E^{(k)}$ — embeddings from all propagation depths are averaged, capturing both local (shallow) and global (deep) structure.
6. **Why simplification works (1 mark):** Empirically, removing non-linearities (i) reduces overfitting on sparse signals, (ii) accelerates training, and (iii) yields more *interpretable* propagation — the resulting embeddings are smooth functions of graph structure.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this checklist. It is the minimum you must know for any KTU exam question on this topic.**

- [x] **Classical CF → MF → Deep learning RS** is the high-level evolution arc.
- [x] **NCF** replaces the inner product with a neural network; **NeuMF** fuses GMF + MLP towers.
- [x] **Wide & Deep** = wide linear path (memorization) + deep DNN (generalization); output logits are summed.
- [x] **DeepFM** replaces the wide path with an FM (no manual feature engineering).
- [x] **SASRec** = self-attention + causal masking + positional embeddings; ideal for long sequences.
- [x] **BERT4Rec** uses *bidirectional* masked-item prediction, but requires a different training objective.
- [x] **LightGCN** removes GCN's non-linearities and weight matrices — a *deliberate* simplification.
- [x] **Graph propagation formula:** $E^{(k+1)} = D^{-1/2} A D^{-1/2} E^{(k)}$; layer combination uses equal weights.
- [x] **LinUCB** = linear reward assumption + Ridge regression + UCB exploration bonus.
- [x] **Bandit algorithms** are essential for *online* recommendation where exploration is critical.
- [x] **Knowledge graph methods** (RippleNet, KGAT) inject side information via triple propagation.
- [x] **Evaluation metrics** for these systems: HR@K, NDCG@K, MRR, Recall@K, AUC.
- [x] **Industrial deployments:** Wide & Deep (Google Play), SASRec (multiple e-commerce platforms), PinSage (Pinterest), LinUCB (Yahoo news).
- [x] **CO mapping:** This topic maps to **CO1** and **CO2** of PECDT741 under KTU 2024 Scheme.
- [x] **Bloom's levels tested:** L2 (Understand), L3 (Apply), L4 (Analyze). No L5/L6 (Evaluate/Create) on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Content-based Recommendation - Content Representation and Content Similarity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT741 - RECOMMENDATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Content-based Recommendation - Content Representation and Content Similarity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Content-Based Recommendation — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Content-Based Recommendation (CBR)* is a recommendation paradigm that suggests items to a user based on the **semantic and attribute-level similarity** between the **content features of items** the user has previously liked (positive interaction history) and the **content features of candidate items** in the catalogue. It is a *knowledge-driven, item-centric* approach that does **not require interactions from other users**, making it immune to the *cold-start user problem* in collaborative filtering.

The two pillars of any Content-Based Recommender System (CBRS), as prescribed in the KTU 2024 PECDT741 syllabus for **Module 1**, are:

1. **Content Representation** — How do we mathematically describe an *item* (or *user preference*) using a feature vector?
2. **Content Similarity** — Given two such representations, how do we *quantify* how close they are in the feature space?

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** This is the conceptual foundation. Almost every Module 1 / Module 2 question traces back to "How do we represent items?" and "How do we measure similarity?" — master these, and the rest of the recommender system course becomes far more approachable.

---

## 1.2 The Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you walk into a **small neighbourhood bookshop in Kochi** where the owner, *Appukuttan*, has known you for ten years. Every time you buy a book, he notes it down. One day you come in asking for a gift. What does Appukuttan do?

He does **not** ask other customers what they liked. He looks at:

- The **subjects** of the books you have previously bought (mystery, history, post-colonial literature)
- The **authors** you prefer (your Krishnan Panikkar, your Amitav Ghosh)
- The **writing style** (long descriptive passages, minimal dialogue)

He then walks to the shelf and pulls out a book that *looks similar* in those features — maybe a new *Arundhati Roy* novel or a historical thriller set in colonial Bengal.

> **That, in essence, is a Content-Based Recommender System.**

| Human Behaviour (Appukuttan) | System Equivalent (CBRS) |
|---|---|
| Notes the books you bought | Builds your **User Profile** |
| Identifies subjects/authors | Extracts **Item Features** (TF-IDF, metadata) |
| Compares new book to your tastes | Computes **Content Similarity** (Cosine, Jaccard) |
| Recommends the closest match | Ranks items by **similarity score** |

The two key technical operations are therefore:
- **Content Representation** → Convert unstructured item data (text, tags, attributes) into a **numerical vector** (a point in high-dimensional space).
- **Content Similarity** → Compute a **numeric score** $\in [0,1]$ or $[-1,1]$ that captures how close two such vectors are.

> [!TIP]
