Suppose a recommender must decide whether to recommend a movie to user $U$. Let:
- $H$ = event "User $U$ likes the movie".
- $E$ = observed event "User $U$ watched similar director's previous film".

Given:
- $P(H) = 0.2$ (prior — sparse data)
- $P(E \mid H) = 0.8$ (likelihood)
- $P(E \mid \neg H) = 0.3$

We apply Bayes' theorem:

$$
P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute the marginal $P(E)$ via total probability:**

$$
P(E) = P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H) + P(E \mid \neg H) \cdot P(\neg H)
$$

$$
P(E) = (0.8)(0.2) + (0.3)(0.8) = 0.16 + 0.24 = 0.40
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bayes' theorem:**

$$
P(H \mid E) = \frac{(0.8)(0.2)}{0.40} = \frac{0.16}{0.40} = 0.40
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

The posterior probability that the user will like the movie **increased from 20% to 40%** after observing the evidence $E$. A recommender with a decision threshold of, say, $0.35$ would now confidently recommend.

> [!TIP]
> KTU frequently tests this exact 3-step Bayesian pattern. **Memorise the marginalisation step — students most often forget to compute $P(E)$ before dividing.**

---

## 3.4 Worked Derivation 4 — Resolution Refutation

We want to prove that $R$ follows from the following KB:

1. $P \Rightarrow Q$ (i.e., $\neg P \lor Q$)
2. $Q \Rightarrow R$ (i.e., $\neg Q \lor R$)
3. $P$ (fact)

**Negate the goal:** $\neg R$

**Convert all to CNF:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1) &\quad \neg P \lor Q \\
(2) &\quad \neg Q \lor R \\
(3) &\quad P \\
(4) &\quad \neg R \quad \text{(negated goal)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Resolve (1) and (3):**

$$
\frac{\neg P \lor Q \quad\quad P}{Q} \quad \text{(resolvent)}
$$

**Resolve new clause with (2):**

$$
\frac{Q \quad\quad \neg Q \lor R}{R}
$$

**Resolve $R$ with (4):**

$$
\frac{R \quad\quad \neg R}{\text{NIL} \; (\text{empty clause})}
$$

**Derivation complete — the empty clause is the contradiction, so the KB entails $R$.** ✓

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Scheme | Expressiveness | Reasoning Support | Typical Use Case | Computational Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Propositional Logic | Low | Truth tables, SAT solvers | Small static KBs | NP-complete |
| Horn Clauses | Medium-Low | Forward / Backward chaining | Expert systems, Prolog | Polynomial |
| Description Logic (DL-Lite) | Medium | Subsumption, classification | Ontologies, OWL | Polynomial (PTime) |
| Description Logic ($\mathcal{SHOIN}$) | High | Subsumption, classification | Medical ontologies (SNOMED) | ExpTime |
| Full First-Order Logic | Very High | Resolution, unification | Mathematical KB, Cyc | Semi-decidable |
| Knowledge Graph Embeddings | High (probabilistic) | Link prediction (TransE, etc.) | Modern recommenders | Polynomial training |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway:** Modern recommendation systems blend **symbolic** KRR (for explainability) with **sub-symbolic** embeddings (for scalability). The KTU syllabus covers this hybrid view in Module 5 — but the foundation established here is essential.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of a Knowledge-Based Recommender

The following Mermaid block illustrates how knowledge representation and reasoning components integrate inside a modern recommendation pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[User Profile Data] --> KB1[Knowledge Base T Box]
    A2[Item Catalogue] --> KB1
    A3[Domain Ontology] --> KB1
    A4[Contextual Logs] --> KB1

    KB1 --> KB2[Knowledge Base A Box Instances]
    KB2 --> RE1[Reasoning Engine Module]

    subgraph ReasoningCore
        RE1 --> RE2[Forward Chainer]
        RE1 --> RE3[Backward Chainer]
        RE1 --> RE4[Bayesian Inferencer]
        RE1 --> RE5[Description Logic Reasoner]
    end

    RE2 --> OUT1[Inferred Preferences]
    RE3 --> OUT1
    RE4 --> OUT1
    RE5 --> OUT1

    OUT1 --> REC[Recommendation Generator]
    REC --> EXPL[Explainability Layer]
    EXPL --> USER[End User]
```

---

## 4.2 Semantic Network Example (Movie Domain)

The diagram below depicts a small semantic network for movies and genres. IS-A edges support inheritance-based reasoning.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    n1[Movie]
    n2[ActionMovie]
    n3[DramaMovie]
    n4[Inception]
    n5[Godfather]
    n6[ChristopherNolan]
    n7[FrancisFordCoppola]
    n8[UserU]
    n9[Action]
    n10[Drama]

    n2 -- IS-A --> n1
    n3 -- IS-A --> n1
    n4 -- IS-A --> n2
    n5 -- IS-A --> n3
    n4 -- directedBy --> n6
    n5 -- directedBy --> n7
    n2 -- hasGenre --> n9
    n3 -- hasGenre --> n10
    n6 -- preferredDirector --> n8
    n7 -- preferredDirector --> n8
    n4 -- watchedBy --> n8
```

**Reasoning trace:** $UserU \rightarrow \text{prefers} \rightarrow \text{ChristopherNolan} \rightarrow \text{directed} \rightarrow \text{Inception}$. Therefore recommend *Inception* (or similar Nolan's films).

---

## 4.3 Frame Representation — Movie Frame

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    F0[Frame Movie Title]
    F0 --> S1[Slot title]
    F0 --> S2[Slot genre default UNKNOWN]
    F0 --> S3[Slot director]
    F0 --> S4[Slot actors cardinality AT_LEAST 1]
    F0 --> S5[Slot rating type FLOAT 0 to 10]
    F0 --> S6[Slot similarTo via PROCEDURE computeSimilarity]
    F0 --> S7[Inheritance IS-A Entertainment]
```

Slots like `rating` carry **type constraints**, while `similarTo` carries an **attached procedural method** — illustrating that frames blend *declarative* and *procedural* knowledge.

---

## 4.4 Forward Chaining vs. Backward Chaining — Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Forward[Forward Chaining Data Driven]
        F1[Start] --> F2[Load Working Memory]
        F2 --> F3{Any rule fires?}
        F3 -- Yes --> F4[Fire rule and add conclusion to WM]
        F4 --> F3
        F3 -- No --> F5[Stop No more inferences]
    end

    subgraph Backward[Backward Chaining Goal Driven]
        B1[Start with goal G] --> B2[Find rules that conclude G]
        B2 --> B3{Any matching rule?}
        B3 -- Yes --> B4[Recursively prove each antecedent]
        B4 --> B5{All antecedents proven?}
        B5 -- Yes --> B6[Success]
        B5 -- No --> B3
        B3 -- No --> B7[Failure]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam cue:** When a question says "explain data-driven reasoning" → it is **forward chaining**. When it says "goal-driven" → it is **backward chaining**. This is a one-line keyword match in the KTU marking scheme.

---

## 4.5 Knowledge Graph Embedding (TransE) — Geometric Intuition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    e1[Head Entity h]
    e2[Relation r vector]
    e3[Tail Entity t]
    e1 --> e2
    e2 --> e3
    e2 -.->|h + r approx t| e1
```

The TransE principle: in the embedding space, the relation $r$ acts as a **translation vector** such that:

$$
h + r \approx t
$$

A triple is *plausible* when the $\ell_2$ distance $\vert h + r - t \vert_2$ is small. This symbolic-to-sub-symbolic bridging is the cornerstone of modern KG-based recommenders.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are **modelled exactly on the KTU 2024 ESE pattern**: short Part A (3 marks, ~50 words) and Part B (14 marks with internal choice, sub-parts of 7 + 7 marks). Solutions follow the KTU Board valuation key style — concise, point-form, with essential equations.

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define Knowledge Representation. List any two knowledge representation schemes used in AI.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Knowledge Representation (KR)** is the method used to encode information about the world into a formal language that a computer can process for problem-solving. **(1 mark)**
> Two schemes: **(1) Semantic Networks** — a graph of concepts and labelled relations. **(2) Frames** — structured data records with slots, defaults, and inheritance. **(2 marks, 1 each)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between forward chaining and backward chaining.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Forward Chaining | Backward Chaining |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Data-driven (facts → conclusions) | Goal-driven (goal → supporting facts) |
| Trigger | New fact in WM | Query to be proved |
| Use case | Monitoring, planning | Diagnosis, Prolog queries |

**(3 marks — 1 for direction, 1 for trigger, 1 for example)**

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks split into two 7-mark sub-parts. Both questions are independent and from the same module.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]**
**Explain the main knowledge representation schemes in Artificial Intelligence. Compare their expressiveness and computational complexity.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Propositional Logic (1 mark):** Uses atomic propositions with connectives. Decidable via truth tables but NP-complete for satisfiability. Limited expressiveness (no variables/quantifiers).

**(ii) First-Order Logic (1 mark):** Adds variables, quantifiers $\forall, \exists$, and predicates. Highly expressive but **semi-decidable** — no guarantee of termination.

**(iii) Semantic Networks (1 mark):** Graph-based, intuitive, but lack formal semantics → ambiguous inference.

**(iv) Frames (1 mark):** Slot-filler structures with defaults and inheritance. Used in object-oriented AI. Reasoning via slot-filling and inheritance.

**(v) Production Rules (1 mark):** IF-THEN rules; very efficient for forward/backward chaining on Horn clauses. Polynomial-time.

**(vi) Description Logics (1 mark):** Formal foundation of OWL. Decidable subsumption reasoning. Tunable complexity (DL-Lite is PTime, $\mathcal{SHOIN}$ is ExpTime).

**(vii) Trade-off (1 mark):**
$$
\text{Expressiveness} \uparrow \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \text{Tractability} \downarrow
$$
Conclude with a small comparison table summarising complexity classes.

---

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]**
**Consider the following knowledge base of Horn rules. Apply forward chaining to determine all derivable facts, given working memory $\{A, B\}$.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1 &: A \Rightarrow C \\
R_2 &: B \Rightarrow D \\
R_3 &: C \land D \Rightarrow E \\
R_4 &: A \land E \Rightarrow F \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Model Solution:**

**Initial WM:** $\{A, B\}$ — (state clearly) **(0.5 mark)**

**Step 1 — Fire $R_1$** (antecedents: $A$) — (1 mark)
$$
A \in \text{WM} \Rightarrow C \text{ added}. \quad \text{WM} = \{A, B, C\}
$$

**Step 2 — Fire $R_2$** (antecedents: $B$) — (1 mark)
$$
B \in \text{WM} \Rightarrow D \text{ added}. \quad \text{WM} = \{A, B, C, D\}
$$

**Step 3 — Fire $R_3$** (antecedents: $C, D$) — (1.5 marks)
$$
C, D \in \text{WM} \Rightarrow E \text{ added}. \quad \text{WM} = \{A, B, C, D, E\}
$$

**Step 4 — Fire $R_4$** (antecedents: $A, E$) — (1.5 marks)
$$
A, E \in \text{WM} \Rightarrow F \text{ added}. \quad \text{WM} = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\}
$$

**Step 5 — Termination:** No new rule fires. **Final WM = $\{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$.** (1 mark)

**Conclusion:** $E$ and $F$ are derived facts. (0.5 mark)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]**
**Describe the architecture of a knowledge-based recommender system. Explain how Description Logic and Knowledge Graphs support reasoning in this context.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Architecture (3 marks):** A knowledge-based recommender consists of:
- **Knowledge Base** (T-Box = ontology schema, A-Box = instance data).
- **Reasoning Engine** (subsumption checker, rule engine, probabilistic reasoner).
- **Recommendation Generator** (uses inferred preferences).
- **Explanation Module** (produces human-readable justifications).
- **User Interface Layer**.

**(ii) Role of Description Logic (2 marks):** DL provides formal semantics for the ontology. Concepts, roles, and individuals are described using constructors ($\sqcap, \sqcup, \neg, \exists, \forall$). The reasoner performs:
- **Subsumption** ($C \sqsubseteq D$?).
- **Consistency** checking.
- **Classification** — placing new individuals in the hierarchy.

**(iii) Role of Knowledge Graphs (2 marks):** KGs store facts as triples $\langle h, r, t \rangle$. They enable:
- **Path-based reasoning** (multi-hop inference).
- **Embedding-based link prediction** (TransE, ComplEx) for missing relations.
- **Contextual recommendations** by traversing the graph.

> *(Draw a small block diagram in the exam — 1 mark extra for clarity.)*

---

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]**
**A recommender observes the following:**
- $P(\text{User likes movie}) = 0.3$
- $P(\text{User watched trailer} \mid \text{likes}) = 0.9$
- $P(\text{User watched trailer} \mid \text{dislikes}) = 0.4$

**Using Bayes' theorem, find the posterior probability that the user likes the movie given that they watched the trailer. Comment on the recommendation decision.**

**Model Solution:**

**Let** $H$ = likes, $E$ = watched trailer. Given: $P(H) = 0.3$, $P(E \mid H) = 0.9$, $P(E \mid \neg H) = 0.4$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 1 — Compute $P(E)$ using total probability:** (2 marks)

$$
P(E) = P(E \mid H) P(H) + P(E \mid \neg H) P(\neg H)
$$

$$
P(E) = (0.9)(0.3) + (0.4)(0.7) = 0.27 + 0.28 = 0.55
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bayes' theorem:** (2 marks)

$$
P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)} = \frac{(0.9)(0.3)}{0.55} = \frac{0.27}{0.55} \approx 0.491
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:** (2 marks)
Posterior $\approx 49.1\%$, which is significantly higher than the prior 30%. If the system threshold is 40%, **recommend** the movie. The evidence (watched trailer) raised belief by ~19 percentage points.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on KRR Questions**
> 1. **Confusing representation with reasoning:** Students often describe a representation scheme (e.g., semantic network) without stating *how reasoning happens on it*. Always include the inference mechanism. **(-2 marks)**
> 2. **Forgetting to compute marginal $P(E)$:** In Bayes' theorem problems, marks are split — 2 for the marginal, 2 for the final posterior. Skipping the marginal loses half. **(-2 marks)**
> 3. **In forward-chaining traces, missing the iteration:** Examiners want to see *each iteration* explicitly. Don't collapse steps. **(-1 to -2 marks)**
> 4. **Writing FOL without proper quantifier scoping:** $\forall x P(x)$ is correct; $\forall x (P(x) \rightarrow Q(x))$ is even more precise. Don't write ambiguous formulas. **(-1 mark)**
> 5. **Calling a knowledge graph "the same as a database":** KGs are *semantic* — relations are first-class, typed, and machine-interpretable. Loose terminology is penalised. **(-1 mark)**

---

## 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (last-night revision)**

- ✅ **KR schemes** (know all six): Semantic Networks, Frames, Ontologies (DL), Production Rules, FOL, Knowledge Graphs.
- ✅ **Deductive, Inductive, Abductive** reasoning — remember the *direction* of inference for each.
- ✅ **Forward Chaining** = data-driven; **Backward Chaining** = goal-driven. Both work on **Horn clauses**.
- ✅ **Modus Ponens** is the workhorse rule. **Resolution** is the refutation engine.
- ✅ **Bayes' Theorem**: Always compute $P(E)$ first. Practice 2–3 numericals.
- ✅ **Description Logics**: T-Box (schema), A-Box (data). Key constructors: $\sqcap, \sqcup, \exists, \forall$.
- ✅ **Knowledge Graph triple**: $\langle h, r, t \rangle$. TransE score: $\vert h + r - t \vert$.
- ✅ **Expressiveness vs. Tractability trade-off** is a guaranteed question in some form.
- ✅ **Production System cycle**: Match → Select → Execute (Rete algorithm is the optimisation).
- ✅ **Certainty Factors** ($CF = MB - MD$) for legacy expert systems; **Dempster-Shafer** for modern evidential reasoning.
- ✅ For KTU: keep answers **structured** with sub-headings and equations — examiners reward clarity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constraints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT741 - RECOMMENDATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Constraints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Recommendation Systems (PECDT741 – Module 2)**, **Constraints** refer to the formal restrictions, requirements, and conditions imposed on the variables (attributes) of items, users, and the recommendation process. They are the backbone of **Constraint-Based Recommender Systems** and **Knowledge-Based Recommender Systems (KBRS)**, where recommendations are derived by solving a **Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP)**.

Formally, a **Constraint Satisfaction Problem** is defined as a triple:

$$ \text{CSP} = (V, D, C) $$

where:
- $V = \{v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n\}$ is a finite set of **variables** (e.g., product features such as price, brand, screen size).
- $D = \{D_1, D_2, \dots, D_n\}$ is a set of **domains** specifying the possible values each variable can take.
- $C = \{c_1, c_2, \dots, c_m\}$ is a set of **constraints** restricting the values that variables can simultaneously assume.

A **solution** to a CSP is an assignment of values to all variables such that every constraint $c_i \in C$ is satisfied.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 2, *Constraints* are studied as the *filtering and reasoning mechanism* in recommendation pipelines, especially when the system must respect hard user requirements (e.g., budget ceilings, technical compatibility) that cannot be relaxed.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk into a **car dealership** and tell the salesperson:

> *"I need a car under ₹15,00,000, with a sunroof, automatic transmission, mileage above 15 kmpl, and available in red or black."*

The salesperson does not just guess. They **filter the inventory** using your specific requirements (your *constraints*). The cars that match all conditions become the *recommendation set*. If no single car matches, the salesperson might *relax* one constraint (e.g., stretch the budget to ₹16,00,000) and propose alternatives.

This real-life interaction is exactly what **constraint-based recommendation** does computationally:
- **Your requirements** = Constraints.
- **The car inventory** = Item database.
- **The filtered cars** = Recommendation set.
- **The salesperson's flexibility** = Constraint relaxation / conflict resolution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** Constraints are *rules of the game* — they tell the recommender what is acceptable, what is not, and what trade-offs are allowed when no perfect match exists.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

Key metrics used in constraint-based recommendation (as per KTU 2024 syllabus expectations):

- **Hard Constraint**: A condition that *must* be satisfied; no compromise is allowed.
- **Soft Constraint (Preference)**: A condition that should ideally be satisfied but may be relaxed if no solution exists.
- **Constraint Violation**: Occurs when a variable assignment breaks one or more constraints.
- **Feasible Region**: The subset of the search space where all hard constraints are satisfied.
- **Constraint Propagation**: The process of inferring tighter domains for variables from existing constraints.
- **Backtracking**: A search strategy that undoes variable assignments when a dead-end is reached.
- **Arc Consistency (AC-3 Algorithm)**: A domain-reduction technique that prunes inconsistent values.

> [!TIP]
> **Remember:** Hard constraints = *non-negotiable*. Soft constraints = *wish-list*. KTU questions frequently test this distinction.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic: How Constraints Work in Recommender Systems

The workflow of a **Constraint-Based Recommender System** can be broken into the following logical steps:

1. **User Requirement Elicitation**  
   The system actively or passively collects the user's requirements (via forms, dialogues, or implicit signals). Each requirement becomes a *variable* $v_i$ with a domain $D_i$.

2. **Constraint Construction**  
   The collected requirements are formalized into constraints $c_j \in C$. These may be:  
   - *Unary* (apply to a single variable, e.g., `price ≤ 15,00,000`).  
   - *Binary* (relate two variables, e.g., `if brand = "X" then warranty ≥ 3 years`).  
   - *Global* (relate multiple variables, e.g., `total_weight ≤ max_load_capacity`).

3. **Constraint Propagation & Filtering**  
   The system applies inference techniques (e.g., arc consistency) to shrink the domains $D_i$ and rule out infeasible items.

4. **Solution Search**  
   Using search algorithms (backtracking, forward checking), the system finds item configurations that satisfy all *hard* constraints.

5. **Ranking by Soft Constraints / Utility**  
   Among feasible solutions, items are ranked by how well they satisfy the *soft* constraints (preferences).

6. **Repair & Explanation (if no solution exists)**  
   If the constraint set is unsatisfiable, the system performs *diagnosis* and proposes a *minimal relaxation* of constraints, explaining why no exact match was found.

## 2.2 Types of Constraints in Recommendation

| **Constraint Type** | **Description** | **Example (Laptop Recommender)** |
|---|---|---|
| **User Constraints** | Stated directly by the user | "RAM ≥ 16 GB" |
| **Product/Item Constraints** | Inherent properties of items | "Weight ≤ 2 kg" |
| **Compatibility/Inter-item Constraints** | Restrictions between products | "OS = Windows ↔ Anti-virus bundled" |
| **Filtering/Filtering Constraints** | Used to narrow catalog | "Price ≤ ₹80,000" |
| **Catalog/Inventory Constraints** | Stock availability | "In stock = true" |
| **Process/Interaction Constraints** | System-level rules | "Show at most 10 results per page" |
| **Soft Constraints (Preferences)** | Desired but relaxable | "Brand preference = Dell" |

## 2.3 Why Constraints Matter — The 'Why' and 'How'

- **Why?** Traditional **collaborative filtering** fails in *cold-start* scenarios (new users, new items) and cannot enforce *hard business rules* (e.g., no out-of-stock items, regulatory restrictions). Constraints fill this gap.
- **How?** By encoding domain knowledge explicitly as logical rules, constraints allow the system to behave predictably and explain its recommendations.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the essential mathematical and logical formalisms for *Constraints* in Recommendation Systems. All are **KTU 2024 Scheme exam-ready**.

| **Concept** | **Mathematical / Logical Form** | **Units / Domain** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| CSP Definition | $\text{CSP} = (V, D, C)$ | Set-theoretic | $V$: variables, $D$: domains, $C$: constraints |
| Hard Constraint | $c(v) = \text{true}$ must hold | Boolean | Non-relaxable |
| Soft Constraint | $w \cdot s(v)$ maximized | Utility score | $w$: weight, $s$: satisfaction |
| Utility Function | $U(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \phi_i(x)$ | Scalar | Used to rank feasible items |
| Arc Consistency | $\forall v \in V,\ \forall a \in D_v,\ \exists b \in D_u : (a, b) \in c$ | Logical | Used in AC-3 algorithm |
| Constraint Violation Count | $V_c = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \mathbb{1}[\neg c_j(x)]$ | Integer | For repair strategies |
| Domain Reduction | $D_v' = \{a \in D_v \mid \exists b \in D_u : (a,b) \in c\}$ | Set | After propagation |
| Search Space Size (worst) | $\prod_{i=1}^{n} \vert D_i \vert$ | Integer | Upper bound on assignments |
| Minimal Relaxation | $\min \sum_{j=1}^{m} \delta_j$ subject to feasibility | Optimization | $\delta_j \in \{0,1\}$ drop indicator |

**Critical Rule for LaTeX in Tables:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value notation.  
✅ Correct: $\vert D_i \vert$  
❌ Incorrect: $\lvert D_i \rvert$ (breaks markdown table parsing)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **E-commerce** (Amazon, Flipkart): Apply stock, price, and shipping constraints.
- **Finance**: Loan recommendation must respect regulatory and credit constraints.
- **Healthcare**: Treatment recommenders must enforce contraindication constraints.
- **Telecom/B2B**: Configure-to-order systems (e.g., servers) use heavy constraint logic.
- **Travel**: Trip planners combine budget, visa, and time-window constraints.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From User Requirements to Recommendation

The following derivation walks through the **complete analytical pipeline** by which user-stated constraints are converted into a ranked recommendation list. Each step is fully expanded for KTU 14-mark answers.

### Step 1 — Variable Identification

A user interacting with a *digital camera recommender* provides the following requirements:

- $v_1$: Price $\leq$ ₹50,000
- $v_2$: Resolution $\geq$ 20 MP
- $v_3$: Brand $\in \{$Canon, Sony, Nikon$\}$
- $v_4$: Optical Zoom $\geq$ 10x
- $v_5$: Has WiFi

The variable set is:

$$ V = \{v_1, v_2, v_3, v_4, v_5\} $$

### Step 2 — Domain Construction

Each variable has an associated domain. For example:

$$ D_{v_1} = [5{,}000,\ 5{,}00{,}000] \quad (\text{rupees}) $$
$$ D_{v_2} = [8,\ 64] \quad (\text{megapixels}) $$
$$ D_{v_3} = \{\text{Canon, Sony, Nikon, Fujifilm, Panasonic}\} $$
$$ D_{v_4} = [3,\ 65] \quad (\text{zoom factor}) $$
$$ D_{v_5} = \{\text{Yes, No}\} $$

### Step 3 — Formal Constraint Construction

We translate user requirements into formal predicates $c_j$:

$$ c_1 : v_1 \leq 50{,}000 $$
$$ c_2 : v_2 \geq 20 $$
$$ c_3 : v_3 \in \{\text{Canon, Sony, Nikon}\} $$
$$ c_4 : v_4 \geq 10 $$
$$ c_5 : v_5 = \text{Yes} $$

The complete constraint set is:

$$ C = \{c_1, c_2, c_3, c_4, c_5\} $$

Thus the formal CSP is:

$$ \text{CSP}_{\text{camera}} = \big(V, D, C\big) $$

### Step 4 — Domain Pruning via Constraint Propagation

Apply each unary constraint to reduce domains:

- After $c_1$: $D_{v_1} = [5{,}000,\ 50{,}000]$
- After $c_2$: $D_{v_2} = [20,\ 64]$
- After $c_3$: $D_{v_3} = \{\text{Canon, Sony, Nikon}\}$
- After $c_4$: $D_{v_4} = [10,\ 65]$
- After $c_5$: $D_{v_5} = \{\text{Yes}\}$

Initial worst-case search space:

$$ \vert D_{v_1} \vert \cdot \vert D_{v_2} \vert \cdot \vert D_{v_3} \vert \cdot \vert D_{v_4} \vert \cdot \vert D_{v_5} \vert = 495{,}001 \times 45 \times 3 \times 56 \times 1 $$

Carrying out the multiplication:

$$ = 495{,}001 \times 45 = 22{,}275{,}045 $$
$$ \times\ 3 = 66{,}825{,}135 $$
$$ \times\ 56 = 3{,}742{,}207{,}560 $$
$$ \times\ 1 = 3{,}742{,}207{,}560 $$

So the **unpruned** search space contains about $3.74 \times 10^9$ candidate assignments. By applying unary constraints, we immediately shrink the feasible region without even invoking binary constraints.

### Step 5 — Utility-Based Ranking Using Soft Constraints

Suppose the user adds a *soft preference*:

> *"I prefer Canon over Sony, and Sony over Nikon."*

We assign weights:

$$ w_{\text{Canon}} = 1.0, \quad w_{\text{Sony}} = 0.6, \quad w_{\text{Nikon}} = 0.3 $$

The utility for a candidate item $x$ is:

$$ U(x) = w_{\text{brand}}(x) + 0.2 \cdot \mathbb{1}[x.\text{zoom} \geq 15] + 0.1 \cdot \mathbb{1}[x.\text{mp} \geq 24] $$

A **Canon** camera with 25 MP and 12x zoom scores:

$$ U = 1.0 + 0.2 \cdot 0 + 0.1 \cdot 1 = 1.1 $$

A **Sony** with 22 MP and 20x zoom scores:

$$ U = 0.6 + 0.2 \cdot 1 + 0.1 \cdot 0 = 0.8 $$

A **Nikon** with 20 MP and 10x zoom scores:

$$ U = 0.3 + 0.2 \cdot 0 + 0.1 \cdot 0 = 0.3 $$

Hence the system ranks Canon > Sony > Nikon for the feasible set.

### Step 6 — Minimal Relaxation (When No Solution Exists)

If after propagation $D_{v_3} = \emptyset$ because the user demanded a brand that is out of stock, the system solves a **MIN-ONE relaxation problem**:

$$ \min \sum_{j=1}^{5} \delta_j \quad \text{subject to feasibility} $$

The optimal solution drops $c_3$ (i.e., $\delta_3 = 1$, all others $= 0$) and proposes cameras of *any* brand.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Constraint Solving Techniques

| **Technique** | **Complexity** | **Best Use Case** | **Limitation** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Generate-and-Test | $O(\prod \vert D_i \vert)$ | Tiny catalogs | Exponential blow-up |
| Backtracking | $O(d^n)$ worst case | Medium-sized CSPs | No early pruning |
| Forward Checking | $O(d^n)$ | Interleaved with backtracking | Domain wiped on conflict |
| Arc Consistency (AC-3) | $O(e \cdot d^3)$ | Pre-processing | Doesn't solve alone |
| Constraint Programming (CP) | Variable | Industrial recommender | Steep learning curve |
| SAT Solvers | Variable | Boolean-heavy constraints | Encoding overhead |

Where:
- $d$ = average domain size
- $n$ = number of variables
- $e$ = number of binary constraints

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Constraint-Based Recommender

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **interactions and data flow** of a constraint-based recommendation engine. Node IDs follow the *Alpha Rule* (purely alphanumeric with letter prefix).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[User Input Form] --> B1[Requirement Elicitation Module]
    B1 --> C1[Variable and Domain Builder]
    C1 --> D1[Constraint Formalizer]
    D1 --> E1[Constraint Store]
    E1 --> F1[Constraint Propagation Engine]
    F1 --> G1[Domain Reduced Catalog]
    G1 --> H1[Solver Backtracking Search]
    H1 --> I1{Feasible Solution Found?}
    I1 -- Yes --> J1[Soft Constraint Ranker]
    J1 --> K1[Top N Recommendation List]
    I1 -- No --> L1[Conflict Diagnoser]
    L1 --> M1[Minimal Relaxation Module]
    M1 --> N1[Proposed Relaxation to User]
    N1 --> A1
    E1 --> O1[Knowledge Base of Domain Rules]
    O1 --> F1
    H1 --> P1[Result Logger and Explainer]
    P1 --> K1
```

### Diagram Explanation

- **A1 → B1 → C1 → D1**: The pipeline that converts raw user utterances into a formal CSP.
- **E1 (Constraint Store)**: Central repository holding all hard and soft constraints.
- **O1 (Knowledge Base)**: Domain-specific compatibility and business rules (e.g., regulatory restrictions).
- **F1 (Propagation Engine)**: Implements AC-3 or similar algorithms for domain reduction.
- **H1 (Solver)**: The backtracking search that explores reduced domains.
- **I1 (Decision Diamond)**: Checks whether a feasible solution exists.
- **J1 (Ranker)**: When solutions exist, applies the utility function $U(x)$ to order them.
- **L1 → M1 → N1 → A1**: The *repair loop* — the system negotiates a constraint relaxation with the user.

## 4.2 State Diagram: Lifecycle of a Constraint During Recommendation

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Created: User states requirement
    Created --> Validated: Syntax and domain check pass
    Validated --> Active: Added to active constraint set
    Active --> Propagating: Constraint propagation phase
    Propagating --> Satisfied: Value assignment consistent
    Propagating --> Violated: Conflict detected
    Violated --> Diagnosed: Diagnoser finds culprit
    Diagnosed --> Relaxed: Minimal relaxation applied
    Relaxed --> Active: Re-injected as soft constraint
    Satisfied --> [*]
```

This diagram illustrates the dynamic life of a constraint from the moment it is created until it is either satisfied or relaxed. It is a common KTU question to ask students to draw or describe such lifecycles.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Part A questions test **Remember** and **Understand** levels of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy. Answers should be concise, definition-driven, and 3–5 sentences long.

### Q1. Define a Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP) in the context of recommendation systems. *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP)** in recommendation systems is a formal framework defined as a triple $(V, D, C)$, where $V$ is a set of variables representing item attributes or user requirements, $D$ is the set of domains specifying allowed values for each variable, and $C$ is a set of constraints restricting the combinations of values the variables may take. A recommendation is a complete assignment of values to all variables such that every constraint in $C$ is satisfied. CSPs are used to model *hard* requirements (e.g., budget limits) that the system must respect while generating personalized suggestions.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between *hard* and *soft* constraints with a recommendation example. *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Hard Constraint** | **Soft Constraint** |
|---|---|---|
| Mandatory? | Yes, non-negotiable | No, relaxable |
| Role | Filters feasible items | Ranks feasible items |
| Example | "Price ≤ ₹80,000" | "Brand preference = Sony" |
| Handling | Must be satisfied in solution | Maximized via utility function |

For instance, in a **laptop recommender**, *"RAM ≥ 16 GB"* is a hard constraint that eliminates all laptops with 8 GB, while *"prefer lightweight laptops under 1.5 kg"* is a soft constraint used to rank the feasible set.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Part B questions test **Apply**, **Analyze**, and **Evaluate** levels. Each question has internal choice — students answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B. Sub-parts are typically (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain the role of constraints in recommendation systems. Discuss the various types of constraints with suitable examples. (7 marks)  
*[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]*

#### **Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**  
Constraints are formal restrictions that govern the values variables may take in a recommendation process. They translate user requirements and domain knowledge into machine-checkable rules, allowing the system to produce personalized, explainable, and rule-compliant recommendations.

**Types of Constraints (5 marks):**

1. **User Constraints** — Directly stated by the user.  
   *Example:* "I want a phone with battery life ≥ 12 hours."

2. **Product/Item Constraints** — Inherent properties of the item.  
   *Example:* "Weight of laptop ≤ 2 kg."

3. **Compatibility/Inter-item Constraints** — Logical relationships between multiple items.  
   *Example:* "If a 4G SIM is selected, the device must support LTE band 40."

4. **Filtering Constraints** — Used to eliminate catalog items not matching requirements.  
   *Example:* "Show only items with rating ≥ 4 stars."

5. **Inventory / Catalog Constraints** — Stock and availability rules.  
   *Example:* "Item.in_stock == true."

6. **Process / Interaction Constraints** — Constraints on the recommendation interaction itself.  
   *Example:* "Show at most 20 results per page."

7. **Soft Constraints (Preferences)** — Desired but relaxable conditions.  
   *Example:* "Brand preference = Apple."

**Conclusion (1 mark):**  
A well-engineered recommendation system combines hard constraints (for filtering) with soft constraints (for ranking) to deliver a robust, personalized user experience.

---

#### (b) With a suitable case study, explain how constraint propagation reduces the search space in a recommender. (7 marks)  
*[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]*

#### **Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Case Study Setup (2 marks):**  
Consider an **e-commerce shoe recommender** with the following user requirements:

- $c_1$: Size $\in \{7, 8, 9, 10\}$
- $c_2$: Color $\in \{$Black, White, Blue, Red$\}$
- $c_3$: Price $\leq$ ₹3,000
- $c_4$: Brand $\in \{$Nike, Adidas, Puma, Reebok$\}$
- $c_5$: Rating $\geq$ 4.0

**Worst-Case Search Space (2 marks):**  
$$ 4 \times 4 \times \text{price domain} \times 4 \times \text{rating domain} \approx \text{large} $$

**Constraint Propagation Step (2 marks):**  
Applying *unary* constraints $c_1, c_3, c_5$ reduces the domains of *Price* and *Rating* drastically. Then *binary* constraint (e.g., *"Nike products in size 10 are not available in Blue"*) eliminates infeasible color-brand combinations. The number of feasible items drops from the original catalog of 1,000 to fewer than 30.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**  
Constraint propagation (e.g., AC-3) dramatically prunes the search space *before* search begins, making the recommender efficient even for large catalogs.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) Describe the Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP) formulation. How is it used to model a hotel recommendation scenario? (7 marks)  
*[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]*

#### **Model Solution (7 marks):**

**CSP Formulation (3 marks):**  
A CSP is the triple $(V, D, C)$:
- $V = \{v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n\}$ — variables.
- $D = \{D_1, D_2, \dots, D_n\}$ — domains.
- $C = \{c_1, c_2, \dots, c_m\}$ — constraints.

A *solution* is an assignment satisfying all $c_j \in C$.

**Hotel Scenario (3 marks):**  
For a hotel recommender:
- $V = \{$city, price_per_night, star_rating, amenities, distance_from_airport$\}$
- $D_{price} = [1{,}000, 50{,}000]$, $D_{rating} = [1, 5]$, $D_{amenities} = \{$WiFi, Pool, Gym, Breakfast$\}$, etc.
- $C$:
  - $c_1$: price_per_night $\leq$ 8,000
  - $c_2$: star_rating $\geq$ 4
  - $c_3$: distance_from_airport $\leq$ 15 km
  - $c_4$: amenities must include "WiFi" AND "Breakfast"

**Conclusion (1 mark):**  
The CSP framework allows the hotel recommender to systematically enforce user requirements and rank feasible options using soft constraints (e.g., preferred amenities).

---

#### (b) Explain conflict resolution and minimal relaxation in constraint-based recommenders. Why are they important? (7 marks)  
*[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]*

#### **Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Conflict Resolution (3 marks):**  
When no item satisfies all hard constraints, the system enters *conflict resolution*. The *Conflict Diagnoser* identifies which subset of constraints caused the infeasibility (the *conflict set*). For example, in a travel package recommender, the conflict set might be $\{c_{\text{budget}},\, c_{\text{5-star}},\, c_{\text{weekend dates}}\}$.

**Minimal Relaxation (3 marks):**  
The system then solves a **MIN-ONE Relaxation Problem**:

$$ \min_{C'} \sum_{j=1}^{m} \mathbb{1}[c_j \notin C'] \quad \text{subject to } C' \text{ being satisfiable} $$

The optimal solution drops the *fewest* constraints to make the rest satisfiable. The user is then shown a *proposal*: *"If we relax your budget by ₹2,000, you can have a 5-star hotel on the requested weekend."*

**Importance (1 mark):**  
Conflict resolution and minimal relaxation ensure the system never returns an empty result silently — it *negotiates* with the user, building trust through transparent, explainable recommendations.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**  
> 1. **Confusing CSP with rule-based systems:** CSP uses *variables, domains, and constraints*; rule-based systems use *IF–THEN production rules*. Examiners deduct marks if these are interchanged.  
> 2. **Forgetting the triple $(V, D, C)$:** Always write the full CSP definition explicitly; even a small omission costs 1 mark.  
> 3. **Mixing up hard and soft constraints:** A *soft* constraint is NOT equivalent to a *hard* constraint with a low weight. They differ in *semantics* — relaxability is a logical property, not a numeric one.  
> 4. **Skipping the search-space size calculation:** When asked about "how propagation helps," compute the *before* and *after* search space sizes numerically. Examiners expect explicit numbers.  
> 5. **Omitting explanation of no-solution cases:** Always close your answer by discussing what happens when constraints are unsatisfiable — this is a favourite KTU follow-up.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **CSP Triple:** Always remember $(V, D, C)$ — Variables, Domains, Constraints.
- ✅ **Hard vs. Soft:** Hard = filter; Soft = rank.
- ✅ **Propagation prunes domains; Search finds solutions.**
- ✅ **AC-3 Algorithm:** $O(e \cdot d^3)$ complexity, used to enforce arc consistency.
- ✅ **Backtracking:** Standard search; pair with forward checking for efficiency.
- ✅ **Utility Function:** $U(x) = \sum w_i \cdot \phi_i(x)$ — used to rank feasible items by soft-constraint satisfaction.
- ✅ **Minimal Relaxation:** Solve $\min \sum \delta_j$ subject to feasibility when no solution exists.
- ✅ **Search Space Bound:** Worst case $\prod \vert D_i \vert$ — always show the reduction after propagation.
- ✅ **Real-world importance:** Constraints handle **cold-start** and **rule compliance**, which collaborative filtering cannot.
- ✅ **Use `\vert` in LaTeX** (not `|`) inside markdown tables to avoid parser errors.
- ✅ **Favourite KTU phrasing:** *"Explain how constraints are used in recommender systems"* — always anchor your answer in the **CSP framework** and finish with a **utility-ranking + relaxation** discussion.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cases and Similarities
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT741 - RECOMMENDATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cases and Similarities

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a "Case" in Recommendation Systems?

In the context of Case-Based Reasoning (CBR) applied to recommendation systems, a **case** is a structured, past experience that the system can reuse to solve or recommend a solution to a new problem. Formally, a case is a contextualized piece of knowledge representing an experience that is *lessons-learned* by an intelligent system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Case):**
> A case is a tuple of the form **C = (P, S, O, K)** where:
> - **P** = Problem (or situation/context description) — *what was the user/query like?*
> - **S** = Solution (or recommendation given) — *what was actually recommended?*
> - **O** = Outcome (feedback/utility achieved) — *how did the user respond?*
> - **K** = Knowledge (generalized lessons, optional)

### 1.2 What is Similarity?

**Similarity** is a scalar measure $\text{sim}(x, y) \in [0, 1]$ (or $[-1, 1]$) that quantifies how alike two entities (users, items, or cases) are in their attribute space. It is the *cornerstone* of nearest-neighbor-based recommenders and case retrieval.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distance vs. Similarity are Duals**
> Similarity and Distance are inverses. A common conversion is:
> $$\text{sim}(x,y) = 1 - d(x,y)$$ or $$\text{sim}(x,y) = \frac{1}{1 + d(x,y)}$$
> Always normalize output to a bounded range so that thresholding is meaningful.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Librarian Recommender"

Imagine you walk into a small independent library and ask the **librarian** for a book recommendation.

| Real-World Action | Recommendation-System Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Librarian asks what you read recently | Problem **P** is built from your interaction history |
| Librarian remembers past customers' tastes | **Case-base** of previous customer experiences |
| Librarian compares you to past customer profiles | **Similarity function** computes $\text{sim}(\text{you}, \text{past customer})$ |
| Librarian recommends a book the *similar* customer loved | **Solution reuse** from the most-similar cases |

The librarian does **not** read every book. Instead, the librarian exploits **cases** (past successful recommendations) and **similarity** (customers like you tended to enjoy certain books). This is exactly the operating principle of case-based and neighborhood-based recommenders.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Cases and Similarities" belongs together:**
> Cases are useless unless the system can *retrieve* them. Retrieval is driven by similarity. The entire pipeline — *represent, retrieve, reuse, revise, retain* — is glued together by the similarity function.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Case-Based Reasoning (CBR) Cycle — Kolodner's 4 R's

CBR is a paradigm formalized by **Janet Kolodner (1992)** and standardized by **Aamodt & Plaza (1994)**. It consists of four operational phases, often depicted as a closed cycle because learning is continuous.

### Phase 1 — **Retrieve**
Given a new problem $P_{\text{new}}$, the system searches the case-base $\mathcal{C} = \{C_1, C_2, \dots, C_N\}$ and retrieves the $k$ cases with **highest similarity** to $P_{\text{new}}$.
$$ \text{Retrieve}(\mathcal{C}, P_{\text{new}}) = \text{TopK}_{C_i \in \mathcal{C}} \big[ \text{sim}(P_{\text{new}}, P_i) \big] $$

### Phase 2 — **Reuse**
The solutions $S_i$ of the retrieved cases are **adapted and combined** to propose a solution $S_{\text{proposed}}$ for $P_{\text{new}}$.
$$ S_{\text{proposed}} = f_{\text{adapt}}\big( S_{i_1}, S_{i_2}, \dots, S_{i_k} \big) $$

### Phase 3 — **Revise**
The proposed solution is evaluated (in real life) and either accepted or corrected. The feedback forms the new **Outcome** $O$.

### Phase 4 — **Retain**
The confirmed experience $(P_{\text{new}}, S_{\text{proposed}}, O)$ is **stored as a new case** in the case-base, expanding system knowledge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The cycle ensures **lifelong learning** — every interaction enriches the case-base, so the recommender gets *smarter* with usage.

## 2.2 Case Representation

A case must be machine-readable. Common representations:

| Representation | Description | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Feature Vector** | $C_i = (c_{i1}, c_{i2}, \dots, c_{im})$ | Numeric attributes (ratings, counts) |
| **Attribute-Value Pairs** | $C_i = \{(a_1, v_1), (a_2, v_2), \dots\}$ | Mixed categorical / numeric |
| **Structured (Graph/Object)** | $C_i$ as an object with fields | Rich relational cases (e.g., travel itineraries) |
| **Textual** | Free-text natural language | Help-desk / FAQ recommenders |
| **Weighted Feature Vector** | $C_i = (w_1 c_{i1}, w_2 c_{i2}, \dots)$ | When some attributes matter more (e.g., genre > year for movie rec.) |

## 2.3 Major Similarity Measures — The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation used throughout:** $r_{u,i}$ = rating user $u$ gave item $i$; $\bar{r}_u$ = mean rating of user $u$; $\bar{r}_i$ = mean rating of item $i$. $U$ = set of users; $I$ = set of items.

| # | Similarity | Formula (LaTeX-safe) | Range | Best Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cosine Similarity** | $$\text{sim}_{\cos}(u,v) = \frac{\sum_{i \in I_{uv}} r_{u,i} \cdot r_{v,i}}{\sqrt{\sum_{i \in I_{u}} r_{u,i}^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i \in I_{v}} r_{v,i}^2}}$$ | $[0, 1]$ | Sparse rating vectors; treats all ratings as positive orientation |
| 2 | **Pearson Correlation** | $$\text{sim}_{\text{pear}}(u,v) = \frac{\sum_{i \in I_{uv}} (r_{u,i} - \bar{r}_u)(r_{v,i} - \bar{r}_v)}{\sqrt{\sum (r_{u,i} - \bar{r}_u)^2} \sqrt{\sum (r_{v,i} - \bar{r}_v)^2}}$$ | $[-1, 1]$ | Captures rating *trends* (a harsh 3 from user = a 5 from another) |
| 3 | **Adjusted Cosine** | $$\text{sim}_{\text{adjcos}}(i,j) = \frac{\sum_{u \in U_{ij}} (r_{u,i} - \bar{r}_u)(r_{u,j} - \bar{r}_u)}{\sqrt{\sum (r_{u,i} - \bar{r}_u)^2} \sqrt{\sum (r_{u,j} - \bar{r}_u)^2}}$$ | $[-1, 1]$ | **Item-item** filtering (removes user-bias) |
| 4 | **Jaccard Index (sets)** | $$\text{sim}_J(A,B) = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$$ | $[0, 1]$ | Binary interactions (purchased / clicked / watched) |
| 5 | **Jaccard (weighted)** | $$\text{sim}_J^{\text{rating}} = \frac{\sum_{i \in I_{uv}} \min(r_{u,i}, r_{v,i})}{\sum_{i \in I_{uv}} \max(r_{u,i}, r_{v,i})}$$ | $[0, 1]$ | Bounded ratings (1–5 stars) |
| 6 | **Spearman Rank** | Pearson on rank positions of ratings | $[-1, 1]$ | When ordinal position matters more than exact value |
| 7 | **Euclidean (normalized)** | $$\text{sim}_{\text{eucl}}(u,v) = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{\sum_{i \in I_{uv}} (r_{u,i} - r_{v,i})^2}}$$ | $(0, 1]$ | Dense, continuous feature vectors |
| 8 | **Manhattan / City-block** | $$\text{sim}_{\text{man}}(u,v) = \frac{1}{1 + \sum_{i \in I_{uv}} \vert r_{u,i} - r_{v,i} \vert}$$ | $(0, 1]$ | High-dimensional sparse vectors; robust to outliers |
| 9 | **Log-Likelihood Similarity** | Ratio of observed vs. expected co-occurrence | $\mathbb{R}$ | Implicit feedback (clicks, views) where ratings absent |
| 10 | **Tanimoto Coefficient** | Generalization of Jaccard to vectors with shared/non-shared features | $[0, 1]$ | Binary fingerprint / molecular recommender systems |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — Pearson's denominator:**
> If two users have **no common items** ($I_{uv} = \emptyset$), Pearson is **undefined** (0/0). Always return 0 (or skip) — do not let the system crash. Board examiners specifically test this edge case.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in Real Recommender Systems

| Industry | Similarity Used | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Netflix / MovieLens** | Adjusted Cosine + Pearson | Item-item scaling, large rating matrices |
| **Amazon** | Item-item cosine on purchase vectors | Sparse, binary implicit feedback |
| **Spotify** | Cosine on audio-feature + listening vectors | Hybrid: content + collaborative |
| **LinkedIn** | Tanimoto / Jaccard on skill sets | Binary skill tags, set-based |
| **Drug Discovery (pharma)** | Tanimoto on molecular fingerprints | Structural similarity → analogous bio-activity |
| **Travel / Trip planning** | CBR over itineraries | Cases = past trips, similarity = profile + context match |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example — Cosine Similarity Derivation on a Mini Rating Matrix

Consider three users **A, B, C** and four items **$i_1, i_2, i_3, i_4$** rated on a 1–5 scale (blank = not rated).

| User | $i_1$ | $i_2$ | $i_3$ | $i_4$ |
|------|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A    | 5   | 3   | 4   | 4   |
| B    | 5   |     | 5   |     |
| C    |     | 2   |     | 1   |

**Goal:** Compute $\text{sim}_{\cos}(A, B)$, $\text{sim}_{\cos}(A, C)$, $\text{sim}_{\text{pear}}(A, B)$.

### 3.1.1 Cosine Similarity between A and B

**Step 1 — Identify common items** $I_{AB}$: ratings exist for both A and B on items $i_1$ and $i_3$.

$$ I_{AB} = \{i_1, i_3\} $$

**Step 2 — Compute numerator (dot product over ALL items rated by A or B, treating blanks as 0):**

$$ \vec{A} = (5, 3, 4, 4), \quad \vec{B} = (5, 0, 5, 0) $$

$$ \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = (5)(5) + (3)(0) + (4)(5) + (4)(0) = 25 + 0 + 20 + 0 = 45 $$

**Step 3 — Compute magnitudes:**

$$ \Vert \vec{A} \Vert = \sqrt{5^2 + 3^2 + 4^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{25 + 9 + 16 + 16} = \sqrt{66} \approx 8.124 $$

$$ \Vert \vec{B} \Vert = \sqrt{5^2 + 0^2 + 5^2 + 0^2} = \sqrt{25 + 25} = \sqrt{50} \approx 7.071 $$

**Step 4 — Final cosine value:**

$$ \text{sim}_{\cos}(A, B) = \frac{45}{8.124 \times 7.071} = \frac{45}{57.445} \approx 0.783 $$

### 3.1.2 Cosine Similarity between A and C

$$ \vec{C} = (0, 2, 0, 1) $$

$$ \vec{A} \cdot \vec{C} = (5)(0) + (3)(2) + (4)(0) + (4)(1) = 0 + 6 + 0 + 4 = 10 $$

$$ \Vert \vec{C} \Vert = \sqrt{0 + 4 + 0 + 1} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236 $$

$$ \text{sim}_{\cos}(A, C) = \frac{10}{8.124 \times 2.236} = \frac{10}{18.16} \approx 0.551 $$

**Interpretation:** $A$ is more similar to $B$ (0.783) than to $C$ (0.551). B is therefore a *better neighbor* of A.

### 3.1.3 Pearson Correlation between A and B

**Step 1 — Means over common items $I_{AB} = \{i_1, i_3\}$:**

$$ \bar{r}_A^{(AB)} = \frac{5 + 4}{2} = 4.5, \quad \bar{r}_B^{(AB)} = \frac{5 + 5}{2} = 5.0 $$

**Step 2 — Center the ratings:**

$$ A_c = (5 - 4.5,\ 4 - 4.5) = (0.5,\ -0.5) $$
$$ B_c = (5 - 5.0,\ 5 - 5.0) = (0.0,\ 0.0) $$

**Step 3 — Pearson formula:**

$$ \text{sim}_{\text{pear}}(A, B) = \frac{(0.5)(0) + (-0.5)(0)}{\sqrt{0.5^2 + (-0.5)^2} \cdot \sqrt{0^2 + 0^2}} = \frac{0}{\sqrt{0.5} \cdot 0} = \frac{0}{0} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Undefined!** Because B's centered ratings are *both zero*, B is a *constant rater* on the common subset. Pearson is undefined. In production code, return **0** (or skip this pair). This is a frequent KTU exam question.

### 3.1.4 Final Decision — Which Similarity to Use?

| Pair | Cosine | Pearson | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| A–B | 0.783 | undefined | Use Cosine |
| A–C | 0.551 | (same trap may apply) | Use Cosine with caution |

For this dataset, **Cosine similarity is more robust** because Pearson fails on the constant-rater edge case.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Choosing a Similarity Function

| Scenario | Recommended Similarity | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Dense, numeric ratings | Pearson or Adjusted Cosine | Removes user-bias |
| Sparse, binary (buy/click) | Jaccard / Tanimoto | No rating magnitude to use |
| Vector embeddings (word2vec, BERT) | Cosine | Vectors are dense and high-dimensional |
| Constant-rate edge case | Cosine or Jaccard | Pearson undefined |
| Ordinal rankings | Spearman | Order matters, not magnitude |
| High-dimensional sparse | Cosine / Tanimoto | Avoids curse of dimensionality |
| Implicit feedback only | Log-Likelihood | Captures statistical co-occurrence, not raw count |

## 3.3 Derivation of Adjusted Cosine from Cosine (the Bias-Correction Step)

**Starting observation:** Two users may agree on the *direction* of preference but differ in *magnitude* (one rates everything high, the other low). Cosine still works, but Pearson is preferred for **items** where users disagree.

**Cosine** is symmetric in user-rating scale.

**Pearson** subtracts user means → user-bias removed → captures *trend* similarity.

**Adjusted Cosine** applies the same trick but on the **item** axis (subtract $\bar{r}_u$ per user) for **item-item** similarity. This solves the cross-rater normalization problem.

$$ \boxed{\text{sim}_{\text{adjcos}}(i, j) = \frac{\sum_{u \in U_{ij}} (r_{u,i} - \bar{r}_u)(r_{u,j} - \bar{r}_u)}{\sqrt{\sum_{u \in U_{ij}} (r_{u,i} - \bar{r}_u)^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{u \in U_{ij}} (r_{u,j} - \bar{r}_u)^2}} $$

> [!TIP]
> The denominator is *zero* when an item is rated by exactly one user — guard against division by zero in code.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
