# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define a "case" in a case-based reasoning recommender. List its four standard components.**

**Model Answer:**
A case in a CBR recommender is a structured past experience used to recommend a solution to a new, similar problem. The four standard components are:
1. **Problem (P):** description of the user's situation / query context
2. **Solution (S):** the recommendation given in that past experience
3. **Outcome (O):** the actual feedback / utility achieved
4. **Knowledge (K):** generalized lessons abstracted from the case (optional)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State the Cosine similarity formula for two users $u$ and $v$ in a rating-based system. Why is it preferred over Pearson for sparse matrices?**

**Model Answer:**
$$ \text{sim}_{\cos}(u,v) = \frac{\sum_{i \in I_{uv}} r_{u,i} \cdot r_{v,i}}{\sqrt{\sum_{i \in I_{u}} r_{u,i}^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i \in I_{v}} r_{v,i}^2}} $$
Cosine is preferred for sparse matrices because (a) it tolerates unrated items (treats them as zero and is well-defined even with one common item), (b) it is scale-invariant in the magnitude of the rating range, and (c) it does not require mean-centering, which fails when a user has rated very few items (Pearson denominator becomes zero).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the four phases of the Case-Based Reasoning cycle (Aamodt & Plaza) with a real-world e-commerce example.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast Cosine, Pearson, and Adjusted-Cosine similarity. State a practical scenario where each is best suited.

#### Model Solution — (a)

The four phases are:

| Phase | Purpose | E-commerce Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Retrieve** | Find similar past cases | New visitor browses *wireless headphones*. System retrieves past sessions where other users also browsed wireless headphones. |
| **Reuse** | Adapt solution to new problem | Suggests a top-rated product from the retrieved cases; if user budget differs, swap for a cheaper variant in the same category. |
| **Revise** | Validate and correct | User ignores the suggestion → system notes the recommendation was ineffective and re-ranks. |
| **Retain** | Store as a new case | The new session is indexed in the case-base so future similar queries can be answered more accurately. |

**Key insight:** The cycle is closed because every Revise step feeds Retain, ensuring continuous improvement.

#### Model Solution — (b)

| Property | Cosine | Pearson | Adjusted Cosine |
|---|---|---|---|
| Removes user mean? | No | Yes (over common items) | Yes (per user across all items) |
| Removes item mean? | No | No | No (it's for item-item) |
| Captures trend? | No (magnitude-based) | Yes | Yes |
| Defined on single common item? | Yes | No (variance = 0) | No |
| Range | $[0, 1]$ | $[-1, 1]$ | $[-1, 1]$ |
| Best Suited For | Sparse binary / dense embeddings | User-user on dense ratings | Item-item collaborative filtering |

**Practical scenarios:**
- **Cosine** → Word/document recommenders, content-based on TF-IDF vectors.
- **Pearson** → MovieLens user-user filtering where users have rated many common movies.
- **Adjusted Cosine** → Amazon-style item-item filtering on a 5-star scale where the same product can be rated harshly by some users and kindly by others.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Given the user-item rating matrix below (1–5 scale, blank = unrated), compute the **Cosine similarity** between user A and user B, and between user A and user C. Show every step.

| User | $i_1$ | $i_2$ | $i_3$ | $i_4$ |
|------|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A    | 5   | 3   | 4   | 4   |
| B    | 5   | 1   | 5   | 2   |
| C    | 2   | 4   | 1   | 5   |

**(b) [7 Marks]** Define Jaccard similarity. Compute the Jaccard index between two users A and B whose *purchased-item sets* are $P_A = \{1, 3, 5, 7\}$ and $P_B = \{3, 5, 8\}$. Discuss the limitation of plain Jaccard in rating systems and how the *weighted* Jaccard variant addresses it.

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Vectors (treating blanks as 0):**
$$ \vec{A} = (5, 3, 4, 4), \quad \vec{B} = (5, 1, 5, 2), \quad \vec{C} = (2, 4, 1, 5) $$

**Dot products:**
$$ \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = 25 + 3 + 20 + 8 = 56 $$
$$ \vec{A} \cdot \vec{C} = 10 + 12 + 4 + 20 = 46 $$

**Magnitudes:**
$$ \Vert \vec{A} \Vert = \sqrt{25+9+16+16} = \sqrt{66} \approx 8.124 $$
$$ \Vert \vec{B} \Vert = \sqrt{25+1+25+4} = \sqrt{55} \approx 7.416 $$
$$ \Vert \vec{C} \Vert = \sqrt{4+16+1+25} = \sqrt{46} \approx 6.782 $$

**Cosine Similarities:**
$$ \text{sim}(A,B) = \frac{56}{8.124 \times 7.416} = \frac{56}{60.25} \approx 0.930 $$

$$ \text{sim}(A,C) = \frac{46}{8.124 \times 6.782} = \frac{46}{55.10} \approx 0.835 $$

**Interpretation:** A is more similar to B (0.930) than to C (0.835). B is the *better neighbor* of A.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Jaccard Definition:**
$$ \text{sim}_J(P_A, P_B) = \frac{\vert P_A \cap P_B \vert}{\vert P_A \cup P_B \vert} $$

**Computation:**
$$ P_A \cap P_B = \{3, 5\} \Rightarrow \vert P_A \cap P_B \vert = 2 $$
$$ P_A \cup P_B = \{1, 3, 5, 7, 8\} \Rightarrow \vert P_A \cup P_B \vert = 5 $$

$$ \text{sim}_J(P_A, P_B) = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4 $$

**Limitation of Plain Jaccard in Rating Systems:**
Plain Jaccard is a *set-based* measure that completely ignores the **rating magnitude**. For example, if A rated item 3 as ★1 and B rated item 3 as ★5, plain Jaccard still considers it a "match" — which is misleading because the users *disagreed strongly*.

**Weighted Jaccard variant:**
$$ \text{sim}_J^{\text{rating}}(A,B) = \frac{\sum_{i \in I_{AB}} \min(r_{A,i}, r_{B,i})}{\sum_{i \in I_{AB}} \max(r_{A,i}, r_{B,i})} $$
This penalizes disagreement by replacing the cardinalities with *ratings*: small $\min / \max$ ratio indicates weak agreement, ratio $\to 1$ indicates strong agreement. It preserves the bounded $[0,1]$ property while incorporating preference intensity.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on "Cases and Similarities":**
> 1. **Forgetting to handle Pearson's 0/0 case** — board examiners *love* to give a constant-rater example to trap students. Always mention the edge case.
> 2. **Confusing Jaccard and Cosine** — Jaccard operates on *sets/binary* presence; Cosine operates on *vectors with magnitude*. Mixing them up loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the case representation in CBR questions** — a 14-mark CBR question *always* expects the 4-tuple $(P, S, O, K)$ and the cyclic nature of the 4 R's.
> 4. **Using Adjusted Cosine for user-user filtering** — Adjusted Cosine is **item-item only** (per-user mean subtraction requires item-pair co-raters). Using it for user-user is a conceptual error.
> 5. **Not normalizing similarity output** — for top-k retrieval, raw similarity must be in $[0,1]$ or $[-1,1]$. If you mix formulas, the threshold breaks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Case** = structured past experience, typically tuple $(P, S, O, K)$.
- **CBR cycle** = Retrieve → Reuse → Revise → Retain (Aamodt & Plaza, 1994). It is **cyclic** and enables lifelong learning.
- **Similarity** quantifies likeness in a bounded scalar; it is the **dual of distance**.
- **Cosine** = angle-based, works on sparse, treats blanks as 0.
- **Pearson** = trend-based, undefined on constant-rater, ideal for dense user-user.
- **Adjusted Cosine** = item-item, removes per-user mean across all items, solves cross-rater bias.
- **Jaccard** = set-based, for binary interactions; **weighted Jaccard** = for ratings.
- **Spearman** = Pearson on ranks — use for ordinal data.
- **Log-Likelihood** = for implicit feedback (clicks, views).
- **Tanimoto** = generalized Jaccard for binary fingerprints / molecular similarity.
- **Always** handle the 0/0 edge case — this is a recurring KTU exam trap.
- **Choose similarity based on data type** (sparse vs dense, binary vs numeric, explicit vs implicit) — refer to the decision matrix in Section 3.2.
- **Similarity ≠ Recommendation** — similarity *retrieves*; reuse/revise/retain *recommends*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interacting with Constraint-based Recommenders - Defaults
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT741 - RECOMMENDATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Interacting with Constraint-based Recommenders - Defaults

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Constraint-based Recommender System (CBR)** is a type of knowledge-based recommender that supports users in finding suitable items (products or services) by exploiting explicit knowledge about how a set of user requirements (constraints) maps onto a set of item properties. Formally, the recommendation problem is modeled as a **Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP)** defined by a triple:

$$ \mathcal{C} = \langle V, D, \text{Constraints} \rangle $$

where $V$ is the set of variables (item properties), $D$ is the set of corresponding domains (possible values), and Constraints is a set of logical or numerical restrictions over $V$.

A **Default** in a constraint-based recommender is a **pre-assigned value** for a requirement (criterion) that the system automatically populates in the user query form when the user has not provided an explicit value, or when the system has no information to determine the user's preference. Defaults are a fundamental **mixed-initiative interaction technique** used to reduce cognitive load and guide the dialogue between the user and the recommender.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Ordering Coffee at a Self-Service Kiosk**
> Imagine walking up to a self-service coffee machine. The screen displays: "Size: ___", "Sugar: ___", "Milk: ___", "Temperature: ___". If you do not touch the "Milk" option, the machine automatically fills in "Whole Milk" (a default). You can change it, but the system gave you a head-start. **That pre-filled value is a default.** A smart system may even change the default to "Oat Milk" if it knows you chose it last time — this is a **personalized default**.

Defaults act as a **safety net** for incomplete user input. Without them, a user who only specifies "Size: Large" would be rejected by the recommender if all four fields were required. With defaults, the recommender can still return a valid result.

### 1.3 Why Defaults Matter in Recommendation Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** In real-world deployment, users of constraint-based recommenders rarely fill in *every* available field. Studies (e.g., on the **AdapTV** system by Felfernig et al.) show that interaction failures are dominated by **over-constrained user queries**. Defaults are the primary mechanism to prevent such failures and to maintain engagement.

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Default Value** | Pre-filled system-assigned value for a missing user requirement. |
| **Static Default** | Same for all users, all sessions, all contexts. |
| **Dynamic Default** | Varies based on user profile, session history, or context. |
| **Personalized Default** | Tailored to an individual user's past interactions. |
| **Median-based Default** | Set to the central value of the underlying population distribution. |
| **Mode-based Default** | Set to the most frequently chosen value. |
| **Repair / Trade-off** | A suggestion offered when the user's query is unsolvable, often relaxing a default. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Role of Defaults in the Interaction Loop

The interaction with a constraint-based recommender is typically a **stepwise refinement dialogue** rather than a one-shot query. The high-level interaction loop is:

1. **System** presents a query form (criteria with defaults).
2. **User** inspects the form, modifies a subset of fields, and submits.
3. **System** converts the form into a CSP and runs a solver.
4. **If solvable:** system returns a list of matching items.
5. **If unsolvable:** system applies **diagnosis** and **repair** (which often involves proposing relaxed defaults or trade-offs).

Defaults are injected at **Step 1** to maximize the chance of reaching **Step 4** (a non-empty result set).

### 2.2 Formal Treatment of Defaults

A default is a function that maps a variable to a domain value. Let $V = \{v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n\}$ be the set of criteria variables. Let $U$ be the set of users and $C$ the current context (time, location, device). A default selection function $\delta$ is:

$$ \delta : V \times U \times C \rightarrow D $$

#### 2.2.1 Types of Default Strategies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strategy Categories (from Felfernig et al., Knowledge-based Recommender Systems):**

- **Random Defaults:** $\delta(v) = \text{RandomElement}(D_v)$. Useful as a baseline; rarely used in production.
- **Static Median / Mode Defaults:** $\delta(v) = \text{median}(D_v)$ or $\text{mode}(D_v)$ over historical query data.
- **Most-Popular Defaults:** $\delta(v) = \arg\max_{d \in D_v} \; \text{count}(\text{users who chose } d \text{ for } v)$.
- **Personalized Defaults:** $\delta(v, u) = \text{most frequent value of } v \text{ in } u\text{'s history}$.
- **Context-Aware Defaults:** $\delta(v, c) = f(v, c)$ where $f$ depends on context $c$ (e.g., season, time of day).
- **Profit-Optimized Defaults:** $\delta(v) = \arg\max_{d \in D_v} \; \text{Margin}(d)$ — used in commercial settings, controversial.
- **Diversity-Optimized Defaults:** $\delta(v) = \arg\max_{d \in D_v} \; \text{DiversityGain}(d, S)$ where $S$ is the current candidate set.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **CSP** | $\mathcal{C} = \langle V, D, \text{Constraints} \rangle$ | Formal problem model |
| 2 | **Default Function** | $\delta : V \times U \times C \rightarrow D$ | Mapping criteria to default value |
| 3 | **Mode-based default** | $\delta(v) = \arg\max_{d \in D_v} \; \text{count}(d)$ | Most common historical choice |
| 4 | **Median-based default (numeric)** | $\delta(v) = \text{median}\{x \in D_v\}$ | Numeric criteria (e.g., price, weight) |
| 5 | **Personalized default** | $\delta(v, u) = \arg\max_{d} \; P(d \mid u)$ | User has prior history |
| 6 | **Constraint relaxation** | $\text{Relax}(c) = c' \mid c' \text{ is strictly weaker than } c$ | Repair step on unsolvable CSP |
| 7 | **Solvability test** | $\text{Solvable}(\mathcal{C}) = \text{True} \iff \exists\, \text{solution} \in D$ | After each user input |
| 8 | **Critique / Repair move** | $\text{Repair}(u) = \arg\min_{c \in \text{violated}} \; \text{UserEffort}(c)$ | Mixed-initiative dialogue |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

Defaults are the **unsung hero of recommender UX**. They directly influence:
- **Query completion rate** (how often users submit a query).
- **Item coverage** (how many catalog items become candidates).
- **Conversion rate** (commercial success metric).
- **Anchoring bias** (the psychological effect where a pre-filled value shifts user choices toward that value).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world deployment example:** In the **VitaMine** nutritional supplement recommender, defaults are computed from the user's age bracket, sex, and lifestyle. A 60-year-old male non-smoker gets a default Vitamin D dosage of 1000 IU, while a 25-year-old female athlete gets 600 IU. This is a **personalized + context-aware** default strategy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Digital Camera Recommender

Consider a simplified constraint-based camera recommender with the following criteria:

- **Price** (numeric, in USD): domain $[100, 1500]$.
- **Megapixels** (numeric): domain $[8, 50]$.
- **Brand** (categorical): domain $\{$Canon, Nikon, Sony, Fujifilm, Panasonic$\}$.

Historical log of past users shows the following distribution of choices for **Brand**:

| Brand | Canon | Nikon | Sony | Fujifilm | Panasonic |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency | 350 | 280 | 410 | 120 | 90 |

Total historical selections $N = 1250$.

#### Step 1 — Choose a Default Strategy for "Brand"

We use the **mode-based default** strategy.

$$ \delta(\text{Brand}) = \arg\max_{b \in D_{\text{Brand}}} \; \text{count}(b) $$

Evaluating:

$$ \text{count}(\text{Canon}) = 350,\quad \text{count}(\text{Nikon}) = 280,\quad \text{count}(\text{Sony}) = 410 $$
$$ \text{count}(\text{Fujifilm}) = 120,\quad \text{count}(\text{Panasonic}) = 90 $$

The maximum is **Sony = 410**.

$$ \therefore \delta(\text{Brand}) = \text{Sony} $$

#### Step 2 — Choose a Default Strategy for "Price" (numeric)

For numeric criteria, the **median-based default** is preferred.

$$ \delta(\text{Price}) = \text{median}(D_{\text{Price}}) = \text{median}([100, 1500]) $$

Computing the median of a uniform range:

$$ \text{median}([a, b]) = \frac{a + b}{2} = \frac{100 + 1500}{2} = 800 $$

$$ \therefore \delta(\text{Price}) = 800 \text{ USD} $$

#### Step 3 — Choose a Default Strategy for "Megapixels"

Median of $[8, 50]$:

$$ \delta(\text{Megapixels}) = \frac{8 + 50}{2} = 29 $$

$$ \therefore \delta(\text{Megapixels}) = 29 \text{ MP} $$

#### Step 4 — Resulting Initial Query Form

The user is shown the form pre-filled as:

| Criterion | Default Value | User's Actual Input |
|---|---|---|
| Brand | Sony | (left unchanged by user) |
| Price | 800 USD | (user changes to 600 USD) |
| Megapixels | 29 MP | (user changes to 24 MP) |

#### Step 5 — CSP Formulation & Solving

The user's effective query is:

$$ \mathcal{C}_{\text{user}} = \langle V, D, \text{Constraints} \rangle $$

where the active constraints are:

- $C_1: \text{Brand} = \text{Sony}$
- $C_2: \text{Price} \le 600$
- $C_3: \text{Megapixels} \ge 24$

The default did not get overridden for **Brand** — so $C_1$ still stands. If the catalog contains a Sony camera at 550 USD with 24 MP, the system returns it; otherwise, it triggers a **repair** dialogue.

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The default for Brand (Sony) was *accepted* by the user. This is the **anchoring effect** — users tend to accept the default unless they have a strong preference. KTU examiners may ask you to discuss this ethical/business implication.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Default Strategy Comparison

| Strategy | Data Required | Pros | Cons | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Random** | None | No bias, simple | Poor UX, unpredictable | Baseline / research only |
| **Mode** | Past query log | Reflects popular choice | Reinforces popularity bias | Consumer goods |
| **Median** | Past query log | Balanced numeric default | May be unrealistic (e.g., 29 MP) | Numeric specs |
| **Personalized** | Per-user history | Highly relevant | Cold-start problem | Returning users |
| **Context-aware** | Context signals | Adapts to situation | Context may be wrong | Mobile / IoT |
| **Profit-optimized** | Margin data | Commercial benefit | Unethical, biased | (Avoid — controversial) |
| **Diversity-optimized** | Catalog graph | Increases catalog coverage | Slower computation | Long-tail catalogs |

### 3.3 Trade-off Between User Effort and Constraint Violation

Let $E$ be the expected user effort (number of fields the user must edit) and $F$ be the failure probability (no solution returned). Defaults reduce $E$ but may **increase** $F$ if they inject values that conflict with the user's unstated preferences.

The optimal default strategy minimizes the combined cost:

$$ \text{Cost} = \alpha \cdot E + \beta \cdot F $$

where $\alpha, \beta \in \mathbb{R}^+$ are weights determined by business priorities (e.g., high $\beta$ when user retention is critical).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** This cost model is rarely given in textbooks but is a frequent exam-style question — "Discuss the trade-off between user effort and system accuracy in choosing defaults."

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Interaction Flow with Defaults

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User opens recommender] --> B[System loads query form]
    B --> C[Inject default values from delta V x U x C]
    C --> D[Display pre-filled form to user]
    D --> E{User edits fields?}
    E -- No --> F[Submit with all defaults]
    E -- Yes --> G[User overrides specific criteria]
    G --> F
    F --> H[Convert form to CSP]
    H --> I{CSP solvable?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Return top-k matching items]
    I -- No --> K[Run diagnosis and repair]
    K --> L[Propose relaxed defaults or trade-offs]
    L --> D
    J --> M[User inspects recommendation]
    M --> N{User satisfied?}
    N -- Yes --> O[End session log interaction]
    N -- No --> D
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of Default Selection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        V[Variable Set V]
        H[Historical Query Log]
        UP[User Profile U]
        CTX[Context Signals C]
    end
    subgraph Default Engine
        S1[Strategy Selector]
        S2[Mode Calculator]
        S3[Median Calculator]
        S4[Personalization Module]
        S5[Context Fusion Module]
    end
    subgraph Output
        DF[Default Vector delta]
    end
    V --> S1
    H --> S2
    H --> S3
    UP --> S4
    CTX --> S5
    S1 --> DF
    S2 --> DF
    S3 --> DF
    S4 --> DF
    S5 --> DF
```

### 4.3 Default Strategy Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Choose default strategy] --> Q1{Cold-start user?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Numeric variable?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Median[Use median of domain]
    Q2 -- No --> Mode[Use mode of historical data]
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{User has rich history?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Personal[Use personalized default]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Context available?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> Ctx[Use context-aware default]
    Q4 -- No --> Popular[Use mode or popularity default]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*What is a default value in the context of constraint-based recommenders? Mention any two strategies used to compute defaults.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **default value** is a pre-filled, system-assigned value for a requirement criterion in a constraint-based recommender. It is used when the user has not explicitly provided a value for that criterion, thereby avoiding query incompleteness and reducing user effort. Two strategies:

1. **Mode-based default:** Set the variable to the most frequently chosen value in historical user queries.
2. **Personalized default:** Set the variable to the value most commonly selected by the current user in past sessions.

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark strategy 1 + 1 mark strategy 2)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Why are static median defaults preferred for numeric criteria such as price, while mode defaults are preferred for categorical criteria such as brand?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
For **numeric** criteria (e.g., price, weight), the underlying domain is continuous or ordered; computing a **median** yields a representative central value that minimizes the expected distance from the user's true preference. For example, the median of $[100, 1500]$ is 800, which is the most balanced anchor.

For **categorical** criteria (e.g., brand), median is meaningless (categories are not ordered). The **mode** — the most frequently chosen category — is the most informative default because it represents the most popular choice in the user population. *(1 mark numeric reasoning + 1 mark categorical reasoning + 1 mark example)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]*

**(a)** Explain in detail the different strategies used to compute default values in a constraint-based recommender. Discuss the **trade-off** between user effort and recommendation accuracy when choosing defaults. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a constraint-based recommender for laptops with the following criteria:
- **Price** (numeric): domain $[300, 2500]$ USD.
- **RAM** (numeric): domain $[4, 64]$ GB.
- **Brand** (categorical): $\{$Dell, HP, Lenovo, Apple, Asus$\}$.
- Historical brand frequencies: Dell = 400, HP = 320, Lenovo = 380, Apple = 250, Asus = 150.

Compute the **default values** using mode-based (Brand) and median-based (Price, RAM) strategies. Formulate the resulting CSP and explain what happens if a user only edits "Price" to 400 USD. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Default Strategies (7 marks):**

The major default strategies are:

1. **Random defaults:** Pick any value uniformly from the domain. No data needed; provides an unbiased baseline but offers no UX benefit. *(1 mark)*

2. **Mode-based defaults:** For categorical variables, choose the most frequently observed value in historical data.
$$ \delta(v) = \arg\max_{d \in D_v} \; \text{count}(d) $$
*(1 mark)*

3. **Median-based defaults:** For numeric variables, choose the central value.
$$ \delta(v) = \text{median}(D_v) $$
*(1 mark)*

4. **Personalized defaults:** Adapt to the current user's past behavior. Requires per-user history. Solves cold-start poorly but maximizes relevance for known users. *(1 mark)*

5. **Context-aware defaults:** Adjust to time, location, or device context (e.g., cheaper products for budget sessions, premium products on weekends). *(1 mark)*

6. **Diversity-optimized defaults:** Choose defaults that maximize catalog coverage, preventing the recommender from always suggesting the same popular item. *(1 mark)*

7. **Trade-off discussion:** A system that fills in many defaults minimizes **user effort** (low $E$) but risks injecting values that conflict with the user's true preferences, leading to **constraint violations** and empty result sets (high $F$). A system that asks for explicit input on every field maximizes accuracy but causes user fatigue and abandonment. The optimal point is determined by the cost function
$$ \text{Cost} = \alpha \cdot E + \beta \cdot F $$
where $\alpha, \beta$ are business-defined weights. *(1 mark)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Brand default (mode-based):**

$$ \delta(\text{Brand}) = \arg\max\{400, 320, 380, 250, 150\} = 400 = \textbf{Dell} $$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Price default (median-based):**

$$ \delta(\text{Price}) = \frac{300 + 2500}{2} = \frac{2800}{2} = \textbf{1400 USD} $$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — RAM default (median-based):**

$$ \delta(\text{RAM}) = \frac{4 + 64}{2} = \frac{68}{2} = \textbf{34 GB} $$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 4 — Initial CSP:**

$$ \mathcal{C} = \langle V = \{\text{Brand}, \text{Price}, \text{RAM}\}, D, C \rangle $$

with active constraints:
- $C_1: \text{Brand} = \text{Dell}$ *(accepted default)*
- $C_2: \text{Price} \le 400$ *(user-edited)*
- $C_3: \text{RAM} \ge 4$ *(from default of 34, but actually becomes RAM = 34)*

**Effective constraints after user edits:**

- $C_1: \text{Brand} = \text{Dell}$
- $C_2: \text{Price} \le 400$
- $C_3: \text{RAM} = 34$ *(since user did not override the default of 34 GB)*

*(1 mark)*

**Step 5 — Solvability check:**

A Dell laptop at $\le 400$ USD with exactly 34 GB RAM is **highly unlikely** in real catalogs. Most budget Dell laptops come with 4–8 GB RAM. Therefore:

$$ \text{Solvable}(\mathcal{C}) = \text{False} $$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 6 — Repair:**

The system enters a **repair dialogue**. It identifies the most "expensive" constraint to relax — likely $C_3$ (RAM = 34) — and proposes:
> "No Dell laptops match all your requirements. Would you like to relax the RAM requirement to 8 GB?"

*(1 mark)*

**Step 7 — Conclusion:**

This example illustrates the **anchoring bias** of defaults: the user did not actually want 34 GB of RAM, but the system assumed they did. A better default strategy here would be to use a **cheaper median** (e.g., the 25th percentile of past queries) for the price-sensitive segment. *(1 mark)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** With a suitable example, explain the **constraint satisfaction problem (CSP)** formulation used in constraint-based recommenders. How are defaults incorporated into the CSP? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **ethical implications** of using **profit-optimized defaults** in commercial constraint-based recommenders. Provide two real-world scenarios where such defaults could harm the user. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution Outline to Question B:**

**(a)** The CSP is the triple $\langle V, D, C \rangle$. Defaults populate variables in $V$ with values from $D$ **before** the CSP is solved. If the user specifies $k$ criteria and the system has $n$ total criteria, defaults fill the remaining $n-k$. Worked example: digital camera scenario from Section 3.1. *(7 marks distributed across definition, example, default injection, solver invocation, and result delivery.)*

**(b)** Profit-optimized defaults use $\delta(v) = \arg\max_{d} \text{Margin}(d)$ rather than user utility. Ethical concerns:
- **Conflict of interest:** The system is no longer a neutral advisor.
- **Anchoring manipulation:** Users accept the default and unknowingly choose higher-margin products.
- **Reduced trust:** Once discovered, users lose confidence in the recommender.
- **Two scenarios:** (i) Health supplements — system pushes a high-margin brand as default, ignoring a clinically superior but cheaper alternative; (ii) Insurance products — system defaults to a high-premium plan that maximizes commission but does not match the user's risk profile. *(7 marks distributed across definition, two scenarios, and ethical analysis.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Many students write only the **definition** of defaults and forget to discuss **strategies** — this typically costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. When solving numerical CSP questions, students often forget to **explicitly list the active constraints** after user edits. The examiner wants to see $C_1, C_2, \dots$ written clearly.
> 3. Avoid saying "median is the average." It is **not** — the median is the middle value. Examiners deduct marks for this confusion.
> 4. In ethics questions, do not write generic "AI is bad." Give **specific, justified scenarios** with named stakeholders and consequences.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A default is a pre-assigned value for a criterion in a constraint-based recommender, used when the user does not specify a value.
- **Core framework:** CBR is formulated as a **CSP** $\langle V, D, C \rangle$; defaults fill the unspecified variables in $V$.
- **Strategies:** Random, Mode, Median, Personalized, Context-aware, Diversity-optimized, Profit-optimized.
- **Numeric vs Categorical:** Numeric variables → **median** default. Categorical variables → **mode** default.
- **Anchoring bias:** Users tend to accept defaults — defaults therefore shape the outcome.
- **Repair loop:** When the CSP becomes unsolvable (often because of conflicting defaults), the system must run **diagnosis** and propose **relaxed defaults / trade-offs**.
- **Ethical red flag:** Profit-optimized defaults = conflict of interest, possible manipulation.
- **Formula to memorize:** $\delta : V \times U \times C \rightarrow D$ and $\text{Cost} = \alpha E + \beta F$.
- **UX principle:** A good default minimizes user effort *and* maximizes solvability, never just one.
- **Cold-start handling:** New users get static (mode/median) defaults; returning users get personalized defaults.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dealing with Unsatisfiable Requirements and Empty Result Set
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT741 - RECOMMENDER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dealing with Unsatisfiable Requirements and Empty Result Set

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Unsatisfiable Requirements** in a Recommender System (RS) refer to a state in which a user's specified set of preferences, constraints, or filter conditions (e.g., price range, genre, brand, technical specifications) cannot be jointly satisfied by any single item (or sufficiently many items) within the underlying item catalog. When the system retrieves zero items (the **Empty Result Set (ERS)** condition), the recommendation process has technically failed, even though the algorithm itself has executed correctly.

Mathematically, given a user query $Q$ composed of $n$ hard constraints $C = \{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_n\}$ and a catalog $\mathcal{I} = \{i_1, i_2, \ldots, i_m\}$, the result set is empty when:

$$
\text{Res}(Q) = \{ i \in \mathcal{I} \mid \forall c_j \in C, \; i \models c_j \} = \emptyset
$$

Here, $i \models c_j$ denotes that item $i$ satisfies constraint $c_j$. The **Empty Result Set Problem (ERSP)** is the subsequent failure of the system to provide a useful, actionable response when $\text{Res}(Q) = \emptyset$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The ERSP is classified as a *usability failure* in conversational, constraint-based, and critiquing-based recommender systems. It is NOT an algorithmic error — it is a **dialogue management** and **knowledge representation** problem.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine walking into a restaurant and telling the waiter: *"I want a vegetarian, gluten-free, peanut-free, low-sodium, spicy, under ₹200 main course that also contains mushrooms and olives."* The waiter checks the menu, finds **no dish matches all six conditions**, and returns with: *"Sorry, nothing fits."* You are now staring at an **Empty Result Set**.

A smart waiter would not just say "no." Instead, he might:
- **Relax one constraint** ("We have a vegetarian, gluten-free, low-sodium dish for ₹220 — would that work?").
- **Show the closest match** ("The closest is this dish, which only differs in one of your requirements.").
- **Offer a compromise** ("If you allow peanuts, here are 5 great options.").
- **Explain why** it failed ("We don't have any dish that is both gluten-free AND contains mushrooms.").

A recommender system must do the *exact same thing* — this is the field of **dealing with unsatisfiable requirements**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world industrial impact:** Studies show that **35–60% of e-commerce search/refinement sessions** end with zero results. Recovering even 10% of these sessions via smart repair can significantly boost conversion rates and customer satisfaction.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

This topic tests a student's ability to:
1. Recognize the *failure mode* of constraint-based filtering.
2. Design **recovery strategies** (relaxation, repair, explanation).
3. Apply utility theory and similarity metrics to rank alternative items.
4. Integrate critiquing-based feedback loops for adaptive re-querying.

These are **Module 2 learning outcomes** tied to the design of interactive and conversational recommender systems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Failure Modes

When a result set is empty, the failure can arise from one of three causes:

1. **Over-constrained query:** Too many hard constraints combined, e.g., price < ₹500 AND brand = Apple AND rating ≥ 4.8.
2. **Impossible individual constraint:** A value the catalog simply does not offer (e.g., a 10,000 mAh battery in a sub-₹5,000 phone).
3. **Catalog sparsity / cold-start:** The item database is incomplete or has insufficient items to satisfy broad queries.

## 2.2 Categories of Recovery Strategies

Recovery strategies are broadly classified into **four families**:

### (A) Constraint Relaxation
- **Concept:** Drop or relax one or more of the user's hard constraints, transforming them into soft (preference-based) constraints.
- **Logic Steps:**
  1. Identify the set of failed constraints $C_{\text{fail}} \subseteq C$.
  2. For each candidate relaxation $\Delta$, compute the new result set size.
  3. Rank relaxations by **minimum information loss** and **maximum result-set yield**.

### (B) Item Repair / Compromise Generation
- **Concept:** Synthetically modify a near-match item to fully satisfy the query (e.g., offer a "you-pay-the-difference" upgrade or a bundle).
- **Logic Steps:**
  1. Find the nearest neighbor items using a distance function $d(i, Q)$.
  2. For each neighbor, enumerate repair operations (substitute component, increase budget, add accessory).
  3. Rank by cost of repair vs. utility gain.

### (C) Explanation-Based Approaches
- **Concept:** Show the user **why** no item matched and offer a structured explanation. Helps users self-relax intelligently.
- **Logic Steps:**
  1. Compute the **Minimal Unsatisfiable Subset (MUS)** of constraints.
  2. Present the MUS to the user with a visual "conflict explanation."
  3. Suggest targeted relaxations on the MUS.

### (D) Diversity & Fallback Strategies
- **Concept:** When no item matches, return a diverse set of *near-misses* or a small curated fallback list (e.g., "Best Sellers", "Editor's Picks").
- **Logic Steps:**
  1. Compute a relevance score $r(i, Q)$ for every item.
  2. Apply a max-sum diversification algorithm (e.g., Maximal Marginal Relevance).
  3. Present the top-$k$ items that are **diverse** yet **relevant**.

## 2.3 The High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — memorize these for the 14-mark derivation question.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Result Set: } & \text{Res}(Q) = \{ i \in \mathcal{I} \mid \forall c_j \in C, \; i \models c_j \} \\
\text{Empty Set Condition: } & \vert \text{Res}(Q) \vert = 0 \\
\text{Hamming Distance to Query: } & d_H(i, Q) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}[i \not\models c_j] \\
\text{Relaxed Utility Score: } & U_{\text{relax}}(i, Q) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot \text{sat}(i, c_j) - \lambda \cdot P_{\text{viol}} \\
\text{Penalty Term: } & P_{\text{viol}} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}[i \not\models c_j] \\
\text{Cosine Similarity: } & \text{sim}(i, Q) = \frac{\vec{i} \cdot \vec{Q}}{\| \vec{i} \| \, \| \vec{Q} \|} \\
\text{MMR Diversification: } & \text{MMR}(i) = \arg\max_{i \in R \setminus S} \left[ \lambda \cdot \text{sim}(i, Q) - (1-\lambda) \cdot \max_{j \in S} \text{sim}(i, j) \right] \\
\text{MUS Cardinality: } & \vert \text{MUS} \vert \leq n, \quad \text{MUS} = \arg\min_{S \subseteq C} \vert S \vert \text{ s.t. } \text{Res}(Q_S) = \emptyset
\end{aligned}
$$

### Parameter Legend (for the KTU Formula Sheet)

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $C$ | Set of user constraints | Integers, $1 \leq \vert C \vert \leq n$ |
| $w_j$ | Importance weight of constraint $c_j$ | $0 \leq w_j \leq 1$, dimensionless |
| $\text{sat}(i, c_j)$ | Satisfaction degree of item $i$ on $c_j$ | $0 \leq \text{sat} \leq 1$ |
| $\lambda$ | Trade-off parameter (relevance vs. diversity) | $0 \leq \lambda \leq 1$ |
| $\mathbb{1}[\cdot]$ | Indicator function | $\{0, 1\}$ |
| $P_{\text{viol}}$ | Total constraint violations by item $i$ | Non-negative integer |
| $R, S$ | Result candidate pool, already-selected set | Subsets of $\mathcal{I}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** In real-time systems, finding the *optimal* relaxation is NP-hard (a subset-selection problem). Heuristic methods such as greedy constraint-dropping or weighted-MUS are used in production.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **E-commerce (Amazon, Flipkart):** "No results found" → triggers "Did you mean to widen your budget?" or "Customers also bought…".
- **Travel platforms (MakeMyTrip, Booking.com):** Empty hotel results due to date+price+location combination → triggers a calendar-based compromise.
- **Streaming (Netflix, Spotify):** Over-narrowed mood filters → suggest "Popular in your region".
- **B2B Configurators (cars, PCs):** Component incompatibility → "Remove SSD → 12 new builds available".
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Strategy

| User Intent | Best Strategy | Reasoning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Casual browser, mild constraints | **Diversity / Fallback** | User is not committed; safe to suggest bestsellers. |
| Focused buyer, multiple hard constraints | **Constraint Relaxation** | User has clear priorities; relax one and re-rank. |
| Complex B2B / expert user | **Item Repair** | User wants a solution; offer tweaks to a near-match. |
| First-time / novice user | **Explanation-Based** | User may not know why no result; educate them. |
| Conversational / critiquing system | **Multi-turn repair loop** | System iteratively refines the query with the user. |

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: From Empty Set to Optimal Relaxation

**Problem Setup:** A user issues a query $Q$ with constraints $C = \{c_1, c_2, c_3, c_4\}$. The catalog $\mathcal{I}$ has $m = 5$ items with the following satisfaction matrix $S$ (1 = satisfies, 0 = violates):

| Item $\setminus$ Constraint | $c_1$ | $c_2$ | $c_3$ | $c_4$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $i_1$ | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| $i_2$ | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| $i_3$ | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| $i_4$ | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| $i_5$ | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 |

**Step 1 — Verify the Empty Result Set:**

We need an item that satisfies *all four* constraints. Let us check each item:

- $i_1$: $(1,1,0,1)$ → fails $c_3$.
- $i_2$: $(0,1,1,0)$ → fails $c_1$ and $c_4$.
- $i_3$: $(1,0,0,1)$ → fails $c_2$ and $c_3$.
- $i_4$: $(0,0,1,0)$ → fails $c_1$, $c_2$, $c_4$.
- $i_5$: $(1,1,1,0)$ → fails $c_4$.

Therefore:

$$
\text{Res}(Q) = \emptyset
$$

The Empty Result Set is confirmed.

**Step 2 — Compute the Hamming Distance (Number of Violations) for Each Item:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_H(i_1, Q) & = \mathbb{1}[i_1 \not\models c_1] + \mathbb{1}[i_1 \not\models c_2] + \mathbb{1}[i_1 \not\models c_3] + \mathbb{1}[i_1 \not\models c_4] \\
& = 0 + 0 + 1 + 0 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Similarly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_H(i_2, Q) & = 1 + 0 + 0 + 1 = 2 \\
d_H(i_3, Q) & = 0 + 1 + 1 + 0 = 2 \\
d_H(i_4, Q) & = 1 + 1 + 0 + 1 = 3 \\
d_H(i_5, Q) & = 0 + 0 + 0 + 1 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Minimal Unsatisfiable Subset (MUS):**

The pair $\{c_3, c_4\}$ is jointly violated by $i_1$ (fails $c_3$) and $i_4$ (fails both). Checking all pairs:

- Drop $\{c_1\}$: items $i_2, i_4$ still remain, but neither satisfies $c_2, c_3, c_4$ together → still empty.
- Drop $\{c_2\}$: $i_2$ satisfies $c_1, c_3$ but fails $c_4$. $i_4$ satisfies $c_3$ but fails $c_1, c_4$. Still empty.
- Drop $\{c_3\}$: $i_1$ satisfies $c_1, c_2, c_4$? No, $i_1$ satisfies $c_1, c_2, c_4$ ✓. Now $i_1$ qualifies!

Therefore $\{c_3\}$ alone is a **MUS-like core** in the sense that removing it yields a non-empty set. The system should propose: *"If you drop constraint $c_3$, item $i_1$ matches."*

**Step 4 — Compute Relaxed Utility Score for Top Candidates:**

Assume the user's constraint weights are $w = (0.4, 0.2, 0.3, 0.1)$ and the trade-off $\lambda = 0.25$. The satisfaction function is binary here, so $\text{sat}(i, c_j) = S_{ij}$.

For item $i_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{\text{relax}}(i_1, Q) & = \sum_{j=1}^{4} w_j \cdot S_{1j} - \lambda \cdot P_{\text{viol}}(i_1) \\
& = (0.4 \cdot 1) + (0.2 \cdot 1) + (0.3 \cdot 0) + (0.1 \cdot 1) - 0.25 \cdot 1 \\
& = 0.4 + 0.2 + 0 + 0.1 - 0.25 \\
& = 0.45
\end{aligned}
$$

For item $i_5$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{\text{relax}}(i_5, Q) & = (0.4 \cdot 1) + (0.2 \cdot 1) + (0.3 \cdot 1) + (0.1 \cdot 0) - 0.25 \cdot 1 \\
& = 0.4 + 0.2 + 0.3 + 0 - 0.25 \\
& = 0.65
\end{aligned}
$$

For item $i_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{\text{relax}}(i_2, Q) & = (0.4 \cdot 0) + (0.2 \cdot 1) + (0.3 \cdot 1) + (0.1 \cdot 0) - 0.25 \cdot 2 \\
& = 0 + 0.2 + 0.3 + 0 - 0.5 \\
& = 0.0
\end{aligned}
$$

For item $i_3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{\text{relax}}(i_3, Q) & = (0.4 \cdot 1) + (0.2 \cdot 0) + (0.3 \cdot 0) + (0.1 \cdot 1) - 0.25 \cdot 2 \\
& = 0.4 + 0 + 0 + 0.1 - 0.5 \\
& = 0.0
\end{aligned}
$$

For item $i_4$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{\text{relax}}(i_4, Q) & = (0.4 \cdot 0) + (0.2 \cdot 0) + (0.3 \cdot 1) + (0.1 \cdot 0) - 0.25 \cdot 3 \\
& = 0 + 0 + 0.3 + 0 - 0.75 \\
& = -0.45
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Rank the Recommendations:**

$$
\text{Ranking: } i_5 \;(0.65) \;\succ\; i_1 \;(0.45) \;\succ\; i_2 = i_3 \;(0.0) \;\succ\; i_4 \;(-0.45)
$$

**Interpretation:** The system should present $i_5$ first, offering the trade-off explanation: *"Item $i_5$ satisfies your top three constraints ($c_1, c_2, c_3$) but slightly exceeds $c_4$. Would you accept this compromise?"*

**Step 6 — Apply Maximal Marginal Relevance (MMR) for Diversity:**

If we want a diverse top-2 (after picking $i_5$), we pick a second item $i^*$ that maximizes:

$$
\text{MMR}(i^*) = \lambda \cdot \text{sim}(i^*, Q) - (1-\lambda) \cdot \text{sim}(i^*, i_5)
$$

With $\lambda = 0.6$ and assuming inter-item similarities reflect Hamming closeness, $i_1$ (Hamming distance 1 from $i_5$) is preferred over $i_2$ or $i_3$ (distance 2). The final set is $\{i_5, i_1\}$, maximizing both relevance **and** diversity.

## 3.3 Summary Table of Computed Values

| Item | $d_H$ | $P_{\text{viol}}$ | $U_{\text{relax}}$ | Rank |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $i_1$ | 1 | 1 | **0.45** | 2 |
| $i_2$ | 2 | 2 | **0.00** | 3 (tie) |
| $i_3$ | 2 | 2 | **0.00** | 3 (tie) |
| $i_4$ | 3 | 3 | **−0.45** | 5 |
| $i_5$ | 1 | 1 | **0.65** | **1** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Empty Result Set Recovery Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **end-to-end functional architecture** of a recovery pipeline, from query ingestion to a successful re-recommendation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Submits Query Q] --> B[Constraint Filter Engine]
    B --> C{Is Result Set Empty?}
    C -- No --> D[Return Top-k Items to User]
    C -- Yes --> E[Activate Recovery Module]
    E --> F[Strategy Selector]
    F --> G1[Constraint Relaxation Path]
    F --> G2[Item Repair Path]
    F --> G3[Explanation Based Path]
    F --> G4[Diversity Fallback Path]
    G1 --> H1[Identify Minimal Unsatisfiable Subset MUS]
    H1 --> H2[Drop or Soften Low Weight Constraint]
    H2 --> H3[Re-Rank with Relaxed Utility Score]
    G2 --> I1[Find Nearest Neighbor Items]
    I1 --> I2[Enumerate Repair Operations]
    I2 --> I3[Rank by Repair Cost vs Utility Gain]
    G3 --> J1[Extract Conflict Explanation]
    J1 --> J2[Present MUS to User with Visual Conflict Map]
    J2 --> J3[Offer Targeted Relaxation Suggestions]
    G4 --> K1[Compute Relevance Score for All Items]
    K1 --> K2[Apply MMR Diversification]
    K2 --> K3[Return Top-k Diverse Near Misses]
    H3 --> L[Final Recommendation List]
    I3 --> L
    J3 --> L
    K3 --> L
    L --> M[User Feedback Loop]
    M --> A
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Constraint Relaxation State Machine

This diagram captures the **state transitions** of a constraint during the relaxation process.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> HardConstraint
    HardConstraint --> HardConstraint: User Insists
    HardConstraint --> SoftConstraint: Weight Below Threshold wj less than epsilon
    SoftConstraint --> Dropped: Re Ranking Yields Empty Set Still
    SoftConstraint --> Satisfied: Item Found Matching Soft Threshold
    Dropped --> [*]
    Satisfied --> [*]
    HardConstraint --> Conflict: Inconsistent with Other Hard Constraints
    Conflict --> MUSCandidate: Added to Minimal Unsatisfiable Set
    MUSCandidate --> ExplanationMode: User Requests Explanation
    ExplanationMode --> SoftConstraint: User Accepts Relaxation
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — User Interaction Loop in Critiquing-Based Recovery

The following sequence diagram explains how a critiquing-based recommender iterates with the user to overcome an empty result set.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant RS as Recommender System
    participant DB as Item Catalog
    U->>RS: Submit Query with Hard Constraints
    RS->>DB: Filter Items
    DB-->>RS: Return Matched Set
    alt Result Set is Non Empty
        RS-->>U: Display Recommendations
    else Result Set is Empty
        RS->>RS: Detect Empty Set
        RS-->>U: Explain Conflict and Offer Critiques
        U->>RS: Accept Critique or Modify Constraint
        RS->>DB: Re Filter with Updated Constraints
        DB-->>RS: New Matched Set
        RS-->>U: Display Updated Recommendations
    end
    U->>RS: Provide Feedback Likes or Dislikes
    RS->>RS: Update User Preference Model
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip:** In the KTU exam, when asked to *"draw a flow diagram"*, students often draw a simple flowchart. The diagrams above are **functional architecture + state + sequence diagrams**, which earn **higher marks** because they demonstrate deeper system-design understanding.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the Empty Result Set Problem (ERSP) in a recommender system. List two common causes for its occurrence.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Empty Result Set Problem (ERSP)** occurs when a user query with multiple constraints yields no items from the catalog that satisfy all the constraints simultaneously. Formally, $\text{Res}(Q) = \emptyset$.

Two common causes are:
1. **Over-constrained query:** Too many hard constraints are imposed by the user, making joint satisfaction impossible.
2. **Impossible individual constraints:** The user specifies attribute values that no item in the catalog offers (e.g., a feature the product line does not have).

*(Examiner's note: A precise formal definition plus any two valid causes carries full 3 marks. Vague answers like "no items found" get only 1 mark.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is a Minimal Unsatisfiable Subset (MUS) of constraints? How is it useful in explaining an empty result set to the user?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Minimal Unsatisfiable Subset (MUS)** is the smallest subset of constraints from the user's query that, by itself, is responsible for the result set being empty. Formally:

$$
\text{MUS} = \arg\min_{S \subseteq C} \vert S \vert \quad \text{subject to} \quad \text{Res}(Q_S) = \emptyset
$$

**Utility for explanation:** The MUS pinpoints the *exact conflict*. For example, if $\{ \text{price} < 200, \text{brand} = \text{Apple} \}$ is the MUS, the system can show the user: *"No item is both Apple-branded AND under ₹200."* This helps the user make an informed relaxation, rather than randomly dropping a constraint.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not skip the formal problem statement.** Students often dive into the strategy without defining ERSP first. This loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Always justify your strategy choice.** The examiner expects a "why this strategy" sentence, not just a "what to do" list. Lose 1 mark if missing.
> 3. **Numerical examples must be fully worked out.** Skipping intermediate arithmetic costs 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Do not confuse MUS with the entire constraint set.** A common error. Be precise.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the four major strategies for handling an empty result set in a constraint-based recommender system. Compare them on the basis of user expertise, computational cost, and trust. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a user query with constraints $C = \{c_1, c_2, c_3\}$ and weights $w = (0.5, 0.3, 0.2)$. Five items have satisfaction vectors: $i_1 = (1,1,0)$, $i_2 = (1,0,1)$, $i_3 = (0,1,1)$, $i_4 = (1,0,0)$, $i_5 = (0,1,0)$. The system is using a relaxed-utility function with $\lambda = 0.2$. Determine the empty result set, then rank all items using $U_{\text{relax}}$, and recommend the top item with justification. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

##### Part (a) — Strategy Comparison (7 marks)

| Strategy | Best User | Compute Cost | Trust Impact |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Constraint Relaxation** | Focused buyer | Low–Medium (greedy subset drop) | High — user retains control |
| **Item Repair** | B2B / expert | High (search for modifications) | Medium — repair may be costly |
| **Explanation-Based** | Novice user | Medium (MUS extraction is NP-hard in worst case) | Very High — educates the user |
| **Diversity Fallback** | Casual browser | Low (re-rank existing items) | Low–Medium — may feel irrelevant |

**Detailed explanation (write 3–4 lines per strategy in the exam):**

1. **Constraint Relaxation:** Softens hard constraints into weighted preferences. Most useful when the user has a clear hierarchy of importance. Cost is dominated by the search for the *optimal* subset to relax.
2. **Item Repair:** Finds an item that almost matches and offers a "modification" (e.g., different color, added accessory). Computationally expensive because it must enumerate possible repairs.
3. **Explanation-Based:** Identifies the MUS and presents the conflict visually. This is highly valued by novices because it teaches them about the domain. The NP-hardness of MUS extraction is offset by modern SAT-solver techniques.
4. **Diversity Fallback:** Returns a varied set of *near-misses* using MMR. This is the cheapest option but is least personalized.

##### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Step 1: Check for Empty Result Set**

We need an item that satisfies $(c_1, c_2, c_3)$ simultaneously:

- $i_1 = (1,1,0)$ — fails $c_3$.
- $i_2 = (1,0,1)$ — fails $c_2$.
- $i_3 = (0,1,1)$ — fails $c_1$.
- $i_4 = (1,0,0)$ — fails $c_2, c_3$.
- $i_5 = (0,1,0)$ — fails $c_1, c_3$.

No item satisfies all three. Therefore $\text{Res}(Q) = \emptyset$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2: Compute $P_{\text{viol}}$ for each item**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{viol}}(i_1) & = 1, \quad P_{\text{viol}}(i_2) = 1, \quad P_{\text{viol}}(i_3) = 1, \\
P_{\text{viol}}(i_4) & = 2, \quad P_{\text{viol}}(i_5) = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 3: Compute $U_{\text{relax}}(i, Q) = \sum_j w_j S_{ij} - \lambda \cdot P_{\text{viol}}$**

For $i_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{\text{relax}}(i_1) & = (0.5 \cdot 1) + (0.3 \cdot 1) + (0.2 \cdot 0) - 0.2 \cdot 1 \\
& = 0.5 + 0.3 + 0 - 0.2 = 0.6
\end{aligned}
$$

For $i_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{\text{relax}}(i_2) & = (0.5 \cdot 1) + (0.3 \cdot 0) + (0.2 \cdot 1) - 0.2 \cdot 1 \\
& = 0.5 + 0 + 0.2 - 0.2 = 0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

For $i_3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{\text{relax}}(i_3) & = (0.5 \cdot 0) + (0.3 \cdot 1) + (0.2 \cdot 1) - 0.2 \cdot 1 \\
& = 0 + 0.3 + 0.2 - 0.2 = 0.3
\end{aligned}
$$

For $i_4$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{\text{relax}}(i_4) & = (0.5 \cdot 1) + (0.3 \cdot 0) + (0.2 \cdot 0) - 0.2 \cdot 2 \\
& = 0.5 + 0 + 0 - 0.4 = 0.1
\end{aligned}
$$

For $i_5$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{\text{relax}}(i_5) & = (0.5 \cdot 0) + (0.3 \cdot 1) + (0.2 \cdot 0) - 0.2 \cdot 2 \\
& = 0 + 0.3 + 0 - 0.4 = -0.1
\end{aligned}
$$

**(3 marks)**

**Step 4: Rank the items**

$$
i_1 \;(0.6) \;\succ\; i_2 \;(0.5) \;\succ\; i_3 \;(0.3) \;\succ\; i_4 \;(0.1) \;\succ\; i_5 \;(-0.1)
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 5: Justify the top recommendation**

The top item $i_1$ satisfies the user's two highest-weighted constraints ($c_1$ and $c_2$, total weight $0.8$) and only violates the lowest-weighted constraint $c_3$ (weight $0.2$). The penalty of $0.2$ is exactly offset, yielding a positive utility of $0.6$. The system should present: *"Item $i_1$ best matches your top priorities. Would you accept a small trade-off on $c_3$?"* **(1 mark)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Describe the role of **critiquing-based recommenders** in handling unsatisfiable requirements. How does a multi-turn critique loop help the system converge to a non-empty, relevant recommendation? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply the **Maximal Marginal Relevance (MMR)** diversification algorithm to a scenario where, after all relaxations, the top-3 candidate items are $i_5, i_1, i_2$ with relevance scores $0.65, 0.45, 0.0$ and pairwise similarities $\text{sim}(i_5, i_1) = 0.4$, $\text{sim}(i_5, i_2) = 0.2$, $\text{sim}(i_1, i_2) = 0.6$. Use $\lambda = 0.6$ and select the top-2 diverse items. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

##### Part (a) — Critiquing-Based Recovery (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):** A critiquing-based recommender allows the user to provide **unit critiques** (e.g., *"cheaper"*, *"larger"*, *"different brand"*) on a recommended item. Each critique modifies one or more constraints and re-triggers the filtering process.

**Multi-turn loop mechanics (3 marks):**
1. The system first presents a recommendation (or admits an empty set).
2. The user critiques: *"Show me a cheaper option"* or *"Change the brand"*.
3. The system relaxes/updates the relevant constraint.
4. The cycle repeats until a non-empty, relevant set is reached.

**Why it helps (2 marks):** Critiquing turns the empty-set problem from a one-shot failure into a **collaborative repair process**. The user has fine-grained control, the system avoids arbitrary relaxations, and trust is preserved because the user actively shapes the result.

##### Part (b) — MMR Diversification (7 marks)

**MMR formula (1 mark):**

$$
\text{MMR}(i) = \lambda \cdot \text{sim}(i, Q) - (1 - \lambda) \cdot \max_{j \in S} \text{sim}(i, j)
$$

**Given:** $\lambda = 0.6$, $1 - \lambda = 0.4$.

**Step 1 — Select the first item.** Pick the highest relevance score, which is $i_5$ with $0.65$. So $S = \{i_5\}$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Compute MMR for $i_1$ and $i_2$ to add the second item.**

For $i_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MMR}(i_1) & = 0.6 \cdot 0.45 - 0.4 \cdot \max( \text{sim}(i_1, i_5) ) \\
& = 0.6 \cdot 0.45 - 0.4 \cdot 0.4 \\
& = 0.27 - 0.16 = 0.11
\end{aligned}
$$

**(1.5 marks)**

For $i_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MMR}(i_2) & = 0.6 \cdot 0.0 - 0.4 \cdot \max( \text{sim}(i_2, i_5) ) \\
& = 0.0 - 0.4 \cdot 0.2 \\
& = -0.08
\end{aligned}
$$

**(1.5 marks)**

**Step 3 — Choose the higher MMR.**

$i_1$ wins with $\text{MMR} = 0.11 > -0.08$. So the final diverse set is $S = \{i_5, i_1\}$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Justify (1 mark):** Even though $i_1$ is less relevant than $i_5$, it brings **diversity** (lower similarity to $i_5$ compared to $i_2$, despite $i_2$ being less similar to $i_5$, the *relevance* of $i_2$ is too low to compensate). The MMR criterion correctly balances both criteria.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — print this out before the exam!**

- **Empty Result Set (ERS):** $\text{Res}(Q) = \emptyset$ — a *usability* failure, not an algorithmic one.
- **Three causes of ERS:** (1) over-constrained queries, (2) impossible individual constraints, (3) catalog sparsity / cold-start.
- **Four recovery strategies:**
  - **Constraint Relaxation** (soften hard → soft).
  - **Item Repair** (modify near-matches).
  - **Explanation-Based** (find the MUS and explain).
  - **Diversity Fallback** (use MMR on near-misses).
- **Minimal Unsatisfiable Subset (MUS):** Smallest subset of $C$ whose relaxation yields a non-empty result.
- **Hamming distance $d_H(i, Q)$:** Counts the number of constraint violations by item $i$.
- **Relaxed utility function:** $U_{\text{relax}}(i, Q) = \sum_j w_j \cdot \text{sat}(i, c_j) - \lambda \cdot P_{\text{viol}}$.
- **Trade-off parameter $\lambda$:** Controls how harshly violations are penalized. Larger $\lambda$ → stricter system.
- **MMR formula:** Combines relevance ($\lambda \cdot \text{sim}$) and novelty ($(1-\lambda) \cdot \text{diversity}$).
- **Critiquing systems:** Turn failure into a *dialogue*; user provides unit critiques that re-shape the query.
- **Examination Tip:** Always state the formal problem ($\text{Res}(Q) = \emptyset$) before applying a strategy.
- **Numerical Tip:** When $w_j$ and $\text{sat}$ values are given, *always show the substitution step* in your derivation to avoid losing method marks.
- **Common Pitfall:** Confusing "no items returned" (algorithmic) with "no items match" (constraint conflict). The latter is the *real* ERSP.
- **Production Insight:** Real systems use a *hybrid* approach: explanation first → relaxation as fallback → diversity as last resort.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Proposing Repairs for Unsatisfiable Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT741 - RECOMMENDATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Proposing Repairs for Unsatisfiable Requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Requirements Engineering (RE)** applied to **Recommendation Systems (RS)**, user preferences, item attributes, business rules, and system constraints are modeled as a **Boolean Constraint Set** $\Phi$. A requirement set is **satisfiable** if there exists at least one assignment of values to decision variables that makes $\Phi$ evaluate to **TRUE**; it is **unsatisfiable** when no such assignment exists.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proposing Repairs for Unsatisfiable Requirements** is the systematic process of computing a minimally invasive modification (an **edit operation** — relax, delete, replace, or extend) to a given unsatisfiable constraint set $\Phi$ such that the resulting set $\Phi'$ becomes satisfiable, while maximizing the preservation of the user's *original intent*, *priority weights*, and *domain semantics*.

Mathematically, given an unsatisfiable formula $\Phi$, the repair problem is expressed as:

$$\Phi' = \text{Repair}(\Phi) \quad \text{where} \quad \Phi' \models \text{SAT} \quad \wedge \quad \min\big[\text{Cost}(\Phi \rightarrow \Phi')\big]$$

The two classical algorithms used to drive this process in KTU-aligned recommendation literature are:
1. **UNSAT Core Extraction** — identifying the *Minimal Unsatisfiable Subset (MUS)*.
2. **MaxSAT / MinCostSAT Solvers** — selecting a minimum-cost repair that maximizes retained constraints.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Wedding Planner" Conflict**
>
> Imagine a couple tells a wedding planner **10 must-have rules**: *outdoor venue, under ₹2 lakh budget, only vegetarian food, on a Sunday, in Kerala, in December, live classical band, no alcohol served, exactly 200 guests, and a beach view*. The constraint solver returns **NO solution** because December in Kerala is monsoon season — beaches are unsuitable, and live classical bands are scarce on Sundays. The planner doesn't reject the entire requirement list. Instead, she **proposes repairs**:
> - **Relax** *"in December"* → *"between November and February"*
> - **Replace** *"beach view"* → *"backwater view"*
> - **Delete** *"live classical band"* (least weighted constraint)
>
> The repaired set is satisfiable, and the **repair cost** is the weighted sum of the edits. This is precisely what RS engines do when user preferences conflict internally.

## 1.3 Domain Mapping to Recommendation Systems

| Recommendation Element | Modeled As | Repair Operation |
|----------------------|------------|------------------|
| User preference (rating > 4) | Hard constraint $c_i$ | Relax to rating > 3.5 |
| Filter (no horror genre) | Negation clause $\neg c_j$ | Relax / Replace |
| Item metadata rule | Horn clause | Delete clause |
| Diversity constraint | Soft clause (weight $w_k$) | Drop if violated |
| Business rule | Mandatory clause | Never drop (locked) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **KTU 2024 PECDT741**, the **Course Outcome (CO2)** of Module 2 emphasizes the student's ability to *"formulate and resolve conflicts in user requirement models for recommendation tasks."* The repair framework is the direct evaluation artifact for this CO.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants (Bolded)

The following **standard repair metrics** must be memorized verbatim for KTU valuations:

- **Repair Cost** ($R_c$): Weighted Hamming distance between original and repaired constraint set.
- **Minimal Unsatisfiable Subset (MUS)**: Smallest set of constraints whose joint evaluation yields **UNSAT**.
- **Maximal Satisfiable Subset (MSS)**: Largest set of constraints that is jointly **SAT**.
- **Group-MUS**: A minimal unsatisfiable subset under equivalence-class grouping.
- **Edit Distance** ($d_E$): Minimum number of relaxed/deleted/replaced clauses.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Unsatisfiability Pipeline — Structured Logic Steps

The repair pipeline for unsatisfiable requirements in RS proceeds through **5 strict stages**:

### Step 1: **Encoding Requirements as a Boolean Formula**
Each requirement $r_i$ is mapped to a Boolean variable $x_i$ (or a clause over a Boolean variable set). The full requirement set becomes:

$$\Phi = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} c_i \quad \text{where} \quad c_i \in \text{CNF}(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_m)$$

> **Why:** Propositional logic provides a **decidable** substrate — SAT solvers can determine satisfiability in worst-case exponential but practically efficient time.

### Step 2: **Invoking the SAT Solver for Initial Check**
A **DPLL / CDCL-based SAT solver** (e.g., MiniSat, Glucose, CaDiCaL) is invoked. The output is either:
- `SAT` → a model $M$ (a valid recommendation set) is returned.
- `UNSAT` → no model exists; proceed to repair.

### Step 3: **Extracting the Unsatisfiable Core**
Using the **assumption-based** or **resolution-based** algorithm, the solver returns an **UNSAT Core** $\mathcal{U} \subseteq \{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_n\}$ such that:

$$\mathcal{U} \models \text{UNSAT} \quad \wedge \quad \forall c_j \notin \mathcal{U}, \quad (\mathcal{U} \setminus \{c_j\}) \models \text{SAT}$$

This is the **Minimal Unsatisfiable Subset (MUS)**.

### Step 4: **Repair Proposal via Optimization**
The repair module enumerates candidate repairs over $\mathcal{U}$ and selects the one minimizing:

$$\Phi^* = \arg\min_{\Phi' : \Phi' \models \text{SAT}} \; \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \mathbb{1}[c_i \neq c_i']$$

where $w_i$ is the **priority weight** of constraint $c_i$ assigned by the user/RS designer.

### Step 5: **Validation Against Domain Semantics**
The repaired formula is checked against **domain ontologies** (e.g., IMDb taxonomy, product category trees) to ensure semantic validity. Pure syntactic repairs that violate real-world meaning are pruned.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

> **Why Step 1 (Encoding):** Real recommendation preferences are not natively Boolean. Encoding converts *"movies like Inception rated above 4.5 released after 2015 in English"* into a finite propositional form that a SAT engine can process.

> **Why Step 3 (Core Extraction):** It is computationally wasteful to consider the *entire* unsatisfiable set. The MUS pinpoints the **epicenter of conflict** — only those constraints need repair, preserving the rest.

> **Why Step 4 (Cost Optimization):** A naive fix (delete everything) trivially satisfies but **destroys user trust**. A cost-minimizing repair maintains the **maximum amount of original preference information**.

> **Why Step 5 (Semantic Validation):** Pure Boolean repairs can produce absurd results — e.g., relaxing *"Italian cuisine"* to *"NOT Italian"* (logical negation) is logically valid but semantically inverted.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **highest-yield** reference for KTU 2024 Module 2 viva and ESE questions on this topic.

| # | Formula / Definition | Symbol | Engineering Use |
|---|----------------------|--------|-----------------|
| 1 | Boolean satisfiability | $\Phi \models \text{SAT}$ | Determines if a recommendation is feasible |
| 2 | Unsatisfiable core | $\mathcal{U} \subseteq \Phi \mid \mathcal{U} \models \text{UNSAT}$ | Isolates conflicting constraints |
| 3 | MUS cardinality | $\vert \mathcal{U}_{\text{MUS}} \vert = \min$ | Smallest conflict set |
| 4 | Repair cost (weighted) | $R_c = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot d_H(c_i, c_i')$ | Quantitative repair selection |
| 5 | Hamming distance | $d_H = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \mathbb{1}[x_j \neq y_j]$ | Measures per-clause change |
| 6 | Edit distance | $d_E = \vert op_{\text{relax}} \vert + \vert op_{\text{del}} \vert + \vert op_{\text{repl}} \vert$ | Counts edit operations |
| 7 | MSS + MUS duality | $\vert \text{MSS} \vert + \vert \text{MUS} \vert = n$ | Fundamental RS constraint duality |
| 8 | MaxSAT objective | $\max \sum_{i \in \text{soft}} w_i \cdot x_i$ | Maximum preserved soft preferences |
| 9 | Group-MUS cost | $C_g = \sum_{g \in G} \alpha_g \cdot \mathbb{1}[g \text{ modified}]$ | Repairs at preference-group level |
| 10 | Domain repair ratio | $\rho = \dfrac{\vert \Phi_{\text{preserved}} \vert}{\vert \Phi \vert}$ | Fraction of original RS constraints retained |

> **Note on notation:** $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes **set cardinality**, not absolute value. In LaTeX we use `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent markdown table breakage.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The repair framework is foundational in:

- **E-commerce RS** (Amazon, Flipkart): Resolving conflicting filters (e.g., "max price ₹500" + "premium brand only").
- **Streaming platforms** (Netflix, Spotify): Reconciling mood + genre + era constraints.
- **Travel planners** (MakeMyTrip, Booking.com): Balancing budget × destination × date feasibility.
- **Self-driving car decision systems**: Resolving safety vs. comfort vs. speed constraints.
- **Smart grid load balancers**: Reconciling user demand preferences with grid capacity hard limits.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From User Preference Set to Repair Cost Function

We will now derive the **weighted repair cost function** $R_c$ used in Step 4 of the pipeline, with full algebraic justification.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A user submits a recommendation request with $n$ constraints $c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_n$ in Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF). The system encodes them as:

$$\Phi = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} c_i = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} \bigvee_{j=1}^{k_i} \ell_{ij}$$

where each $\ell_{ij}$ is a literal (variable or its negation), and $k_i$ is the clause width.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step A: Define the Original and Repaired Formula} \\
&\Phi = \{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_n\} \quad \text{(Original unsatisfiable formula)} \\
&\Phi' = \{c_1', c_2', \ldots, c_n'\} \quad \text{(Repaired candidate formula)} \\
&\text{where } c_i' = \text{ApplyEdit}(c_i, op_i), \text{ and } op_i \in \{\text{relax, delete, replace, extend}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step B: Define the Per-Constraint Cost} \\
&\text{For each constraint } c_i, \text{ the edit cost is the Hamming distance:} \\
&d_H(c_i, c_i') = \sum_{j=1}^{k_i} \mathbb{1}\big[\ell_{ij} \neq \ell_{ij}'\big] \\
&\text{where } \mathbb{1}[\cdot] = 1 \text{ if true, else } 0.
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step C: Introduce Priority Weights} \\
&\text{User assigns weight } w_i \in (0, 1] \text{ to each constraint. The total repair cost is:} \\
&R_c(\Phi \rightarrow \Phi') = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot d_H(c_i, c_i') \\
&\text{Equivalently, since } d_H \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots\}: \\
&R_c = \sum_{i : c_i \neq c_i'} w_i \cdot d_H(c_i, c_i')
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step D: Add the Hard-Constraint Locking Term} \\
&\text{Business-critical constraints are tagged "hard". For these, } w_i = \infty \text{, so:} \\
&R_c^{\text{final}} = R_c + \sum_{i \in \text{Hard}} M \cdot \mathbb{1}[c_i \neq c_i] \\
&\text{where } M \text{ is a large constant (Big-M method) preventing any hard-constraint violation.}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step E: Formulate the Optimization Problem} \\
&\text{The repair problem is now a constrained optimization:} \\
&\min_{\Phi'} \; R_c^{\text{final}}(\Phi, \Phi') \\
&\text{subject to:} \quad \Phi' \models \text{SAT} \\
&\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\; \text{HardConstraints}(\Phi') = \text{HardConstraints}(\Phi) \\
&\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\; c_i' \in \text{Domain}(\text{Ontology})
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step F: Express via MaxSAT Equivalence} \\
&\text{A repair is equivalent to a } \textbf{Partial MaxSAT} \text{ problem:} \\
&\text{Hard clauses: } H = \{c_i \mid i \in \text{Hard}\} \\
&\text{Soft clauses: } S = \{(c_i, w_i) \mid i \in \text{Soft}\} \\
&\text{Goal: maximize } \sum_{(c_i, w_i) \in S, \; c_i \text{ true}} w_i \\
&\text{Equivalent repair cost: } R_c = W_{\text{total}} - \max \sum w_i
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example

> **Scenario:** A user wants to watch a movie. The Boolean requirement set is:
> - $c_1$: Director = Nolan (encoded as $x_1$)
> - $c_2$: Rating > 4.5 (encoded as $x_2$)
> - $c_3$: Released in 2023 (encoded as $x_3$)
> - $c_4$: Genre = Sci-Fi (encoded as $x_4$)
> - $c_5$: NOT Horror (encoded as $\neg x_5$)
>
> **Constraint:** $c_1 \wedge c_2 \wedge c_3 \wedge c_4 \wedge c_5 = \text{UNSAT}$ (Nolan released no 2023 Sci-Fi at rating > 4.5 — a factual UNSAT in current Netflix catalog).

**Repair Process:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 1: Detect UNSAT, Extract Core} \\
&\mathcal{U}_{\text{MUS}} = \{c_1, c_2, c_3, c_4\} \quad (\text{conflict set}) \\
&c_5 \text{ is satisfiable on its own.}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 2: Compute Edit Options} \\
&\text{Option A: Relax } c_2 \text{ to Rating > 4.0 (cost } w_2 = 0.7\text{)} \\
&\text{Option B: Delete } c_3 \text{ (cost } w_3 = 0.4\text{)} \\
&\text{Option C: Replace } c_1 \text{ with "Director = Nolan OR Villeneuve" (cost } w_1 = 0.6\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 3: Select Minimum Cost Repair} \\
&R_c^{\text{A}} = 0.7 \cdot 1 = 0.7 \\
&R_c^{\text{B}} = 0.4 \cdot 1 = 0.4 \quad \text{(Minimum)} \\
&R_c^{\text{C}} = 0.6 \cdot 1 = 0.6 \\
&\text{Chosen repair: Option B → Repaired } \Phi' = \{c_1, c_2, c_4, c_5\}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 4: Verify Satisfaction} \\
&\Phi' = x_1 \wedge x_2 \wedge x_4 \wedge \neg x_5 \models \text{SAT} \\
&\text{Recommended movie: } \textbf{Dunkirk (2017)} \text{ — Nolan, 4.3, Sci-Fi/Drama, not Horror.}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Repair Strategies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is the most heavily weighted comparative table in KTU 2024 RS Module 2. Memorize the trade-offs.

| Strategy | Cost | Preserves User Intent | Semantic Risk | Computational Cost | Best Use Case |
|----------|------|----------------------|---------------|-------------------|---------------|
| **Constraint Deletion** | Low | Low (1 constraint lost) | Low | $O(n)$ | Conflicting low-priority items |
| **Constraint Relaxation** | Medium | High (clause weakened) | Medium | $O(n \cdot k)$ | Threshold-type preferences |
| **Constraint Replacement** | High | Highest (semantic swap) | Low if done carefully | $O(n^2)$ | Ontology-supported alternatives |
| **Domain Expansion** | High | High | Medium-High | $O(2^d)$ | Attribute value broadening |
| **Weight Reassignment** | Zero structural | Preserves all clauses | Low | $O(n \log n)$ | Soft vs hard reclassification |
| **Group-MUS Repair** | Medium | Group-level fidelity | Low | $O(n \cdot g)$ | Multi-criteria RS systems |

---

## 3.3 Derivations: From MaxSAT to Repair Selection

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Theorem (Repair Optimality via MaxSAT)} \\
&\text{For an unsatisfiable CNF } \Phi = H \cup S, \text{ where } H = \text{Hard}, S = \text{Soft with weights } w: \\
&\text{Let } T \subseteq S \text{ be the soft clauses that remain true in the optimal MaxSAT model.}\\
&\text{Then the minimum-cost repair is: } R_c^{*} = \sum_{i \in S \setminus T} w_i \\
&\text{Proof Sketch:} \\
&\text{1. MaxSAT maximizes } \sum_{i \in T} w_i \text{ such that } H \cup T \models \text{SAT}. \\
&\text{2. Total soft weight } W_S = \sum_{i \in S} w_i. \\
&\text{3. Repair cost is the weight of dropped soft clauses: } W_S - \sum_{i \in T} w_i. \\
&\text{4. MaxSAT maximizing } \sum_{i \in T} w_i \iff \text{Minimizing } W_S - \sum_{i \in T} w_i. \\
&\text{5. Hence, MaxSAT is the dual of the minimum-cost repair. } \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$
