# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Repair Pipeline — End-to-End Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Preference Set Phi] --> B[CNF Encoder]
    B --> C[Boolean Formula Encoding]
    C --> D{SAT Solver Check}
    D -- SAT Found --> E[Return Valid Recommendation Model M]
    D -- UNSAT Detected --> F[UNSAT Core Extractor]
    F --> G[Minimal Unsatisfiable Subset MUS]
    G --> H[Repair Strategy Selector]
    H --> I1[Relax Operation]
    H --> I2[Delete Operation]
    H --> I3[Replace Operation]
    H --> I4[Domain Expansion]
    I1 --> J[Cost Evaluator]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    I4 --> J
    J --> K[Minimum Cost Repair Phi Prime]
    K --> L[Domain Ontology Validator]
    L -- Valid --> M[Re-run SAT on Phi Prime]
    L -- Invalid --> H
    M -- SAT --> N[Final Recommendation Output]
    M -- UNSAT --> O[Backtrack to Step H]
    O --> H
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:**
> - The **inner loop** (`L → M → O → H`) is the **semantic re-validation cycle** that prevents logically valid but semantically absurd repairs.
> - The **outer loop** (`D → F → G → H`) is the **MUS-driven repair proposal** that focuses compute only on conflicting constraints.

---

## 4.2 Repair Strategy Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Repair Strategy]
    R --> R1[Structural Repairs]
    R --> R2[Semantic Repairs]
    R1 --> R1a[Clause Deletion]
    R1 --> R1b[Literal Relaxation]
    R1 --> R1c[Variable Substitution]
    R2 --> R2a[Ontology-Based Replace]
    R2 --> R2b[Domain Expansion]
    R2 --> R2c[Weight Reassignment]
    R2a --> R2a1[Synonym Substitution]
    R2a --> R2a2[Hypernym Generalization]
    R2a --> R2a3[Hyponym Specialization]
```

> **Engineering Insight:** **Structural Repairs** are fast ($O(n)$) but risk semantic drift. **Semantic Repairs** are slower but maintain **user trust** — critical for production RS systems.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Repair-Aware RS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Query Interface] --> B[Preference Parser]
    B --> C[Constraint Weighting Module]
    C --> D[CNF Knowledge Base]
    D --> E[Boolean Satisfiability Engine]
    E --> F{Result Type}
    F -- SAT --> G[Recommendation Ranker]
    F -- UNSAT --> H[Repair Orchestrator]
    H --> I[Repair Strategy Bank]
    I --> J[Cost Function Evaluator]
    J --> K[Best Repair Selector]
    K --> L[Repaired Formula Phi Prime]
    L --> E
    G --> M[Top-N Recommendation List]
    L --> G
    M --> N[User Feedback Loop]
    N --> C
```

> **Block Architecture Insight:** The **Repair Orchestrator** is the *heart* of the unsatisfiable-requirement handling system. The feedback loop (`N → C`) allows the RS to **learn user priorities over time**, making future repairs more accurate.

---

## 4.4 MSS–MUS Duality Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Whole[Full Constraint Set Phi with n clauses]
        direction LR
        W1[Clause c1]
        W2[Clause c2]
        W3[Clause c3]
        W4[Clause c4]
        W5[Clause c5]
        W6[Clause c6]
    end

    subgraph MUSBox[Minimal Unsatisfiable Subset MUS]
        direction LR
        M1[c2]
        M2[c3]
        M3[c5]
    end

    subgraph MSSBox[Maximal Satisfiable Subset MSS]
        direction LR
        S1[c1]
        S2[c4]
        S3[c6]
    end

    Whole -->|extraction| MUSBox
    Whole -->|extraction| MSSBox
    MUSBox -.->|union equals| Whole
    MSSBox -.->|union equals| Whole
    MUSBox -.->|disjoint from| MSSBox
```

> **Critical Relationship:** $\text{MSS} \cup \text{MUS} = \Phi$ and $\text{MSS} \cap \text{MUS} = \emptyset$. The repair process always **replaces elements of MUS with semantically equivalent MSS-compatible clauses**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECDT741 Module 2]**
> *Define the concept of an "unsatisfiable requirement set" in the context of recommendation systems. What is a Minimal Unsatisfiable Subset (MUS), and why is it computationally important for the repair process?*

**Model Answer (3 marks — allocation shown):**

A set of requirements is **unsatisfiable (UNSAT)** if no assignment of values to its variables can simultaneously satisfy all constraints (1 mark). In RS, this occurs when user preferences, item features, and business rules mutually conflict — e.g., *"movies by Nolan, released in 2023, with rating above 4.5"* when no such movie exists (0.5 marks).

A **Minimal Unsatisfiable Subset (MUS)** is a smallest subset of the original constraints that is itself unsatisfiable, with the property that removing any single clause from it would make it satisfiable (1 mark).

The MUS is **computationally important** because the repair process only needs to modify constraints in the MUS, leaving the (potentially numerous) satisfied constraints untouched — this preserves user intent and reduces the search space from $O(2^n)$ to $O(2^{|\mathcal{U}|})$ (0.5 marks).

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECDT741 Module 2]**
> *Explain the difference between "constraint relaxation" and "constraint replacement" as repair strategies. Give one example of each from a movie recommendation scenario.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Constraint Relaxation** *weakens* an existing clause while preserving its variable set (1 mark). Example: changing the rating threshold from *"rating > 4.5"* to *"rating > 4.0"* — same variable, weaker condition (0.5 marks).

**Constraint Replacement** *swaps* a clause with a semantically related but distinct clause, typically using an ontology (1 mark). Example: replacing *"Director = Nolan"* with *"Director = Nolan OR Villeneuve"*, leveraging the ontological similarity between the two directors' filmography (0.5 marks).

Relaxation is cheaper ($O(k)$ edit cost) but may produce lower-quality results; replacement preserves user intent better but requires external knowledge graphs.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Full ESE Pattern**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECDT741 Module 2, Dec 2024 Pattern]**
> *A user submits the following preference set to a movie recommendation system:*
> *(i) Genre = Science Fiction*
> *(ii) Language = Korean*
> *(iii) Director = Bong Joon-ho*
> *(iv) Released in 2024*
> *(v) Rating > 4.7*
> *(vi) Duration < 120 minutes*
>
> *(a) [7 marks]* Formalize this requirement set as a Boolean CNF formula. Identify the unsatisfiable core (MUS) by examining a realistic film catalog, and justify why the set is unsatisfiable.
>
> *(b) [7 marks]* Apply a *minimum-cost repair* strategy using the following weights: $w_1 = 0.5$, $w_2 = 0.9$, $w_3 = 0.7$, $w_4 = 0.4$, $w_5 = 0.6$, $w_6 = 0.3$. Show the derivation of the repair cost function. Propose two alternative repairs and select the minimum-cost one. State the final recommended output.

---

#### **Model Solution to (a) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — CNF Encoding (2 marks):**

Let $x_1$ = "Genre is Sci-Fi", $x_2$ = "Language is Korean", $x_3$ = "Director is Bong Joon-ho", $x_4$ = "Released in 2024", $x_5$ = "Rating > 4.7", $x_6$ = "Duration < 120 min".

$$\Phi = c_1 \wedge c_2 \wedge c_3 \wedge c_4 \wedge c_5 \wedge c_6$$

where $c_i = x_i$ (each clause is a single positive literal in this simplified encoding).

**Step 2 — UNSAT Justification (2 marks):**

A realistic film catalog (as of Module 2 study period) contains Bong Joon-ho's recent works primarily between 2003 and 2019. His 2024 catalog entry is `Mickey 17` (English-language, 137 minutes, rating 4.3). No 2024 Korean-language Bong Joon-ho film exists in public databases. Therefore, $c_2 \wedge c_3 \wedge c_4$ is **UNSAT**.

**Step 3 — MUS Identification (3 marks):**

$$\mathcal{U}_{\text{MUS}} = \{c_2, c_3, c_4\}$$

Verification: $\{c_2, c_3, c_4\}$ is UNSAT; removing any single clause from it yields a satisfiable subset. The other clauses ($c_1, c_5, c_6$) are satisfiable on their own and can be jointly satisfied with relaxed versions of $c_2, c_3, c_4$.

---

#### **Model Solution to (b) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Define Cost Function (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_c(\Phi \rightarrow \Phi') = \sum_{i=1}^{6} w_i \cdot d_H(c_i, c_i') \\
\text{where } d_H \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots\} \text{ counts the literals changed.}
\end{aligned}
$$

For single-literal clauses, $d_H = 1$ if the clause is modified (relaxed/deleted/replaced), else $d_H = 0$.

**Step 2 — Enumerate Repair Options (3 marks):**

**Repair Option 1:** Relax $c_4$ (Released 2024 → Released 2023 or 2024), and Relax $c_2$ (Korean → Korean OR English).

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_c^{(1)} = w_4 \cdot 1 + w_2 \cdot 1 = 0.4 + 0.9 = 1.3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Repair Option 2:** Delete $c_4$ (release year constraint) entirely, and Replace $c_3$ (Bong Joon-ho → Bong Joon-ho OR Park Chan-wook, both Korean auteurs).

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_c^{(2)} = w_4 \cdot 1 + w_3 \cdot 1 = 0.4 + 0.7 = 1.1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Repair Option 3:** Relax $c_5$ (Rating > 4.7 → Rating > 4.3), keeping all other clauses intact.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_c^{(3)} = w_5 \cdot 1 = 0.6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Select Minimum (2 marks):**

$$R_c^{(3)} = 0.6 = \min(R_c^{(1)}, R_c^{(2)}, R_c^{(3)})$$

**Selected Repair:** Relax $c_5$ from *"Rating > 4.7"* to *"Rating > 4.3"*.

**Repaired Formula:**

$$\Phi' = c_1 \wedge c_2 \wedge c_3 \wedge c_4 \wedge c_5' \wedge c_6$$

**Final Recommended Output:**

> **Movie:** *Parasite* (2019) — Korean, Bong Joon-ho, Sci-Fi/Thriller, Rating 4.6, Duration 132 min. (Note: Duration clause $c_6$ also requires a small relaxation; if the cost $w_6 = 0.3$ is added, the total repair cost becomes $0.6 + 0.3 = 0.9$, still less than $R_c^{(1)}$ and $R_c^{(2)}$.)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECDT741 Module 2, Dec 2024 Pattern]**
> *(a) [7 marks]* With a suitable block diagram, explain the complete architecture of a repair-aware recommendation system. Describe the function of each module and explain how the feedback loop improves future repairs.
>
> *(b) [7 marks]* Compare and contrast: (i) Constraint Deletion vs. Constraint Relaxation, (ii) Structural vs. Semantic Repairs, (iii) Local Repair vs. Global Repair. Use a music recommendation example to illustrate each contrast.

---

#### **Model Solution to (a) [7 marks]**

The complete architecture (from **Section 4.3 diagram**) consists of the following modules:

| Module | Function (1.5 marks each, totalling 7) |
|--------|----------------------------------------|
| **User Query Interface** | Accepts free-text or structured preference input; invokes Preference Parser. |
| **Preference Parser** | Tokenizes the query, identifies entities (artist, genre, era, mood), maps to internal constraint schema. |
| **Constraint Weighting Module** | Assigns priority weights $w_i$ to each parsed constraint, often via learned user models or explicit user input. |
| **CNF Knowledge Base** | Persistent storage of ontologies, item metadata, and previously satisfied constraint sets. |
| **Boolean Satisfiability Engine** | Core solver (MiniSat/Glucose/CaDiCaL); executes SAT/UNSAT check on encoded formula. |
| **Repair Orchestrator** | Triggered only on UNSAT; coordinates the repair strategy bank and cost function evaluator. |
| **Feedback Loop** | Captures user acceptance/rejection of repaired recommendations; updates the weighting module. |

**The Feedback Loop's Role (1 mark integrated):** When the user accepts a relaxed recommendation, the system **increases** the weight of the relaxed constraint ($w_i \uparrow$), making future repairs more likely to preserve that constraint. Conversely, rejected repairs **decrease** the weight, making that constraint a candidate for future deletion.

---

#### **Model Solution to (b) [7 marks]**

**(i) Deletion vs. Relaxation (2 marks):**
- **Deletion** removes a clause entirely. Example: User wants *"songs by AR Rahman released after 2020"*; catalog shows none → **delete** the year constraint (cost: $w_{\text{year}}$). Output: All AR Rahman songs regardless of year.
- **Relaxation** weakens a clause. Example: Same scenario, but **relax** *"after 2020"* to *"after 2015"*. Output: AR Rahman songs from 2015 onwards. Relaxation preserves more user intent.

**(ii) Structural vs. Semantic Repairs (2.5 marks):**
- **Structural Repairs** operate on the Boolean formula's syntax — add/delete/relax literals. They are fast but may produce semantically absurd results (e.g., a "song by AR Rahman" that is actually a tribute cover, not an original).
- **Semantic Repairs** consult an external knowledge graph. For *"AR Rahman song"*, the semantic repair may **replace** with *"AR Rahman OR A.R. Rahman OR A.R.R. (alternative spellings)"* — preserving meaning. Semantic repairs require ontology lookup and are slower but more accurate.

**(iii) Local vs. Global Repair (2.5 marks):**
- **Local Repair** modifies only constraints in the detected UNSAT core, leaving all other constraints untouched. Low cost, fast, but may miss cascading effects.
- **Global Repair** re-optimizes the entire constraint set as a unified MaxSAT problem, potentially modifying constraints **outside** the UNSAT core if doing so yields a globally lower cost. Example: A local repair relaxes the year clause for a single song, but a global repair might instead relax both the year and the language constraint to surface a more popular artist match — resulting in **higher user satisfaction** despite slightly higher repair cost.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PECDT741 Module 2 Repair Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to specify clause type** — Students often write CNF formulas without identifying which clauses are *hard* vs. *soft*. Markers deduct **1 mark** if hard constraints are not explicitly marked.
> 2. **Confusing MUS with UNSAT core** — The **MUS** is *minimal*; the UNSAT core is *any* unsatisfiable subset. Many students use these terms interchangeably. **2-mark penalty** if the distinction is not stated.
> 3. **Skipping weight justification** — In cost-based repair questions, students must **show the weight assignment** and **compute the cost**, not just state the answer. A repair without cost computation receives **partial credit only**.
> 4. **Ignoring semantic validation** — A purely Boolean repair is **not complete**. Always mention the ontology/domain check, even briefly. **0.5 mark** is reserved for this.
> 5. **No feedback loop mention** — Long-answer questions on architecture **require** a feedback loop explanation for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2, Topic: Proposing Repairs for Unsatisfiable Requirements**

- ✅ **Unsatisfiable requirement set** = no Boolean assignment satisfies all clauses.
- ✅ **MUS** = minimal UNSAT subset; **MSS** = maximal SAT subset; they are **complementary** ($\text{MUS} \cup \text{MSS} = \Phi$, disjoint).
- ✅ **Repair** = transformation $\Phi \rightarrow \Phi'$ where $\Phi' \models \text{SAT}$.
- ✅ **Minimum-cost repair** = weighted Hamming distance minimization.
- ✅ **Four repair operations**: *Relax*, *Delete*, *Replace*, *Extend (Domain Expansion)*.
- ✅ **Cost function**: $R_c = \sum w_i \cdot d_H(c_i, c_i')$ with $w_i = \infty$ for hard constraints.
- ✅ **MaxSAT equivalence**: Minimum-cost repair $\iff$ MaxSAT dual of dropping soft clauses.
- ✅ **Group-MUS** = extension of MUS to equivalence-class groups; relevant for multi-criteria RS.
- ✅ **Repair cost ratio** $\rho = \frac{\vert \Phi_{\text{preserved}} \vert}{\vert \Phi \vert}$ measures **intent retention**.
- ✅ **Five-stage pipeline**: Encode → Solve → Extract Core → Repair → Validate.
- ✅ **Tools/Algorithms to know by name**: DPLL, CDCL, MiniSat, Glucose, MaxSAT solvers (e.g., MaxHS, Open-WBO), MUS extractors (e.g., MUSer, TMUS).
- ✅ **Local vs. Global repair** distinction is a **favourite 2-mark ESE question**.
- ✅ **Semantic validation** through ontologies is **mandatory** in any complete answer.
- ✅ **Feedback loop** updates weights to make future repairs more aligned with user intent.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ranking the Items / Utility-based Recommendation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT741 - RECOMMENDATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ranking the Items / Utility-based Recommendation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Utility-based Recommender System** is a non-personalized or semi-personalized recommendation paradigm in which each candidate item **i** is associated with a scalar (or vector) **utility value** `u(c, i)` that quantifies the *degree to which item i satisfies the active customer c's needs*. Recommendations are then produced by **ranking** all candidate items in descending order of their estimated utility and presenting the **top-N** items to the user.

Formally, a utility function is a mapping:

$$u : \text{Users} \times \text{Items} \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}$$

such that the system's ranking action is defined as:

$$\text{TopN}(c) = \underset{i \in I}{\arg\max_{N}} \; u(c, i)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 distinguishes between **content-based**, **collaborative**, **knowledge-based** and **utility-based** recommenders. Utility-based systems differ because they **explicitly model the user's current need** (e.g., a query, constraint, or requirement specification) rather than relying on historical preference patterns.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Travel Agent**
>
> Imagine you walk into a travel agency and say: *"I need a 4-star hotel in Kerala, budget under ₹5000/night, with a pool and free Wi-Fi, for a family of four during Christmas week."* The travel agent does not look at your past trips; instead, they evaluate every available hotel package against your **stated requirements** and **rank** the hotels from "best fit" to "worst fit" — giving you the top 3.
>
> That *fit score* the agent computes is precisely the **utility** of the item for the customer. The travel agent is performing **utility-based recommendation** in real time.

### Why "Utility"?
The term is borrowed from **microeconomics** (the classical von Neumann–Morgenstern utility theory), where consumers choose the option that **maximises their personal satisfaction**. The recommendation engine assumes users behave rationally to maximise utility.

## 1.3 Types of Utility in Recommender Systems

| Utility Type | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Scalar Utility** | Single real-valued score per (user, item) pair | Movie ratings, e-commerce relevance |
| **Vector / Multi-criteria Utility** | K-dimensional rating vector `(r₁, r₂, …, r_K)` | Restaurants (food, ambience, service) |
| **Binary Utility** | {0, 1} — bought / not bought, like / not like | Implicit feedback systems |
| **Probabilistic Utility** | `P(preference)` ∈ [0, 1] | Bayesian recommenders |
| **Adversarial / Multi-attribute Utility** | Weighted aggregation of conflicting criteria | Decision-support systems |

## 1.4 The Ranking Action — Plain Definition

**Ranking** is the deterministic operation that takes the utility matrix and produces a **total order** over the item set. Given a customer `c` and a set of items `I`:

$$\text{Rank}(c) = \bigl[ i_1 \succ i_2 \succ i_3 \succ \dots \succ i_{|I|} \bigr] \quad \text{where} \quad u(c, i_1) \geq u(c, i_2) \geq \dots \geq u(c, i_{|I|})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tie-breaking rule (KTU board-exam favourite):** When two items `i_a` and `i_b` have equal utility `u(c, i_a) = u(c, i_b)`, the standard KTU-accepted tie-breaker is **lexicographic ordering on the item-id** (alphanumerically smaller id wins). Examiners will deduct 1 mark if you omit the tie-breaking policy in your answer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Utility-Based Recommender

A utility-based recommender pipeline consists of **four logical layers**:

1. **Input / Query Layer** — The user supplies a *current need description* (constraints, preferences, hard filters). This is the **distinguishing feature** vs. collaborative filtering, which never asks the user anything explicit.
2. **Knowledge Base Layer** — Domain knowledge (case base, ontology, similarity measures, product catalog with attributes) is consulted to evaluate each candidate.
3. **Utility Computation Layer** — The system's `u(c, i)` function is evaluated for every (user, item) pair. This is the **computational heart** of the system.
4. **Ranking & Presentation Layer** — Items are sorted by utility, post-filtered by any remaining hard constraints, and the **top-N** are returned to the user.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Utility-based? — Engineering Justification**
> Utility-based recommenders are the **only** family that gracefully handles the **cold-start problem** for new items (no ratings needed) and for new users (no purchase history needed). They are the workhorse in **high-stakes B2B contexts** (industrial procurement, medical equipment, financial products) where buyers articulate explicit requirements.

## 2.2 Mathematical Formulation — Single Criterion

For a single criterion (e.g., a 1-to-5 star rating), the utility is simply the **estimated rating** that user `c` would assign to item `i`:

$$\hat{u}(c, i) = \hat{r}_{c,i}$$

A common estimation approach uses **collaborative filtering regression**:

$$\hat{r}_{c,i} = \bar{r}_c + \frac{\sum_{c' \in \text{Neighbours}(c)} \text{sim}(c, c') \cdot (r_{c', i} - \bar{r}_{c'})}{\sum_{c' \in \text{Neighbours}(c)} \vert \text{sim}(c, c') \vert}$$

where `sim(c, c')` is the **Pearson correlation** or **cosine similarity** between users `c` and `c'`.

## 2.3 Mathematical Formulation — Multi-Criteria Utility

This is a **high-yield KTU topic** — every Module-2 question paper since 2019 has had at least one multi-criteria sub-part. The general model:

$$u(c, i) = F\bigl(r_1(c, i), \, r_2(c, i), \, \dots, \, r_K(c, i)\bigr)$$

Three classical **aggregation functions** are accepted in the KTU syllabus:

| Aggregation Strategy | Formula | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Weighted Average** | $u(c, i) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} w_k \cdot r_k(c, i)$ | Criteria are compensatory (a great *food* rating can offset poor *ambience*) |
| **Minimum (Pessimistic / Worst-Case)** | $u(c, i) = \min_{k} \, r_k(c, i)$ | User wants *no weak dimension* (e.g., safety-critical purchases) |
| **Weighted Minimum** | $u(c, i) = \min_{k} \, w_k \cdot r_k(c, i)$ | Mix of priority and safety constraints |
| **Multiplicative** | $u(c, i) = \prod_{k=1}^{K} r_k(c, i)$ | A zero on any criterion zeroes the utility |

with the standard constraints:

$$\sum_{k=1}^{K} w_k = 1, \qquad w_k \geq 0, \qquad r_k(c, i) \in [r_{\min}, r_{\max}]$$

## 2.4 The Utility Matrix

The **utility matrix** is a `|U| × |I|` tabular structure, with one row per user and one column per item. Entry `(c, i)` contains the observed (or predicted) utility of item `i` for user `c`. **Most entries are unknown** (sparse matrix), and the recommender's job is exactly to **fill in the blanks** so that ranking becomes possible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sparsity is the fundamental challenge.** For a matrix with 1 million users and 100,000 items, even a 50 % rating-coverage still leaves ~50 billion unknown cells. Every KTU board problem that asks "why is recommendation hard?" ultimately points back to **sparsity** in this matrix.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table — Memorise every row.** KTU Module-2 questions often present a fully worked numerical table and ask for a single utility value or a rank.

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Glossary | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Utility mapping | $u : U \times I \to \mathbb{R}$ | `U` = user set, `I` = item set | unitless score |
| 2 | Top-N extraction | $\text{TopN}(c) = \arg\max_{N} u(c, i)$ | `argmax_N` = N items with max utility | — |
| 3 | Pearson similarity | $\text{sim}(c, c') = \dfrac{\sum_{i \in I_{cc'}} (r_{c,i} - \bar{r}_c)(r_{c',i} - \bar{r}_{c'})}{\sqrt{\sum_{i \in I_{cc'}}(r_{c,i} - \bar{r}_c)^2} \sqrt{\sum_{i \in I_{cc'}}(r_{c',i} - \bar{r}_{c'})^2}}$ | $I_{cc'}$ = co-rated items | dimensionless, $[-1, +1]$ |
| 4 | Cosine similarity | $\text{sim}(c, c') = \dfrac{\vec{r}_c \cdot \vec{r}_{c'}}{\Vert \vec{r}_c \Vert_2 \, \Vert \vec{r}_{c'} \Vert_2}$ | dot-product over item vectors | dimensionless, $[-1, +1]$ |
| 5 | Predicted rating (CF) | $\hat{r}_{c,i} = \bar{r}_c + \dfrac{\sum_{c'} \text{sim}(c, c')(r_{c',i} - \bar{r}_{c'})}{\sum_{c'} \vert \text{sim}(c, c') \vert}$ | $\bar{r}_c$ = mean rating of `c` | stars / score |
| 6 | Multi-criteria weighted avg | $u(c, i) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} w_k r_k(c, i)$ | $w_k$ = criterion weight | score |
| 7 | Multi-criteria min | $u(c, i) = \min_k r_k(c, i)$ | pessimistic aggregator | score |
| 8 | Multi-criteria product | $u(c, i) = \prod_{k=1}^{K} r_k(c, i)$ | conjunctive | score |
| 9 | RMSE (evaluation) | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\vert T \vert}\sum_{(c,i) \in T} (r_{c,i} - \hat{r}_{c,i})^2}$ | `T` = test set | same as rating scale |
| 10 | MAE (evaluation) | $\text{MAE} = \dfrac{1}{\vert T \vert}\sum_{(c,i) \in T} \vert r_{c,i} - \hat{r}_{c,i} \vert$ | absolute deviation | same as rating scale |
| 11 | Precision@N | $\text{P@N} = \dfrac{\text{Relevant} \cap \text{TopN}}{\vert \text{TopN} \vert}$ | ranking metric | dimensionless |
| 12 | Recall@N | $\text{R@N} = \dfrac{\text{Relevant} \cap \text{TopN}}{\vert \text{Relevant} \vert}$ | ranking metric | dimensionless |
| 13 | NDCG@N | $\text{NDCG@N} = \dfrac{\text{DCG@N}}{\text{IDCG@N}}$ | graded relevance | dimensionless |
| 14 | Tie-break order | $\text{lex}(i_a) < \text{lex}(i_b) \Rightarrow i_a \succ i_b$ | lexicographic on item id | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units note for examiners:** Utility is conventionally **unitless** because it represents a *subjective preference* on an arbitrary scale (1–5, 1–10, 0–1, etc.). RMSE and MAE inherit the units of the underlying rating scale.

## 2.6 Relationship Between Ranking and Utility

The ranking operation is **monotonic** with respect to utility: a higher utility always yields a better rank (closer to position 1). This is a **strictly monotonic transformation** property that justifies post-utility scaling (e.g., min-max normalisation) before ranking:

$$\text{Rank}(i) = \text{Rank}(j) \iff u(c, i) = u(c, j)$$

$$\text{Rank}(i) < \text{Rank}(j) \iff u(c, i) > u(c, j)$$

This property is what allows the recommender to use any **affine transformation** of utility (e.g., `u'(c, i) = α u(c, i) + β` with `α > 0`) without changing the ranking result.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Example

## 3.1 Derivation of the Multi-Criteria Weighted Aggregator

We start from the generic multi-criteria utility definition and derive the weighted average form that the KTU board expects you to write in exams.

**Step 1 — Start with the general definition of multi-criteria utility.**

$$u(c, i) = F\bigl( r_1(c, i), r_2(c, i), \dots, r_K(c, i) \bigr)$$

**Step 2 — Assume a linear (weighted) aggregation form**, which is the most common and the one accepted as the default in the KTU syllabus unless the question explicitly states otherwise.

$$F = \sum_{k=1}^{K} w_k \cdot r_k(c, i)$$

**Step 3 — Impose the convexity constraint on weights** (this is the *axiom* that makes the aggregation a true weighted average — without it, the weights could be negative and the result unbounded below).

$$\sum_{k=1}^{K} w_k = 1, \qquad w_k \geq 0$$

**Step 4 — Show that the function is bounded** between the minimum and maximum rating values of any criterion (this is the KTU board's favourite sanity-check question).

$$r_{\min} = \min_{k} r_{\min, k} \leq u(c, i) \leq \max_{k} r_{\max, k} = r_{\max}$$

Proof: substitute the lower-bound ratings into the formula:

$$\sum_{k=1}^{K} w_k \cdot r_{\min, k} \leq \sum_{k=1}^{K} w_k \cdot r_k(c, i) \leq \sum_{k=1}^{K} w_k \cdot r_{\max, k}$$

and using $\sum_k w_k = 1$:

$$u_{\min} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} w_k \cdot r_{\min, k}, \qquad u_{\max} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} w_k \cdot r_{\max, k}$$

**Step 5 — Final form to write in the exam:**

$$\boxed{u(c, i) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} w_k \cdot r_k(c, i), \qquad \sum_{k=1}^{K} w_k = 1, \quad w_k \geq 0}$$

## 3.2 KTU-Style Fully Solved Numerical Example

This is a **board-style question** that has appeared in KTU Model Question Papers. Work through it carefully.

> **Problem (KTU 2024 Scheme style):**
> A hotel-recommender system uses three criteria — *Food* `r₁`, *Ambience* `r₂`, *Service* `r₃` — each rated on a 1–5 scale. User weights are $w_1 = 0.5$, $w_2 = 0.2$, $w_3 = 0.3$. The following multi-criteria ratings have been collected for four hotels:

| Hotel | $r_1$ (Food) | $r_2$ (Ambience) | $r_3$ (Service) |
|---|---|---|---|
| H1 | 4 | 3 | 5 |
| H2 | 5 | 2 | 4 |
| H3 | 3 | 5 | 3 |
| H4 | 4 | 4 | 4 |

> Compute the utility of each hotel and produce the ranked list (use lexicographic tie-breaking on `H{id}`).

### Step-by-step Solution

**Step 1 — Verify the weights sum to unity.**

$$\sum_{k=1}^{3} w_k = 0.5 + 0.2 + 0.3 = 1.0 \quad \checkmark$$

This satisfies the convexity constraint, so the weighted average is well-defined.

**Step 2 — Compute utility for hotel H1.**

$$u(c, H1) = (0.5)(4) + (0.2)(3) + (0.3)(5)$$

$$= 2.0 + 0.6 + 1.5$$

$$= 4.10$$

**Step 3 — Compute utility for hotel H2.**

$$u(c, H2) = (0.5)(5) + (0.2)(2) + (0.3)(4)$$

$$= 2.5 + 0.4 + 1.2$$

$$= 4.10$$

**Step 4 — Compute utility for hotel H3.**

$$u(c, H3) = (0.5)(3) + (0.2)(5) + (0.3)(3)$$

$$= 1.5 + 1.0 + 0.9$$

$$= 3.40$$

**Step 5 — Compute utility for hotel H4.**

$$u(c, H4) = (0.5)(4) + (0.2)(4) + (0.3)(4)$$

$$= 2.0 + 0.8 + 1.2$$

$$= 4.00$$

**Step 6 — Collect utilities in a summary table.**

| Hotel | $u(c, i)$ |
|---|---|
| H1 | **4.10** |
| H2 | **4.10** |
| H3 | **3.40** |
| H4 | **4.00** |

**Step 7 — Apply ranking in descending order of utility, then lexicographic tie-break.**

$$u(H1) = u(H2) = 4.10 \Rightarrow \text{tie. Lexicographic: } H1 \prec H2 \text{ (since "H1" < "H2")}$$

$$u(H4) = 4.00 \Rightarrow \text{rank 3}$$

$$u(H3) = 3.40 \Rightarrow \text{rank 4}$$

**Final Ranked List:**

$$\boxed{H2 \succ H1 \succ H4 \succ H3}$$

**Step 8 — Top-N extraction** (if the user asks for top-2):

$$\text{Top-2}(c) = \{ H2, H1 \}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Mistake:** Students often present the **final ranked list** but forget to show the individual utility calculations. You will lose **2 of 7 marks** in a 7-mark sub-part if the working is missing. Always show every hotel's `u(c, i)` arithmetic on the answer sheet.

## 3.3 Alternative — Pessimistic (Minimum) Aggregator

For safety-critical decisions (e.g., a hospital equipment purchase), KTU may ask you to repeat the example using the **worst-case** aggregator:

$$u_{\min}(c, i) = \min_{k} r_k(c, i)$$

For our example:

| Hotel | $r_1$ | $r_2$ | $r_3$ | $\min$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| H1 | 4 | 3 | 5 | **3** |
| H2 | 5 | 2 | 4 | **2** |
| H3 | 3 | 5 | 3 | **3** |
| H4 | 4 | 4 | 4 | **4** |

Ranking by min: `H4 (4) > H1 (3) = H3 (3) > H2 (2)`. With tie-break on lex: **H4, H1, H3, H2**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation worth memorising:** Notice how the ranking **completely changes** when we switch from *weighted average* to *minimum* — H2 drops from 1st place to last! This is a classic *compensatory vs non-compensatory* trade-off that KTU examiners love to ask.

## 3.4 Evaluation of a Ranking — Worked Numerical

Suppose we know the *ground-truth relevant* hotels for the user are `{H2, H4}`. The system's top-2 recommendation is `{H2, H1}`.

**Precision@2:**

$$P@2 = \frac{\vert \{H2, H4\} \cap \{H2, H1\} \vert}{2} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$$

**Recall@2:**

$$R@2 = \frac{\vert \{H2, H4\} \cap \{H2, H1\} \vert}{\vert \{H2, H4\} \vert} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$$

This shows the system has correctly retrieved one of the two relevant items in the top-2 slot.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Utility-Based Recommender Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[User Input / Query]:::input --> nodeB[Constraint Parser]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Hard Filter]:::filter
    nodeC --> nodeD[Item Catalog / Knowledge Base]:::db
    nodeD --> nodeE[Utility Function u c i]:::compute
    nodeE --> nodeF[Utility Matrix M]:::matrix
    nodeF --> nodeG[Sorter / Ranking Engine]:::rank
    nodeG --> nodeH[Tie Breaker Lex Order]:::rank
    nodeH --> nodeI[Top N Selector]:::rank
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Final Ranked List]:::output
    nodeE --> nodeK[Multi Criteria Aggregator]:::compute
    nodeK --> nodeE
    classDef input fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#d97706,color:#000
    classDef filter fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#dc2626,color:#000
    classDef db fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000
    classDef compute fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#000
    classDef matrix fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#6d28d9,color:#000
    classDef rank fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#be185d,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#f3f4f6,stroke:#374151,color:#000
```

## 4.2 The Multi-Criteria Utility Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeM1[Criterion 1 Rating r1]:::c1 --> nodeAgg[Weighted Aggregator]:::agg
    nodeM2[Criterion 2 Rating r2]:::c2 --> nodeAgg
    nodeM3[Criterion 3 Rating r3]:::c3 --> nodeAgg
    nodeM4[Criterion K Rating rK]:::c4 --> nodeAgg
    nodeAgg --> nodeU[Utility Value u c i]:::out
    nodeW1[Weight w1]:::wt --> nodeAgg
    nodeW2[Weight w2]:::wt --> nodeAgg
    nodeW3[Weight w3]:::wt --> nodeAgg
    nodeW4[Weight wK]:::wt --> nodeAgg
    classDef c1 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000
    classDef c2 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    classDef c3 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,color:#000
    classDef c4 fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000
    classDef wt fill:#f3e8ff,stroke:#6b21a8,color:#000
    classDef agg fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Ranking Operation — Utility to Order

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Items i1 i2 i3 i4 i5]:::start --> nodeUtil[Compute u c i for all i]:::compute
    nodeUtil --> nodeTable[Utility Table]:::tab
    nodeTable --> nodeSort[Sort Descending by u]:::sort
    nodeSort --> nodeTie[Tie Break Lex Order]:::sort
    nodeTie --> nodeOut[Ranked Output: i2 i1 i4 i5 i3]:::out
    classDef start fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1,color:#000
    classDef compute fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#a16207,color:#000
    classDef tab fill:#fae8ff,stroke:#86198f,color:#000
    classDef sort fill:#ffe4e6,stroke:#9f1239,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Utility Matrix Visual Block

```
                +------------+ ITEM COLUMNS ------------+
                |   i1   |   i2   |   i3   |   i4   |   i5   |
   +------------+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------+
   | USER  c1   |   5    |   ?    |   3    |   ?    |   4    |
   |------------|--------|--------|--------|--------|--------|
   | USER  c2   |   ?    |   4    |   ?    |   2    |   ?    |
   |------------|--------|--------|--------|--------|--------|
   | USER  c3   |   3    |   ?    |   5    |   ?    |   1    |
   |------------|--------|--------|--------|--------|--------|
   | USER  c4   |   ?    |   5    |   ?    |   4    |   ?    |
   +------------+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------+
                      ↑     ↑     ↑     ↑     ↑
                  Most entries are UNKNOWN ('?')
                  The recommender FILLS these in to
                  enable ranking.
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the matrix:** A `?` cell means the user has not yet rated the item. The recommender's job is to **predict** the unknown utilities (e.g., via collaborative filtering) and then **rank** items column-wise (per user) to produce personalised top-N lists.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, S8 B.Tech CSE]`

> **Define a utility function in the context of a recommender system. How does it differ from a similarity function?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A utility function `u(c, i) : U × I → ℝ` is a mapping that assigns a **single real-valued score** to a (customer, item) pair, quantifying the *usefulness* or *preference* of the item for that user. It is **user-specific and item-specific**; the recommender **ranks** items by sorting them in descending order of `u(c, i)` and returns the top-N.

A similarity function `sim(c, c') : U × U → ℝ`, in contrast, quantifies the **resemblance between two users** (or two items) and is typically a building block used *inside* a utility-computation pipeline (e.g., as a weight in the collaborative-filtering predicted-rating formula). **Utility = absolute preference score; Similarity = relative closeness measure between entities.**

**(Marks distribution: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for utility vs similarity distinction, 1 mark for the role of each in ranking.)**

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, S8 B.Tech CSE]`

> **List any three limitations of utility-based recommenders.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Knowledge-acquisition bottleneck** — Utility functions require a rich, well-engineered *knowledge base* (product attributes, user requirement schema, domain ontology). Building and maintaining this is **expensive and time-consuming**.
2. **Static user model** — The user must **articulate preferences** at query time; if the user cannot express their need precisely (e.g., "I want a movie I will like"), the system cannot infer it.
3. **No serendipity** — Because recommendations are *optimised* against the stated query, the system tends to return *obvious* matches and rarely surfaces surprising or *serendipitous* items.
4. *(Bonus 1 mark for a 4th valid point, e.g., "scalability issues when $|I|$ is in millions, since every item is scored against the query.")*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

> **Note:** As per the KTU 2024 scheme, Part-B Module-2 questions carry **14 marks** and offer **internal choice**. Both sub-parts (a) and (b) carry 7 marks each. Below, we present **Question A (Choice 1)** and **Question B (Choice 2)** — answer *either* A or B in the exam.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU Model Paper 2024, PECDT741]`

> **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the architecture of a utility-based recommender system with a neat block diagram. Compare it briefly with content-based recommendation.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** A customer evaluates laptops on three criteria: *Performance* (P), *Battery* (B), *Design* (D), each on a 1–5 scale. The user-declared weights are $w_P = 0.4$, $w_B = 0.3$, $w_D = 0.3$. Four candidate laptops have the following multi-criteria ratings:
>
> | Laptop | P | B | D |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | L1 | 4 | 3 | 5 |
> | L2 | 5 | 4 | 4 |
> | L3 | 3 | 5 | 3 |
> | L4 | 4 | 2 | 4 |
>
> Compute the utility of each laptop using the **weighted-average aggregator**, produce the **ranked list** (with lexicographic tie-break), and identify the **top-2** recommendations.

### Model Answer — Question A

#### (a) Architecture of a Utility-Based Recommender

**Introduction (2 marks):** A utility-based recommender generates recommendations by computing a **utility score** `u(c, i)` for every (user, item) pair and ranking items in descending order of `u`. Unlike content-based systems that rely on **historical item profiles**, utility-based systems rely on the **user's current need specification**.

**Architecture (4 marks):**

The system has the following sequential components:

1. **User Need / Query Input** — The user supplies constraints (e.g., budget, feature requirements).
2. **Knowledge Base** — A structured catalog containing item attributes, similarity measures, and domain rules.
3. **Utility Computation Engine** — Evaluates the utility function `u(c, i)` for each candidate item, possibly decomposing it into a multi-criteria aggregator.
4. **Ranking Module** — Sorts items by utility, applies tie-breaking (lexicographic on item-id), and selects top-N.
5. **Presentation Layer** — Displays the ranked list to the user with optional explanations.

**Comparison with Content-Based (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Content-Based | Utility-Based |
|---|---|---|
| Input | Item history of the user | Explicit query / requirements |
| Cold start (item) | OK (uses attributes) | OK (uses attributes / rules) |
| Cold start (user) | Poor (needs history) | Excellent (needs only query) |
| Personalisation source | Past behaviour | Current need |

**(Marks: 2 + 4 + 1 = 7)**

---

#### (b) Numerical Solution

**Step 1 — Verify weights (0.5 mark):**

$$w_P + w_B + w_D = 0.4 + 0.3 + 0.3 = 1.0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Apply weighted-average formula (4 marks for four calculations):**

$$u(c, L) = 0.4 \cdot P + 0.3 \cdot B + 0.3 \cdot D$$

**Laptop L1:**

$$u(c, L1) = (0.4)(4) + (0.3)(3) + (0.3)(5) = 1.6 + 0.9 + 1.5 = 4.00$$

**Laptop L2:**

$$u(c, L2) = (0.4)(5) + (0.3)(4) + (0.3)(4) = 2.0 + 1.2 + 1.2 = 4.40$$

**Laptop L3:**

$$u(c, L3) = (0.4)(3) + (0.3)(5) + (0.3)(3) = 1.2 + 1.5 + 0.9 = 3.60$$

**Laptop L4:**

$$u(c, L4) = (0.4)(4) + (0.3)(2) + (0.3)(4) = 1.6 + 0.6 + 1.2 = 3.40$$

**Step 3 — Compile the utility table (1 mark):**

| Laptop | $u(c, L)$ |
|---|---|
| L1 | 4.00 |
| L2 | **4.40** |
| L3 | 3.60 |
| L4 | 3.40 |

**Step 4 — Rank and select top-2 (1.5 marks):**

Descending order: `L2 (4.40) ≻ L1 (4.00) ≻ L3 (3.60) ≻ L4 (3.40)`.

No ties, so no tie-breaker needed.

$$\boxed{\text{Top-2}(c) = \{ L2, L1 \}}$$

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU Model Paper 2024, PECDT741 — Alternative]`

> **(a) [7 marks]** Define the *utility matrix* in a recommender system. Discuss the **sparsity problem** with a numerical example. Explain how utility-based recommenders can mitigate sparsity for **new items** (cold-start).
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** The same laptop scenario as Question A is given, but the user now specifies that *Battery* is a **non-compensatory hard constraint** (minimum acceptable score = 3). Re-compute the utilities using the **pessimistic (minimum) aggregator**, apply the hard constraint, and produce the new ranked list.

### Model Answer — Question B

#### (a) Utility Matrix & Sparsity

**Definition (2 marks):** The utility matrix `M` is a `|U| × |I|` table where each row corresponds to a user, each column to an item, and the entry `M[c, i] = u(c, i)` is the observed (or predicted) utility. For a real platform with millions of users and items, **most entries are missing** because users only rate a tiny fraction of available items.

**Numerical example of sparsity (3 marks):**

Suppose a system has 1000 users and 5000 movies. The maximum possible entries are $1000 \times 5000 = 5{,}000{,}000$. In practice, the average user has rated only ~50 movies. Hence, observed entries $= 1000 \times 50 = 50{,}000$. Sparsity:

$$\text{Sparsity} = 1 - \frac{50{,}000}{5{,}000{,}000} = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99 = 99\%$$

So 99 % of the matrix is unknown. This makes accurate ranking extremely difficult.

**Mitigation for new items (2 marks):** Utility-based recommenders do **not require historical ratings** of an item to compute its utility. They use the **attribute profile** of the new item (e.g., CPU speed, RAM, weight) and the user's **stated query** (e.g., "laptop with ≥ 8 GB RAM and < ₹70 000"). The utility function directly evaluates the attribute match — a *brand-new* item can be ranked on the very day it is added to the catalog, completely bypassing the cold-start problem that plagues collaborative filtering.

---

#### (b) Pessimistic Aggregator with Hard Constraint

**Step 1 — Apply minimum aggregator (3 marks):**

$$u_{\min}(c, L) = \min(P, B, D)$$

| Laptop | P | B | D | $\min$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| L1 | 4 | 3 | 5 | **3** |
| L2 | 5 | 4 | 4 | **4** |
| L3 | 3 | 5 | 3 | **3** |
| L4 | 4 | 2 | 4 | **2** |

**Step 2 — Apply hard constraint B ≥ 3 (2 marks):**

Laptop L4 is eliminated because its battery rating is 2 < 3.

Remaining candidates: L1, L2, L3.

**Step 3 — Rank the survivors (2 marks):**

$$u_{\min}(L2) = 4 \succ u_{\min}(L1) = 3 = u_{\min}(L3) = 3$$

Apply lexicographic tie-break: L1 precedes L3 (since "L1" < "L3").

**Final ranked list after filtering:**

$$\boxed{L2 \succ L1 \succ L3 \qquad (\text{L4 eliminated by hard constraint})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison with Question A:** Under the weighted average, the ranking was `L2 > L1 > L3 > L4`. Under the pessimistic aggregator with the hard battery constraint, it becomes `L2 > L1 > L3` with L4 **removed entirely**. This illustrates how the choice of aggregator and constraint dramatically changes the recommendation list.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in this topic — and how to avoid each loss**
>
> | # | Common Mistake | Marks Lost | How to Avoid |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | Writing the utility formula without showing the **weight normalisation** constraint $\sum w_k = 1$ | 1 | Always state the constraint explicitly the first time you write the formula |
> | 2 | Forgetting to **show the working** for each item's utility | 2 | Write every calculation line-by-line on the answer sheet |
> | 3 | **Skipping the tie-breaking rule** when two items have equal utility | 1 | State "lexicographic on item-id" before producing the final list |
> | 4 | Confusing **utility** with **similarity** in definitions | 1 | Memorise: utility = absolute preference; similarity = relative closeness |
> | 5 | Presenting a **partial table** in multi-criteria problems | 1 | Always show the full $K$-column rating table before the utility column |
> | 6 | Not mentioning the **cold-start advantage** when asked to compare recommenders | 1 | Include at least one comparative sentence in every architecture question |

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module-2: Utility-based Recommendation**
>
> - **Utility function** `u : U × I → ℝ` is the *scalar satisfaction score* assigned to a (user, item) pair.
> - **Ranking** is the *descending sort* of items by `u`; ties broken **lexicographically on item-id**.
> - **Top-N** recommendation is the first `N` items of the ranked list.
> - **Utility matrix** is `|U| × |I|` and is **sparse** — the central data structure of every recommender.
> - **Single-criterion utility** = predicted rating `r̂_{c,i}` from collaborative filtering.
> - **Multi-criteria utility** = an aggregation of `K` ratings, with three classical aggregators:
>   - Weighted average: $u = \sum_k w_k r_k$ (compensatory, default)
>   - Minimum: $u = \min_k r_k$ (pessimistic, safety-critical)
>   - Product: $u = \prod_k r_k$ (conjunctive, any zero kills utility)
> - Weights must satisfy $\sum_k w_k = 1$, $w_k \geq 0$.
> - **Advantages of utility-based**: no cold-start for new users/items, transparent, no history needed, great for high-stakes purchases.
> - **Disadvantages**: requires rich knowledge base, user must articulate query, no serendipity, knowledge-engineering cost.
> - **Hard constraints** (e.g., battery ≥ 3) are applied *before* ranking to eliminate infeasible items; this is a *post-utility, pre-rank* filtering step.
> - **Evaluation metrics** for ranking quality: Precision@N, Recall@N, NDCG@N, MAP, MRR — these measure how well the top-N matches the ground-truth relevant set.
> - **Engineering best practice**: always use **affine-invariant scaling** before ranking if utility scores come from heterogeneous sources (so that $\alpha u + \beta$, $\alpha > 0$ preserves the rank order).
> - **Cold-start for items**: utility-based systems compute utility from item *attributes* matched against the *user query* — no historical rating data needed.
> - **Cold-start for users**: utility-based systems ask the user for an *explicit requirement specification* at query time — no purchase/rating history needed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interacting with Case-based Recommenders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT741 - RECOMMENDATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Interacting with Case-based Recommenders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Case-Based Recommenders (CBR Recommenders)** are recommendation systems that solve new problems by retrieving and adapting solutions from a stored library of past *cases*, where each case is a structured representation of a previously experienced problem–solution pair. The user's interaction with such a system is typically an **interactive, mixed-initiative dialog** in which the user can *query, navigate, critique, and refine* the proposed recommendations until an acceptable solution is reached.

The process of **interacting with case-based recommenders** refers to the user-system communication loop in which the user incrementally reveals preferences, the system retrieves similar cases from the case base using a similarity function, and the system may apply **adaptation rules** to tailor retrieved solutions to the user's current context.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** A *case* is a tuple $\mathcal{C} = \langle P, S, O \rangle$ where $P$ denotes the **Problem specification** (input attributes / user requirements), $S$ denotes the **Solution description** (recommended item / action), and $O$ denotes the **Outcome metadata** (utility, feedback, contextual tags).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you walk into a tailor who has stitched **1,000 shirts** before. You don't describe your requirements from scratch in machine terms. Instead, you point at a *display shirt* and say:

- *"I like this one, but make the sleeves shorter."* (critique)
- *"Show me something similar but in a darker color."* (similarity-based navigation)
- *"What did you stitch for someone with a build like mine last year?"* (case retrieval)
- *"Can we combine the collar of one design with the fabric of another?"* (adaptation)

A **case-based recommender behaves exactly like this experienced tailor**. It remembers thousands of past "problem → solution" experiences (cases), retrieves the most similar ones to your current need, and adapts them intelligently based on your critiques and feedback.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Collaborative Filtering:** Case-based recommenders reason over **content attributes of items** using *similarity functions*, not over aggregated user-item rating matrices. They are essentially a specialized form of **content-based reasoning** with explicit, retrievable memory.

### 1.3 Why Interaction Matters in CBR

Interaction is fundamental to CBR recommenders because:

1. **Sparse initial query** — Users rarely specify all requirements upfront. A dialog is needed to elicit preferences.
2. **Similarity is approximate** — Retrieved cases are *nearest neighbors*, not exact matches. Users must critique and refine.
3. **Adaptation is knowledge-intensive** — Modifying a retrieved solution to fit a new problem requires user input on which attributes matter more.
4. **Conversational preference elicitation** — Each interaction round narrows the search space.

> [!TIP]
> **Remember this acronym for the KTU exam — the "4 REs" of CBR:**
> **R**etrieve $\rightarrow$ **R**euse $\rightarrow$ **R**evise $\rightarrow$ **R**etain

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Case-Based Reasoning (CBR) Cycle — Operational Steps

The classical **Aamodt–Plaza CBR cycle** governs how case-based recommenders interact with users:

1. **Retrieve** — Given a new target problem $Q$, compute similarity $\text{sim}(Q, P_i)$ against every stored case $\mathcal{C}_i = \langle P_i, S_i, O_i \rangle$ in the case base $\mathcal{B}$. Return the top-$k$ most similar cases.
2. **Reuse** — Map the solution $S^*$ of the most similar retrieved case onto the target problem $Q$. This may be a direct copy or a candidate to be adapted.
3. **Revise** — Test the proposed solution against the user's actual constraints and apply **adaptation rules** (substitution, transformation, specialization) based on user critique.
4. **Retain** — If the revised solution is accepted, store the new problem-solution pair as a fresh case in $\mathcal{B}$, enabling system learning.

### 2.2 User Interaction Paradigms in Case-Based Recommenders

Case-based recommenders support **four principal interaction styles**:

| Interaction Style | Mechanism | When Used | KTU Exam Cue |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Query-Based** | User enters attribute-value constraints; system retrieves similar cases | Initial cold-start phase | "Specify your requirements" |
| **Similarity-Based Navigation** | User clicks a displayed case; system finds *more like this* | Browsing/exploration | "Find similar items" |
| **Critique-Based** | User critiques retrieved cases ("cheaper", "lighter", "more compact") | Refining an acceptable candidate | "Unit critiques" / "dynamic critiquing" |
| **Conversational / Mixed-Initiative** | Multi-turn dialog where system asks, user answers | Complex multi-attribute problems | "Preference elicitation dialog" |

### 2.3 Critique Types — A Critical KTU Topic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critiques** are the dominant interaction mechanism in case-based recommenders because they allow users to express *preference directions* rather than absolute values.

The three standard critique types are:

- **Unit Critiques** — Modify a single attribute by a fixed unit (e.g., *"cheaper by \$50"*).
- **Compound Critiques** — Modify multiple attributes simultaneously (e.g., *"lighter and cheaper"*).
- **Dynamic Critiques** — Critiques generated automatically by the system by analyzing the *difference* between the currently displayed case and other candidate cases in the case base.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes every high-yield equation for this topic. Pay special attention to the similarity, adaptation, and confidence formulas.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Case Tuple | $\mathcal{C} = \langle P, S, O \rangle$ | Problem–Solution–Outcome structure |
| 2 | Local Similarity (numeric attr.) | $\text{sim}_a(v_q, v_c) = 1 - \dfrac{\vert v_q - v_c \vert}{\text{range}(a)}$ | Attribute-level similarity (0 to 1) |
| 3 | Local Similarity (sym. binary) | $\text{sim}_a = \dfrac{2 \cdot (T_1 \cap T_2)}{\vert T_1 \vert + \vert T_2 \vert}$ | Dice coefficient on attribute sets |
| 4 | Global Similarity | $\text{sim}(Q, P_i) = \dfrac{\sum_{a \in A} w_a \cdot \text{sim}_a(Q, P_i)}{\sum_{a \in A} w_a}$ | Weighted nearest-neighbor retrieval |
| 5 | Adaptation (Substitution) | $S^* = S_{\text{retrieved}} \oplus \Delta$ | Apply delta to retrieved solution |
| 6 | Adaptation (Weight Update) | $w_a^{(t+1)} = w_a^{(t)} + \eta \cdot (\text{feedback}_a - \text{expected}_a)$ | User-feedback weight learning |
| 7 | Confidence of Retrieval | $\text{conf}(Q) = \dfrac{\text{sim}(Q, P_{\text{top}}) - \text{sim}(Q, P_{\text{top-}k})}{\text{sim}(Q, P_{\text{top}})}$ | Gap between best and $k$-th match |
| 8 | Critiquing Coverage | $\text{Cov}(c) = \vert \mathcal{B}_c \vert / \vert \mathcal{B} \vert$ | Fraction of cases reachable by critique $c$ |
| 9 | Compound Critique Utility | $U(c) = \text{Cov}(c) \cdot (1 - \text{Overlap}(c))$ | Trade-off between coverage and redundancy |
| 10 | Case Base Growth | $\vert \mathcal{B}_{t+1} \vert = \vert \mathcal{B}_t \vert + \mathbb{1}[\text{retain}]$ | Cases added per accepted revision |

> **Constraint Reminder:** All absolute-value expressions use the safer $\vert \cdot \vert$ rendering to remain compatible with markdown tables. In LaTeX display math, you may write $|x|$ freely.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Case-based recommenders are heavily used in:
- **E-commerce configurators** (PC builder, car configurator)
- **CAD/Engineering design reuse** (retrieving past mechanical parts)
- **Medical diagnosis support** (retrieving similar patient histories)
- **Help-desk and troubleshooting systems** (retrieving past incident resolutions)
- **Legal AI** (retrieving precedents similar to a new case)

The **interaction layer** is what differentiates a static CBR system from a deployable, user-facing recommender — making this topic a frequent KTU question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Global Similarity Function (Nearest-Neighbor Retrieval)

The most critical mathematical object in a case-based recommender is the **weighted nearest-neighbor similarity** used to retrieve cases. Below is the complete derivation that KTU examiners expect.

**Step 1 — Define the problem space.**

A new query (target problem) $Q$ is presented to the system. The case base $\mathcal{B}$ contains $n$ stored cases:

$$\mathcal{B} = \{\mathcal{C}_1, \mathcal{C}_2, \dots, \mathcal{C}_n\}$$

Each case $\mathcal{C}_i$ has a problem part $P_i$ described over an attribute set $A = \{a_1, a_2, \dots, a_m\}$.

**Step 2 — Compute local (per-attribute) similarity.**

For each attribute $a_j \in A$, a local similarity function $\text{sim}_a(v_q, v_c)$ returns a value in $[0, 1]$. For a numeric attribute, this is computed as the normalized complement of the absolute distance:

$$\text{sim}_{a_j}(v_q, v_{c}) = 1 - \frac{\vert v_q - v_c \vert}{\max(a_j) - \min(a_j)}$$

*Conversion logic:* If the two values are equal, distance is 0 and similarity is 1 (perfect). If they differ by the full attribute range, similarity is 0 (worst).

**Step 3 — Aggregate the local similarities with weights.**

Each attribute $a_j$ is assigned a weight $w_j \in [0, 1]$ reflecting its relative importance. The global similarity is the **weighted arithmetic mean** of the local similarities:

$$\text{sim}(Q, P_i) = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{m} w_j \cdot \text{sim}_{a_j}(Q, P_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{m} w_j}$$

*Conversion logic:* This is simply a convex combination (assuming weights are normalized) of per-attribute scores. Attributes with higher $w_j$ contribute more to the overall similarity verdict.

**Step 4 — Rank and retrieve the top-$k$.**

After computing $\text{sim}(Q, P_i)$ for every $i \in \{1, \dots, n\}$, sort in descending order and return the top-$k$ cases:

$$\text{Retrieved} = \text{argsort}\big(\text{sim}(Q, P_i)\big)_{i=1}^{k}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Common KTU pitfall:** Students often forget to **normalize** the weights in the denominator. If weights are unnormalized, the formula above is correct *only if* the denominator is included. An alternative compact form assumes $\sum_j w_j = 1$.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Similarity Computation

Consider a car-recommender with attributes: **Price**, **Mileage**, **Year**, **Fuel Type**.

| Case | Price (₹ Lakh) | Mileage (kmpl) | Year | Fuel |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Target $Q$ | 8.0 | 18 | 2022 | Petrol |
| Case $\mathcal{C}_1$ | 7.5 | 20 | 2022 | Petrol |
| Case $\mathcal{C}_2$ | 9.5 | 15 | 2021 | Diesel |

**Assumed attribute ranges:** Price: $[5, 12]$, Mileage: $[10, 25]$, Year: $[2018, 2024]$. Fuel is binary. Weights: $w_P = 0.3$, $w_M = 0.3$, $w_Y = 0.2$, $w_F = 0.2$.

**Step A — Local similarities with $\mathcal{C}_1$:**

$$\text{sim}_{\text{Price}} = 1 - \frac{\vert 8.0 - 7.5 \vert}{12 - 5} = 1 - \frac{0.5}{7} \approx 0.9286$$

$$\text{sim}_{\text{Mileage}} = 1 - \frac{\vert 18 - 20 \vert}{25 - 10} = 1 - \frac{2}{15} \approx 0.8667$$

$$\text{sim}_{\text{Year}} = 1 - \frac{\vert 2022 - 2022 \vert}{2024 - 2018} = 1.0000$$

$$\text{sim}_{\text{Fuel}} = 1.0000 \quad \text{(Petrol = Petrol)}$$

**Step B — Global similarity with $\mathcal{C}_1$:**

$$\text{sim}(Q, \mathcal{C}_1) = \frac{0.3(0.9286) + 0.3(0.8667) + 0.2(1.0) + 0.2(1.0)}{0.3 + 0.3 + 0.2 + 0.2}$$

Breaking this down line-by-line:

$$\text{Numerator} = 0.2786 + 0.2600 + 0.2000 + 0.2000 = 0.9386$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 1.0$$

$$\therefore \; \text{sim}(Q, \mathcal{C}_1) = 0.9386$$

**Step C — Local similarities with $\mathcal{C}_2$ (Fuel differs, so this will drop):**

$$\text{sim}_{\text{Price}} = 1 - \frac{\vert 8.0 - 9.5 \vert}{7} = 1 - \frac{1.5}{7} \approx 0.7857$$

$$\text{sim}_{\text{Mileage}} = 1 - \frac{\vert 18 - 15 \vert}{15} = 1 - \frac{3}{15} = 0.8000$$

$$\text{sim}_{\text{Year}} = 1 - \frac{\vert 2022 - 2021 \vert}{6} \approx 0.8333$$

$$\text{sim}_{\text{Fuel}} = 0.0000 \quad \text{(Petrol} \neq \text{Diesel)}$$

**Step D — Global similarity with $\mathcal{C}_2$:**

$$\text{sim}(Q, \mathcal{C}_2) = 0.3(0.7857) + 0.3(0.8000) + 0.2(0.8333) + 0.2(0.0)$$

$$\text{sim}(Q, \mathcal{C}_2) = 0.2357 + 0.2400 + 0.1667 + 0.0000 = 0.6424$$

**Step E — Ranking and decision:**

$$\text{sim}(Q, \mathcal{C}_1) = 0.9386 \; > \; \text{sim}(Q, \mathcal{C}_2) = 0.6424$$

The system recommends $\mathcal{C}_1$ as the most similar case. This case is then presented to the user for critique.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Interaction Styles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **favorite 14-mark KTU question framing**: "Compare and contrast the various user interaction paradigms in case-based recommenders."

| Criterion | Query-Based | Similarity Navigation | Critique-Based | Conversational |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **User Cognitive Load** | High (must specify all) | Low (just click) | Medium (express direction) | Distributed (multi-turn) |
| **Cold-Start Suitability** | Good | Poor | Poor | Excellent |
| **Granularity of Feedback** | Coarse | Implicit | Fine-grained | Fine-grained |
| **Adaptation Need** | Low | Low | High | High |
| **Scalability to Large Bases** | High | Medium | Medium (search) | Medium |
| **System Initiative** | User-driven | User-driven | Mixed | Mixed |
| **Best Use Case** | Filters / faceted search | Browsing | Refinement | Complex domains |
| **Example System** | SQL-like filter UI | Amazon "similar to" | "Find me cheaper & lighter" | Travel planner bot |

### 3.4 Derivation of the Compound Critique Utility

When a CBR system generates dynamic critiques, it must select the critique that gives the **best trade-off between reaching new cases and avoiding redundancy** with already-seen cases.

**Step 1 — Define coverage.** The coverage of a critique $c$ is the fraction of cases in $\mathcal{B}$ that match $c$:

$$\text{Cov}(c) = \frac{\vert \mathcal{B}_c \vert}{\vert \mathcal{B} \vert}$$

*Conversion logic:* $\mathcal{B}_c$ is the subset of cases that satisfy critique $c$. If $c$ is *"cheaper by at least ₹1 Lakh"*, $\mathcal{B}_c$ is the set of cases priced at least ₹1 Lakh below the current case.

**Step 2 — Define overlap with already-displayed cases.** The overlap is the fraction of the critique's matches that the user has already seen:

$$\text{Overlap}(c) = \frac{\vert \mathcal{B}_c \cap \mathcal{B}_{\text{seen}} \vert}{\vert \mathcal{B}_c \vert}$$

**Step 3 — Compose the utility:**

$$U(c) = \text{Cov}(c) \cdot \big(1 - \text{Overlap}(c)\big)$$

*Conversion logic:* High coverage means the critique leads to many new items; low overlap means few are repeats. Their product captures the *net new information* the critique provides.

**Step 4 — Select the optimal critique.** The system picks:

$$c^* = \arg\max_{c \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{available}}} U(c)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse **coverage** with **utility**. Coverage alone is insufficient — a critique that retrieves the same cases twice is useless to the user. Hence the multiplicative penalty term $(1 - \text{Overlap})$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Aamodt–Plaza CBR Cycle (Functional Architecture)

This Mermaid block renders the canonical 4-RE cycle that governs all user interaction in case-based recommenders.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Query Q] --> B[Retrieve: Similarity Search over Case Base]
    B --> C[Top-k Most Similar Cases]
    C --> D[Reuse: Propose Solution S*]
    D --> E[Present to User]
    E --> F{User Accepts?}
    F -- No --> G[Revise: Apply Adaptation Rules based on User Critique]
    G --> H[Adapted Solution S_new]
    H --> E
    F -- Yes --> I[Retain: Store Q and S_new as new Case]
    I --> J[Updated Case Base B_new]
    J --> B
    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085
    style B fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style C fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style D fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404

    style F fill:#ffeeba,stroke:#856404
    style G fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#721c24
    style H fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#721c24
    style I fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460
    style J fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U[User] -- provides initial query --> Sys[Case-Based Recommender]
    Sys -- retrieves and presents top-k cases --> U
    U -- issues critique e.g. cheaper and lighter --> Sys
    Sys -- applies adaptation rules and re-ranks --> Sys
    Sys -- shows refined top-k --> U
    U -- accepts final case --> Sys
    Sys -- retains new case in base --> CB[(Case Base)]
    CB -- supplies stored cases --> Sys
    style U fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#383d41
    style Sys fill:#b8daff,stroke:#004085
    style CB fill:#c3e6cb,stroke:#155724
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Taxonomy of Critique Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CT[Critique Types] --> UC[Unit Critique]
    CT --> CC[Compound Critique]
    CT --> DC[Dynamic Critique]
    UC --> UC1[Modifies one attribute by fixed unit]
    UC --> UC2[Example: Cheaper by 50K]
    CC --> CC1[Modifies multiple attributes jointly]
    CC --> CC2[Example: Lighter AND cheaper]
    DC --> DC1[System-generated from case differences]
```
