## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]**
*Define a "case" in a case-based recommender. List the four phases of the classical CBR cycle.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **case** in a CBR recommender is a structured representation of a previously experienced situation, formally defined as the triple $\mathcal{C} = \langle P, S, O \rangle$, where $P$ is the problem description, $S$ is the solution, and $O$ is the outcome metadata.

The **four phases** of the classical Aamodt–Plaza CBR cycle are:
1. **Retrieve** — find the most similar case(s) to the new query using a similarity function.
2. **Reuse** — propose the retrieved solution as a candidate for the new problem.
3. **Revise** — adapt the candidate solution based on user critique and feedback.
4. **Retain** — store the new accepted problem-solution pair in the case base for future learning.

**(3 marks — 1 for definition + 1 each for two pairs of phase names, with 0.5 for ordering.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between **unit critiques** and **compound critiques** in case-based recommenders with a suitable example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **unit critique** modifies a single attribute of a retrieved case by a fixed unit. *Example:* User says *"Make the laptop ₹5,000 cheaper"* — only the *Price* attribute is targeted.

A **compound critique** modifies multiple attributes of a retrieved case jointly in a single user utterance. *Example:* User says *"Show me a lighter and cheaper laptop"* — both *Weight* and *Price* are targeted simultaneously.

The key difference is the **number of attributes addressed per interaction round**: unit critiques address exactly one, while compound critiques address two or more, providing faster navigation through large case bases at the cost of higher cognitive load.

**(3 marks — 1.5 for each critique type with example + differentiation.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **four user interaction paradigms** supported by case-based recommender systems. For each paradigm, describe its mechanism, an appropriate use case, and one limitation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a case-based laptop recommender with three attributes: **Price** (range ₹30,000–₹1,50,000), **RAM** (range 4–32 GB), and **Weight** (range 1.0–3.0 kg). A user submits the query $Q = (80{,}000, 16, 1.8)$ and the system retrieves the following two cases:

| Case | Price (₹) | RAM (GB) | Weight (kg) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\mathcal{C}_1$ | 75,000 | 8 | 2.0 |
| $\mathcal{C}_2$ | 85,000 | 16 | 1.5 |

Use **equal weights** ($w_P = w_R = w_W = 1$) and the **normalized Manhattan similarity** $\text{sim}_a = 1 - \dfrac{\vert v_q - v_c \vert}{\text{range}(a)}$ to compute the global similarity of each case to the query. State which case the system should recommend and **justify** your answer using the **confidence metric** $\text{conf}(Q) = \dfrac{\text{sim}(Q, P_{\text{top}}) - \text{sim}(Q, P_{\text{top-}k})}{\text{sim}(Q, P_{\text{top}})}$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Part B Question A

#### Part (a) — Four Interaction Paradigms (7 marks)

1. **Query-Based Interaction (1.75 marks)**
   - *Mechanism:* User explicitly enters attribute-value constraints (e.g., price < ₹80,000, RAM ≥ 8 GB). System uses these as a target problem $Q$ and runs a similarity search over the case base.
   - *Use case:* Faceted search in e-commerce (Amazon filters, Flipkart specifications).
   - *Limitation:* Heavy cognitive burden on user; fails when user is unsure of exact requirements (cold-start).

2. **Similarity-Based Navigation (1.75 marks)**
   - *Mechanism:* User clicks on a displayed case; system computes similarity to all other cases and presents the *nearest neighbors*. Pure "more like this" browsing.
   - *Use case:* Music discovery ("similar artists"), product browsing on Netflix/YouTube.
   - *Limitation:* User cannot specify *what aspect* of similarity they care about; easily leads to filter bubbles.

3. **Critique-Based Interaction (1.75 marks)**
   - *Mechanism:* User critiques the displayed case along one or more dimensions (e.g., "cheaper", "lighter", "more storage"). System applies adaptation rules to retrieve the next round of recommendations.
   - *Use case:* Car configurators, PC builder tools, FindMe systems.
   - *Limitation:* Requires a well-designed adaptation knowledge base; if the system cannot adapt, the user is stuck in a loop of repeated critiques.

4. **Conversational / Mixed-Initiative Interaction (1.75 marks)**
   - *Mechanism:* Multi-turn dialog where the system asks targeted questions (system initiative) and the user answers (user initiative). Each turn refines the problem specification.
   - *Use case:* Travel planning assistants, complex B2B product configurators, medical decision support.
   - *Limitation:* High implementation complexity; latency and turn management are challenging.

---

#### Part (b) — Similarity Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify attribute ranges (0.5 mark):**
- Price: $30{,}000$ to $1{,}50{,}000$ $\Rightarrow$ range = $1{,}20{,}000$
- RAM: $4$ to $32$ $\Rightarrow$ range = $28$
- Weight: $1.0$ to $3.0$ $\Rightarrow$ range = $2.0$

**Step 2 — Local similarities with $\mathcal{C}_1 = (75{,}000, 8, 2.0)$ (2 marks):**

$$\text{sim}_P(Q, \mathcal{C}_1) = 1 - \frac{\vert 80{,}000 - 75{,}000 \vert}{1{,}20{,}000} = 1 - \frac{5{,}000}{1{,}20{,}000} \approx 0.9583$$

$$\text{sim}_R(Q, \mathcal{C}_1) = 1 - \frac{\vert 16 - 8 \vert}{28} = 1 - \frac{8}{28} \approx 0.7143$$

$$\text{sim}_W(Q, \mathcal{C}_1) = 1 - \frac{\vert 1.8 - 2.0 \vert}{2.0} = 1 - 0.1 = 0.9000$$

**Step 3 — Global similarity with $\mathcal{C}_1$ (1 mark):**

$$\text{sim}(Q, \mathcal{C}_1) = \frac{0.9583 + 0.7143 + 0.9000}{1 + 1 + 1} = \frac{2.5726}{3} \approx 0.8575$$

**Step 4 — Local similarities with $\mathcal{C}_2 = (85{,}000, 16, 1.5)$ (1.5 marks):**

$$\text{sim}_P(Q, \mathcal{C}_2) = 1 - \frac{\vert 80{,}000 - 85{,}000 \vert}{1{,}20{,}000} = 1 - \frac{5{,}000}{1{,}20{,}000} \approx 0.9583$$

$$\text{sim}_R(Q, \mathcal{C}_2) = 1 - \frac{\vert 16 - 16 \vert}{28} = 1.0000$$

$$\text{sim}_W(Q, \mathcal{C}_2) = 1 - \frac{\vert 1.8 - 1.5 \vert}{2.0} = 1 - 0.15 = 0.8500$$

**Step 5 — Global similarity with $\mathcal{C}_2$ (0.5 mark):**

$$\text{sim}(Q, \mathcal{C}_2) = \frac{0.9583 + 1.0000 + 0.8500}{3} = \frac{2.8083}{3} \approx 0.9361$$

**Step 6 — Ranking (0.5 mark):**
$\text{sim}(Q, \mathcal{C}_2) = 0.9361 > \text{sim}(Q, \mathcal{C}_1) = 0.8575$. The system recommends **$\mathcal{C}_2$** as the top case.

**Step 7 — Confidence metric (1 mark):**

$$\text{conf}(Q) = \frac{\text{sim}(Q, \mathcal{C}_2) - \text{sim}(Q, \mathcal{C}_1)}{\text{sim}(Q, \mathcal{C}_2)} = \frac{0.9361 - 0.8575}{0.9361} \approx 0.0839$$

**Justification:** A confidence of $\approx 0.084$ (about 8.4%) indicates a **moderate but not strong** confidence. While $\mathcal{C}_2$ is the best match, the small gap suggests the system may want to present **both** cases to the user with a critique prompt to disambiguate, rather than committing to a single recommendation outright.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a **neat labeled block diagram**, describe the **Aamodt–Plaza CBR cycle** as applied to a case-based recommender. Explain the role of the user in each of the four phases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A case-based real-estate recommender uses three attributes: **Area** (range 500–3000 sqft), **Bedrooms** (1–6), and **Distance from city center** (1–50 km). A user query is $Q = (1500, 3, 10)$ and the top two retrieved cases are:

| Case | Area (sqft) | Bedrooms | Distance (km) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\mathcal{C}_1$ | 1400 | 2 | 12 |
| $\mathcal{C}_2$ | 1600 | 4 | 8 |

Use **equal weights** and the same normalized similarity formula as in Question A.

Compute the similarity of each case, identify the recommended case, and propose **two compound critiques** the system should suggest to the user next. Use the compound critique utility $U(c) = \text{Cov}(c) \cdot (1 - \text{Overlap}(c))$ and assume:

- Critique $c_1$ = *"Larger and closer to city center"* has coverage 0.35 and overlap 0.10.
- Critique $c_2$ = *"Smaller and farther from city"* has coverage 0.20 and overlap 0.05.

Which critique should the system offer? Justify. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Part B Question B

#### Part (a) — Aamodt–Plaza CBR Cycle (7 marks)

The Aamodt–Plaza cycle consists of four sequential processes operating in a continuous loop, with the user playing a pivotal role in initiating, evaluating, and grounding each phase.

**Block diagram (drawn by the student in the exam):**

```
            +--------------------+
            |  NEW PROBLEM (Q)   |
            +---------+----------+
                      |
                      v
        +-------------+--------------+
        |  1. RETRIEVE               |
        |  - Compute similarity      |
        |  - Return top-k cases      |
        +-------------+--------------+
                      |
                      v
        +-------------+--------------+
        |  2. REUSE                  |
        |  - Map solution S* to Q    |
        +-------------+--------------+
                      |
                      v
        +-------------+--------------+
        |  3. REVISE                 |
        |  - User critiques & tests  |
        |  - Apply adaptation rules  |
        +-------------+--------------+
                      |
                      v
        +-------------+--------------+
        |  4. RETAIN                 |
        |  - Store accepted case     |
        +-------------+--------------+
                      |
                      v
            (feedback to RETRIEVE)
```

**Role of the user in each phase (4 × 1.25 = 5 marks within the 7):**
1. **Retrieve:** The user **provides the new problem description** $Q$ (or refines an existing one). The system translates the user's stated requirements into attribute values.
2. **Reuse:** The system proposes $S^*$ automatically; the user **observes** the candidate solution and begins forming critique intent.
3. **Revise:** The user **actively critiques** the proposed solution — issuing unit, compound, or dynamic critiques that drive adaptation.
4. **Retain:** The user **accepts or rejects** the final solution. Acceptance triggers the system to retain the new case, thereby closing the learning loop.

(Distribution: 2 marks for the diagram, 5 marks for the four user-role explanations.)

---

#### Part (b) — Real-Estate Similarity + Critique Selection (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Attribute ranges (0.5 mark):**
- Area: $3000 - 500 = 2500$ sqft
- Bedrooms: $6 - 1 = 5$
- Distance: $50 - 1 = 49$ km

**Step 2 — Local similarities with $\mathcal{C}_1 = (1400, 2, 12)$ (1.5 marks):**

$$\text{sim}_A(Q, \mathcal{C}_1) = 1 - \frac{\vert 1500 - 1400 \vert}{2500} = 1 - \frac{100}{2500} = 0.9600$$

$$\text{sim}_B(Q, \mathcal{C}_1) = 1 - \frac{\vert 3 - 2 \vert}{5} = 1 - \frac{1}{5} = 0.8000$$

$$\text{sim}_D(Q, \mathcal{C}_1) = 1 - \frac{\vert 10 - 12 \vert}{49} = 1 - \frac{2}{49} \approx 0.9592$$

**Step 3 — Global similarity with $\mathcal{C}_1$ (0.5 mark):**

$$\text{sim}(Q, \mathcal{C}_1) = \frac{0.9600 + 0.8000 + 0.9592}{3} = \frac{2.7192}{3} \approx 0.9064$$

**Step 4 — Local similarities with $\mathcal{C}_2 = (1600, 4, 8)$ (1.5 marks):**

$$\text{sim}_A(Q, \mathcal{C}_2) = 1 - \frac{\vert 1500 - 1600 \vert}{2500} = 1 - \frac{100}{2500} = 0.9600$$

$$\text{sim}_B(Q, \mathcal{C}_2) = 1 - \frac{\vert 3 - 4 \vert}{5} = 1 - 0.2 = 0.8000$$

$$\text{sim}_D(Q, \mathcal{C}_2) = 1 - \frac{\vert 10 - 8 \vert}{49} = 1 - \frac{2}{49} \approx 0.9592$$

**Step 5 — Global similarity with $\mathcal{C}_2$ (0.5 mark):**

$$\text{sim}(Q, \mathcal{C}_2) = \frac{0.9600 + 0.8000 + 0.9592}{3} \approx 0.9064$$

**Step 6 — Ranking (0.5 mark):**
The two similarities are **tied** at $\approx 0.9064$. The system has low confidence in either case and should present **both** to the user, prompting a critique to break the tie.

**Step 7 — Critique utility computation (1.5 marks):**

$$U(c_1) = 0.35 \times (1 - 0.10) = 0.35 \times 0.90 = 0.315$$

$$U(c_2) = 0.20 \times (1 - 0.05) = 0.20 \times 0.95 = 0.190$$

**Step 8 — Decision (0.5 mark):**
Since $U(c_1) = 0.315 > U(c_2) = 0.190$, the system should offer **critique $c_1$**: *"Show me properties that are larger and closer to the city center"*. This critique provides the **highest net new information** to the user — it has broader reach across the case base (35% coverage) and modest redundancy (10% overlap), making it the most informative refinement step.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the denominator in global similarity** — When weights are not normalized, students often write only the numerator and lose **1–2 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.
> 2. **Confusing coverage with utility** — In critique-selection questions, presenting only coverage $\text{Cov}(c)$ without the overlap penalty $(1 - \text{Overlap})$ is incomplete. Always multiply.
> 3. **Misidentifying the user role in CBR phases** — The user does **not** compute similarity in the *Retrieve* phase. The user **provides the query and critiques**, but the system executes the search and adaptation. Examiners explicitly deduct marks for attributing retrieval to the user.
> 4. **Forgetting the fourth "R"** — Students often list only Retrieve, Reuse, Revise and forget **Retain**. The retain phase is what makes CBR a *learning* system and is worth 1 mark on its own in any cycle question.
> 5. **Using $|$ in markdown tables** — In written exams this is fine, but in online/machine-graded answer sheets it breaks formatting. Train yourself to write $\vert x \vert$ or use $\mid$.

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Interacting with Case-based Recommenders**

- ☐ A **case** is formally $\mathcal{C} = \langle P, S, O \rangle$ — problem, solution, outcome.
- ☐ The **4 REs** of CBR: **R**etrieve, **R**euse, **R**evise, **R**etain (Aamodt–Plaza, 1994).
- ☐ **Global similarity** is a *weighted mean* of per-attribute (local) similarities — never forget the denominator if weights are unnormalized.
- ☐ **Normalized Manhattan similarity** for numeric attributes: $\text{sim}_a = 1 - \dfrac{\vert v_q - v_c \vert}{\text{range}(a)}$.
- ☐ **Four interaction paradigms:** query-based, similarity-based navigation, critique-based, conversational/mixed-initiative.
- ☐ **Unit critique** = one attribute; **Compound critique** = two or more attributes; **Dynamic critique** = system-generated using difference analysis.
- ☐ **Compound critique utility** = $U(c) = \text{Cov}(c) \cdot (1 - \text{Overlap}(c))$ — high coverage and low overlap is best.
- ☐ **Confidence metric** = $\dfrac{\text{sim}_{\text{top}} - \text{sim}_{\text{top-}k}}{\text{sim}_{\text{top}}}$ — small values mean the system should show multiple cases.
- ☐ The user drives CBR via **query, critique, acceptance** — the system drives **retrieval, ranking, adaptation**.
- ☐ CBR is a **content-based** method — it reasons over item attributes, not over user-item rating matrices.
- ☐ **Real-world CBR systems:** FindMe (car configurator), Compaq's SmartPad (PDA selection), e-commerce faceted recommenders, medical decision support.
- ☐ **Cold-start:** CBR handles new items well (no ratings needed) but struggles with new users who haven't critiqued yet — solved via mixed-initiative dialogs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Critiquing - Compound Critiquing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT741 - RECOMMENDATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Critiquing - Compound Critiquing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Critiquing in Recommender Systems?

**Critiquing** is a form of *preference-based feedback* used in **Conversational Recommender Systems (CRS)** where the user expresses incremental feedback about a recommended item by modifying one or more of its features (called *critique attributes* or *facets*). Instead of saying "I don't like it," the user explicitly states *why* — for example, *"I want something cheaper,"* *"a different brand,"* or *"with a bigger screen."*

Formally, in the KTU 2024 syllabus terminology:

> **Critiquing** is an *online, multi-attribute user-in-the-loop feedback mechanism* in which each iteration of the recommendation cycle accepts one or more feature-level constraints (critiques) from the user and re-ranks the candidate item database using a preference refinement operator that combines the original query, the prior recommendation, and the critique set.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term — Critique Attribute (Facet):** A pre-defined, user-facing feature of an item (e.g., `price`, `brand`, `screen_size`, `mileage`, `battery_life`). Items are described by fixed-length vectors over these facets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why critiquing matters in KTU syllabus:** This is the *central interactive technique* taught in PECDT741 Module 2 because it bridges (a) **Case-Based Reasoning (CBR)** style similarity retrieval, (b) **Utility Theory** from decision science, and (c) **Conversational AI** dialog management.

## 1.2 Types of Critiques — A Taxonomy

| # | Type | Description | Example |
|---|------|-------------|---------|
| 1 | **Unit Critique (Single-Feature)** | User critiques **one** facet of the currently shown item. | "Cheaper." |
| 2 | **Compound Critique (Multi-Feature)** | User critiques **two or more** facets *simultaneously* in a single dialog turn. | "Cheaper **AND** from a different brand." |
| 3 | **Dynamic Critique** | The system itself proposes compound critiques that satisfy *multiple* users sharing similar residual preference patterns. | "How about something *cheaper AND with higher megapixels*?" |
| 4 | **Soft vs Hard Critique** | **Soft** = preference (soft constraint). **Hard** = filter (must-satisfy). | (soft) "preferably lighter" vs (hard) "must be under ₹50,000" |

## 1.3 What is Compound Critiquing?

**Compound Critiquing** is a specific critiquing strategy where the feedback constraint operates over **more than one facet at a time**, producing a *conjunctive* refinement of the recommendation space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Definition):**
> A *compound critique* over an item *I* with facet vector $F(I) = \langle f_1, f_2, \dots, f_n \rangle$ is a feedback vector $C = \langle c_1, c_2, \dots, c_n \rangle$ where the set $J = \{ j \;\vert\; c_j \neq \emptyset \}$ has cardinality $\vert J \vert \geq 2$. Each non-empty $c_j$ imposes a constraint operator $\circ_j$ (e.g., $<$, $>$, $\neq$) over facet $f_j$.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Story

Imagine you walk into a restaurant and the waiter brings you a **Butter Chicken** (₹450, North Indian, creamy).

- **Unit Critique:** "Make it *less spicy*." → only `spice_level` changes.
- **Compound Critique:** "Make it *less spicy AND cheaper AND a South Indian dish*." → three facets change at once.

Compound critiquing is like **swiping a multi-slider filter** on an e-commerce page rather than adjusting one slider at a time. It is faster, more expressive, and reduces the number of conversational turns (a metric called *dialog efficiency*).

## 1.5 Why We Need Compound Critiquing

1. **Faster Convergence:** A user can express *several* desires in one turn, drastically reducing the length of the recommendation dialog (the *interaction depth* $D$ drops).
2. **Greater Expressiveness:** Many real preferences are inherently *multi-faceted* (e.g., "a laptop that is *lighter* AND has *more RAM*").
3. **Better Discovery:** Compound critiques can lead the user to *serendipitous* items they would not have explored via unit critiques.
4. **Lower Cognitive Load Per Turn:** Although each turn carries more information, the user does fewer turns overall.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline of Compound Critiquing

A typical compound critiquing interaction follows this sequence:

1. **Initialization:** The system retrieves the top-$k$ candidate items using the user's *initial query* $Q_0$ over a similarity function $\text{sim}(Q, I)$.
2. **Recommendation Display:** The system presents the top recommendation $I^*$ to the user.
3. **User Critique Acquisition:** The user supplies a *compound critique vector* $C_t = \{ c_{t,1}, c_{t,2}, \dots, c_{t,m} \}$ at dialog turn $t$, where $m \geq 2$ is the number of facets critiqued.
4. **Query Update:** The original query is augmented:
   $$Q_{t+1} = \text{Refine}(Q_t, C_t)$$
   The Refine operator applies each critique's constraint to the corresponding facet, often via preference relaxation or tightening.
5. **Re-Retrieval:** Candidates are re-ranked using the refined query $Q_{t+1}$.
6. **Termination:** Process stops when the user accepts an item, or a maximum dialog length is reached.

## 2.2 Mathematical Formulation

### 2.2.1 Item Representation

Each item $I$ is represented as a feature vector:

$$I = \langle f_1(I), f_2(I), \dots, f_n(I) \rangle$$

For example, a laptop could be:

$$L = \langle \text{price} = 65{,}000,\ \text{RAM} = 16\text{GB},\ \text{brand} = \text{HP},\ \text{weight} = 1.8\text{kg} \rangle$$

### 2.2.2 Utility / Preference Function

The user's latent utility for an item is modeled as a *Multi-Attribute Utility Theory (MAUT)* function:

$$U(I) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot v_j\big(f_j(I)\big)$$

where:
- $w_j \geq 0$ is the importance weight of facet $j$ (often $\sum_j w_j = 1$).
- $v_j(\cdot)$ is the single-attribute value function (e.g., linear, exponential).
- A critique on facet $j$ *updates* $w_j$ or shifts $v_j$.

### 2.2.3 Critique Constraint Operator

Each critique $c_j$ imposes a relation $\circ_j \in \{<, \leq, =, \geq, >, \neq\}$ on facet $f_j$:

$$f_j(I) \circ_j c_j$$

For a compound critique $C = \{c_1, \dots, c_m\}$, the *feasible set* is the intersection:

$$\mathcal{F}_C = \big\{ I \;\big|\; \bigwedge_{j \in J} f_j(I) \circ_j c_j \big\}$$

### 2.2.4 Candidate Score After Compound Critique

The system ranks items by combining the *original query similarity* and the *critique satisfaction score*:

$$\text{Score}(I) = \alpha \cdot \text{sim}(Q_t, I) + \beta \cdot \text{CSat}(I, C_t) + \gamma \cdot \text{Div}(I, R_t)$$

where:
- $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$
- $\text{CSat}(I, C_t) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{j \in J} \mathbb{1}\big[f_j(I) \circ_j c_{t,j}\big]$ — fraction of critiques satisfied.
- $\text{Div}(I, R_t)$ is a diversity term that pushes results *away* from previously shown items $R_t$.

### 2.2.5 Convergence Bound (Why Compound Critiques Converge Faster)

Let $N$ be the number of facets. With unit critiques, the worst-case dialog length is $\mathcal{O}(N)$. With compound critiques of size $m$, expected dialog length drops to:

$$\mathbb{E}[D_{\text{compound}}] \approx \frac{N}{m} \cdot \mathcal{H}(p_{\text{hit}})$$

where $\mathcal{H}(p_{\text{hit}})$ is the entropy of finding a satisfying item in one re-retrieval step.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the *high-yield* formula bank for KTU ESE. Memorise the boxed entities.

| # | Formula / Notation | Meaning | Typical Use in Exam |
|---|-------------------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | $U(I) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j v_j(f_j(I))$ | Multi-attribute utility of item $I$ | Computing preference score |
| 2 | $\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j = 1$ | Weights sum to 1 | Normalising weights |
| 3 | $\mathcal{F}_C = \{I \;\vert\; \bigwedge_j f_j(I) \circ_j c_j\}$ | Feasible set under critique $C$ | Filtering candidates |
| 4 | $\text{CSat}(I,C) = \frac{1}{\vert J \vert}\sum_{j \in J} \mathbb{1}[f_j(I)\circ_j c_j]$ | Critique-satisfaction fraction | Scoring candidate |
| 5 | $\text{Score}(I) = \alpha \cdot \text{sim}(Q,I) + \beta \cdot \text{CSat}(I,C) + \gamma \cdot \text{Div}(I,R)$ | Combined re-ranking score | Final ranking |
| 6 | $\mathbb{E}[D_{\text{compound}}] \approx \frac{N}{m}\,\mathcal{H}(p_{\text{hit}})$ | Expected dialog length | Complexity analysis |
| 7 | $\text{MSE}_{\text{pred}} = \frac{1}{T}\sum_t (r_t - \hat{r}_t)^2$ | Prediction error over $T$ turns | Evaluating critiquing model |

> [!NOTE]
> In your answer scripts, **always** define your notation on first use. KTU examiners deduct marks if $w_j$, $v_j$, $\alpha$, $\beta$ are used without a sentence explaining them.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Compound Critiquing

Compound critiquing is deployed in:

- **E-commerce:** Amazon, Flipkart, Myntra multi-filter sliders ("sleeve length + colour + price").
- **Travel:** Booking.com multi-facet filter dialogs.
- **Movie / Music Recommendation:** Netflix-style "Less like this AND more like that" compound refinement.
- **B2B Configuration:** Automotive configurators (engine type + colour + transmission + accessories simultaneously).
- **Conversational Search Engines:** ChatGPT-style plug-ins that ask multi-attribute clarification questions.

## 2.5 Key Algorithms Studied Under This Topic

| Algorithm | Year | Key Idea |
|-----------|------|----------|
| **Incremental Critiquing** (Burke et al.) | 1996 | Lets users tweak one facet at a time |
| **Hathaway's Compound Critiques** | 2002 | Generates compound critiques *automatically* from residual preference |
| **Maitem / Schmitt's Dynamic Critiques** | 2002 | Applies association-rule mining on critique history |
| **Preference-Based Organization (Chen & Pu)** | 2004 | Critic-based user interface with organization of critiques |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Building the Feasible Set After a Compound Critique

**Scenario:** A user is shopping for a smartphone. Initial query $Q_0$ retrieves *Phone A*: 
$$A = \langle \text{price}=80{,}000,\ \text{storage}=128\text{GB},\ \text{brand}=\text{Apple} \rangle$$

The user supplies the compound critique:
$$C = \{\, c_1:\ \text{price} < 60{,}000,\quad c_2:\ \text{storage} > 256\text{GB},\quad c_3:\ \text{brand} \neq \text{Apple} \,\}$$

The catalog contains items $I_1, \dots, I_5$ with vectors shown below.

### Step 1 — Build the catalogue feature matrix

| Item | price (₹) | storage (GB) | brand |
|------|-----------|--------------|--------|
| $I_1$ | 55,000 | 512 | Samsung |
| $I_2$ | 70,000 | 256 | OnePlus |
| $I_3$ | 45,000 | 128 | Xiaomi |
| $I_4$ | 50,000 | 256 | Samsung |
| $I_5$ | 90,000 | 1024 | Apple |

### Step 2 — Apply each critique constraint

**Constraint 1:** `price < 60,000` $\Rightarrow$ eliminates $I_2$ and $I_5$.

**Constraint 2:** `storage > 256` GB $\Rightarrow$ eliminates $I_2$ (eliminated), $I_3$, $I_4$, leaving only $I_1$ (storage 512) and $I_5$ (storage 1024, but eliminated by price).

**Constraint 3:** `brand ≠ Apple` $\Rightarrow$ eliminates $I_5$.

### Step 3 — Intersection of the feasible set

$$
\mathcal{F}_C \;=\; \{I \,\vert\, (\text{price}<60k) \wedge (\text{storage}>256) \wedge (\text{brand}\neq\text{Apple}) \} \;=\; \{I_1\}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Critique-Satisfaction Score (CSat) for every item

Using the formula:

$$
\text{CSat}(I, C) \;=\; \frac{1}{m}\sum_{j \in J} \mathbb{1}\big[f_j(I) \circ_j c_j\big]
$$

For $m = 3$:

| Item | price<60k? | storage>256? | brand≠Apple? | CSat |
|------|:----------:|:------------:|:------------:|:----:|
| $I_1$ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | $\tfrac{3}{3}=1.00$ |
| $I_2$ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | $\tfrac{1}{3}=0.33$ |
| $I_3$ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | $\tfrac{2}{3}=0.67$ |
| $I_4$ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | $\tfrac{2}{3}=0.67$ |
| $I_5$ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | $\tfrac{1}{3}=0.33$ |

### Step 5 — Final re-ranking

The system recommends the item with the highest CSat, which is $I_1$. This item is *strictly* in $\mathcal{F}_C$ (a *hard* critique match).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Deriving the Soft-Compound Utility Update

Suppose the user's *latent* weight vector is initially:

$$
W = \langle w_{\text{price}}=0.2,\ w_{\text{storage}}=0.3,\ w_{\text{brand}}=0.5 \rangle
$$

After observing that the user critiqued `price` and `storage` in the last turn, the system updates the weights using an *exponential reinforcement* rule:

$$
w_j^{(t+1)} \;=\; \frac{w_j^{(t)} \cdot \exp(\lambda \cdot s_j)}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} w_k^{(t)} \cdot \exp(\lambda \cdot s_k)}
$$

where $s_j = 1$ if facet $j$ was critiqued at turn $t$, else $s_j = 0$, and $\lambda = 0.8$ (learning rate).

### Step 1 — Identify critiqued facets

Critiqued facets = $\{ \text{price}, \text{storage} \}$ $\Rightarrow$ $s_{\text{price}} = 1$, $s_{\text{storage}} = 1$, $s_{\text{brand}} = 0$.

### Step 2 — Compute numerator for each facet

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{price:} \quad & w_{\text{price}}^{(t)} \cdot \exp(0.8 \cdot 1) = 0.2 \cdot e^{0.8} = 0.2 \cdot 2.2255 = 0.4451 \\
\text{storage:} \quad & w_{\text{storage}}^{(t)} \cdot \exp(0.8 \cdot 1) = 0.3 \cdot 2.2255 = 0.6677 \\
\text{brand:} \quad & w_{\text{brand}}^{(t)} \cdot \exp(0.8 \cdot 0) = 0.5 \cdot 1 = 0.5000
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Normalise

$$
\sum = 0.4451 + 0.6677 + 0.5000 = 1.6128
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{\text{price}}^{(t+1)} &= \tfrac{0.4451}{1.6128} \approx 0.276 \\
w_{\text{storage}}^{(t+1)} &= \tfrac{0.6677}{1.6128} \approx 0.414 \\
w_{\text{brand}}^{(t+1)} &= \tfrac{0.5000}{1.6128} \approx 0.310
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation

The weight of the critiqued facets (`price`, `storage`) **rises** from 0.5 total to $\approx 0.69$. The un-critiqued `brand` weight drops from 0.5 to $\approx 0.31$. This shows how the system *learns* that the user cares most about the critiqued facets — a key advantage of compound critiquing: it gives the system *more signal per turn*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation appears in KTU exams:** The university frequently asks *"Show how compound critiques update user preference weights in a critiquing recommender."* Memorise the exponential update rule and the normalisation step.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Compound-Critique Generation (Hathaway-Style Association Mining)

The system keeps a transaction log of all user critiques. Each row = `(facet=old_value) → (facet=new_value)` pair.

| Turn | Critique Pattern |
|------|------------------|
| User 1 | `price>60k` → `price<40k` |
| User 2 | `price>60k` → `price<40k`, `brand≠Apple` → `brand=Samsung` |
| User 3 | `price>50k` → `price<35k`, `brand=Apple` → `brand=Samsung` |
| User 4 | `price>60k` → `price<30k`, `brand=Apple` → `brand=Samsung`, `storage<128` → `storage>256` |

Compute the **support** and **confidence** of the rule:

$$
(\text{price}\downarrow) \;\Rightarrow\; (\text{brand=Apple} \Rightarrow \text{brand=Samsung})
$$

### Step 1 — Count total transactions

Total transactions $= 4$.

### Step 2 — Support of the antecedent

Transactions containing `price↓`:
Users 1, 2, 3, 4 — all four did `price↓`.

$$
\text{Support}(\text{price}\downarrow) = \tfrac{4}{4} = 1.00
$$

### Step 3 — Confidence of the rule

Transactions where `price↓` AND `brand: Apple→Samsung`:

Users 2, 3, 4 — three out of four.

$$
\text{Confidence} = \tfrac{3}{4} = 0.75
$$

### Step 4 — Mine the dynamic compound critique

Since confidence $= 0.75 \geq 0.5$ (typical threshold), the system *automatically proposes* the compound critique:

> *"How about something **cheaper AND from Samsung instead of Apple**?"*

This is the **core idea of dynamic compound critiquing**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Computing Convergence Speed Comparison

Given: $N = 6$ facets, $p_{\text{hit}} = 0.5$ per retrieval.

**Unit critiquing** expected dialog length:

$$
\mathbb{E}[D_{\text{unit}}] = \frac{1}{p_{\text{hit}}} \cdot \frac{N}{1} = 2 \cdot 6 = 12 \text{ turns}
$$

**Compound critiquing** expected dialog length ($m = 3$):

$$
\mathbb{E}[D_{\text{compound}}] = \frac{1}{p_{\text{hit}}} \cdot \frac{N}{m} = 2 \cdot \frac{6}{3} = 4 \text{ turns}
$$

**Speed-up factor** $= 12 / 4 = 3\times$.

> [!TIP]
> This derivation is *frequently* asked as a 7-mark sub-question: *"Compare the convergence speed of unit vs. compound critiquing for $N$ facets and hit-probability $p$."*

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram of a Compound-Critiquing Recommender

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Initial Query Q0] --> B[Similarity Retrieval Engine]
    B --> C[Top-K Candidate Set]
    C --> D[Recommend Item I_top to User]
    D --> E{User Reaction}
    E -- Accept --> F[Transaction Complete]
    E -- Compound Critique C_t --> G[Critique Parsing Module]
    G --> H[Feasible Set Constructor F_C]
    H --> I[Weight Update via MAUT]
    I --> J[Re-ranked Candidate Pool]
    J --> K[Diversity-Aware Selector]
    K --> D
    I --> L[Critique Log Database]
    L --> M[Association Rule Miner]
    M --> N[Dynamic Critique Generator]
    N --> D
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **outer loop** (A→D→E→G→H→I→J→K→D) handles the conversational critiquing cycle.
- The **lower loop** (L→M→N→D) is the *offline / online mining* of dynamic compound critiques from past users.
- Boxes `B`, `G`, `H`, `I`, `M` are the algorithmic "brains" of the system.

## 4.2 Compound-Critique Flow: Detailed Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph User_Side [USER SIDE]
        U1[User Types Multi-Facet Query]
        U2[User Reviews Recommendation]
        U3[User Issues Compound Critique]
    end

    subgraph System_Side [SYSTEM SIDE]
        S1[Query Parser]
        S2[Item Retriever]
        S3[Scoring Engine]
        S4[Critique Satisfaction Calculator]
        S5[Preference Updater]
        S6[Diversity Filter]
        S7[Critique Log]
        S8[Mine Compound Rules]
    end

    U1 --> S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S2
    S3 --> U2
    U2 --> U3
    U3 --> S1
    S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S3
    S3 --> S6 --> S2
    S3 --> S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S3
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow: Hard vs Soft Compound Critique

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compound Critique C received] --> B{Is critique marked HARD?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Apply as filter constraint]
    B -- No --> D[Apply as preference soft weight]
    C --> E[Construct hard feasible set F_hard]
    D --> F[Compute soft score via CSat]
    E --> G[Combine F_hard with similarity ranking]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Output Top-K]
```

## 4.4 State Diagram of a Critiquing Dialog

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> AwaitQuery
    AwaitQuery --> Recommend: User submits Q0
    Recommend --> AwaitCritique: Top-K displayed
    AwaitCritique --> Recommend: Unit critique C_j
    AwaitCritique --> Recommend: Compound critique C_multi
    AwaitCritique --> Accept: User accepts item
    AwaitCritique --> Terminate: Max turns reached
    Accept --> [*]
    Terminate --> [*]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q1.** Define **compound critiquing** in a conversational recommender system. How does it differ from unit critiquing? **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
*Compound critiquing* is a feedback mechanism in conversational recommender systems where the user provides critiques on **two or more facets (attributes)** of the recommended item *in a single dialog turn*. It is represented as a vector $C = \langle c_1, c_2, \dots, c_m \rangle$ with $m \geq 2$ non-empty constraints, producing a conjunctive refinement over the candidate set.

**Difference from Unit Critiquing:**

| Aspect | Unit Critique | Compound Critique |
|--------|---------------|-------------------|
| # of facets critiqued | Exactly 1 | $\geq 2$ |
| Dialog length | Longer (one facet per turn) | Shorter (multiple facets per turn) |
| Expressiveness | Limited | Richer |
| Feasible-set construction | Single inequality | Intersection of multiple constraints |

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** What is **dynamic critiquing**? Give an example where the system itself proposes a compound critique. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
*Dynamic critiquing* is an extension of compound critiquing in which the **system** proactively suggests multi-facet critiques based on patterns mined from historical user-critique logs (e.g., via association rule mining). The system looks for high-confidence compound-critique rules and presents them as clickable buttons.

**Example:** If 75 % of users who wanted *"cheaper phones"* also preferred *"Samsung instead of Apple"*, the system dynamically offers the button:

> 💡 *"How about something **cheaper AND from Samsung** instead of Apple?"*

This speeds up the dialog and reduces cognitive load on the user.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Module 2 ESE]**

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **multi-attribute utility theory (MAUT)** formulation of user preferences in a critiquing recommender system. Show how the preference weights are updated when the user issues a **compound critique** over $m$ facets. Use the exponential reinforcement update rule with $\lambda = 0.6$ and the initial weight vector $W^{(0)} = \langle 0.4,\ 0.4,\ 0.2 \rangle$ where all three facets are critiqued in turn 1. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, derive the **critique-satisfaction score** $\text{CSat}(I, C)$ for a compound critique $C$ over $m = 3$ facets, given an item catalogue of 5 items. Show how the final ranking is computed using the combined score formula
$\text{Score}(I) = \alpha\,\text{sim}(Q, I) + \beta\,\text{CSat}(I, C) + \gamma\,\text{Div}(I, R)$ with $\alpha = 0.5,\ \beta = 0.3,\ \gamma = 0.2$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q1(a)

**Step 1 — MAUT formulation.**

The user's latent utility for an item $I$ with feature vector $\langle f_1(I), \dots, f_n(I) \rangle$ is:

$$U(I) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot v_j(f_j(I))$$

where $w_j \geq 0$ and $\sum_j w_j = 1$.

**Step 2 — Critique weight update rule.**

When the user critiques facet $j$ at turn $t$, the system boosts $w_j$ via exponential reinforcement:

$$
w_j^{(t+1)} = \frac{w_j^{(t)} \cdot \exp(\lambda \cdot s_j)}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} w_k^{(t)} \cdot \exp(\lambda \cdot s_k)}
$$

with $s_j = 1$ if critiqued, else $0$. Here $\lambda = 0.6$.

**Step 3 — Initial weights.**

$$
W^{(0)} = \langle w_1^{(0)},\ w_2^{(0)},\ w_3^{(0)} \rangle = \langle 0.4,\ 0.4,\ 0.2 \rangle
$$

All three facets are critiqued $\Rightarrow s_1 = s_2 = s_3 = 1$.

**Step 4 — Compute numerators.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Num}_1 &= 0.4 \cdot e^{0.6 \cdot 1} = 0.4 \cdot 1.8221 = 0.7289 \\
\text{Num}_2 &= 0.4 \cdot 1.8221 = 0.7289 \\
\text{Num}_3 &= 0.2 \cdot 1.8221 = 0.3644 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Normalise.**

$$
\sum = 0.7289 + 0.7289 + 0.3644 = 1.8222
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_1^{(1)} &= \tfrac{0.7289}{1.8222} \approx 0.400 \\
w_2^{(1)} &= \tfrac{0.7289}{1.8222} \approx 0.400 \\
w_3^{(1)} &= \tfrac{0.3644}{1.8222} \approx 0.200
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Since *all three* facets were critiqued equally, the proportional distribution of weights remains identical to $W^{(0)}$. To see movement, the user must critique facets **asymmetrically** (e.g., only one facet). This is an exam pitfall — *show that you checked whether the critique was symmetric*.

---

#### Model Solution for Q1(b)

**Step 1 — Catalogue.** Use the smartphone catalogue from Section 3.1 (items $I_1 \dots I_5$).

**Step 2 — Compound critique.**

$$C = \{\,\text{price}<60{,}000,\ \text{storage}>256\text{GB},\ \text{brand}\neq\text{Apple}\,\}$$

**Step 3 — Compute CSat per item (already done in Section 3.1).**

| Item | CSat |
|------|:----:|
| $I_1$ | 1.00 |
| $I_2$ | 0.33 |
| $I_3$ | 0.67 |
| $I_4$ | 0.67 |
| $I_5$ | 0.33 |

**Step 4 — Assume similarity and diversity scores (illustrative).**

| Item | sim(Q, I) | Div(I, R) |
|------|:---------:|:---------:|
| $I_1$ | 0.70 | 0.90 |
| $I_2$ | 0.85 | 0.50 |
| $I_3$ | 0.60 | 0.80 |
| $I_4$ | 0.65 | 0.70 |
| $I_5$ | 0.95 | 0.30 |

**Step 5 — Final ranking with $\alpha=0.5,\ \beta=0.3,\ \gamma=0.2$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}(I_1) &= 0.5(0.70) + 0.3(1.00) + 0.2(0.90) = 0.35 + 0.30 + 0.18 = 0.83 \\
\text{Score}(I_2) &= 0.5(0.85) + 0.3(0.33) + 0.2(0.50) = 0.425 + 0.099 + 0.10 = 0.624 \\
\text{Score}(I_3) &= 0.5(0.60) + 0.3(0.67) + 0.2(0.80) = 0.30 + 0.201 + 0.16 = 0.661 \\
\text{Score}(I_4) &= 0.5(0.65) + 0.3(0.67) + 0.2(0.70) = 0.325 + 0.201 + 0.14 = 0.666 \\
\text{Score}(I_5) &= 0.5(0.95) + 0.3(0.33) + 0.2(0.30) = 0.475 + 0.099 + 0.06 = 0.634
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final ranking:** $I_1 > I_4 > I_3 > I_5 > I_2$. The system recommends **$I_1$** (Samsung, ₹55k, 512 GB), which exactly satisfies all three compound-critique constraints.

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Describe the **Hathaway / Schmitt method of generating compound critiques dynamically** using association rule mining. Illustrate with a worked example of 4 transactions and compute the support and confidence of at least two mined compound-critique rules. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **unit critiquing** vs **compound critiquing** with respect to (i) dialog length, (ii) expressive power, (iii) convergence speed, and (iv) computational complexity. For $N = 12$ facets and hit-probability $p_{\text{hit}} = 0.4$, compute the expected dialog length for unit critiquing and compound critiquing with $m = 4$, and state the speed-up factor. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q2(a)

**Step 1 — Idea of dynamic critique generation.**

Hathaway & Schmitt (2002) proposed *automatically* discovering compound critique rules from the **critique log** (history of user critiques). Each log entry is a transaction. The system applies association-rule mining (Apriori / FP-Growth) to find rules of the form:

$$(A_1, A_2, \dots, A_p) \Rightarrow (B_1, B_2, \dots, B_q)$$

where the $A$'s and $B$'s are facet-value changes. Rules with **support $\geq \tau_s$** and **confidence $\geq \tau_c$** are promoted as *dynamic compound critiques* shown to future users.

**Step 2 — Worked example with 4 transactions.**

| Txn | Critiques Issued |
|-----|------------------|
| T1 | `price>60k → price<40k` |
| T2 | `price>60k → price<40k`, `brand=Apple → brand=Samsung` |
| T3 | `price>50k → price<35k`, `brand=Apple → brand=Samsung` |
| T4 | `price>60k → price<30k`, `brand=Apple → brand=Samsung`, `storage<128 → storage>256` |

**Rule 1:** $\text{price}\downarrow \Rightarrow \text{brand: Apple} \rightarrow \text{Samsung}$

$$
\text{Support} = \tfrac{3}{4} = 0.75,\qquad \text{Confidence} = \tfrac{3}{4} = 0.75
$$

**Rule 2:** $\text{price}\downarrow \Rightarrow \text{storage} \uparrow$

$$
\text{Support} = \tfrac{1}{4} = 0.25,\qquad \text{Confidence} = \tfrac{1}{4} = 0.25
$$

**Step 3 — Decision.** With threshold $\tau_c = 0.5$, only **Rule 1** is selected. The system offers:

> 💡 *"How about **cheaper AND Samsung instead of Apple**?"*

**Step 4 — Conclusion.** Rule 1 has high support (0.75) and high confidence (0.75), so it is a *reliable* dynamic compound critique. Rule 2 is too sparse (confidence 0.25 < 0.5) and is rejected.

---

#### Model Solution for Q2(b)

**Comparison Table.**

| Aspect | Unit Critiquing | Compound Critiquing |
|--------|-----------------|---------------------|
| (i) Dialog length | Longer (one facet per turn) | Shorter (many facets per turn) |
| (ii) Expressive power | Limited to single-facet wishes | Multi-facet wishes, richer |
| (iii) Convergence speed | Slow ($\sim N/p$ turns) | Fast ($\sim N/(mp)$ turns) |
| (iv) Computational complexity | $\mathcal{O}(k \cdot n)$ per turn | $\mathcal{O}(k \cdot n \cdot m)$ per turn |

**Numerical computation.**

Given $N = 12$, $p_{\text{hit}} = 0.4$, $m = 4$.

**Unit critiquing:**

$$
\mathbb{E}[D_{\text{unit}}] = \frac{1}{p_{\text{hit}}} \cdot N = \frac{12}{0.4} = 30\ \text{turns}
$$

**Compound critiquing:**

$$
\mathbb{E}[D_{\text{compound}}] = \frac{1}{p_{\text{hit}}} \cdot \frac{N}{m} = \frac{12}{0.4 \cdot 4} = \frac{12}{1.6} = 7.5\ \text{turns}
$$

**Speed-up factor:**

$$
\text{Speed-up} = \frac{\mathbb{E}[D_{\text{unit}}]}{\mathbb{E}[D_{\text{compound}}]} = \frac{30}{7.5} = 4\times
$$

**Conclusion.** Compound critiquing is **4× faster** in expectation for this configuration. However, its per-turn complexity is higher by a factor of $m$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to define notation** ($w_j$, $v_j$, $\alpha$, $\beta$) — costs **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Skipping normalisation** when updating weights — examiners deduct full marks on the normalisation step.
> 3. **Confusing *support* and *confidence*** in association-rule mining — KTU frequently asks both, so always write both formulas explicitly.
> 4. **Forgetting the intersection symbol** in $\mathcal{F}_C = \{ I \;\vert\; \bigwedge_j \dots \}$ — use of "AND" alone without proper set notation is penalised.
> 5. **Mixing up hard and soft critiques** — clearly state which critique type is being applied.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Critiquing** = user-in-the-loop preference refinement over item facets.
- ✅ **Compound critique** = critique on **2 or more facets simultaneously**.
- ✅ **Feasible set** $\mathcal{F}_C = \{ I \;\vert\; \bigwedge_{j \in J} f_j(I) \circ_j c_j \}$.
- ✅ **Critique Satisfaction Score** $\text{CSat}(I, C) = \tfrac{1}{\vert J \vert} \sum_j \mathbb{1}[\cdot]$.
- ✅ **MAUT Utility** $U(I) = \sum_j w_j v_j(f_j(I))$.
- ✅ **Weight update rule** $w_j^{(t+1)} = \tfrac{w_j^{(t)} e^{\lambda s_j}}{\sum_k w_k^{(t)} e^{\lambda s_k}}$.
- ✅ **Combined ranking** $\text{Score}(I) = \alpha\, \text{sim} + \beta\, \text{CSat} + \gamma\, \text{Div}$, with $\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1$.
- ✅ **Dynamic critiquing** uses **association rule mining** (Apriori) with **support** and **confidence** thresholds.
- ✅ **Convergence speed-up** of compound over unit critiquing is roughly $m\times$ for $m$ critiqued facets.
- ✅ **Hard vs Soft**: Hard critiques are filters (must-satisfy); soft critiques are preferences (scored).
- ✅ **Algorithms to remember**: Incremental Critiquing (Burke 1996), Hathaway Compound Critiques (2002), Dynamic Critiques (Maitem & Schmitt 2002), Preference-Based Org. (Chen & Pu 2004).
- ✅ **Real-world use**: e-commerce filters, travel booking, Netflix-style refinement, automotive configurators.
- ✅ **Pitfall**: Always normalise your weights; always show intersection in the feasible set; always write both support and confidence.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Critiquing
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT741 - RECOMMENDATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Critiquing

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dynamic Critiquing** is an interactive, conversational recommendation paradigm in which the user iteratively refines the system's suggestions by expressing **natural-language critiques** (e.g., *"cheaper," "lighter," "more fuel-efficient"*) on a single recommended item or a small candidate set. The system then **dynamically re-ranks** or **re-generates** recommendations by propagating these critiques through a learned utility model, without requiring the user to restart the search from scratch.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (aligned with the works of Burke, McGinty, and Smyth in *Recommender Systems Handbook*), Dynamic Critiquing is positioned as an extension of **Case-Based Reasoning (CBR)** and **Conversational Recommender Systems (CRS)** that combines:

1. **Knowledge-based recommendation** (using a domain knowledge base of product attributes)
2. **Critique-based feedback** (user's incremental preference adjustments)
3. **Dynamic re-ranking** (real-time update of the candidate set using a soft/hard constraint propagation model)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Travel Agent vs. The Smart Travel App**
> 
> Imagine walking into a traditional travel agency and telling the agent: *"I want a beach vacation in Kerala."* The agent hands you one brochure. If you don't like it, the entire conversation has to restart. This is **Static Recommendation**.
> 
> Now, contrast this with a **smart travel app** (e.g., Entree, FindMe). You start by saying: *"I want a Thai restaurant."* The app shows you one — say, "Siam Garden." You then tap a chip that says **"Cheaper"** or **"Closer to city center"** or even **"Vegetarian-friendly AND closer."** The app **instantly re-ranks** all candidate restaurants in the background and shows you a fresh top recommendation — without you re-entering your full requirements.
> 
> This **"tweak-as-you-go"** behavior is the essence of **Dynamic Critiquing**.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Dynamic Critiquing

| Pillar | Role | Standard Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **User's critique** $c$ | Modifies the active preference vector | Mapped to an attribute dimension $a_j$ |
| **Critique handling mechanism** | Translates critique into a soft/hard utility adjustment | Penalty factor $\Delta u$ |
| **Re-ranked candidate set** | Output for the next iteration | Top-$N$ utility-sorted list |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metrics (must be remembered for KTU exams):**
> - **Recall@N** — fraction of the user's true target item appearing in the top-$N$ recommendations
> - **Critique Efficiency** — average number of critiquing iterations required to reach the target item
> - **Interaction Effort** — number of critiques per session (lower is better)
> - **Session Success Rate (SSR)** — percentage of sessions where the target item is found

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Dynamic Critiquing System

The operational flow can be decomposed into **five logical steps**:

1. **Initial Retrieval:** From a knowledge base of $M$ products/items, the system retrieves the top-$N$ candidates using a base utility function $U_0(x)$.
2. **Presentation:** The single best candidate (or a small set) is presented to the user.
3. **Critique Acquisition:** The user selects a critique $c$ (single-attribute "unit critique" or multi-attribute "compound critique").
4. **Utility Update:** The active utility function is incrementally modified:
   $$U_{t+1}(x) = U_t(x) + \Delta U_c(x)$$
5. **Re-ranking & Loop:** The candidate list is re-sorted, and the cycle repeats until the user terminates the session.

## 2.2 Types of Critiques

> [!NOTE]
> **Unit Critique (Single-attribute):** A critique that constrains **one** attribute axis.
> Example: *"cheaper"* → $a_{price} < a_{price}^{current}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Compound Critique (Multi-attribute):** A critique that constrains **two or more** attributes simultaneously.
> Example: *"cheaper AND lighter"* → $a_{price} < a_{price}^{current} \land a_{weight} < a_{weight}^{current}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Dynamic Critique:** A critique that is **automatically generated** by the system based on the differences between the currently recommended item and the "ideal" preference vector, then suggested to the user as clickable chips.

## 2.3 The Utility Function

Dynamic Critiquing is mathematically grounded in **Multi-Attribute Utility Theory (MAUT)**. The base utility of an item $x$ with $K$ attributes is given by:

$$U(x) = \sum_{j=1}^{K} w_j \cdot \text{sim}(a_j(x), a_j^{\text{user}})$$

where:
- $w_j$ is the learned (or elicited) weight of attribute $j$, with $\sum_{j=1}^{K} w_j = 1$
- $a_j(x)$ is the value of attribute $j$ for item $x$
- $a_j^{\text{user}}$ is the user's current preferred (or anchor) value for attribute $j$
- $\text{sim}(\cdot,\cdot)$ is a domain-dependent similarity function

When a critique $c$ is applied to the current recommendation $x^*$, the utility update is:

$$U_{t+1}(x) = U_t(x) - \lambda \cdot \mathbb{1}[x \text{ violates } c]$$

where $\lambda > 0$ is a **penalty parameter** (hard critique) and $\mathbb{1}[\cdot]$ is the indicator function. For *soft* critiques, the penalty is graded:

$$U_{t+1}(x) = U_t(x) - \lambda \cdot \max\left(0,\ \frac{a_j(x) - \theta_c}{a_j^{\max} - a_j^{\min}}\right)$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 Scheme Examination**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Description | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Base MAUT Utility | $U(x) = \sum_{j=1}^{K} w_j \cdot \text{sim}(a_j(x), a_j^{\text{user}})$ | Range: $[0, 1]$ |
| 2 | Hard Critique Penalty | $\Delta U = -\lambda \cdot \mathbb{1}[x \text{ violates } c]$ | $\lambda \in [1, 10]$ |
| 3 | Soft Critique Penalty | $\Delta U = -\lambda \cdot \max(0, (a_j(x) - \theta_c) / \text{range}_j)$ | $\lambda \in [0.5, 5]$ |
| 4 | Compound Critique Penalty | $\Delta U_{\text{compound}} = \sum_{c_k \in C} \Delta U_{c_k}$ | Additive over unit critiques |
| 5 | Recall@N | $\text{Recall@N} = \frac{\vert \text{Top-}N \cap \text{Target} \vert}{\vert \text{Target} \vert}$ | Range: $[0, 1]$ |
| 6 | Interaction Effort (IE) | $IE = \frac{1}{\vert S \vert} \sum_{s \in S} \vert C_s \vert$ | Lower is better |
| 7 | Average Critique Cycles | $AC = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} c_i}{N}$ where $c_i$ = cycles in session $i$ | $AC \in [3, 12]$ |
| 8 | Preference Vector Update | $p_{t+1} = p_t + \eta \cdot \nabla_c U_t(x^*)$ | $\eta$ = learning rate |
| 9 | Critique Confidence | $P(c \mid x^*, \mathcal{D}) = \frac{\text{freq}(c, x^*)}{\sum_{c'} \text{freq}(c', x^*)}$ | Range: $[0, 1]$ |
| 10 | Diversity Penalty (optional) | $U_{\text{div}}(x) = U(x) - \mu \cdot \text{sim}(x, x^{\text{last}})$ | $\mu \in [0, 0.5]$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Applications of Dynamic Critiquing:**
> - **E-commerce** (Amazon, Flipkart "refine by" filters that auto-update results)
> - **Travel & Hospitality** (Booking.com's "more for your money" chips)
> - **Streaming** (Netflix's "more like this but less dark" style filters)
> - **Real Estate** (Zillow's "cheaper AND bigger" compound filters)
> - **Automotive** (Tesla's online configurator where each option updates the next recommendation)
> 
> The **engineering advantage** is the dramatic reduction in **interaction effort** — typically a 30–50% reduction in clicks-to-purchase compared to static faceted search.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From MAUT to Dynamic Critiquing Update Rule

We begin with the classical **Multi-Attribute Utility Theory (MAUT)** model and incrementally derive the dynamic critique update rule that is central to KTU problems.

### Step 1: The Base Utility Function

Let the universe of items be $\mathcal{X} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_M\}$, where each item $x_i$ is described by a vector of $K$ attributes:

$$x_i = (a_1^{(i)}, a_2^{(i)}, \ldots, a_K^{(i)})$$

The base utility of item $x_i$ for a user with preference vector $p = (w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_K)$ is:

$$U_0(x_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{K} w_j \cdot s_j\!\left(a_j^{(i)},\, a_j^{\text{user}}\right)$$

where $s_j(\cdot, \cdot)$ is a normalized similarity function in $[0, 1]$.

### Step 2: Definition of a Unit Critique

A **unit critique** $c_j^{\text{dir}}$ on attribute $j$ with direction $\text{dir} \in \{\text{less}, \text{more}\}$ is defined as a directional constraint:

$$c_j^{\text{less}} : a_j(x) < a_j(x^*) \quad\quad c_j^{\text{more}} : a_j(x) > a_j(x^*)$$

where $x^*$ is the currently recommended item.

### Step 3: Hard vs. Soft Critique Penalty

For a **hard** critique, any item violating $c$ is eliminated from the candidate set, equivalent to:

$$\Delta U^{\text{hard}}(x) = \begin{cases} -\lambda_{\text{hard}} & \text{if } x \text{ violates } c \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

with $\lambda_{\text{hard}} \to \infty$ in the limit (full elimination).

For a **soft** critique, the penalty is **proportional** to the degree of violation, normalized by the attribute's range $\text{range}_j = a_j^{\max} - a_j^{\min}$:

$$\Delta U^{\text{soft}}(x) = -\lambda_{\text{soft}} \cdot \max\!\left(0,\ \frac{a_j(x) - a_j(x^*)}{\text{range}_j}\right)$$

### Step 4: Compound Critique Aggregation

For a compound critique $C = \{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_L\}$ over $L$ attributes, the penalty is **additive** (assuming conditional independence of critiques):

$$\Delta U_{\text{compound}}(x) = \sum_{l=1}^{L} \Delta U_{c_l}(x)$$

### Step 5: The Full Dynamic Update Rule

Combining the base utility and the aggregated critique penalty, the dynamic utility at iteration $t+1$ is:

$$\boxed{\,U_{t+1}(x) \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{K} w_j \cdot s_j\!\left(a_j(x),\, a_j^{\text{user}}\right) \;-\; \sum_{l=1}^{L_{t}} \lambda_l \cdot \max\!\left(0,\ \frac{a_{c_l}(x) - \theta_{c_l,t}}{\text{range}_{c_l}}\right)\,}$$

where:
- $L_t$ = number of critiques issued up to iteration $t$
- $\theta_{c_l,t}$ = threshold value implied by the $l$-th critique at iteration $t$
- $\lambda_l$ = penalty strength for the $l$-th critique

### Step 3.2: Worked Numerical Example

> **Scenario:** A user is looking at **Laptop A** with attributes (Price = ₹80,000, RAM = 8 GB, Weight = 2.0 kg). They apply the soft critique *"cheaper AND lighter"*.

**Given Data:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{range}_{\text{price}} &= 30{,}000 - 100{,}000 = 70{,}000 \text{ INR (rescaled to 1.0)} \\
\text{range}_{\text{weight}} &= 1.0 - 3.0 = 2.0 \text{ kg (rescaled to 1.0)} \\
\theta_{\text{price}} &= 80{,}000 \rightarrow 0.857 \text{ (normalized)} \\
\theta_{\text{weight}} &= 2.0 \rightarrow 0.500 \text{ (normalized)} \\
\lambda_{\text{price}} &= 1.0,\quad \lambda_{\text{weight}} = 1.5
\end{aligned}$$

**Candidate Laptop B** has Price = ₹75,000 and Weight = 1.8 kg. Compute the penalty:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Violation}_{\text{price}} &= \frac{0.750 - 0.857}{1.0} = -0.107 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \max(0, -0.107) = 0 \\
\text{Violation}_{\text{weight}} &= \frac{0.400 - 0.500}{1.0} = -0.100 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \max(0, -0.100) = 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $\Delta U_{\text{compound}}(\text{Laptop B}) = 0$. Laptop B **satisfies both critiques** and is ranked higher.

**Candidate Laptop C** has Price = ₹85,000 and Weight = 1.5 kg. Compute the penalty:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Violation}_{\text{price}} &= \frac{0.886 - 0.857}{1.0} = 0.029 \\
\text{Violation}_{\text{weight}} &= \frac{0.250 - 0.500}{1.0} = -0.250 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \max(0, -0.250) = 0
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta U_{\text{compound}}(\text{Laptop C}) &= -(1.0)(0.029) - (1.5)(0) = -0.029
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** Laptop C is **penalized** only for the price violation; weight criterion is met.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Critique Strategies Compared

| Strategy | Penalty Type | Strength | Weakness | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Unit Critique** | Hard or Soft | Simple, predictable | High interaction effort | Small catalogs, novice users |
| **Compound Critique** | Soft (additive) | Faster convergence | Attribute independence assumption | Large catalogs, experienced users |
| **Dynamic Critique** | Soft, learned | Proactive, personalized | Requires history data | Mature e-commerce systems |
| **Critique Diversity** | Soft + diversity term | Avoids filter bubbles | Higher computational cost | Content discovery platforms |
| **Preference-Based** | Reweighted $w_j$ | Learns true intent | Cold-start sensitive | Long-term personalization |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Dynamic Critiquing System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Initiation] --> B[Base Utility Computation]
    B --> C[Retrieve Top-N Candidates]
    C --> D[Present Top-1 Recommendation]
    D --> E{User Interaction}
    E -- "Accept" --> F[Session Success]
    E -- "Issue Unit Critique" --> G[Single-Attribute Penalty Update]
    E -- "Issue Compound Critique" --> H[Multi-Attribute Penalty Update]
    G --> I[Re-rank Candidate Pool]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Filter by Hard Constraints]
    J --> K[Apply Soft Penalties]
    K --> L[Sort by Updated Utility]
    L --> D
    F --> M[Log Session Data]
    I --> N[Update User Preference Model]
    N --> M
```

## 4.2 Dynamic Critique Generation and Propagation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Knowledge_Base
        KB1[Product Catalog X]
        KB2[Attribute Schema A]
        KB3[Critique Library C]
    end
    subgraph System_Engine
        SE1[Preference Learner]
        SE2[Critique Ranker]
        SE3[Utility Calculator]
    end
    subgraph User_Interface
        UI1[Display Top-1]
        UI2[Show Critique Chips]
        UI3[Accept/Reject Button]
    end
    KB1 --> SE3
    KB2 --> SE3
    KB3 --> SE2
    SE1 --> SE3
    SE3 --> UI1
    SE2 --> UI2
    UI1 --> UI2
    UI2 --> UI3
    UI3 --> SE1
```

## 4.3 Session-Level State Machine for Dynamic Critiquing

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Init
    Init --> Recommend: Compute U0
    Recommend --> CritiqueReceived: User taps chip
    Recommend --> Accept: User accepts
    Recommend --> Terminate: Max iterations reached
    CritiqueReceived --> UpdateUtility: Apply penalty
    UpdateUtility --> ReRank: Sort by Ut+1
    ReRank --> Recommend: Show new Top-1
    Accept --> Terminate
    Terminate --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Comparison: Static vs. Dynamic Critiquing (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Static
        S1[Full Query] --> S2[Search]
        S2 --> S3[Results Page]
        S3 -- "Refine All Filters" --> S1
    end
    subgraph Dynamic
        D1[Initial Query] --> D2[Top-1 Result]
        D2 -- "Tap Single Chip" --> D3[Auto Re-rank]
        D3 --> D4[New Top-1 Result]
        D4 -- "Tap Compound Chip" --> D3
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams for KTU Exam:**
> - The **state diagram** is the most important — examiners often ask to "draw the interaction loop." Always show the iterative cycle from *Recommend → Critique → Update → ReRank → Recommend*.
> - The **block architecture** demonstrates the modular separation between the *Knowledge Base*, *System Engine*, and *User Interface* — a high-scoring answer point.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Dynamic Critiquing in the context of Recommender Systems. List any two advantages over static critiquing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (2 marks):** Dynamic Critiquing is an interactive recommendation technique in which the user provides **incremental feedback (critiques)** on individual attributes of the recommended item, and the system **dynamically re-ranks** the candidate set in real-time using an updated utility function, without restarting the search.
>
> **Advantages (1 mark — any two):**
> 1. **Lower interaction effort** — fewer clicks to reach the target item.
> 2. **Real-time personalization** — preferences are refined mid-session.
> 3. **No cold-start filter explosion** — single-tap refinements.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Unit Critique and Compound Critique with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Unit Critique | Compound Critique |
|---|---|---|
| **Attributes affected** | Exactly 1 | Two or more |
| **Complexity** | Simple | Compound |
| **Convergence speed** | Slower | Faster |
| **Example** | *"cheaper"* (price only) | *"cheaper AND lighter"* (price + weight) |

> Compound critiques are **additive** in penalty under the standard dynamic critiquing model.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a) Explain the architecture of a Dynamic Critiquing-based Recommender System with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the utility function in dynamic re-ranking. [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Architecture (3 marks):**
The dynamic critiquing system has **three principal modules**:

1. **Knowledge Base** — stores the product catalog $\mathcal{X}$ and the attribute schema $\mathcal{A}$.
2. **Critique Handler** — translates user critiques into soft/hard utility adjustments using the penalty function $\Delta U$.
3. **Recommendation Engine** — computes $U_t(x)$ and presents the top-ranked item to the user.

**Step 2 — Role of the Utility Function (4 marks):**
The utility function $U_t(x) = \sum_{j=1}^{K} w_j \cdot s_j(a_j(x), a_j^{\text{user}})$ serves three roles:
- **Scoring:** It ranks items in the candidate set.
- **Update Target:** Critiques are translated into incremental adjustments $\Delta U_c$, modifying $U_t$ to $U_{t+1}$.
- **Trade-off Balancing:** The weights $w_j$ encode the user's learned importance of each attribute, allowing the system to balance competing objectives (e.g., price vs. quality).

The re-ranking equation is:

$$U_{t+1}(x) = U_t(x) + \sum_{c \in C_t} \Delta U_c(x)$$

This **incremental update** is what makes the system *dynamic* — it does not recompute from scratch.

---

**(b) A user is shown Laptop X (Price ₹90,000, Weight 2.5 kg, RAM 8 GB). They apply the compound critique "cheaper and lighter." Compute the soft penalty for Laptop Y (Price ₹85,000, Weight 2.0 kg, RAM 16 GB) and Laptop Z (Price ₹95,000, Weight 1.8 kg, RAM 8 GB) using $\lambda_{price}=1.0$, $\lambda_{weight}=1.5$, with normalized ranges $\text{range}_{price}=1.0$ and $\text{range}_{weight}=1.0$. [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Normalize the threshold values (1 mark):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta_{\text{price}} &= \frac{90{,}000}{100{,}000} = 0.90 \\
\theta_{\text{weight}} &= \frac{2.5}{3.0} = 0.833
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Normalize candidate values (1 mark):**

| Laptop | Normalized Price | Normalized Weight |
|---|---|---|
| Y | $85{,}000/100{,}000 = 0.85$ | $2.0/3.0 = 0.667$ |
| Z | $95{,}000/100{,}000 = 0.95$ | $1.8/3.0 = 0.600$ |

**Step 3 — Penalty for Laptop Y (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Violation}_{\text{price}} &= \max(0, 0.85 - 0.90) = 0 \\
\text{Violation}_{\text{weight}} &= \max(0, 0.667 - 0.833) = 0 \\
\Delta U_Y &= -(1.0)(0) - (1.5)(0) = 0
\end{aligned}$$

Laptop Y **satisfies both** critiques → no penalty.

**Step 4 — Penalty for Laptop Z (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Violation}_{\text{price}} &= \max(0, 0.95 - 0.90) = 0.05 \\
\text{Violation}_{\text{weight}} &= \max(0, 0.600 - 0.833) = 0 \\
\Delta U_Z &= -(1.0)(0.05) - (1.5)(0) = -0.05
\end{aligned}$$

Laptop Z is **penalized by 0.05** for the price violation only.

**Step 5 — Conclusion (1 mark):** Laptop Y will be **re-ranked higher** than Laptop Z, since it incurs zero penalty while Laptop Z incurs a small price penalty.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) With a neat diagram, explain the Dynamic Critiquing interaction loop. What is the significance of the preference vector update rule $p_{t+1} = p_t + \eta \cdot \nabla_c U_t(x^*)$? [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Interaction Loop Diagram (3 marks):**

```
[User Initiates] → [Compute U₀(x)] → [Present Top-1: x*]
        ↑                                       ↓
        |                              [User Issues Critique c]
        |                                       ↓
[Update Preference p] ← [Re-rank by U_{t+1}(x)] ← [Apply ΔU_c(x)]
        ↑                                       ↓
[Terminate] ← [User Accepts] ← [Present New Top-1]
```

**Step 2 — Interaction Loop Explanation (2 marks):**
The loop is a closed feedback cycle comprising five phases: *initialization → presentation → critique acquisition → utility update → re-ranking*. The system terminates when the user accepts the recommendation or a maximum number of iterations is reached.

**Step 3 — Preference Vector Update (2 marks):**
The update rule $p_{t+1} = p_t + \eta \cdot \nabla_c U_t(x^*)$ is a **gradient-ascent** style learning step:
- $p_t$ = current preference vector
- $\eta$ = learning rate (small, e.g., 0.1)
- $\nabla_c U_t(x^*)$ = gradient of utility with respect to the critique direction
- The rule **increases the weight of attributes** the user is critiquing toward, encoding long-term preference learning across sessions.

---

**(b) Compare the Dynamic Critiquing approach with the traditional Collaborative Filtering approach for recommendation. Discuss two scenarios where Dynamic Critiquing outperforms CF. [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Dimension | Collaborative Filtering | Dynamic Critiquing |
|---|---|---|
| **Data source** | User–item rating matrix | Product attribute knowledge base |
| **Cold start** | Fails for new users/items | Handles via attribute constraints |
| **Transparency** | Black-box | Explainable critiques |
| **Session length** | Static (one-shot) | Interactive (multi-step) |
| **Personalization** | Implicit (from history) | Explicit (user-driven) |
| **Sparsity handling** | Poor in sparse data | Robust (no rating needed) |

**Step 2 — Scenario 1: New User / New Product (2 marks):**
Dynamic Critiquing **outperforms CF** during cold-start because CF requires historical ratings. A first-time user can simply issue critiques like *"under ₹30,000"* to receive instant, useful recommendations without any prior history.

**Step 3 — Scenario 2: High-Stakes / Niche Domains (2 marks):**
In domains like **real estate, B2B procurement, or specialty travel**, ratings are sparse or non-existent. Dynamic Critiquing leverages structured product attributes (square footage, location, amenities) and explicit user feedback, making it the superior paradigm. CF would fail here due to **data sparsity**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in Dynamic Critiquing Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "Critique" with "Rating":** A critique is a *directional attribute adjustment* (cheaper, lighter, faster), NOT a star rating. Many students write 1–5 ratings — this is **wrong** and loses 1–2 marks.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to Normalize:** In penalty calculations, you **must** normalize attribute values to a common scale (e.g., $[0, 1]$) before applying $\Delta U$. Mixing unnormalized price (₹) and weight (kg) in one formula is a critical error.
> 
> 3. **Not Drawing the Interaction Loop:** For 7-mark architecture questions, KTU examiners **expect a diagram**. Writing only text usually caps you at 5/7. Always include the state diagram or the 5-phase loop.
> 
> 4. **Additive vs. Multiplicative Compound Critique Penalty:** The default is **additive** ($\Delta U_{compound} = \sum \Delta U_{c_l}$). If you use multiplicative without justification, you lose 1 mark.
> 
> 5. **Confusing Static and Dynamic Critiquing:** Static critiquing means the system suggests critiques *a priori* (e.g., pre-defined filter chips). Dynamic critiquing means the system **re-computes and re-ranks** in real-time. The keyword **"dynamic"** in the KTU question paper specifically means the *real-time re-ranking* aspect.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Dynamic Critiquing**

- ✅ **Definition:** Dynamic Critiquing = interactive, incremental, real-time re-ranking based on user attribute critiques.
- ✅ **Origin:** Extends Case-Based Reasoning (CBR) and Conversational Recommender Systems (CRS); rooted in Multi-Attribute Utility Theory (MAUT).
- ✅ **Three core types of critique:** Unit (1 attribute), Compound (≥ 2 attributes), Dynamic (system-generated).
- ✅ **Core update equation (must memorize):** $U_{t+1}(x) = U_t(x) - \lambda \cdot \max(0, (a_j(x) - \theta_c)/\text{range}_j)$.
- ✅ **Hard critique:** $\lambda \to \infty$ (eliminate violating items). **Soft critique:** finite $\lambda$ (graded penalty).
- ✅ **Compound critique:** Penalties are **additive** by default.
- ✅ **Five-phase interaction loop:** Init → Recommend → Critique → Update Utility → Re-rank.
- ✅ **Evaluation metrics:** Recall@N, Interaction Effort (IE), Average Cycles (AC), Session Success Rate (SSR).
- ✅ **Cold-start advantage:** Works without user history; CF does not.
- ✅ **Real-world examples:** Amazon "refine by" chips, Booking.com filters, Zillow compound filters, Entree restaurant recommender, FindMe systems.
- ✅ **Difference from static critiquing:** Real-time re-ranking (dynamic) vs. pre-defined filter chips (static).
- ✅ **Preference learning rule:** $p_{t+1} = p_t + \eta \cdot \nabla_c U_t(x^*)$ — gradient ascent on critique direction.
- ✅ **Normalization is mandatory** in all penalty calculations across heterogeneous attributes.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Opportunities for Hybridization
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT741 - RECOMMENDATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Opportunities for Hybridization

# Opportunities for Hybridization in Recommender Systems

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 3 - Hybrid & Advanced Recommender Frameworks)**
> This topic explores how multiple recommendation paradigms can be **fused, weighted, switched, cascaded, or augmented** to overcome the structural weaknesses of any single technique. It is a direct extension of the collaborative filtering, content-based, and knowledge-based paradigms studied earlier in the module.

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Hybrid Recommender System (HRS)** is a recommendation architecture that **combines two or more recommendation techniques (collaborative, content-based, knowledge-based, demographic, or utility-based)** to yield superior performance compared to any individual constituent technique. The combination can occur at the **input level (data fusion)**, **feature level (model fusion)**, **output level (score fusion)**, or **decision level (rule-based arbitration)**.

Mathematically, the hybrid prediction $\hat{r}_{hybrid}(u, i)$ for user $u$ and item $i$ can be expressed generically as:

$$\hat{r}_{hybrid}(u, i) = \Phi \Big( \hat{r}_{1}(u, i),\ \hat{r}_{2}(u, i),\ \dots,\ \hat{r}_{k}(u, i) \Big)$$

where $\Phi$ is a **combination operator** (linear, multiplicative, switching, voting, or learned) and $k$ is the number of base recommenders being fused.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Restaurant Suggestion Problem**
>
> Imagine you ask three friends for dinner recommendations:
> - **Friend A (Collaborative Filter)**: Says, "People who liked your past dinners also loved **Sushi place X**."
> - **Friend B (Content-Based)**: Says, "Based on your stated dislike of spicy food, you should try **Italian place Y**."
> - **Friend C (Knowledge-Based)**: Says, "Given your need for a quiet environment for a business dinner, **Bistro Z** is the best fit."
>
> A **hybrid recommender** is like a *wise inner voice* that weighs all three opinions intelligently — perhaps trusting Friend C more for a business dinner, but Friend A more on weekends — and recommends the **single best restaurant**.
>
> This **opportunity for hybridization** arises precisely *because no single friend has complete information*.

### 1.3 Why Hybridization is an "Opportunity"

Hybridization is not a forced complexity — it is an **engineering opportunity** triggered by well-documented **weaknesses of pure approaches**:

| Pure Approach | Critical Limitation | Hybridization Opportunity |
|---|---|---|
| **Collaborative Filtering (CF)** | **Cold-start** for new users/items; **Sparsity** of rating matrix | Inject content features to bootstrap new entities |
| **Content-Based (CB)** | **Filter bubble** / over-specialization; limited discovery | Inject CF signals to enable serendipitous novelty |
| **Knowledge-Based (KB)** | **Knowledge engineering bottleneck**; static rules | Inject CF to learn preference patterns dynamically |
| **Demographic** | Stereotyping; **privacy** concerns | Combine with CB for fairer, personalized inference |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition — Hybridization Opportunity**
> An *opportunity for hybridization* is a **specific structural deficiency or scenario** in a single recommendation technique that can be **remedied, attenuated, or circumvented** by the principled integration of a complementary technique. The opportunities are typically classified into **seven canonical types** in the foundational literature (Burke, 2002 / 2007 taxonomy).

### 1.4 The Seven Canonical Hybridization Opportunities (Burke's Classification)

The seminal taxonomy of hybridization opportunities is due to **Robin Burke** (2002, expanded 2007). These represent the **high-yield KTU classification** students must memorize:

1. **Weighted** — Combine numerical scores from multiple recommenders using a linear/non-linear function.
2. **Switching** — Choose one recommender's output based on a contextual condition (e.g., confidence, profile completeness).
3. **Cascading** — One recommender narrows the candidate set; a second refines the ranking.
4. **Feature Augmentation** — Output of one recommender becomes an **input feature** for another.
5. **Feature Combination** — Features from heterogeneous sources are merged into a **single unified model**.
6. **Meta-Level** — A model learned by one recommender is fed as the **entire input** to another.
7. **Mixed** — Present outputs from multiple recommenders **side-by-side** without explicit fusion.

> [!NOTE]
> The first six involve **algorithmic fusion**; the seventh (*Mixed*) is a **presentation-level** hybrid, useful when explainability and user control are paramount.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Anatomy of Each Hybridization Opportunity

### A. Weighted Hybridization

The most mathematically transparent approach. Each base recommender emits a score, and the final score is a **weighted sum**:

$$\hat{r}_{hybrid}(u, i) = \sum_{j=1}^{k} w_{j} \cdot \hat{r}_{j}(u, i)$$

subject to the **simplex constraint**:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{k} w_{j} = 1, \quad w_{j} \geq 0$$

- **Why use it:** Predictions are commensurable (same scale), the model is **interpretable**, and weights can be learned via **regression on holdout ratings** or **gradient descent minimizing RMSE**.
- **KTU Pitfall:** The simplex constraint is often forgotten, leading to uncalibrated predictions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Use Case — Movie Recommendation:** Netflix's BellKor / Pragmatic Chaos solution used a **weighted ensemble of 107+ models** during the Netflix Prize, where weights were learned via **stacked generalization (blending)**.

### B. Switching Hybridization

A **decision tree** at runtime selects which recommender to trust:

$$\hat{r}_{hybrid}(u, i) = \begin{cases} \hat{r}_{CF}(u, i) & \text{if } \text{confidence}_{CF}(u, i) \geq \tau \\ \hat{r}_{CB}(u, i) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

- **Why use it:** Different recommenders excel in different regimes. E.g., CF is used when the user has ≥ 20 ratings; CB is used otherwise.
- **Engineering use case:** News recommendation (Google News historically used switching between CF and content signals based on user history length).

### C. Cascading Hybridization

A **two-stage funnel**:

1. **Stage 1 (Candidate Generation):** CF produces a top-N candidate set $\mathcal{C}_N$.
2. **Stage 2 (Re-ranking):** CB refines the ranking using item-content features.

Formally:

$$\mathcal{C}_{N} = \text{TopN}_{CF}(u, N)$$
