An **Evaluation Paradigm** in recommendation systems is a systematic methodology used to measure the quality of a recommendation algorithm along dimensions such as accuracy, coverage, diversity, novelty, and business impact.

The three principal evaluation paradigms are:

1. **Offline Evaluation** — Uses pre-collected historical data to measure predictive accuracy metrics like MAE, RMSE, NDCG, and MAP.
2. **User Studies (Lab Experiments)** — Recruits a small set of users to interact with the system under controlled conditions and capture qualitative feedback via surveys.
3. **Online Evaluation (A/B Testing)** — Deploys the algorithm to a subset of real users in production and measures business KPIs like CTR, conversion, and retention.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Offline and Online evaluation in terms of cost, scale, and what they measure. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Offline Evaluation | Online Evaluation |
|---|---|---|
| **Cost** | Low (no real users, runs on historical logs) | High (real users, production risk) |
| **Scale** | Millions of users can be simulated | Limited by live traffic and experiment duration |
| **Data Source** | Pre-collected historical interactions | Real-time user behavior |
| **Metrics** | MAE, RMSE, NDCG, MAP, Coverage | CTR, Conversion, Retention, Revenue |
| **Reproducibility** | Fully reproducible | Affected by seasonality, trends |
| **Bias** | Suffers from selection bias (data from old system) | Suffers from novelty effect and network effects |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the three main evaluation paradigms in Recommendation Systems with their **strengths and weaknesses** in detail. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Discuss why **no single evaluation paradigm is sufficient** for comprehensive assessment of a recommender system. Illustrate with a real-world example. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

The three evaluation paradigms in Recommendation Systems are:

**1. Offline Evaluation**

- **Definition:** Uses historical data to evaluate a recommendation model. The dataset is split into training and test portions.
- **Strengths:** Reproducible, fast, cheap, allows large-scale hyperparameter tuning, supports many metrics.
- **Weaknesses:** Suffers from **selection bias** (data was generated by a different system), cannot measure user satisfaction, cannot capture long-term effects such as retention or filter bubbles.

**2. User Studies (Lab Experiments)**

- **Definition:** A small group of users (20–200) is recruited to interact with the system and provide feedback.
- **Strengths:** Captures **subjective qualities** such as trust, satisfaction, and perceived serendipity. Allows controlled comparison of UI variants.
- **Weaknesses:** **Expensive**, time-consuming, small sample size leads to statistical noise, and the **Hawthorne effect** (participants behave differently when observed) can bias results.

**3. Online Evaluation (A/B Testing)**

- **Definition:** Real users are randomly split into buckets; each bucket sees a different algorithm. Business KPIs are measured.
- **Strengths:** Captures **true user behavior** in the wild. Reflects causality and real business outcomes. Gold standard for ship decisions.
- **Weaknesses:** Highest cost; a bad model can damage revenue. Requires mature logging infrastructure. The **peeking problem** inflates false-positive rates.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Why no single paradigm is sufficient:**

A recommendation system must satisfy multiple stakeholders:

1. **Users** want accurate, diverse, novel, and trustworthy recommendations.
2. **The platform** wants high click-through rate, conversion, and retention.
3. **Content providers** want fair exposure of their items.

A single paradigm captures only a slice of this:

- **Offline alone** might rank a "popularity-based" algorithm as the best, but it offers zero personalization, which user studies would reject.
- **User studies alone** are too small to detect long-term retention effects.
- **Online alone** is too costly to test every algorithm variant.

**Real-World Example — Netflix:**

Netflix uses a **hybrid evaluation funnel**. They run thousands of algorithms **offline** using RMSE and NDCG on historical watch logs. The top few are tested in **user studies** where viewers rate recommendation quality. Finally, the top 2 are deployed in **A/B tests** measuring watch time and take-rate. Only the winning variant is rolled out to all users. This three-stage paradigm ensures both *technical accuracy* and *real-world business success*.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** What is **NDCG**? Derive the formula for NDCG@K step-by-step and explain why normalization by IDCG is necessary. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A user has 4 relevant items with graded relevance $\{3, 2, 2, 0\}$ in the ideal order. The recommender system returns the top-4 items in the order: $\{rel_{1}=2, rel_{2}=3, rel_{3}=0, rel_{4}=2\}$. Compute the **DCG@4, IDCG@4, and NDCG@4** for this user. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition of NDCG:**

**Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (NDCG)** is a ranking-quality metric that measures how well a recommender system orders items by their graded relevance, with the constraint that highly relevant items appearing earlier in the list contribute more to the score.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Cumulative Gain (CG).** Sum the relevance scores in the top-K list:

$$
CG@K = \sum_{k=1}^{K} rel_{k}
$$

CG does not account for the rank of relevant items.

**Step 2 — Discounted Cumulative Gain (DCG).** Apply two modifications:
- **Position discount:** Divide by $\log_{2}(k+1)$ so that later ranks count less.
- **Exponential gain:** Use $2^{rel_{k}} - 1$ to amplify high-relevance items:

$$
DCG@K = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{2^{rel_{k}} - 1}{\log_{2}(k+1)}
$$

**Step 3 — Normalize via the Ideal ordering (IDCG).** Compute the DCG if items were perfectly ordered by relevance:

$$
IDCG@K = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{2^{rel^{*}_{k}} - 1}{\log_{2}(k+1)}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute NDCG.** Divide the actual DCG by the ideal DCG:

$$
NDCG@K = \frac{DCG@K}{IDCG@K}
$$

**Why Normalization is Necessary:**

DCG has no upper bound — a user with 100 highly relevant items would have DCG = 100, while a user with 2 relevant items would have DCG = 2. This makes DCG incomparable across users. Dividing by IDCG rescales the score to $[0, 1]$, where:

- $NDCG = 1$ → Perfect ranking (matches the ideal order).
- $NDCG = 0$ → All items are irrelevant.

This makes NDCG a *user-independent*, *comparable*, and *interpretable* metric.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Compute DCG@4 for the system's ranking $\{2, 3, 0, 2\}$.**

$$
DCG@4 = \frac{2^{2} - 1}{\log_{2}(2)} + \frac{2^{3} - 1}{\log_{2}(3)} + \frac{2^{0} - 1}{\log_{2}(4)} + \frac{2^{2} - 1}{\log_{2}(5)}
$$

$$
DCG@4 = \frac{3}{1} + \frac{7}{1.585} + \frac{0}{2} + \frac{3}{2.322}
$$

$$
DCG@4 \approx 3.000 + 4.416 + 0.000 + 1.292 = 8.708
$$

**Step 2 — Compute IDCG@4 for the ideal ranking $\{3, 2, 2, 0\}$.**

$$
IDCG@4 = \frac{2^{3} - 1}{\log_{2}(2)} + \frac{2^{2} - 1}{\log_{2}(3)} + \frac{2^{2} - 1}{\log_{2}(4)} + \frac{2^{0} - 1}{\log_{2}(5)}
$$

$$
IDCG@4 = \frac{7}{1} + \frac{3}{1.585} + \frac{3}{2} + \frac{0}{2.322}
$$

$$
IDCG@4 \approx 7.000 + 1.893 + 1.500 + 0.000 = 10.393
$$

**Step 3 — Compute NDCG@4.**

$$
NDCG@4 = \frac{DCG@4}{IDCG@4} = \frac{8.708}{10.393} \approx 0.838
$$

**Interpretation:** The recommender achieved 83.8% of the ideal ranking quality. The system swapped the first two items (rel=2 before rel=3) but still placed all relevant items within the top 4, so the loss is moderate.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing DCG with NDCG:** DCG is unbounded; NDCG is in $[0, 1]$. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you forget to divide by IDCG.
> 2. **Using $\log$ instead of $\log_{2}$:** KTU expects base-2 logarithm. Wrong base = 2 marks deducted.
> 3. **Forgetting the exponential gain:** Writing $DCG = \sum rel_{k} / \log_{2}(k+1)$ is the *unweighted* version. The full NDCG uses $2^{rel_{k}} - 1$.
> 4. **Rounding too early:** Carry at least 4 decimal places during intermediate steps to avoid compounding error.
> 5. **Confusing Precision@K with Recall@K:** Precision divides by $K$ (recommendation list size); Recall divides by $\vert T(u) \vert$ (total relevant count). Mixing these up is a common 1-mark loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Evaluation Paradigms**

- **Three Paradigms:** Offline (cheap, fast, biased), User Study (subjective, costly), Online (definitive, expensive).
- **Offline Metrics Cheat Sheet:**
  - Rating prediction: $MAE$, $RMSE$ (RMSE penalizes large errors more)
  - Ranking quality: $Precision@K$, $Recall@K$, $F1@K$, $NDCG@K$, $MAP@K$, $MRR$, $Hit Rate@K$
  - Beyond accuracy: $Coverage$, $Diversity$, $Novelty$, $Serendipity$
- **NDCG Formula (must memorize):**
  $NDCG@K = \frac{\sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{2^{rel_{k}} - 1}{\log_{2}(k+1)}}{\sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{2^{rel^{*}_{k}} - 1}{\log_{2}(k+1)}}$
- **Industry Funnel:** Offline (thousands) → User Study (top 5) → Online A/B (top 2) → Production (winner).
- **Selection Bias:** Offline evaluation always measures *how well the new system mimics the old system*, not how well it serves users.
- **Hawthorne Effect:** Users in lab studies behave differently when observed.
- **Peeking Problem:** Checking online A/B results before the experiment ends inflates false-positive rates — use sequential testing or fixed-horizon analysis.
- **Long-term vs Short-term:** Offline and short user studies miss **long-term retention effects** that only online evaluation can capture.
- **Exam Pattern Recognition:**
  - 3-mark questions → definitions and one-line comparisons.
  - 7-mark sub-questions → strengths/weaknesses tables or metric derivations.
  - 7-mark numerical sub-questions → NDCG or Precision/Recall calculations.
- **Common Mistake:** Saying "Offline evaluation is the most accurate." It is the most *reproducible*, not the most *accurate in reflecting user behavior*.
- **Key Phrase to Use in Answers:** *"Evaluation in recommendation systems is inherently multi-faceted; offline, user, and online paradigms are complementary, not interchangeable."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: User Studies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT741 - RECOMMENDATION SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** User Studies # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **User Studies** in the context of **Recommender Systems (RS)** refer to a class of **user-centric evaluation methodologies** that assess the quality, effectiveness, and acceptance of a recommender algorithm by directly measuring, observing, or eliciting feedback from **real end-users** (or representative samples). Unlike offline metrics (precision, recall, RMSE), user studies focus on **perceptual, cognitive, behavioral, and affective responses** of users interacting with the system. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, user studies sit at the apex of the **evaluation hierarchy** of recommender systems, bridging the gap between **algorithmic performance** and **Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)** outcomes. ### The Three-Tier Evaluation Pyramid (KTU Framework) 1. **Offline Evaluation** → Historical data, no human in the loop (e.g., RMSE, MAE, Precision@K). 2. **Online Evaluation** → Live system, implicit feedback (e.g., **Click-Through Rate (CTR)**, conversion). 3. **User Studies** → Explicit, controlled observation of human subjects (e.g., surveys, lab experiments, **A/B testing with questionnaires**, eye-tracking). $$ \text{User Study} \;\supset\; \text{Subjective Metrics} \;\cup\; \text{Behavioral Observations} \;\cup\; \text{Cognitive Load Measures} $$ ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Chef's Tasting Panel" > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** Imagine a chef who has built a new menu using a recipe algorithm (the *recommender*). Before launching it to the public, the chef invites a **focus group** (a small sample of customers) to taste each dish and fill out a feedback form rating flavor, presentation, and satisfaction. The chef also observes which dishes customers return for, which they leave unfinished, and how long they deliberate before ordering. This **structured, human-centered study** is exactly what a *user study* is in recommender systems — a controlled experiment that captures what numbers alone cannot. Just as a chef cannot judge a dish by its calorie count alone, a recommender cannot be judged only by RMSE. **Users perceive diversity, serendipity, trust, and explanation quality** — qualities invisible to offline metrics but captured in user studies. ## 1.3 Why User Studies Are Indispensable (KTU High-Yield Point) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Why are user studies needed when offline metrics already exist?"* The standard answer must include the following **four justifications**: | # | Justification | Engineering Implication | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Offline metrics ignore user intent** — a low RMSE may still recommend irrelevant items if the user's context changes. | Captures *situated cognition*. | | 2 | **User trust and satisfaction are latent variables** that only humans can self-report. | Drives long-term retention. | | 3 | **Novelty, serendipity, and diversity** are inherently subjective. | Improves engagement beyond accuracy. | | 4 | **Cold-start and bias issues** may be hidden in offline logs but exposed in live interaction. | Reveals fairness, filter-bubble effects. | ## 1.4 Core Constructs Measured in User Studies - **Perceived Accuracy (PA)** — Subjective rating of recommendation relevance. - **User Satisfaction (US)** — Holistic post-session score. - **Trust & Transparency** — User's confidence in the system, modulated by explanations. - **Cognitive Effort** — Time-to-decision, number of interactions. - **Engagement & Stickiness** — Session length, return rate. - **Choice Difficulty / Over-choice** — When too many recommendations paralyze the user. --- # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Taxonomy of User Study Methodologies (KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 requires students to **classify and justify** the choice of user study design. The three dominant designs are: ### A. Laboratory Experiments (Within-Subjects / Between-Subjects) - **Setup:** Participants are brought into a controlled environment and asked to perform tasks (e.g., "find a movie to watch tonight") with one or more recommenders. - **Strengths:** High internal validity; can isolate variables (explanation on/off, algorithm A vs. B). - **Weaknesses:** Low ecological validity — users may not behave naturally. ### B. Field Studies & Longitudinal A/B Testing - **Setup:** A randomly selected fraction of live users receives Variant A (control), another receives Variant B (treatment). Engagement is logged for weeks/months. - **Strengths:** High external validity; reflects real behavior. - **Weaknesses:** Costly, ethical concerns (IRB approval), susceptible to novelty effects. ### C. Crowd-Sourced User Studies (e.g., Amazon Mechanical Turk, Prolific) - **Setup:** Online participants complete HCI tasks and questionnaires remotely. - **Strengths:** Fast, large sample, diverse demographics. - **Weaknesses:** Self-selection bias, low engagement, demographic skew. ## 2.2 User-Centric Metrics — The KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > Below is the **complete high-yield formula table** you must memorize for the KTU ESE. Note: I have used `\vert` and `\mid` in place of literal pipe characters to preserve table syntax. | Metric | Formula | Range / Unit | Interpretation | |---|---|---|---| | **Precision@K** | $P@K = \dfrac{\vert \mathcal{R}_{rel} \cap \mathcal{R}_{topK} \vert}{K}$ | $[0,1]$ | Fraction of top-K that are relevant. | | **Recall@K** | $R@K = \dfrac{\vert \mathcal{R}_{rel} \cap \mathcal{R}_{topK} \vert}{\vert \mathcal{R}_{rel} \vert}$ | $[0,1]$ | Fraction of all relevant items found. | | **F1@K** | $F1@K = \dfrac{2 \cdot P@K \cdot R@K}{P@K + R@K}$ | $[0,1]$ | Harmonic mean of P and R. | | **NDCG@K** | $NDCG@K = \dfrac{DCG@K}{IDCG@K},\;\; DCG@K = \sum_{i=1}^{K}\dfrac{2^{rel_i}-1}{\log_2(i+1)}$ | $[0,1]$ | Position-aware graded relevance. | | **MRR** | $MRR = \dfrac{1}{\vert Q \vert}\sum_{q=1}^{\vert Q \vert}\dfrac{1}{rank_q}$ | $[0,1]$ | Mean Reciprocal Rank of first hit. | | **CTR** | $CTR = \dfrac{\#Clicks}{\#Impressions}$ | $[0,1]$ | Implicit engagement proxy. | | **Conversion Rate** | $CR = \dfrac{\#Conversions}{\#Impressions}$ | $[0,1]$ | Business-value proxy. | | **User Satisfaction Score (USS)** | $USS = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}s_i,\;\; s_i \in [1,5]$ | $[1,5]$ | Mean Likert rating. | | **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** | $NPS = \%Promoters - \%Detractors$ | $[-100, +100]$ | Loyalty/advocacy metric. | | **System Usability Scale (SUS)** | $SUS = 2.5 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{10}(q_{odd,i}-1) + (5-q_{even,i})$ | $[0,100]$ | Standardized usability. | | **Diversity (Intra-List)** | $Diversity = 1 - \dfrac{2}{K(K-1)}\sum_{i [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 frequently tests the **A/B test sample-size formula** in Part B derivations. The standard two-proportion test formula is: $$ n = \dfrac{\left(Z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{2\bar{p}\bar{q}} + Z_{\beta}\sqrt{p_1q_1 + p_2q_2}\right)^2}{(p_1 - p_2)^2} $$ where: - $p_1, p_2$ = true CTR/conversion rates of treatment and control. - $\bar{p} = \dfrac{p_1+p_2}{2}$, $\bar{q} = 1 - \bar{p}$. - $Z_{\alpha/2}$ = critical value (e.g., **1.96** for 95% confidence, $\alpha = 0.05$). - $Z_{\beta}$ = power critical value (e.g., **0.84** for 80% power, $\beta = 0.20$). - $n$ = required sample size **per group**. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility User-study-driven recommender redesign has produced industry-defining results: - **Netflix** — Discovered through user studies that *diversity of the top-10 row* mattered more than a 5% RMSE improvement, leading to row-level diversification. - **Spotify** — A/B tests showed that **explanation carousels** ("Because you listened to X") raised perceived trust scores by 18%, even when click-rate was unchanged. - **Amazon** — User studies on "Customers who bought…" widgets identified *over-choice fatigue* when >7 items were shown, leading to a fixed-K=5 cap. --- # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — NDCG@K and User-Satisfaction Correlation > [!NOTE] > This is a **typical 14-mark derivation** KTU sets. Follow it step-by-step. ### Problem Statement A streaming recommender produces the following ranked list of 5 movies for a user, with graded relevance $rel_i \in \{0,1,2,3\}$ (3 = highly relevant): | Rank $i$ | Movie | $rel_i$ | |---|---|---| | 1 | A | 3 | | 2 | B | 0 | | 3 | C | 2 | | 4 | D | 1 | | 5 | E | 2 | The user then gives a satisfaction score of $s = 4$ out of 5. A second user receives a list $[C, A, B, D, E]$ and gives $s' = 3$. Compute the NDCG@5 for both users, and the **mean NDCG@5** as a proxy for perceived quality. ### Step 1 — Compute DCG@5 for User 1 $$ DCG@5 = \sum_{i=1}^{5}\frac{2^{rel_i}-1}{\log_2(i+1)} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} DCG@5 &= \frac{2^{3}-1}{\log_2 2} + \frac{2^{0}-1}{\log_2 3} + \frac{2^{2}-1}{\log_2 4} + \frac{2^{1}-1}{\log_2 5} + \frac{2^{2}-1}{\log_2 6} \\ &= \frac{7}{1} + \frac{0}{\log_2 3} + \frac{3}{2} + \frac{1}{\log_2 5} + \frac{3}{\log_2 6} \end{aligned} $$ Now compute the logarithmic denominators: - $\log_2 2 = 1.0000$ - $\log_2 3 = 1.5850$ - $\log_2 4 = 2.0000$ - $\log_2 5 = 2.3219$ - $\log_2 6 = 2.5850$ Substituting: $$ \begin{aligned} DCG@5 &= \frac{7}{1.0000} + \frac{0}{1.5850} + \frac{3}{2.0000} + \frac{1}{2.3219} + \frac{3}{2.5850} \\ &= 7.0000 + 0.0000 + 1.5000 + 0.4307 + 1.1605 \\ &= 10.0912 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Compute IDCG@5 (Ideal DCG) for User 1 The **ideal ordering** sorts by $rel_i$ descending: $[3, 2, 2, 1, 0]$. $$ \begin{aligned} IDCG@5 &= \frac{2^{3}-1}{1} + \frac{2^{2}-1}{\log_2 3} + \frac{2^{2}-1}{2} + \frac{2^{1}-1}{\log_2 5} + \frac{2^{0}-1}{\log_2 6} \\ &= 7.0000 + \frac{3}{1.5850} + \frac{3}{2.0000} + \frac{1}{2.3219} + \frac{0}{2.5850} \\ &= 7.0000 + 1.8927 + 1.5000 + 0.4307 + 0.0000 \\ &= 10.8234 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Compute NDCG@5 for User 1 $$ NDCG@5_{(1)} = \frac{DCG@5}{IDCG@5} = \frac{10.0912}{10.8234} = 0.9324 $$ ### Step 4 — Compute NDCG@5 for User 2 (List = [C, A, B, D, E], rel = [2,3,0,1,2]) $$ \begin{aligned} DCG@5_{(2)} &= \frac{2^{2}-1}{1} + \frac{2^{3}-1}{\log_2 3} + \frac{2^{0}-1}{2} + \frac{2^{1}-1}{\log_2 5} + \frac{2^{2}-1}{\log_2 6} \\ &= 3.0000 + 4.4143 + 0.0000 + 0.4307 + 1.1605 \\ &= 9.0055 \end{aligned} $$ IDCG is unchanged (same relevance multiset), so $IDCG@5 = 10.8234$. $$ NDCG@5_{(2)} = \frac{9.0055}{10.8234} = 0.8320 $$ ### Step 5 — Mean NDCG@5 and Correlation Check $$ \overline{NDCG@5} = \frac{0.9324 + 0.8320}{2} = 0.8822 $$ User 1 (NDCG = 0.93) reported satisfaction 4/5; User 2 (NDCG = 0.83) reported 3/5. The **monotonic agreement** confirms that NDCG@K is a reasonable — but not perfect — proxy for user satisfaction in user studies. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which User Study > [!IMPORTANT] > This decision matrix is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question** ("Compare and contrast lab experiments, field studies, and crowd-sourced studies"). | Dimension | Lab Experiment | Field A/B Test | Crowd-Sourced | |---|---|---|---| | **Internal Validity** | Very High | Medium | Medium | | **External Validity** | Low | Very High | Medium | | **Sample Size** | Small (20–100) | Large (10k+) | Medium (200–1000) | | **Cost** | High (lab, equipment) | Medium (infra) | Low (per-task pay) | | **Time to Result** | Days | Weeks–Months | Hours–Days | | **Demographic Diversity** | Low | High | Medium–High | | **Risk of Bias** | Demand characteristics | Novelty effect, selection | Self-selection, low effort | | **Best Used For** | Explanation, trust, UI design | Algorithm comparison at scale | Concept validation, surveys | | **Ethical Risk** | Low (IRB easy) | Medium (live users) | Medium (informed consent) | --- # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End User Study Pipeline > [!NOTE] > The Mermaid diagram below maps the **complete workflow** of a user study in a recommender system, from hypothesis formulation to deployment decision. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Hypothesis Formulation
e.g. Explanations increase trust] --> B[Study Design Selection
Lab / Field A/B / Crowd] B --> C[Participant Recruitment
Demographics and IRB] C --> D[Task Definition
Find a movie to watch] D --> E[System Exposure
Control vs Treatment Variant] E --> F[Data Collection
Click logs surveys eye tracker] F --> G[Metric Computation
USS NDCG CTR SUS] G --> H[Statistical Test
t-test chi-square p-value] H --> I{Null Rejected?} I -- Yes --> J[Deploy Treatment Variant] I -- No --> K[Iterate and Re-test] J --> L[Long-term Monitoring] ``` ## 4.2 A/B Test Functional Architecture for Recommender Evaluation ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Routing R[User Request] --> X{Random Assignment
0.5 Probability} end X -- Variant A --> M1[Model A
Baseline] X -- Variant B --> M2[Model B
Treatment] M1 --> L1[Logger A] M2 --> L2[Logger B] L1 --> DB[(Event Store)] L2 --> DB DB --> ANALYTICS[Analytics Pipeline
CTR USS Retention] ANALYTICS --> DECIDE[Statistical Decision
p less than 0.05] ``` ## 4.3 User-Centric Metric Hierarchy (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD USER[User Interaction with RS] --> BEH[Behavioral Metrics
CTR Conversion Dwell Time] USER --> SUB[Subjective Metrics
USS Trust NPS] USER --> COG[Cognitive Metrics
Time to Decision Effort] USER --> AFF[Affective Metrics
Serendipity Surprise Delight] BEH --> AGG[Aggregated Quality Score] SUB --> AGG COG --> AGG AFF --> AGG AGG --> SYS[System Improvement Decision] ``` --- # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define user studies in recommender systems. List any two limitations of offline evaluation that user studies address.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **User studies** are user-centric evaluation methods that measure how real users perceive, interact with, and are affected by a recommender system, typically through controlled experiments, surveys, or live A/B tests. **Two limitations of offline evaluation that user studies address:** 1. **Inability to capture subjective constructs** — offline metrics (RMSE, Precision) cannot measure user trust, satisfaction, or perceived diversity, which directly influence long-term engagement. 2. **Ignorance of user intent drift and context** — historical logs assume past behavior predicts future intent, but user studies reveal how context (mood, time-of-day, device) alters relevance. *(Accept any two valid limitations — diversity blindness, cold-start masking, explanation quality, novelty effect, etc.)* — **(3 marks)** ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between within-subjects and between-subjects experimental design in user studies for recommender systems.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Within-Subjects | Between-Subjects | |---|---|---| | Definition | Same user evaluates **all** variants. | Different user groups evaluate each variant. | | Sample Size | Smaller (each user counts for all conditions). | Larger (each user belongs to one condition). | | Risk | Carry-over / order effects. | Individual differences confound results. | | Countermeasure | Latin-square / randomization of order. | Random assignment + stratification. | | Use Case | Lab studies with explanations. | Large-scale A/B tests in production. | **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 ESE provides **internal choice** in Part B. You must attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both are provided below with exhaustive solutions. --- ### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **three-tier evaluation hierarchy** of recommender systems. Discuss, with examples, why user studies are considered the most informative yet the most resource-intensive tier. **(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the **NDCG@3** for a recommender that returned the top-3 items with graded relevance $[3, 1, 2]$ for a user. Given that the ideal ordering for that user is $[3, 2, 1]$, also report the **Precision@3** and **Recall@3** if the user has 4 relevant items in total in the catalog. --- #### Solution to Question A(a) — Model Answer (7 Marks) > [!NOTE] > **The Three-Tier Evaluation Hierarchy:** **Tier 1 — Offline Evaluation:** Uses pre-collected datasets (MovieLens, Amazon Reviews). Computes metrics like RMSE, MAE, Precision@K, Recall@K, NDCG, MAP. It is **fast, cheap, and reproducible** but ignores user interaction, context, and perception. *Example:* Computing RMSE on MovieLens-1M after training a Matrix Factorization model. **Tier 2 — Online Evaluation:** The system is deployed in shadow or live mode; metrics are computed from implicit signals like **CTR, conversion rate, dwell time, bounce rate**. It captures real behavior but is sensitive to novelty effects and selection bias. *Example:* A 1% traffic slice in Netflix sees a new collaborative-filtering model; their CTR is compared with the control. **Tier 3 — User Studies:** Explicit, controlled observation of human subjects via surveys, lab tasks, A/B tests with questionnaires, or eye-tracking. Captures **trust, satisfaction, perceived diversity, explanation quality, cognitive load**. *Example:* Asking 200 Prolific workers to rate satisfaction on a 5-point Likert scale after a 10-minute interaction with two recommendation UIs. **Why user studies are most informative:** - They capture *latent, subjective* variables no log can record (e.g., *trust after a misleading recommendation*). - They detect *interaction effects* between algorithm and UI that offline metrics miss. - They are the **only** valid way to measure **serendipity, novelty, and over-choice**. **Why they are most resource-intensive:** - Require **IRB approval**, participant recruitment, payment, and trained moderators. - Long duration (longitudinal studies may run months). - Statistical analysis needs large samples for adequate **power**, especially for small effect sizes. - Cannot be parallelized cheaply — the *human* is the bottleneck. **(7 marks)** — Examiner: 2 marks for hierarchy, 2 marks for examples, 2 marks for informativity, 1 mark for cost discussion. --- #### Solution to Question A(b) — Model Answer (7 Marks) **Given:** Recommended list $L = [3, 1, 2]$ (3 items, graded relevance at ranks 1, 2, 3). Ideal list $L^* = [3, 2, 1]$. Total relevant items in catalog = 4. **Step 1 — DCG@3:** $$ \begin{aligned} DCG@3 &= \sum_{i=1}^{3}\frac{2^{rel_i}-1}{\log_2(i+1)} \\ &= \frac{2^{3}-1}{\log_2 2} + \frac{2^{1}-1}{\log_2 3} + \frac{2^{2}-1}{\log_2 4} \\ &= \frac{7}{1} + \frac{1}{1.5850} + \frac{3}{2} \\ &= 7.0000 + 0.6309 + 1.5000 \\ &= 9.1309 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — IDCG@3 (using ideal ordering [3,2,1]):** $$ \begin{aligned} IDCG@3 &= \frac{7}{1} + \frac{3}{\log_2 3} + \frac{1}{2} \\ &= 7.0000 + \frac{3}{1.5850} + 0.5000 \\ &= 7.0000 + 1.8927 + 0.5000 \\ &= 9.3927 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — NDCG@3:** $$ NDCG@3 = \frac{DCG@3}{IDCG@3} = \frac{9.1309}{9.3927} = 0.9721 $$ **Step 4 — Precision@3:** The set of relevant items in the top-3 is $\{A, C\}$ (rel = 3, 2) — wait, careful. In graded relevance, an item is typically considered relevant if $rel_i \geq 2$ (binary cut-off). $$ P@3 = \frac{2}{3} = 0.6667 $$ **Step 5 — Recall@3:** $$ R@3 = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5000 $$ **Final Answer:** $$ NDCG@3 = 0.9721, \quad P@3 = 0.6667, \quad R@3 = 0.5000 $$ **(7 marks)** — Examiner: 3 marks for NDCG, 2 marks for Precision, 2 marks for Recall. --- ### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **A/B testing methodology** for evaluating a new recommendation algorithm. Derive the formula for **minimum sample size per group** required to detect a given effect size at 95% confidence and 80% power. **(b) [7 Marks]** An e-commerce recommender has a current **CTR of 4.0%**. The product team proposes a new algorithm they expect will raise CTR to **4.5%**. Using $Z_{0.025} = 1.96$ and $Z_{0.20} = 0.84$, calculate the **minimum sample size per group** for a two-proportion A/B test. Comment on whether launching the test with **50,000 users per arm** is sufficient. --- #### Solution to Question B(a) — Model Answer (7 Marks) **A/B Testing Methodology — Step by Step:** 1. **Formulate Hypothesis** — $H_0: p_1 = p_2$ (no difference in CTR); $H_1: p_1 \neq p_2$. 2. **Random Assignment** — Each incoming user is randomly bucketed into **Control (A)** or **Treatment (B)** with 50/50 probability (or other fixed split). 3. **Define Primary Metric** — e.g., CTR, conversion, dwell time. Optionally define *guardrail metrics* to detect regressions. 4. **Determine Sample Size** — Using the power-analysis formula below. 5. **Run Experiment** — Duration must cover full business cycles (a week, a month) to avoid weekday/seasonality bias. 6. **Collect Data** — Log impressions, clicks, conversions per arm. 7. **Statistical Test** — Two-proportion $z$-test or chi-square; compute $p$-value. 8. **Decision** — If $p < \alpha$, reject $H_0$ and ship treatment. Otherwise iterate. **Derivation of Sample-Size Formula (Two-Proportion Test):** The test statistic for comparing two proportions is: $$ z = \frac{\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2}{\sqrt{\bar{p}\bar{q}\left(\frac{1}{n_1}+\frac{1}{n_2}\right)}} $$ where $\bar{p} = \dfrac{p_1+p_2}{2}$, $\bar{q} = 1-\bar{p}$, and $n_1 = n_2 = n$ for equal allocation. Under $H_0$, this is standard normal. To detect a true difference $\delta = p_1 - p_2$ with significance $\alpha$ (two-sided) and power $1-\beta$: $$ \begin{aligned} z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\frac{2\bar{p}\bar{q}}{n}} + z_{\beta}\sqrt{\frac{p_1q_1 + p_2q_2}{n}} &= \delta \\ \sqrt{n}\cdot \delta &= z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{2\bar{p}\bar{q}} + z_{\beta}\sqrt{p_1q_1+p_2q_2} \\ n &= \frac{\left(z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{2\bar{p}\bar{q}} + z_{\beta}\sqrt{p_1q_1+p_2q_2}\right)^2}{\delta^2} \end{aligned} $$ **Final formula:** $$ \boxed{\,n = \frac{\left(z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{2\bar{p}\bar{q}} + z_{\beta}\sqrt{p_1q_1+p_2q_2}\right)^2}{(p_1-p_2)^2}\,} $$ **(7 marks)** — Examiner: 3 marks for methodology, 4 marks for derivation. --- #### Solution to Question B(b) — Model Answer (7 Marks) **Given:** $p_1 = 0.040$, $p_2 = 0.045$, $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-sided), power $= 0.80$. **Step 1 — Compute auxiliary values:** - $\delta = p_1 - p_2 = 0.040 - 0.045 = -0.005$ (use magnitude $0.005$). - $\bar{p} = \dfrac{0.040 + 0.045}{2} = 0.0425$ - $\bar{q} = 1 - 0.0425 = 0.9575$ - $p_1 q_1 = 0.040 \times 0.960 = 0.0384$ - $p_2 q_2 = 0.045 \times 0.955 = 0.042975$ - $p_1 q_1 + p_2 q_2 = 0.0384 + 0.042975 = 0.081375$ - $2\bar{p}\bar{q} = 2 \times 0.0425 \times 0.9575 = 0.081388$ **Step 2 — Numerator inside the square:** $$ \begin{aligned} A &= z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{2\bar{p}\bar{q}} + z_{\beta}\sqrt{p_1q_1+p_2q_2} \\ &= 1.96 \times \sqrt{0.081388} + 0.84 \times \sqrt{0.081375} \\ &= 1.96 \times 0.28529 + 0.84 \times 0.28527 \\ &= 0.55917 + 0.23963 \\ &= 0.79880 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Denominator:** $$ \delta^2 = (0.005)^2 = 0.000025 $$ **Step 4 — Compute $n$:** $$ \begin{aligned} n &= \frac{(0.79880)^2}{0.000025} \\ &= \frac{0.63808}{0.000025} \\ &= 25{,}523.2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Round up and conclude:** $$ n \approx 25{,}524 \text{ users per arm} $$ **Comment on $n = 50{,}000$ per arm:** 50,000 **exceeds** the required 25,524, so the test is **statistically powered**. However, the test must also run for a **full business cycle** (typically 1–2 weeks) to mitigate weekday/seasonality effects. With sufficient duration and traffic, launching with 50,000/arm is appropriate. **(7 marks)** — Examiner: 3 marks computation, 2 marks numerical result, 2 marks interpretation. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU students lose marks in Module 4 User Studies questions:** > > 1. **Confusing "NDCG with log base 2 vs. log base e"** — KTU always uses $\log_2$. Writing $\ln$ will lose 1 mark. > 2. **Forgetting the "ideal" ordering in IDCG** — Many students compute IDCG using the *given* list instead of sorting relevance in descending order. This is a 2-mark deduction. > 3. **In A/B test derivations, omitting $z_{\beta}\sqrt{p_1q_1+p_2q_2}$** — Using only the first term underestimates $n$ by ~30%, leading to an **under-powered** test. KTU strictly checks this term. > 4. **Mixing up "perceived accuracy" with "perceived satisfaction"** — These are distinct constructs. Perceived accuracy is task-level; satisfaction is session-level. > 5. **Not stating assumptions** — In sample-size problems, always state: equal allocation, two-sided test, independence, normal approximation valid (i.e., $np \geq 5$, $nq \geq 5$). > 6. **Ignoring carry-over effects in within-subjects designs** — KTU expects mention of Latin-square counter-balancing. --- ## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: User Studies** - ✅ User studies are the **top tier** of the evaluation hierarchy — most informative, most expensive. - ✅ They capture **subjective, behavioral, cognitive, and affective** responses — not just accuracy. - ✅ Three main designs: **Lab (high control), Field A/B (high realism), Crowd (fast & cheap)**. - ✅ **Within-subjects** = same user, all variants; **Between-subjects** = different users, one variant each. - ✅ Key user-centric metrics to memorize: **Precision@K, Recall@K, NDCG@K, MRR, CTR, USS, SUS, NPS, Diversity, Serendipity, Coverage**. - ✅ NDCG formula uses **$\log_2$**; DCG uses $\dfrac{2^{rel_i}-1}{\log_2(i+1)}$; IDCG uses **ideal (sorted) ordering**. - ✅ A/B test sample-size formula: $n = \dfrac{\left(z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{2\bar{p}\bar{q}} + z_{\beta}\sqrt{p_1q_1+p_2q_2}\right)^2}{(p_1-p_2)^2}$. - ✅ Standard $z$-values: $z_{0.025} = 1.96$, $z_{0.20} = 0.84$. - ✅ Always state **assumptions** in derivations: two-sided test, equal allocation, independence, normal approximation valid. - ✅ **User satisfaction is NOT the same as click-through rate** — satisfaction is holistic and explicit, CTR is implicit. - ✅ User studies must address **ecological validity** (lab), **novelty effect** (field), and **self-selection bias** (crowd). - ✅ **Trust, transparency, and explainability** are central modern concerns — they cannot be measured offline. - ✅ For 14-mark derivation questions: always show **all** intermediate arithmetic; examiners award step-marks. - ✅ KTU 2024 emphasizes **user-centric and fairness-aware** evaluation — be ready to discuss *filter bubbles* and *homogenization*. - ✅ **Counter-balancing** (Latin square) is the standard fix for order effects in within-subjects designs.

topics.title: Online Evaluation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT741 - RECOMMENDATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Online Evaluation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Online Evaluation** in the context of recommendation systems refers to the methodology of assessing the performance, quality, and business impact of a recommender system by deploying it (or a variant of it) into a live, production environment and measuring real-time user interactions. Unlike offline evaluation (which uses historical logs and held-out datasets), online evaluation captures the **causal effect** of recommendations on actual user behavior.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> Online Evaluation is the process of measuring the effectiveness of a recommendation algorithm by exposing it to real users in a live system, typically through controlled experiments such as A/B tests, interleaving, or multi-armed bandit approaches, and quantifying metrics like Click-Through Rate (CTR), engagement time, conversion rate, and revenue lift.

The three primary paradigms in online evaluation are:

1. **A/B Testing (Randomized Controlled Trials)** — Users are randomly split into mutually exclusive buckets; each bucket receives a different algorithm variant.
2. **Interleaving** — Outputs of two (or more) rankers are merged into a single list shown to the same user; clicks are attributed to the ranker that contributed the clicked item.
3. **Multi-Armed Bandits (MAB)** — A sequential decision-making framework that dynamically allocates traffic to better-performing variants over time.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Restaurant Taste-Testing Analogy**
> Imagine you own a chain of restaurants and have developed two new soup recipes. You can't just trust a chemist's lab analysis (offline evaluation) — you need real customers to taste both. You randomly give Soup A to Table 1 and Soup B to Table 2, then measure which gets reordered (A/B test). Alternatively, you serve a single bowl containing a blend of both soups, then ask customers which flavors they preferred (interleaving). If one recipe is clearly winning, you start serving it to more tables (bandit). **Online evaluation is the "taste test" for recommendation algorithms using real users in production.**

### 1.3 Key Terminology

- **Treatment (Variant)**: The recommendation algorithm being tested.
- **Control**: The baseline / existing production algorithm.
- **Bucket / Arm**: A user segment exposed to a specific variant.
- **Unit of Diversion**: The entity randomized (user ID, cookie, session, device).
- **Statistical Power (1−β)**: Probability of detecting a true effect; conventionally set to **0.8**.
- **Significance Level (α)**: Probability of false positive; conventionally set to **0.05**.
- **Minimum Detectable Effect (MDE)**: Smallest lift you want to detect.
- **Interleaving Credit**: Attribution score given to a ranker when its item is clicked.
- **Explore/Exploit Dilemma**: Core trade-off in bandit-based evaluation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Online Evaluation Matters in Industry**
> Platforms like **Netflix, YouTube, Spotify, Amazon, and LinkedIn** rely on online evaluation as the *gold standard* for validating deployment. A model that wins offline benchmarks can still fail in production due to position bias, presentation effects, popularity drift, and feedback loops that only manifest in real user sessions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Online Evaluation

#### Pillar 1 — A/B Testing (Bucket Testing)
A/B testing is a **frequentist hypothesis testing framework** where users are randomly assigned to either a **Control** (current algorithm) or a **Treatment** (new algorithm). The core scientific question is:

> "Is the observed difference in metric $X$ between the two groups statistically significant, or could it be due to random chance?"

**Operational Steps:**
1. **Formulate Hypotheses** — State a null hypothesis $H_0$ and an alternative $H_1$.
2. **Determine Sample Size** — Use power analysis to compute required users per bucket.
3. **Randomize Users** — Use a hashing function (e.g., `hash(user_id) mod 100`) for stable assignment.
4. **Run the Experiment** — Hold treatment constant for the experiment duration (typically **7–14 days** to capture weekly seasonality).
5. **Compute Test Statistic** — Apply a two-proportion z-test for binary metrics, or Welch's t-test for continuous metrics.
6. **Decide** — Reject $H_0$ if $p < \alpha$ and the effect size exceeds the MDE.

#### Pillar 2 — Interleaving
Interleaving was popularized by **Netflix (2016, Chapelle et al.)** to overcome the **sensitivity problem** of A/B tests. Instead of comparing two separate lists, both algorithms generate candidate lists that are **merged into a single interleaved list** presented to the same user.

**Interleaving Algorithms:**
- **Team-Draft Interleaving** — Each ranker "drafts" items alternately; credit is given to the ranker who picked the clicked item.
- **Balanced Interleaving** — Probabilistic fair assignment ensuring equal expected team size.
- **Optimized Interleaving** — Probabilistic interleaving optimized for sensitivity and unbiasedness.

#### Pillar 3 — Multi-Armed Bandits (MAB)
The MAB framework treats algorithm selection as a sequential decision problem. Each algorithm is an "arm" with an unknown reward distribution; the evaluator must **explore** (test unknown arms) and **exploit** (favor the best-known arm).

**Canonical Algorithms:**
- **ε-Greedy**: Choose the best arm with probability $1-\varepsilon$, random arm with probability $\varepsilon$.
- **Upper Confidence Bound (UCB)**: Optimism in face of uncertainty.
- **Thompson Sampling**: Bayesian posterior sampling.
- **Contextual Bandits (LinUCB)**: Arms chosen based on user/context features.

### 2.2 Core Online Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Use Case | Type |
|--------|---------|----------|------|
| **CTR (Click-Through Rate)** | $\text{CTR} = \frac{\text{Clicks}}{\text{Impressions}}$ | Ad/recommendation relevance | Binary ratio |
| **Conversion Rate** | $\text{CVR} = \frac{\text{Conversions}}{\text{Clicks}}$ | E-commerce bottom-funnel | Binary ratio |
| **Engagement Rate** | $\text{ER} = \frac{\text{Engaged Sessions}}{\text{Total Sessions}}$ | Media streaming | Binary ratio |
| **Average Dwell Time** | $\text{ADT} = \frac{\sum \text{time on item}}{\text{Items viewed}}$ | Content consumption | Continuous |
| **Revenue per User (RPU)** | $\text{RPU} = \frac{\text{Total Revenue}}{\text{Active Users}}$ | Monetization | Continuous |
| **Session Length** | $\text{SL} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{duration}_i$ | Stickiness | Continuous |
| **Coverage** | $\text{Cov} = \frac{\vert \text{Recommended Items} \vert}{\vert \text{Catalog} \vert}$ | Catalog exposure | Catalog ratio |
| **Serendipity** | $\text{Ser} = \text{Usefulness} \times \text{Unexpectedness}$ | Discovery quality | Composite |
| **Novelty** | $\text{Novelty} = -\log_2 p(\text{item})$ | Long-tail promotion | Information-theoretic |
| **NDCG@k** | $\text{NDCG@k} = \frac{\text{DCG@k}}{\text{IDCG@k}}$ | Ranking quality | Rank-aware |
| **Regret** | $R_T = T \cdot \mu^* - \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mu_{a_t}$ | Bandit efficiency | Cumulative loss |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Statistical Significance

**Two-Proportion Z-Test (for CTR/CVR comparison):**

$$Z = \frac{\hat{p}_T - \hat{p}_C}{\sqrt{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})\left(\frac{1}{n_T} + \frac{1}{n_C}\right)}}$$

where $\hat{p} = \frac{x_T + x_C}{n_T + n_C}$ is the pooled proportion.

**Sample Size Formula (per variant):**

$$n = \frac{(Z_{1-\alpha/2}\sqrt{2\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})} + Z_{1-\beta}\sqrt{p_T(1-p_T) + p_C(1-p_C)})^2}{(p_T - p_C)^2}$$

**Confidence Interval for Difference in Means:**

$$(\bar{x}_T - \bar{x}_C) \pm t_{1-\alpha/2, \, df} \cdot s_{\text{pooled}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{n_T} + \frac{1}{n_C}}$$

**Welch's t-statistic (unequal variances):**

$$t = \frac{\bar{x}_T - \bar{x}_C}{\sqrt{\frac{s_T^2}{n_T} + \frac{s_C^2}{n_C}}}$$

**Thompson Sampling Posterior (Bernoulli reward):**

$$\theta_a \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha_a + r_a, \, \beta_a + n_a - r_a)$$

**UCB1 Index:**

$$\text{UCB}_a = \hat{\mu}_a + c \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\ln T}{n_a}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility**
> In production systems, online evaluation is the **only ground truth** for whether a recommender helps the business. Major platforms run **thousands of concurrent A/B tests** (Google, Microsoft Bing report >10,000/year), with dedicated experimentation platforms (e.g., Google's **REX**, Netflix's **A/B Platform**, Microsoft's **ExP**). Online evaluation informs **launch/no-launch decisions** worth millions in revenue.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Sample Size for an A/B Test on CTR

**Problem Setup:** A streaming platform's current recommendation algorithm has a CTR of $p_C = 0.10$. The data science team proposes a new model and wants to detect a **relative lift of 5%** (i.e., $p_T = 0.105$) with statistical significance $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-tailed) and power $1 - \beta = 0.80$. Derive the required sample size per arm.

**Step 1 — Identify Critical Z-Values**

For a two-tailed test at $\alpha = 0.05$:
$$Z_{1-\alpha/2} = Z_{0.975} = 1.96$$

For power $1 - \beta = 0.80$:
$$Z_{1-\beta} = Z_{0.80} = 0.84$$

**Step 2 — Compute Average Proportion**

$$\bar{p} = \frac{p_C + p_T}{2} = \frac{0.10 + 0.105}{2} = 0.1025$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Sample Size Formula**

$$n = \frac{\left(Z_{1-\alpha/2}\sqrt{2\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})} + Z_{1-\beta}\sqrt{p_T(1-p_T) + p_C(1-p_C)}\right)^2}{(p_T - p_C)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Each Sub-Expression**

$$\sqrt{2\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})} = \sqrt{2 \times 0.1025 \times 0.8975} = \sqrt{0.18399} = 0.42894$$

$$p_T(1-p_T) + p_C(1-p_C) = (0.105 \times 0.895) + (0.10 \times 0.90) = 0.093975 + 0.09 = 0.183975$$

$$\sqrt{0.183975} = 0.42893$$

$$(p_T - p_C)^2 = (0.005)^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and Solve**

$$\text{Numerator} = \left(1.96 \times 0.42894 + 0.84 \times 0.42893\right)^2 = \left(0.84072 + 0.36030\right)^2 = (1.20102)^2 = 1.44245$$

$$n = \frac{1.44245}{2.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 57{,}698 \text{ users per arm}$$

**Step 6 — Account for Multiple Variants and Attrition**

Multiply by 2 (two arms) and inflate by ~15% for users who never see the recommendation:
$$N_{\text{total}} = 2 \times 57{,}698 \times 1.15 \approx 132{,}705 \text{ users}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** To detect a *0.5 percentage point* absolute lift in CTR (from 10% → 10.5%), the team needs ~57,700 users per arm. Smaller effects require exponentially larger samples — this is the **sensitivity curse of A/B testing**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Thompson Sampling Update Rule

**Problem Setup:** We have two recommendation algorithms, A and B, whose true CTRs are unknown. We model each as a Bernoulli reward with Beta prior. After observing rewards, derive the posterior update.

**Step 1 — Initialize Priors**

We use a non-informative prior $\text{Beta}(1,1)$ (uniform on $[0,1]$) for each algorithm:
$$\theta_A^{(0)} \sim \text{Beta}(1, 1), \quad \theta_B^{(0)} \sim \text{Beta}(1, 1)$$

**Step 2 — Bernoulli Likelihood**

If algorithm $a$ is shown to $n_a$ users and receives $r_a$ clicks, the likelihood is:
$$P(\text{data} \mid \theta_a) = \theta_a^{r_a} (1 - \theta_a)^{n_a - r_a}$$

**Step 3 — Conjugate Posterior Update**

Beta is the conjugate prior of Bernoulli, so the posterior is also Beta:
$$P(\theta_a \mid \text{data}) \propto \theta_a^{r_a}(1-\theta_a)^{n_a-r_a} \cdot \theta_a^{\alpha_a - 1}(1-\theta_a)^{\beta_a - 1}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_a \mid \text{data} \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha_a + r_a, \, \beta_a + n_a - r_a)}$$

**Step 4 — Sampling Decision Rule**

At each round $t$, sample $\tilde{\theta}_a^{(t)}$ from each arm's posterior and select:
$$a_t = \arg\max_a \tilde{\theta}_a^{(t)}$$

**Step 5 — Observe Reward and Update**

After observing reward $r_t \in \{0,1\}$:
$$\alpha_{a_t} \leftarrow \alpha_{a_t} + r_t$$
$$\beta_{a_t} \leftarrow \beta_{a_t} + (1 - r_t)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Thompson Sampling Works in Recommendation Systems**
> It naturally handles the **cold-start** of new algorithms (wide priors early → lots of exploration) and **converges** to the best arm as data accumulates (sharp posteriors → exploitation). This makes it ideal for online evaluation of recommenders where traffic is precious and exploration costs (bad recommendations) must be minimized.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Team-Draft Interleaving Credit Function

**Problem Setup:** Two rankers, R1 and R2, each produce a list of 5 items. We interleave them using the team-draft rule. A user clicks on item $i$ that was drafted by R1. Compute the credit assigned to R1.

**Step 1 — Initialization**

Set $\text{credit}_{R1} = 0$, $\text{credit}_{R2} = 0$. Maintain a "coin" that flips between rankers. Define a draft order function $D(\cdot)$.

**Step 2 — Draft Items Alternately**

For slot $k = 1, 2, \ldots, K$:
- If $D(k) = R1$, place R1's next unused item at slot $k$; tag it as "R1's pick."
- Else, place R2's next unused item; tag it as "R2's pick."

**Step 3 — User Interaction and Credit Assignment**

When a user clicks item at position $k$:
$$\text{credit}_{\text{picker}} \mathrel{+}= 1$$
$$\text{credit}_{\text{non-picker}} \mathrel{+}= 0$$

So if the user clicks an R1-picked item:
$$\text{credit}_{R1} = 1, \quad \text{credit}_{R2} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Aggregate Over Session**

If user clicks items at positions $k_1, k_2, \ldots, k_m$ in a session, the total credit vector is:
$$\mathbf{c} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \mathbf{e}_{\pi(k_j)}$$

where $\pi(k)$ is the picker of position $k$ and $\mathbf{e}_a$ is the unit vector for ranker $a$.

**Step 5 — Probability of Winning**

Over many sessions, the ranker that wins more often is preferred. Under **balanced interleaving**, if R1 produces a strictly better list (according to user preferences), then:
$$P(\text{R1 wins}) > 0.5$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sensitivity Advantage:** Interleaving requires **~50–100× fewer observations** than A/B testing to detect the same effect size because every session produces comparative signal — both rankers are judged *in the same context* (same user, same session, same page), removing between-user variance.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: A/B Testing vs Interleaving vs Bandits

| Dimension | A/B Testing | Interleaving | Multi-Armed Bandits |
|-----------|-------------|--------------|---------------------|
| **Sample Size Needed** | Very Large | Very Small | Medium |
| **Sensitivity** | Low | Very High | High (for short-term) |
| **Bias Risk** | Low (with proper randomization) | Medium (presentation bias) | Medium (exploration cost) |
| **Statistical Guarantee** | Frequentist p-value | Probabilistic consistency | Regret bound $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{T \log K})$ |
| **Real-World Users Affected** | Many (entire bucket) | Few (single bucket) | Adaptive |
| **Multiple Variants** | Easy (K buckets) | Hard (pairwise) | Native (K arms) |
| **Winner Determination** | Post-hoc (end of test) | Continuous | Real-time |
| **Best Use Case** | Final ship/no-ship decisions | Rapid algorithm comparison | Exploration-heavy or cold-start |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Practice (Netflix, Google, Microsoft):** A typical pipeline uses **interleaving first** (rapid comparison) → **A/B test** (validation + business metrics) → **bandit** (production serving with ongoing exploration). Each method complements the others.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Architecture: Online Evaluation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Request Arrives] --> B[Unit of Diversion Module]
    B --> C{User Bucket}
    C -->|hash mod 100 < 50| D[Control Bucket - Algorithm A]
    C -->|hash mod 100 >= 50| E[Treatment Bucket - Algorithm B]
    D --> F[Recommendation Generator]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Response Logger]
    G --> H[Clickstream Database]
    H --> I[Metrics Aggregator]
    I --> J[Statistical Analysis Engine]
    J --> K{Is p-value less than 0.05?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Reject H0: Algorithm B Wins]
    K -->|No| M[Fail to Reject H0: No Significant Difference]
    L --> N[Launch Decision Dashboard]
    M --> N
    I --> O[Guardrail Metrics Check]
    O --> P{Session Length, Latency OK?}
    P -->|Yes| N
    P -->|No| Q[Auto-Rollback Alert]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Every user request is hashed into a deterministic bucket (ensuring consistent user experience), routed to a variant, and the response is logged. The metrics aggregator computes online KPIs in near real-time, while the statistical engine applies hypothesis tests. A separate guardrail check monitors non-target metrics (latency, crashes) to prevent shipping a model that improves CTR but degrades UX.

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow: Interleaving Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ranker A List] --> C[Team-Draft Interleaver]
    B[Ranker B List] --> C
    C --> D[Interleaved List Shown to User]
    D --> E{User Clicks Item}
    E -->|Click Picked by A| F[Credit to A: +1]
    E -->|Click Picked by B| G[Credit to B: +1]
    F --> H[Credit Aggregator]
    G --> H
    H --> I{Is A credit greater than B credit statistically?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Declare A Winner]
    I -->|No| K[Declare B Winner or Tie]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Flow: Multi-Armed Bandit Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize Beta Priors for K Arms] --> B[Sample theta from Each Arm Posterior]
    B --> C[Select Arm with Maximum Sampled theta]
    C --> D[Serve Recommendation from Selected Arm]
    D --> E[Observe User Reward: Click or No Click]
    E --> F[Update Selected Arms Posterior Distribution]
    F --> G{Convergence Reached}
    G -->|No| B
    G -->|Yes| H[Lock Best Arm for Production]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in KTU Exams:** Examiners frequently ask students to **draw a labeled block diagram** of an online evaluation system and identify the role of each component. Memorize the three pipeline blocks above: **Diversion → Generation → Logging → Aggregation → Decision**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Differentiate between offline evaluation and online evaluation of a recommender system. List any two online evaluation metrics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Offline Evaluation | Online Evaluation |
|--------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **Data Source** | Historical logs / held-out datasets | Live production traffic |
| **User Involvement** | None (replay) | Real users |
| **Metrics** | RMSE, Precision@k, Recall@k, MAP, NDCG | CTR, Conversion, Engagement, Revenue |
| **Causality** | Correlation only | Causal effect |
| **Cost** | Low | High (engineering + risk) |
| **Speed** | Fast (batch) | Days to weeks |
| **Bias** | Suffers from exposure/selection bias | Captures real user behavior |

**Two online metrics:** *CTR (Click-Through Rate)* and *Conversion Rate*.

*(Award 2 marks for the table + 1 mark for metrics.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**What is interleaving in recommendation system evaluation? State one advantage over A/B testing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Interleaving is an online evaluation technique where the candidate lists from two or more recommendation algorithms are **merged into a single combined list** and presented to the same user. When the user clicks an item, the click is attributed as credit to the algorithm that originally picked that item (e.g., via team-draft interleaving). The algorithm with higher accumulated credit is declared the winner.

**Advantage (1 mark):** Interleaving is **far more sensitive** than A/B testing — it typically requires **50–100× fewer observations** to detect the same effect size, because both algorithms are judged under identical user, session, and contextual conditions, eliminating between-user variance.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **A/B testing methodology** for evaluating a recommender system. Include a discussion on hypothesis formulation, randomization, sample size determination, and choice of unit of diversion.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A music streaming app's current "Discover Weekly" recommender has a **CTR of 8%**. The team proposes a transformer-based model expected to improve CTR to **9%**. Using a two-proportion z-test framework with $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-tailed) and power $1-\beta = 0.80$:
   (i) Compute the required sample size per arm.
   (ii) How does the sample size change if the MDE is relaxed to detect a 2% absolute lift?

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**1. Hypothesis Formulation (2 marks)**

State the null and alternative hypotheses:
- $H_0: p_T = p_C$ (new model has same CTR as old)
- $H_1: p_T \neq p_C$ (two-tailed) — or $H_1: p_T > p_C$ (one-tailed) for lift detection.

**2. Randomization & Unit of Diversion (2 marks)**

- **Unit of Diversion:** The randomization key. Common choices include:
  - `user_id` (most common — ensures consistent experience)
  - `cookie_id` (browser-level, weaker)
  - `session_id` (worst — high variance, inconsistent)
- **Implementation:** Use a deterministic hash: `bucket = hash(user_id + salt) mod 100`. This is reproducible and prevents bucket leakage.
- **Why user_id?** It preserves independence of observations and prevents contamination (a user doesn't see both variants).

**3. Sample Size Determination (2 marks)**

Use the power-analysis formula (derived in Section 3.1):
$$n = \frac{(Z_{1-\alpha/2}\sqrt{2\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})} + Z_{1-\beta}\sqrt{p_T(1-p_T) + p_C(1-p_C)})^2}{(p_T - p_C)^2}$$

**4. Test Execution and Decision (1 mark)**

Run for 7–14 days (capture weekly cycles), compute z-statistic, check guardrail metrics (latency, session length), then make a launch decision based on $p$-value and effect size.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Sample Size for 8% → 9% (5 marks)**

Given: $p_C = 0.08$, $p_T = 0.09$, $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-tailed), $1-\beta = 0.80$.
- $Z_{1-\alpha/2} = 1.96$, $Z_{1-\beta} = 0.84$
- $\bar{p} = (0.08 + 0.09)/2 = 0.085$

$$n = \frac{(1.96\sqrt{2 \times 0.085 \times 0.915} + 0.84\sqrt{0.09 \times 0.91 + 0.08 \times 0.92})^2}{(0.09 - 0.08)^2}$$

Step-by-step:
- $2 \times 0.085 \times 0.915 = 0.15555$ → $\sqrt{} = 0.39440$
- $0.09 \times 0.91 + 0.08 \times 0.92 = 0.0819 + 0.0736 = 0.1555$ → $\sqrt{} = 0.39434$
- Numerator: $(1.96 \times 0.39440 + 0.84 \times 0.39434)^2 = (0.77302 + 0.33125)^2 = (1.10427)^2 = 1.21941$
- Denominator: $0.01^2 = 0.0001$

$$n = \frac{1.21941}{0.0001} = 12{,}194 \text{ users per arm}$$

**(ii) Effect of Relaxed MDE (2 marks)**

If MDE is relaxed from **1% to 2% absolute** (i.e., $p_T = 0.10$):
- $\bar{p} = (0.08 + 0.10)/2 = 0.09$
- Numerator: $(1.96 \sqrt{2 \times 0.09 \times 0.91} + 0.84 \sqrt{0.10 \times 0.90 + 0.08 \times 0.92})^2$
- $= (1.96 \times 0.40497 + 0.84 \times 0.40299)^2 = (0.79374 + 0.33851)^2 = (1.13225)^2 = 1.28199$
- Denominator: $0.02^2 = 0.0004$

$$n = \frac{1.28199}{0.0004} = 3{,}205 \text{ users per arm}$$

**Inference (1 mark):** Relaxing the MDE from 1% to 2% absolute lift **reduces the sample size by ~74%** (from 12,194 → 3,205). This demonstrates that **A/B tests are extremely sensitive to the MDE choice** — detecting smaller effects requires exponentially more data.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 — Alternative]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **multi-armed bandit (MAB) framework** for online evaluation of recommender systems. Compare the **ε-greedy**, **UCB1**, and **Thompson Sampling** algorithms in terms of their exploration strategy and regret bounds.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Two recommendation algorithms, A and B, are compared using Thompson Sampling with Beta priors initialized at $\text{Beta}(1,1)$ for each. After 1000 impressions, the following data is observed:

| Algorithm | Impressions | Clicks |
|-----------|-------------|--------|
| A | 500 | 60 |
| B | 500 | 40 |

   (i) Compute the posterior distributions for both algorithms.
   (ii) Estimate the probability that A is the better algorithm by Monte Carlo simulation logic.
   (iii) Comment on whether the platform should prefer A or B.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**MAB Framework (2 marks)**

A multi-armed bandit is a sequential decision-making model where an agent must choose among $K$ actions (arms), each with an unknown reward distribution. In recommendation systems:
- **Arms** = different recommendation algorithms or models.
- **Reward** = user engagement (click, like, purchase).
- **Goal** = maximize cumulative reward by balancing exploration (learning arm quality) and exploitation (using the best-known arm).

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Algorithm | Exploration Strategy | Posterior/Estimate | Regret Bound | Pros/Cons |
|-----------|---------------------|-------------------|--------------|-----------|
| **ε-Greedy** | Random arm with probability ε | Sample mean $\hat{\mu}_a$ | $\mathcal{O}(\varepsilon T)$ linear | Simple; ignores uncertainty |
| **UCB1** | Optimistic in face of uncertainty | $\hat{\mu}_a + c\sqrt{\frac{\ln T}{n_a}}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{KT \ln T})$ | No randomness; provable bound |
| **Thompson Sampling** | Sample from posterior | $\theta_a \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha_a + r_a, \beta_a + n_a - r_a)$ | $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{KT \ln T})$ | Probabilistic, fast convergence, handles delayed feedback |

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Posterior Distributions (2 marks)**

For algorithm A: $n_A = 500$, $r_A = 60$
$$\theta_A \sim \text{Beta}(1 + 60, \, 1 + 500 - 60) = \text{Beta}(61, 441)$$
- Mean: $\hat{\mu}_A = 61/502 = 0.1215$

For algorithm B: $n_B = 500$, $r_B = 40$
$$\theta_B \sim \text{Beta}(1 + 40, \, 1 + 500 - 40) = \text{Beta}(41, 461)$$
- Mean: $\hat{\mu}_B = 41/502 = 0.0817$

**(ii) Monte Carlo Probability (3 marks)**

We estimate $P(\theta_A > \theta_B)$ by sampling:
- Draw $\tilde{\theta}_A^{(i)} \sim \text{Beta}(61, 441)$
- Draw $\tilde{\theta}_B^{(i)} \sim \text{Beta}(41, 461)$
- Indicator $I_i = \mathbb{1}[\tilde{\theta}_A^{(i)} > \tilde{\theta}_B^{(i)}]$

$$\hat{P} = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{i=1}^{M} I_i \approx 0.987$$

(The exact value can be computed via the formula for the probability that one Beta exceeds another; numerical integration yields ~0.987 — strongly favoring A.)

**(iii) Recommendation (2 marks)**

Since $\hat{P}(\theta_A > \theta_B) \approx 98.7\% \gg 95\%$, the platform should **prefer algorithm A** for production serving. The posterior distributions barely overlap, indicating a statistically significant and practically meaningful difference of ~4 percentage points in CTR (12.15% vs 8.17%), representing a **~49% relative lift** in click-through rate.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions**
> 1. **Confusing Offline with Online Metrics:** Students often write "RMSE" or "Precision@10" as online metrics. These are **offline**. Online metrics are CTR, CVR, dwell time, revenue.
> 2. **Forgetting the Two-Tailed Z-Value:** Using $Z = 1.645$ (one-tailed) when $\alpha = 0.05$ **two-tailed** is specified. Always check the test direction.
> 3. **Skipping Randomization Justification:** Examiners expect you to justify *why* `user_id` is the correct unit of diversion — not just state it.
> 4. **Not Computing Posterior Updates Correctly:** Common error: writing $\text{Beta}(r_a, n_a - r_a)$ instead of $\text{Beta}(1 + r_a, 1 + n_a - r_a)$. The "+1" reflects the prior pseudo-counts.
> 5. **Ignoring Practical Significance:** Even if $p < 0.05$, the *lift* may be too small to justify the engineering cost. Always comment on **effect size** alongside $p$-values.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Online Evaluation**

- ✅ **Definition:** Online evaluation assesses recommender performance using **live user interactions** in production.
- ✅ **Three Paradigms:**
  - **A/B Testing** — Most rigorous, slow, requires large samples.
  - **Interleaving** — Fastest, most sensitive, requires only ~1–2% of A/B test traffic.
  - **Multi-Armed Bandits** — Adaptive, balances explore/exploit, ideal for ongoing traffic allocation.
- ✅ **Key Online Metrics:** CTR, CVR, Engagement Rate, Dwell Time, RPU, Session Length.
- ✅ **Sample Size Formula:** $n = \frac{(Z_{1-\alpha/2}\sqrt{2\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})} + Z_{1-\beta}\sqrt{p_T(1-p_T) + p_C(1-p_C)})^2}{(p_T - p_C)^2}$
- ✅ **Z-Values to Memorize:** $Z_{0.975} = 1.96$, $Z_{0.80} = 0.84$, $Z_{0.95} = 1.645$.
- ✅ **Thompson Sampling Posterior:** $\text{Beta}(\alpha_a + r_a, \beta_a + n_a - r_a)$.
- ✅ **UCB1 Formula:** $\hat{\mu}_a + c\sqrt{\frac{\ln T}{n_a}}$.
- ✅ **Unit of Diversion:** Prefer `user_id` (stable, low variance).
- ✅ **MDE Trade-off:** Smaller MDE → exponentially larger sample size.
- ✅ **Interleaving Sensitivity:** ~50–100× more sensitive than A/B testing.
- ✅ **Best Practice Pipeline:** Interleaving → A/B test → Bandit deployment.
- ✅ **Guardrail Metrics:** Always monitor latency, crashes, session length — not just the target KPI.
- ✅ **Exploration vs Exploitation:** Core dilemma; ε-greedy uses fixed ε, UCB uses confidence bounds, Thompson Sampling uses posterior sampling.
- ✅ **Regret Bound:** $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{KT \ln T})$ for UCB1 and Thompson Sampling.
- ✅ **Pitfall:** A model can win offline but lose online due to position bias, presentation effects, or feedback loops.
- ✅ **Industry Use:** Netflix, YouTube, Spotify, Google, Amazon, LinkedIn all run large-scale online evaluation as the **gold standard** for deployment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Offline Evaluation with Historical Data Sets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT741 - RECOMMENDER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Offline Evaluation with Historical Data Sets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Offline Evaluation** is the process of measuring the quality, accuracy, and effectiveness of a recommender system using **pre-collected, historical user–item interaction data** in a non-realtime, laboratory-style setting. It forms the **first stage of the evaluation pipeline** (offline → user study → online A/B test) and is the principal mechanism by which candidate algorithms are compared *before* being exposed to real users.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 / Outcome-Based Education)** vocabulary, offline evaluation is mapped to the following Course Outcomes:

- **CO4 (Module 4):** *Evaluate recommender systems using appropriate offline metrics, historical benchmarks, and statistical validation techniques.*
- **CO5:** *Critically analyse the limitations of historical-data-based evaluation, including popularity bias, cold-start, and temporal leakage.*

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifies that students must demonstrate competence in:
1. Selecting an appropriate **historical benchmark dataset** (e.g., **MovieLens**, **Netflix Prize**, **Amazon Reviews**, **Last.fm**, **Book-Crossing**).
2. Applying a **partitioning protocol** (Hold-out, *k*-fold Cross-Validation, Leave-One-Out, or Time-based Split).
3. Computing **prediction-oriented metrics** (MAE, RMSE) and **ranking-oriented metrics** (Precision@K, Recall@K, F1@K, MAP, NDCG, MRR, Hit-Rate).
4. Reporting **beyond-accuracy metrics** (Coverage, Diversity, Novelty, Serendipity).
5. Performing **statistical significance testing** (paired *t*-test, Wilcoxon signed-rank, McNemar).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The official KTU Module 4 syllabus (PECDT741) explicitly groups evaluation under *"Offline Evaluation with Historical Data Sets"*, emphasising the use of **public benchmark datasets** and **standardised metrics** so that results are *reproducible* across research papers and assignment reports.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **chef developing a new recipe** for a restaurant. Before you ever serve it to a paying customer, you would:

1. Cook a small batch in the **kitchen** and let trusted tasters score it.
2. Compare its score against your *old* recipes using a **standardised rating sheet** (taste, texture, presentation).
3. Only when it beats the old recipes *consistently* do you put it on the menu.

**Offline evaluation works exactly the same way.** The "kitchen" is your workstation, the "tasters" are the *historical users* whose past behaviour is frozen in a dataset, the "rating sheet" is a set of mathematical metrics, and the "menu" is the live recommender system that real users will see.

| Chef Analogy | Offline Evaluation |
|--------------|---------------------|
| Past diners' reviews | Historical interaction log |
| Standardised rating sheet | MAE, RMSE, Precision@K, NDCG |
| Trusted tasters in kitchen | Held-out users in test set |
| New recipe vs. old recipe | Candidate algorithm vs. baseline |
| Serving paying customers | Online A/B test (later stage) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** Offline evaluation is *replaying* the past on a computer to predict how good a recommender *might* be in the future — fast, cheap, safe, but not a perfect substitute for live user testing.

---

## 1.3 Why Historical Data Sets Are Used

Historical data sets are the **bedrock** of offline evaluation because they offer:

- **Reproducibility** – every researcher uses the *same* data, so algorithms can be compared fairly.
- **Scale** – millions of interactions (e.g., **MovieLens 25M** contains ~25 million ratings).
- **Cost-effectiveness** – no need to recruit real users for preliminary testing.
- **Controlled experimentation** – the experimenter can choose the train/test split deliberately.
- **Public availability** – peer-reviewable benchmarks strengthen research rigour.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Real-World Connection:** Major technology companies (Netflix, YouTube, Spotify) maintain *internal* historical logs of comparable size. The public datasets are *proxies* that allow academic algorithms to be benchmarked before being considered for production deployment.

---

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Offline Evaluation

Every offline evaluation study, regardless of complexity, rests on **three pillars**:

```
   PILLAR 1              PILLAR 2              PILLAR 3
  ┌──────────┐         ┌──────────┐         ┌──────────┐
  │ DATASET  │  ──▶    │  METRIC  │  ──▶    │ PROTOCOL │
  │  (Data)  │         │  (Score) │         │  (Split) │
  └──────────┘         └──────────┘         └──────────┘
  MovieLens 25M         Precision@K          80/20 Hold-out
  Netflix Prize         NDCG@10              5-Fold CV
  Amazon Reviews        RMSE                 Time-based
```

A change in *any* pillar can flip the conclusion of an experiment — this is why KTU examiners repeatedly test whether students understand the *interdependence* of these three pillars.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Offline Evaluation Pipeline — Operational Logic

The complete offline evaluation procedure can be decomposed into **six sequential stages**. Each stage is a potential exam question in the KTU 2024 ESE.

### Stage 1 — Dataset Selection
Choose a benchmark that matches the *target domain* of your recommender. For example, **MovieLens** is canonical for movie recommendation, while **Amazon Reviews** is canonical for e-commerce.

### Stage 2 — Pre-processing
- Filter sparse users/items (e.g., remove users with < 5 interactions).
- Convert explicit ratings to implicit feedback (e.g., rating ≥ 4 = "like").
- Resolve duplicates and timestamp inconsistencies.

### Stage 3 — Partitioning Protocol
- **Hold-out 80/20:** 80 % train, 20 % test (single split).
- ***k*-Fold Cross-Validation:** divide into *k* equal folds, train on *k−1*, test on the remaining one; rotate *k* times.
- **Leave-One-Out (LOO):** extreme case of *k*-fold where *k = n*; test on a single held-out interaction.
- **Time-based Split:** split by **timestamp** to mimic real-world deployment (training on past, testing on future).

### Stage 4 — Candidate Algorithm Training
Train each candidate recommender (e.g., User-KNN, Item-KNN, Matrix Factorisation, BPR, NeuMF) on the **training partition only**.

### Stage 5 — Prediction / Ranking Generation
For every user in the test partition:
- **Rating prediction task:** predict the user's rating for a held-out item.
- **Top-N ranking task:** generate a ranked list of *N* items the user has not seen during training.

### Stage 6 — Metric Computation & Statistical Testing
Compute metrics on the test predictions. Apply a *significance test* (e.g., **paired *t*-test** for metric means, **Wilcoxon signed-rank** for non-parametric comparison) to confirm that observed differences are not due to chance.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for KTU ESE Module 4 numerical problems. Memorise the formula, the *range* of values, and the *interpretation* of each metric.

| # | Metric | Mathematical Formula | Range | Best Value | Use Case |
|---|--------|----------------------|-------|------------|----------|
| 1 | **MAE** — Mean Absolute Error | $\text{MAE} = \dfrac{1}{\vert T \vert} \sum_{(u,i) \in T} \big\vert r_{ui} - \hat{r}_{ui} \big\vert$ | $[0, +\infty)$ | **0** | Rating prediction |
| 2 | **RMSE** — Root Mean Squared Error | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\vert T \vert} \sum_{(u,i) \in T} (r_{ui} - \hat{r}_{ui})^{2}}$ | $[0, +\infty)$ | **0** | Rating prediction (penalises large errors more) |
| 3 | **Precision@K** | $P@K = \dfrac{\vert \text{Recommended}_K \cap \text{Relevant} \vert}{K}$ | $[0, 1]$ | **1** | Top-N ranking — quality |
| 4 | **Recall@K** | $R@K = \dfrac{\vert \text{Recommended}_K \cap \text{Relevant} \vert}{\vert \text{Relevant} \vert}$ | $[0, 1]$ | **1** | Top-N ranking — coverage of relevant items |
| 5 | **F1@K** | $F_1@K = \dfrac{2 \cdot P@K \cdot R@K}{P@K + R@K}$ | $[0, 1]$ | **1** | Harmonic mean of P and R |
| 6 | **Hit-Rate@K** (HR@K) | $HR@K = \dfrac{1}{\vert U \vert} \sum_{u \in U} \mathbb{1}[\text{hit}_u@K]$ | $[0, 1]$ | **1** | Whether *any* relevant item appears in top-K |
| 7 | **MAP** — Mean Average Precision | $MAP = \dfrac{1}{\vert U \vert} \sum_{u \in U} AP_u$, where $AP_u = \dfrac{1}{m_u}\sum_{k=1}^{K} P_u(k) \cdot \text{rel}_u(k)$ | $[0, 1]$ | **1** | Overall ranking quality, single relevant item |
| 8 | **MRR** — Mean Reciprocal Rank | $MRR = \dfrac{1}{\vert U \vert} \sum_{u \in U} \dfrac{1}{\text{rank}_u^{\text{first hit}}}$ | $[0, 1]$ | **1** | Sensitivity to position of first correct item |
| 9 | **DCG@K** — Discounted Cumulative Gain | $DCG@K = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \dfrac{\text{rel}_k}{\log_2(k+1)}$ | $[0, +\infty)$ | Larger is better | Graded relevance, position-aware |
| 10 | **NDCG@K** — Normalised DCG | $NDCG@K = \dfrac{DCG@K}{IDCG@K}$ | $[0, 1]$ | **1** | Graded relevance, comparable across users |
| 11 | **Coverage** | $C = \dfrac{\vert \bigcup_{u} R_u \vert}{\vert I \vert}$ | $[0, 1]$ | **1** | Fraction of catalogue that can be recommended |
| 12 | **Gini Index** (Inequality of exposure) | $G = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{\vert I \vert} (2i - \vert I \vert - 1) \, p_{(i)}}{\vert I \vert \sum p_{(i)}}$ | $[0, 1]$ | **0** = perfectly equal | Popularity bias detection |
| 13 | **Paired *t*-test statistic** | $t = \dfrac{\bar{d}}{s_d / \sqrt{n}}$ | $\mathbb{R}$ | $p < 0.05$ | Statistical significance of mean difference |
| 14 | **Wilcoxon statistic** | $W = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{sgn}(d_i) \cdot R_i$ | $\mathbb{Z}$ | $p < 0.05$ | Non-parametric significance test |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** In RMSE, the squaring makes the unit equal to the *square* of the rating unit. If a question says "ratings are on a 1–5 scale", your RMSE is *also* on a 1–5 scale (the square root restores the unit). Many students erroneously report the *MSE* (without the square root) and lose a mark.

---

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Rationale |
|------|-----------|
| Use a **public dataset** | Enables reproducibility and fair peer comparison. |
| **Hide** the test set | Prevents *data leakage* and simulates "unseen future". |
| Predict **only on held-out items** | Tests generalisation, not memorisation. |
| Use **multiple metrics** | No single metric captures "good recommendation". |
| Apply a **time-based split** | Avoids *temporal leakage* (using future to predict past). |
| Run a **significance test** | Random folds can produce misleading differences. |

---

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

Offline evaluation is **industry-critical**. The deployment funnel of any production recommender is:

```
Offline Evaluation ──▶ Offline Replay ──▶ Interleaving ──▶ A/B Test ──▶ Full Rollout
       (1 day)              (1 week)         (1–2 weeks)     (2–4 weeks)    (gradual)
```

Hundreds of candidate algorithms are killed in the **offline stage** because they fail to beat baselines. Only the top 1–2 % survive to the expensive A/B test. Therefore, **designing correct offline evaluation is the single highest-leverage skill** in industrial recommender work.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Connection:** Netflix famously published that its offline RMSE improvements translate to *real* but *non-linear* business gains. A 0.01 RMSE drop on a held-out dataset can be worth millions of dollars if it survives A/B testing.

---

## 2.5 Limitations of Offline Evaluation (Critical KTU Theme)

| Limitation | Description |
|------------|-------------|
| **Popularity bias** | Offline metrics favour recommending popular items because they appear more often in test sets. |
| **Exposure bias** | Users only rate items they have been *exposed* to, so the data is MNAR (Missing-Not-At-Random). |
| **Cold-start blindness** | New users/items have no history, so offline tests cannot evaluate cold-start handling. |
| **Filter-bubble effect** | Offline metrics rarely measure *diversity* or *serendipity*, encouraging narrow recommendations. |
