<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define shilling attack in recommender systems. List any four types of shilling attacks.*

**Model Answer:**
A **shilling attack** (or profile injection attack) is a malicious act where an adversary injects a set of fake user profiles into a recommender system with the goal of **promoting** (push attack) or **demoting** (nuke attack) the recommendation frequency of a specific target item $i_t$.

Four types of shilling attacks:
1. **Random Attack** — filler items rated randomly
2. **Average Attack** — filler items rated at global mean
3. **Bandwagon Attack** — selected items are popular items
4. **Segment Attack** — selected items belong to one category

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]**
*Distinguish between robustness and stability of a recommender system with an example each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Robustness | Stability |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Resilience against malicious / adversarial input | Consistency of output under small input changes |
| **Threat** | Fake profiles, data poisoning | Natural perturbation, concept drift |
| **Metric** | $\Delta HR@N$, attack impact ratio | Prediction Shift, Rank Shift |
| **Example** | System ignores 500 fake 5-star reviews of a bad product | User's recommendation list does not shuffle wildly when he adds one new rating |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Robustness Focused*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper, Modified Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the structural components of a shilling attack profile. Illustrate with a diagram how a **bandwagon attack** is constructed. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A shilling attack profile has four components:
1. **Target item $i_t$** — the item to influence
2. **Selected items $I_S$** — items chosen strategically
3. **Filler items $I_F$** — random items for disguise
4. **Unrated items** — left blank for realism

For a **bandwagon push attack**:
* $i_t$ receives $r_{max}$ (e.g., 5/5)
* $I_S$ = list of popular items (e.g., *Avengers*, *Inception*, *Titanic*) — these also get $r_{max}$
* $I_F$ = randomly chosen items rated at $r_{min}$ to maintain plausibility
* Remaining items are unrated

**Why it works:** The attacker becomes "similar" to many genuine users who like popular movies, so neighborhood-based CF recommends $i_t$ to those users.

**Diagram (textual):**

```
Attack Profile
├── Target item i_t        → r_max
├── Selected items I_S     → r_max (popular blockbusters)
├── Filler items I_F       → r_min (disguise)
└── Unrated items          → blank
```

**(b)** Compute the **prediction shift** for a user whose predicted ratings changed as follows:
$M$: $[4.0, 3.5, 5.0, 2.0]$
$M'$: $[4.6, 3.4, 4.5, 2.8]$
Also state what the result implies about stability. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute per-item absolute difference.

$$
\delta = [\vert 4.6-4.0\vert, \vert 3.4-3.5\vert, \vert 4.5-5.0\vert, \vert 2.8-2.0\vert] = [0.6, 0.1, 0.5, 0.8]
$$

**Step 2:** Compute mean prediction shift.

$$
PS = \frac{1}{4}(0.6 + 0.1 + 0.5 + 0.8) = \frac{2.0}{4} = 0.5
$$

**Step 3:** Interpretation.

A $PS = 0.5$ on a 5-point rating scale represents a **10% deviation**, indicating **moderate stability loss**. The system is moderately sensitive to the perturbation; production deployment would require additional regularization (e.g., temporal smoothing, L2 penalty, or ensemble methods) to enhance stability.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Stability Focused*

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper, Module 4 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Define **stability** in recommender systems. Derive the **Spearman Rank Correlation** formula for measuring rank stability between two recommendation lists. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Stability is the property that small changes in input data, user behavior, or training set produce only proportionally small changes in the recommendation output.

**Derivation of Spearman Rank Correlation:**

Let $L_u = \{i_1, i_2, ..., i_N\}$ and $L'_u = \{i_1, i_2, ..., i_N\}$ be two top-N lists.

**Step 1:** Assign rank positions: $r_i$ for item $i$ in $L_u$ and $r'_i$ in $L'_u$.

**Step 2:** Compute squared rank difference.

$$
d_i^2 = (r_i - r'_i)^2
$$

**Step 3:** Sum over all N items.

$$
D = \sum_{i=1}^{N} d_i^2
$$

**Step 4:** Apply Spearman formula (derived from Pearson correlation on ranks).

$$
RS = 1 - \frac{6D}{N(N^2 - 1)}
$$

**Step 5:** Range and meaning.
* $RS = +1$: identical ranking — perfect stability
* $RS = -1$: perfectly inverted ranking — maximum instability
* $RS = 0$: no rank correlation

**(b)** The top-5 recommended items for user $u$ under models $M$ and $M'$ are:
$M$: [A, B, C, D, E]
$M'$: [B, A, D, C, E]
Compute the **Spearman rank correlation** and interpret. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Assign ranks (position index).

| Item | Rank in M ($r_i$) | Rank in M' ($r'_i$) | $d_i$ | $d_i^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 1 | 2 | -1 | 1 |
| B | 2 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| C | 3 | 4 | -1 | 1 |
| D | 4 | 3 | 1 | 1 |
| E | 5 | 5 | 0 | 0 |

**Step 2:** Sum of $d_i^2$.

$$
D = 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 0 = 4
$$

**Step 3:** Apply formula with $N = 5$.

$$
RS = 1 - \frac{6 \times 4}{5(25 - 1)} = 1 - \frac{24}{120} = 1 - 0.2 = 0.8
$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation.

$RS = 0.8$ indicates **strong rank stability**. Only minor reordering (A↔B and C↔D) occurred; the system is largely consistent across the two model states — desirable for production deployment.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing robustness with stability** — These are distinct concepts (robustness = adversarial, stability = sensitivity). Examiners award 0 if the definition is swapped.
> 2. **Forgetting units in PS** — Always state "out of 5" or scale used; otherwise 1 mark deduction.
> 3. **Wrong denominator in Spearman** — Common mistake: using $N^3$ instead of $N(N^2 - 1)$.
> 4. **Not interpreting the result** — A numerical answer without "what it implies" loses 2 marks easily.
> 5. **Mixing push vs. nuke attacks** — Always specify which one when discussing bandwagon/segment attacks.
> 6. **Skipping the structural components of an attack profile** — Examiners expect $i_t, I_S, I_F$ to be listed explicitly.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* **Robustness** → defense against **adversarial / malicious input**
* **Stability** → consistency of output under **small input changes**
* **Shilling attack profile** has **4 components**: $i_t$, $I_S$, $I_F$, unrated items
* **5 attack types**: Random, Average, Bandwagon, Segment, Love/Hate
* **Prediction Shift (PS)** formula uses absolute difference averaged over items
* **Spearman Rank Correlation** formula: $RS = 1 - \frac{6 \sum d_i^2}{N(N^2-1)}$
* **Jaccard similarity** of top-N lists measures list-level stability
* **Robustness drop** = $HR@N_{clean} - HR@N_{attacked}$
* **Stable system** → $PS \approx 0$, $RS \approx 1$
* **Robust system** → $\Delta HR@N \approx 0$ under attack
* **Defense strategies**: outlier detection, mean-deviation filtering, popularity-debiasing, rating clipping, cluster-aware pruning
* **KTU exam tip**: Always pair numerical answers with interpretation; always define acronyms (PS, RS, HR@N) on first use
* **Production reality**: Netflix, Amazon, and Spotify all run stability and robustness monitoring continuously; any system without these guarantees is **not deployment-ready**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scalability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT741 - RECOMMENDATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Scalability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scalability** in a Recommendation System is the capacity of the algorithmic and architectural framework to maintain acceptable levels of **accuracy**, **latency**, and **throughput** as the size of the user base ($U$), the item catalog ($I$), the interaction log ($|R|$), and the request rate ($\lambda$) grow — often by **orders of magnitude** — without a proportional blow-up in computational cost, memory footprint, or response time.

In formal terms, a recommender algorithm is said to be **horizontally scalable** if its runtime complexity grows **sub-quadratically** with respect to the number of users and items, and **vertically scalable** if it can exploit additional compute, memory, and storage resources (CPU cores, RAM, distributed nodes) to sustain performance as data volume increases.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology (must use in answers):**
> - **Data Scalability** – ability to grow with dataset size.
> - **Computational Scalability** – ability to grow with available compute.
> - **Latency ($\tau$)** – end-to-end response time, typically in **milliseconds (ms)**.
> - **Throughput ($\lambda$)** – recommendations served per second (**req/s**).
> - **Memory Footprint** – RAM consumed by index structures, in **GB / TB**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **local kirana store** (small shop) where the shopkeeper personally remembers every customer and recommends products. This works for 50 customers and 500 products — but it **collapses** when the store becomes a **supermarket** with 50,000 customers and 50,000 products.

> **The shopkeeper problem = the naive recommender problem.**

To scale, the supermarket must:
1. **Pre-arrange products** in aisles (≡ **indexing / clustering**).
2. **Train floor managers** who know *categories*, not every customer (≡ **model-based CF / matrix factorization**).
3. **Use barcodes & computer systems** for fast billing (≡ **approximate nearest-neighbor search**).
4. **Open multiple billing counters** during rush hours (≡ **distributed / parallel computing**).
5. **Recommend online** using past purchase data (≡ **offline pre-computation + online lookup**).

Every one of these is a **scalability technique** used in real-world recommenders like Netflix, Amazon, and Spotify.

---

## 1.3 Where Scalability Fits in the Recommendation Pipeline

The classical pipeline has three stages, each with its own scalability bottleneck:

| Stage | Operation | Scalability Concern |
|---|---|---|
| **Offline** | Model training on full user–item matrix | Training time, RAM, storage |
| **Nearline** | Incremental updates, candidate generation | Mini-batch latency |
| **Online** | Real-time ranking & serving | Query latency, QPS |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever a question asks *"Why can't we use User-User CF on a billion-user platform?"*, the answer must always mention the **$O(U^2 \cdot I)$** complexity, the **memory explosion** of the user-similarity matrix, and the **online latency** of neighborhood lookups.

---

## 1.4 Dimensions of Scalability

A KTU-style answer should explicitly name the four orthogonal dimensions:

1. **User Scalability** – system handles growing $U$.
2. **Item Scalability** – system handles growing $I$.
3. **Rating Scalability** – system handles growing $|R|$ (interactions).
4. **Real-time Scalability** – system handles growing query load $\lambda$ with bounded $\tau$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Scalability Problem — Complexity Analysis

The fundamental scalability issue is that the **naïve collaborative filtering** approach has prohibitive asymptotic costs.

### 2.1.1 User-User Collaborative Filtering

To compute top-$N$ recommendations for **one active user**:
- Build the user–item rating matrix $R \in \mathbb{R}^{U \times I}$.
- Compute similarity (e.g., Pearson, cosine) between the active user and **all** other users: $U - 1$ similarity computations.
- For each similarity, scan all common items: $O(I)$ in the worst case.

**Per-query complexity:**

$$T_{\text{user-CF}}(N) = O\bigl((U-1) \cdot I\bigr) = O(U \cdot I)$$

**Full-system complexity (one recommendation for all users):**

$$T_{\text{user-CF}}^{\text{full}} = O(U^2 \cdot I)$$

**Memory required to store the user-similarity matrix:**

$$M_{\text{user-sim}} = O\!\left(\frac{U(U-1)}{2}\right) \text{ floats}$$

For $U = 10^8$ users (≈ Netflix scale), this matrix needs ≈ **40 petabytes** even at 8-byte floats — clearly infeasible.

### 2.1.2 Item-Item Collaborative Filtering

Item similarities are more stable over time, so they can be **pre-computed offline**:

$$T_{\text{item-CF}}^{\text{offline}} = O(U \cdot I^2)$$

$$M_{\text{item-sim}} = O\!\left(\frac{I(I-1)}{2}\right)$$

But online querying becomes cheap:

$$T_{\text{item-CF}}^{\text{online}}(N) = O(I \cdot k)$$

where $k$ is the number of items the active user has rated. This is the **key reason item-item CF scales better** than user-user CF — and why Amazon famously uses it.

### 2.1.3 Matrix Factorization

Latent factor models project users and items into a $d$-dimensional space:

$$\hat{r}_{ui} = \mathbf{p}_u^{\top} \mathbf{q}_i = \sum_{f=1}^{d} p_{uf} \cdot q_{if}$$

- **Training complexity (one epoch over $|R|$ ratings):** $O(|R| \cdot d \cdot \text{iter})$
- **Storage:** $O((U + I) \cdot d)$ — linear in $U$ and $I$.
- **Online prediction:** $O(d)$ — constant time per user–item pair.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Matrix Factorization reduces the storage from $O(U^2 + I^2)$ to $O\bigl((U+I) \cdot d\bigr)$ where $d \ll \min(U, I)$. For Netflix with $U = 5 \times 10^8$ and $I = 10^5$, $d = 100$ gives a **10-million-fold reduction** in storage.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains **every formula you must memorize** for the Scalability topic in the KTU 2024 ESE.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Typical Range (Industry) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | User-User CF full complexity | $O(U^2 \cdot I)$ | operations | Infeasible at $U > 10^6$ |
| 2 | Item-Item CF offline complexity | $O(U \cdot I^2)$ | operations | Feasible for moderate $I$ |
| 3 | Item-Item CF online complexity | $O(I \cdot k)$ | operations | $k$ = # rated items |
| 4 | Matrix Factorization storage | $O((U+I) \cdot d)$ | floats | $\sim 10^{11}$ floats |
| 5 | Latent factor prediction | $\hat{r}_{ui} = \mathbf{p}_u^{\top} \mathbf{q}_i + b_u + b_i + \mu$ | rating | range $[1, 5]$ |
| 6 | SVD reconstruction (rank-$d$) | $\hat{R}_d = U_d \Sigma_d V_d^{\top}$ | rating matrix | rank $\le d$ |
| 7 | LSH collision probability (sim) | $P(\text{collision}) = 1 - (1 - s^r)^b$ | probability | $s$ = Jaccard, $r, b$ = # hash fns |
| 8 | MinHash signature complexity | $O(n \cdot h)$ per set | operations | $h$ = # permutations |
| 9 | Cosine similarity | $\text{sim}(u,v) = \frac{\mathbf{r}_u \cdot \mathbf{r}_v}{\Vert \mathbf{r}_u \Vert_2 \Vert \mathbf{r}_v \Vert_2}$ | unitless | $[-1, 1]$ |
| 10 | Pearson similarity | $\text{sim}(u,v) = \frac{\sum_i (r_{ui}-\bar r_u)(r_{vi}-\bar r_v)}{\sqrt{\sum_i (r_{ui}-\bar r_u)^2}\sqrt{\sum_i (r_{vi}-\bar r_v)^2}}$ | unitless | $[-1, 1]$ |
| 11 | RMSE objective (MF) | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\vert T \vert}\sum_{(u,i) \in T}(\hat r_{ui} - r_{ui})^2}$ | rating | lower = better |
| 12 | SGD update rule (MF) | $p_{uf} \leftarrow p_{uf} + \eta (e_{ui} q_{if} - \lambda p_{uf})$ | float | $\eta$ = learning rate |
| 13 | ALS per-iteration cost | $O((U+I) \cdot d^2 + \vert R \vert \cdot d)$ | operations | parallelizable |
| 14 | Sharding key size (user-based) | $S = \lceil U / N_{\text{shards}} \rceil$ | users/shard | $N_{\text{shards}}$ = nodes |
| 15 | Throughput (Little's Law) | $\lambda = \frac{N_{\text{requests}}}{\Delta t}$ | req/s | industry: 10$^3$–10$^6$ |
| 16 | Latency budget (online) | $\tau_{\text{online}} \le 100$ ms | ms | web: $\le 200$ ms |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** Do **NOT** confuse user-user and item-item complexity. A common mark-losing error is to write $O(I^2 \cdot U)$ for user-user CF. The dimension along which you compute similarities determines the complexity.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Where Scalability Techniques Are Used |
|---|---|
| **E-commerce (Amazon)** | Item-item CF pre-computed offline; ANN search for candidate retrieval |
| **Streaming (Netflix, Spotify)** | Matrix factorization + deep embeddings; FAISS for ANN lookup |
| **Social Networks (Facebook, X)** | Graph-based recommendations using distributed GraphX / Spark |
| **News (Google News)** | MinHash + LSH for near-duplicate detection across millions of articles |
| **Ad-Tech (Google Ads)** | Distributed logistic regression on billion-row click logs |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From $O(U^2 \cdot I)$ to $O(d^3 + (U+I)d^2)$ via Matrix Factorization

We derive the asymptotic gain of using **SVD-based dimensionality reduction** over naïve user-user CF.

### Step 1 — Start with the User-Item Rating Matrix

Let $R \in \mathbb{R}^{U \times I}$ be the sparse user-item rating matrix with $U$ users, $I$ items, and $|R|$ observed entries.

$$R = \begin{bmatrix} r_{11} & r_{12} & \cdots & r_{1I} \\ r_{21} & r_{22} & \cdots & r_{2I} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ r_{U1} & r_{U2} & \cdots & r_{UI} \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Full Singular Value Decomposition

By the SVD theorem, any rectangular matrix $R \in \mathbb{R}^{U \times I}$ can be factorized as:

$$R = U \Sigma V^{\top}$$

where:
- $U \in \mathbb{R}^{U \times U}$ is an orthogonal matrix of left singular vectors.
- $\Sigma \in \mathbb{R}^{U \times I}$ is a diagonal matrix of singular values $\sigma_1 \ge \sigma_2 \ge \cdots \ge \sigma_{\min(U,I)} \ge 0$.
- $V \in \mathbb{R}^{I \times I}$ is an orthogonal matrix of right singular vectors.

### Step 3 — Truncate to Rank-$d$ Approximation

Keeping only the top $d$ singular values gives the best rank-$d$ approximation in the **Frobenius norm** (Eckart–Young–Mirsky theorem):

$$\hat R_d = U_d \Sigma_d V_d^{\top}$$

where $U_d \in \mathbb{R}^{U \times d}$, $\Sigma_d \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$, $V_d \in \mathbb{R}^{I \times d}$.

### Step 4 — Compute the Latent Factor Matrices

Define the user-latent matrix $P = U_d \Sigma_d^{1/2} \in \mathbb{R}^{U \times d}$ and item-latent matrix $Q = \Sigma_d^{1/2} V_d^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times I}$, so:

$$\hat R_d = P Q$$

For any user $u$ and item $i$:

$$\hat r_{ui} = \mathbf{p}_u^{\top} \mathbf{q}_i = \sum_{f=1}^{d} p_{uf} \cdot q_{if}$$

### Step 5 — Storage Cost Comparison

**Naïve user-similarity matrix storage:**

$$M_{\text{user-sim}} = \binom{U}{2} = \frac{U(U-1)}{2} \approx \frac{U^2}{2} \text{ floats}$$

**MF storage:**

$$M_{\text{MF}} = (U + I) \cdot d \text{ floats}$$

**Storage ratio:**

$$\rho_{\text{storage}} = \frac{M_{\text{MF}}}{M_{\text{user-sim}}} = \frac{2(U+I)d}{U(U-1)} \approx \frac{2d}{U} \text{ for } U \gg I$$

### Step 6 — Computational Cost Comparison

**Naïve user-user CF (one prediction for all users):**

$$T_{\text{naive}} = O(U^2 \cdot I)$$

**MF training (one SGD epoch over $|R|$ ratings, with $d$ features):**

$$T_{\text{MF}} = O(|R| \cdot d)$$

**MF online prediction (one user–item pair):**

$$T_{\text{predict}} = O(d)$$

### Step 7 — Numerical Example (Plug Realistic Numbers)

Assume $U = 10^7$ users, $I = 10^5$ items, $d = 100$, $|R| = 10^9$ ratings.

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{user-sim}} &\approx \frac{(10^7)^2}{2} = 5 \times 10^{13} \text{ floats} \approx 400 \text{ TB} \\[4pt]
M_{\text{MF}} &= (10^7 + 10^5) \times 100 \approx 1.01 \times 10^9 \text{ floats} \approx 8 \text{ GB} \\[4pt]
T_{\text{naive}} &\propto 10^7 \times 10^7 \times 10^5 = 10^{19} \text{ ops} \\[4pt]
T_{\text{MF, train}} &\propto 10^9 \times 100 = 10^{11} \text{ ops}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** MF achieves a **$\mathbf{10^8$-fold reduction** in storage and **$\mathbf{10^8}$-fold reduction** in training compute.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trick:** Always quote the Eckart–Young theorem and the exact numerical substitution. Marks are awarded for *showing the math*, not just stating "MF is faster."

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Scalability Techniques Mapped to Bottlenecks

| Bottleneck | Technique | How it scales | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| **$O(U^2)$ similarity matrix** | Matrix Factorization (SVD / ALS) | $O((U+I)d)$ | Loses some neighborhood interpretability |
| **Sparse matrix too large** | Dimensionality Reduction (PCA, SVD) | $O(d^2(U+I))$ | Approximation error |
| **Slow nearest-neighbor lookup** | Approximate NN (LSH, FAISS, Annoy) | Sub-linear query time | Small loss in recall |
| **Long training time** | Mini-batch SGD, ALS, distributed Spark | Linear in #workers | Communication overhead |
| **Real-time serving latency** | Pre-compute + cache + ANN index | $O(d)$ online | Stale embeddings |
| **Cold-start items** | Content-based + hybrid fallback | Per-item feature lookup | Lower accuracy for new items |
| **Billions of users** | Sharding / partitioning by user-ID | $O(U / N)$ per shard | Cross-shard joins expensive |
| **Streaming data** | Incremental / online learning (FTRL, River) | $O(d)$ per update | Drift over time |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Scalability Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **end-to-end scalable recommendation architecture** as deployed at industry scale. It maps how data flows through the offline, nearline, and online layers, and where each scalability technique is applied.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Raw Interaction Logs Clicks Views Purchases] --> B[Data Lake HDFS S3]
    B --> C[Preprocessing Spark ETL]
    C --> D[Feature Store User and Item Features]
    D --> E[Offline Trainer Spark MLlib ALS SGD]
    E --> F[Model Zoo P Q b_u b_i]
    F --> G[Embeddings Store Redis FAISS HNSW]
    F --> H[Item Similarity Cache Pre-computed]
    G --> I[Candidate Retrieval ANN FAISS Top 500]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Ranking Model Deep Learning GBM]
    J --> K[Business Rules Filters Diversification]
    K --> L[Online Serving API gRPC REST]
    L --> M[Client Browser Mobile App]

    subgraph OfflineLayer[Offline Layer Hours to Days]
        B
        C
        D
        E
        F
    end

    subgraph NearlineLayer[Nearline Layer Minutes]
        G
        H
        I
    end

    subgraph OnlineLayer[Online Layer Under 100 ms]
        J
        K
        L
    end
```

### Reading the Diagram

- **Offline Layer** handles **batch training** of matrix factorization or deep models using Spark / MapReduce.
- **Nearline Layer** keeps the **ANN index** and **item-similarity cache** fresh, generating ~500 candidate items per query in <10 ms.
- **Online Layer** runs the **heavy ranker** (a deep neural net or gradient-boosted tree) on the small candidate set, then applies business rules and serves in <100 ms.

---

## 4.2 Scalability Bottleneck Resolution Flowchart

This diagram shows **how the system decides** which scalability technique to apply when a bottleneck is detected.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S[Start System Grows] --> Q1{User Count Increases?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Computing Pairwise Similarity?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| A1[Apply Matrix Factorization ALS SVD]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Real Time Latency Too High?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q4{Item Count Increases?}

    Q3 -->|Yes| A2[Use Approximate Nearest Neighbor FAISS Annoy LSH]
    Q3 -->|No| A3[Cluster Users and Items Co-Clustering]

    Q4 -->|Yes| Q5{Item Catalog Very Large?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| A4[Two Tower Embedding Models]
    Q5 -->|No| A6[Item Item CF with Sharding]

    A1 --> Z[Sub Linear Storage and Latency Achieved]
    A2 --> Z
    A3 --> Z
    A4 --> Z
    A6 --> Z
```

---

## 4.3 Complexity Comparison Visual

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[Naive User User CF O of U squared times I] -.->|10^19 ops| X[Infeasible]
    N2[Item Item CF Pre-compute] -.->|10^12 ops| Y[Feasible for moderate scale]
    N3[Matrix Factorization ALS] -.->|10^11 ops| W[Industry Standard]
    N4[Two Tower Deep + ANN] -.->|10^9 ops| V[Hyperscale]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to draw a scalability diagram, always include the **three-layer split (offline / nearline / online)** and label the **technique used at each layer** with its time budget.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define scalability in the context of recommendation systems. List any two challenges that arise when a recommender system is scaled to millions of users.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Scalability is the ability of a recommendation system to maintain acceptable accuracy, latency, and throughput as the number of users, items, and interactions grows without a proportional increase in computational cost, memory, or response time.

**Two Challenges (1.5 marks):**
1. **Memory explosion** — the user-user or item-item similarity matrix grows as $O(U^2)$ or $O(I^2)$, exceeding available RAM at millions of users.
2. **Latency degradation** — online nearest-neighbor queries become too slow (>200 ms) when scanning millions of candidates, breaking real-time SLAs.
3. *(any other valid)* Cold-start latency for new users, distributed join overhead, embedding staleness.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*Differentiate between horizontal scalability and vertical scalability. Give one real-world example of each in a recommender system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Horizontal Scalability | Vertical Scalability |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition (1 mark)** | Adding more machines/nodes to distribute the load. | Adding more CPU/RAM/disk to a single machine. |
| **Example in recommender (1 mark each)** | Sharding users across 1000 Spark workers for ALS training. | Upgrading a single FAISS index server from 64 GB to 1 TB RAM. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a clear block diagram, explain the **three-tier scalable architecture** of a modern recommendation system (offline, nearline, online). For each tier, mention **one scalability technique** and its typical **time budget**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **user-user collaborative filtering** and **matrix factorization** in terms of (i) storage complexity, (ii) online prediction latency, and (iii) handling of new users. Show the mathematical expressions clearly.

---

### Model Answer — Question A

#### Part (a) — Three-Tier Scalable Architecture (7 marks)

| Tier | Time Budget | Operations | Scalability Technique |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Offline (1.5 marks)** | Hours – Days | Batch training on full user-item log | **Distributed Matrix Factorization** using Spark MLlib ALS — runs on hundreds of workers; complexity $O((U+I) d^2 + \vert R \vert d)$ per iteration, parallelized across data shards. |
| **Nearline (1.5 marks)** | Seconds – Minutes | Incremental embedding updates, candidate generation | **Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) indexing** using FAISS, Annoy, or ScaNN — sub-linear query time using HNSW or IVF-PQ graphs; serves top-500 candidates in <10 ms. |
| **Online (1.5 marks)** | < 100 ms | Final ranking, business rules, response | **Two-stage retrieval-then-rank** with pre-computed feature store; pre-load user embedding in Redis cache; LightGBM or shallow DNN for re-ranking over the small candidate set. |

**Block Diagram (1 mark):**

```
   ┌──────────── Offline (Hours-Days) ─────────────┐
   │  Logs → Spark ETL → ALS Training → Embeddings  │
   └────────────────────┬───────────────────────────┘
                        ▼
   ┌──────────── Nearline (Seconds-Minutes) ────────┐
   │  Embeddings → FAISS Index → Top-500 Candidates │
   └────────────────────┬───────────────────────────┘
                        ▼
   ┌──────────── Online (<100 ms) ─────────────────┐
   │  Ranking → Business Rules → API Response        │
   └────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Conclusion (1 mark):** This decoupled architecture allows each tier to scale *independently* — offline scales by adding compute nodes, nearline by sharding the ANN index, online by replicating API servers behind a load balancer.

---

#### Part (b) — User-User CF vs Matrix Factorization (7 marks)

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Criterion | User-User CF | Matrix Factorization |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Storage Complexity** | $O\!\left(\dfrac{U(U-1)}{2}\right) \approx O(U^2)$ similarity values; explodes to TBs at $U = 10^8$. | $O\bigl((U+I) \cdot d\bigr)$ latent vectors; $d \ll \min(U,I)$ — typically GBs. |
| **(ii) Online Latency** | $O(U \cdot I)$ per query — must scan all users and items. | $O(d)$ per query — single dot product $\mathbf{p}_u^{\top} \mathbf{q}_i$. |
| **(iii) New User Handling** | Requires recomputing similarity with **all** existing users; slow. | Requires training a **single** new latent vector $\mathbf{p}_{\text{new}}$; $O(|R_{\text{new}}| \cdot d)$. |

**Mathematical Expression (3 marks):**

For **user-user CF**, the predicted rating is a weighted average over the $K$ most similar users $v \in \mathcal{N}_K(u)$:

$$\hat r_{ui} = \bar r_u + \frac{\sum_{v \in \mathcal{N}_K(u)} \text{sim}(u, v) \cdot (r_{vi} - \bar r_v)}{\sum_{v \in \mathcal{N}_K(u)} \vert \text{sim}(u, v) \vert}$$

For **matrix factorization**, the prediction is a dot product in latent space:

$$\hat r_{ui} = \mathbf{p}_u^{\top} \mathbf{q}_i = \sum_{f=1}^{d} p_{uf} \, q_{if}$$

**Numerical Justification (1 mark):** For $U = 10^7$, $I = 10^5$, $d = 100$:
- User-User CF storage $\approx 5 \times 10^{13}$ floats ($\sim$400 TB).
- MF storage $\approx 1.01 \times 10^9$ floats ($\sim$8 GB).
- **Reduction factor ≈ $5 \times 10^4$.**

---

### Question B — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model — Alternative]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **dimensionality reduction using Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)** as a scalability technique for collaborative filtering. Derive the rank-$d$ approximation formula and state the Eckart–Young theorem.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Locality-Sensitive Hashing (LSH)** technique for approximate nearest-neighbor search in large-scale recommender systems. Show how MinHash is used to estimate Jaccard similarity.

---

### Model Answer — Question B

#### Part (a) — SVD-based Dimensionality Reduction (7 marks)

**Conceptual Setup (1.5 marks):** The user-item rating matrix $R \in \mathbb{R}^{U \times I}$ is sparse and high-dimensional. SVD projects both users and items onto a low-dimensional latent space where recommendations are computed via simple dot products.

**SVD Theorem (1.5 marks):** For any $R \in \mathbb{R}^{U \times I}$, there exist orthogonal $U, V$ and diagonal $\Sigma$ such that:

$$R = U \Sigma V^{\top}$$

**Rank-$d$ Truncation (2 marks):** Keeping only the top $d$ singular values $\sigma_1, \dots, \sigma_d$:

$$\hat R_d = U_d \Sigma_d V_d^{\top} = \sum_{f=1}^{d} \sigma_f \, \mathbf{u}_f \mathbf{v}_f^{\top}$$

Defining $\mathbf{p}_u = (\sigma_1 u_{u1}, \dots, \sigma_d u_{ud})$ and $\mathbf{q}_i = (v_{1i}, \dots, v_{di})$:

$$\hat r_{ui} = \mathbf{p}_u^{\top} \mathbf{q}_i$$

**Eckart–Young–Mirsky Theorem (1 mark):** Among all rank-$d$ matrices $A$, the truncated SVD $\hat R_d$ minimizes the Frobenius norm error:

$$\hat R_d = \arg\min_{\text{rank}(A) \le d} \Vert R - A \Vert_F$$

**Why this scales (1 mark):** Complexity drops from $O(U \cdot I)$ storage to $O\bigl((U+I) \cdot d\bigr)$, and online prediction is $O(d)$ instead of $O(U \cdot I)$.

---

#### Part (b) — LSH and MinHash (7 marks)

**Motivation (1 mark):** Exact nearest-neighbor search over millions of users/items is $O(N)$ per query. LSH reduces this to **sub-linear** expected time by hashing similar items into the same bucket with high probability.

**MinHash for Jaccard Similarity (3 marks):** Given two sets $A, B$ (e.g., the set of users who rated an item), their Jaccard similarity is:

$$J(A, B) = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$$

A **MinHash** function $h_\pi$ applies a random permutation $\pi$ to the universal set and returns the index of the first element in the permuted order that belongs to $A$. The key property is:

$$P\bigl(h_\pi(A) = h_\pi(B)\bigr) = J(A, B)$$

Using $h$ independent permutations, the signature $\text{sig}(A) = (h_1(A), h_2(A), \dots, h_h(A))$ is computed in $O(\vert A \vert \cdot h)$ time. Jaccard similarity is then estimated as:

$$\hat J(A, B) = \frac{1}{h} \sum_{j=1}^{h} \mathbb{1}\bigl[\text{sig}_j(A) = \text{sig}_j(B)\bigr]$$

**LSH Banding (2 marks):** Split the $h$ signatures into $b$ bands of $r$ rows each ($h = b \cdot r$). Two items are declared candidates if they agree in **at least one full band**. The collision probability is:

$$P(\text{collision}) = 1 - \left(1 - s^r\right)^b$$

where $s = J(A, B)$. This S-curve lets us tune $b$ and $r$ to retrieve pairs with similarity above a threshold $s_t$ with high probability and reject pairs below it.

**Why this scales (1 mark):** Querying a bucket is $O(1)$ expected time. Total indexing cost is $O(N \cdot h)$, and per-query cost is $O(h \cdot b)$, both sub-linear in the catalog size.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in the Scalability Question (Examiner-observed):**
> 1. **Writing $O(n^2)$ without specifying $n$.** Always state whether $n$ is users ($U$), items ($I$), or both.
> 2. **Confusing offline vs online complexity.** A full similarity matrix is computed **offline**, but the question may ask for **online prediction** cost.
> 3. **Skipping numerical substitution.** A KTU 14-mark answer **must** include at least one numerical example with realistic values (e.g., $U = 10^7$, $I = 10^5$, $d = 100$).
> 4. **Forgetting the time budget** when describing the three-tier architecture. The **<100 ms online budget** is the most-tested fact.
> 5. **Naming "LSH" without explaining banding** $b \cdot r = h$ — the S-curve is the heart of the answer.
> 6. **Using non-KTU terminology** like "embedding DB" or "vector store" without linking to the KTU textbook concept of "latent factor models."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 2-Minute Revision Checklist — Scalability in Recommendation Systems**

- [ ] **Definition** — ability to handle growing $U, I, |R|, \lambda$ without proportional cost growth.
- [ ] **Four dimensions** — user, item, rating, real-time scalability.
- [ ] **User-User CF cost** — offline $O(U^2 I)$, online $O(U I)$ → **infeasible at scale**.
- [ ] **Item-Item CF cost** — offline $O(U I^2)$, online $O(I k)$ → **Amazon's choice**.
- [ ] **Matrix Factorization** — prediction $\hat r_{ui} = \mathbf{p}_u^{\top} \mathbf{q}_i$; storage $O((U+I)d)$; online $O(d)$.
- [ ] **SVD truncation** — $\hat R_d = U_d \Sigma_d V_d^{\top}$; **Eckart–Young theorem** guarantees minimum Frobenius error.
- [ ] **Three-tier architecture** — Offline (hours, ALS/Spark) → Nearline (seconds, FAISS/ANN) → Online (<100 ms, ranker + cache).
- [ ] **ANN techniques** — FAISS, Annoy, ScaNN, HNSW, LSH — sub-linear query time, ~99% recall.
- [ ] **MinHash** — estimates Jaccard similarity $J(A,B)$ using random permutations.
- [ ] **LSH banding** — $P(\text{collision}) = 1 - (1 - s^r)^b$ with $h = b \cdot r$.
- [ ] **ALS** — alternative to SGD for MF; complexity $O((U+I)d^2 + |R|d)$; parallelizable.
- [ ] **Cold-start** — new users/items degrade scalability-aware models; hybrid content-based fallback required.
- [ ] **Real-world systems** — Netflix (MF + ANN), Amazon (item-item CF), Spotify (Two-Tower + FAISS), Google News (MinHash).
- [ ] **Key numerical fact to remember** — for $U = 10^7, I = 10^5, d = 100$, MF gives **$\sim 5 \times 10^4\times$ storage reduction** over user-user CF.
- [ ] **Latency budgets** — Offline: hours; Nearline: seconds–minutes; Online: **<100 ms** (industry SLAs: web ≤ 200 ms).

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"Naïve CF does not scale. Always substitute dimensionality reduction + approximate search + distributed training before claiming scalability."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Issues in Offline Recommender Evaluation - Case Study of the Netflix Prize Data Set
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT741 - RECOMMENDATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design Issues in Offline Recommender Evaluation - Case Study of the Netflix Prize Data Set

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Offline Recommender Evaluation** is the methodological framework of measuring the predictive accuracy, ranking quality, and business utility of a recommendation algorithm by replaying it against a *historical, static snapshot* of user-item interaction data, *without* any live interaction with real users. It is the engineering standard for benchmarking recommender systems during the model development lifecycle (model selection, hyperparameter tuning, and ablation studies) before any deployment to production traffic.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** framework for PECDT741, this topic directly maps to the following Course Outcomes:

| Course Outcome | Mapping Description |
|---|---|
| **CO4** (Module 4) | Evaluate recommendation algorithms using appropriate offline metrics and real-world datasets. |
| **CO5** (Module 4) | Critically analyse the design trade-offs (bias, variance, sparsity, generalisability) that influence offline evaluation validity. |

## 1.2 The Netflix Prize Data Set — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Netflix Prize Data Set** is a canonical, large-scale, public benchmark for collaborative filtering released by **Netflix Inc. in October 2006** as the foundation of the famous **Netflix Prize competition**. It contains **100,480,507 ratings** given by **480,189 anonymised users** to **17,770 movies**, where each rating is an integer on a **1–5 star scale**. The data was collected between **October 1998 and December 2005**. A held-out qualifying set was used by participants to submit predictions scored via **Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Time-Capsule Judge"

Imagine you are a **chess coach** who wants to evaluate which of two opening strategies ("Strategy A" and "Strategy B") is better. You obviously cannot make your students play tens of thousands of live games in real time — that would be slow, expensive, and disruptive. Instead, you:

1. **Open an archive** of millions of historical games played by grandmasters (this is your **historical dataset**).
2. **Replay** both strategies mentally against the archive and see which one would have predicted the master's next move more accurately.
3. **Score** them by counting how often their predicted move matches the actual move (this is your **offline metric**, e.g., RMSE).

That is exactly what **offline evaluation of recommenders** does. The *Netflix Prize* archive is the grandmaster game library. Your algorithm is the strategy. **RMSE** is the move-match score. The catch — and the entire reason "Design Issues" exist as a separate syllabus topic — is that the *time capsule is not the tournament hall*. A strategy that wins on archived games may not be the best in a live setting because users drift, contexts change, and the data is biased by what users chose to rate in the first place.

## 1.4 Why "Design Issues" Deserve a Dedicated Module

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 of PECDT741 isolates *design issues* because a naïve application of offline evaluation can mislead engineering teams into deploying models that *win the leaderboard but lose the user*. The Netflix Prize is the perfect case study because its 3-year history (2006–2009) surfaced every classical pitfall in recommender evaluation — sparsity, temporal drift, selection bias, metric overfitting, and the famous "progress illusion" caused by RMSE gaming.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Design Issues in Offline Evaluation

The design issues can be categorised into **five orthogonal pillars** that examiners frequently test:

### Pillar 1 — *Data Splitting & Sampling Design*
- **Random Split** (e.g., 80/20): The naïve baseline; assumes IID data, which is **violated** by recommender data.
- **Temporal Split** (train on past, test on future): The *correct* design for time-aware recommenders. The Netflix Prize used this — the *probe* set was a **uniformly random subset** of the most recent ratings, ensuring the test set was *temporally aligned* with realistic deployment.
- **User-Leave-Out / Item-Leave-Out** (a form of *k*-fold cross-validation): Used to evaluate **cold-start** robustness.
- **Bootstrap Sampling**: Used to estimate confidence intervals around metrics.

### Pillar 2 — *Metric Selection*
Different aspects of recommendation quality require **different metrics**. Choosing only RMSE (as the Netflix Prize did) is a *deliberate* design decision that biases the competition toward *rating-prediction accuracy* and *away from* ranking quality, diversity, novelty, and serendipity.

### Pillar 3 — *Sparsity & Coverage*
The Netflix matrix had a density of:

$$ \text{Density} = \frac{100{,}480{,}507}{480{,}189 \times 17{,}770} \approx 0.01177 = 1.177\% $$

This means **98.82% of the user-item matrix is unknown** — a textbook sparse regime where neighbourhood and latent-factor methods both struggle in distinct ways.

### Pillar 4 — *Selection & Popularity Bias*
Users do not rate movies uniformly. They rate what they watched, and they watched what was popular. The *observed* ratings are not a random sample of user preferences — they are a *self-selected* sample. Algorithms trained on this data inherit the bias.

### Pillar 5 — *Generalisability & Deployment Gap*
Offline RMSE improvements do **not** always translate to online business KPIs (Click-Through Rate, Watch Time, Subscription Retention). This is the **"last-mile problem"** of recommender evaluation.

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Memorise the formulas below with their **boundary behaviour** (i.e., what happens when a denominator approaches 0). Examiners often test *metric failure modes* by giving synthetic predictions.

| # | Metric | Formula | Range | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **MAE** (Mean Absolute Error) | $\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{\vert T \vert}\sum_{(u,i) \in T} \vert \hat{r}_{ui} - r_{ui} \vert$ | $[0, 4]$ | Average absolute deviation; robust to outliers. |
| 2 | **RMSE** (Root Mean Squared Error) | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\vert T \vert}\sum_{(u,i) \in T} (\hat{r}_{ui} - r_{ui})^2}$ | $[0, 4]$ | Penalises large errors quadratically; the **Netflix Prize metric**. |
| 3 | **Precision@K** | $\text{P@K} = \frac{\vert \text{Top-}K \cap \text{Relevant} \vert}{K}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Fraction of top-K recommendations that are relevant. |
| 4 | **Recall@K** | $\text{R@K} = \frac{\vert \text{Top-}K \cap \text{Relevant} \vert}{\vert \text{Relevant} \vert}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Fraction of all relevant items recovered in top-K. |
| 5 | **F1@K** | $\text{F1} = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall. |
| 6 | **Hit Rate@K** | $\text{HR@K} = \frac{1}{\vert U \vert}\sum_{u \in U} \mathbb{1}[\text{hit}_u]$ | $[0, 1]$ | Fraction of users with $\geq 1$ relevant item in top-K. |
| 7 | **NDCG@K** | $\text{NDCG@K} = \frac{\text{DCG@K}}{\text{IDCG@K}}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Position-aware ranking quality. |
| 8 | **MAP** (Mean Average Precision) | $\text{MAP} = \frac{1}{\vert U \vert}\sum_{u}\text{AP}_u$ | $[0, 1]$ | Mean of per-user average precisions. |
| 9 | **MRR** (Mean Reciprocal Rank) | $\text{MRR} = \frac{1}{\vert U \vert}\sum_{u}\frac{1}{\text{rank}_u^{\text{first hit}}}$ | $[0, 1]$ | How quickly the first relevant item appears. |
| 10 | **Coverage** | $\text{Cov} = \frac{\vert \bigcup_{u} \text{Rec}_u \vert}{\vert I \vert}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Fraction of catalogue ever recommended. |
| 11 | **Diversity (ILD)** | $\text{ILD} = \frac{1}{\vert U \vert K(K-1) \vert}\sum_{u}\sum_{i \neq j \in \text{Top-}K}\text{dist}(i,j)$ | $[0, 1]$ | Intra-list diversity of recommendations. |
| 12 | **Novelty** | $\text{Novelty} = -\log_2 p(i)$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Information-theoretic surprise of recommended item. |

**Boundary Behaviour (KTU favourite):**
- If $K = 0$ in P@K, R@K, NDCG@K → the metric is **undefined (0/0)**. Always define a *default return value* (typically 0).
- If $\hat{r}_{ui} = r_{ui}$ for *all* (u,i) → RMSE = 0 (perfect).
- If the user has no relevant item in test → **AP = 0** (not undefined); this convention affects MAP.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Netflix Prize's focus on RMSE drove an entire generation of **latent-factor models** (SVD, FunkSVD, SVD++, time-SVD++, RBM-based collaborative filtering). Modern industrial systems (YouTube, Spotify, Amazon) have *moved beyond RMSE* because:
1. **RMSE optimises for users who rate many items** (heavy raters) and is dominated by them.
2. **RMSE cannot distinguish** between "predicting 4.1 instead of 4.0" and "predicting 1.0 instead of 5.0" from a *business* perspective — both are errors, but only the latter loses a customer.
3. **Top-K ranking metrics** better correlate with the actual user interface (a 5-row list).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why RMSE Penalises Large Errors Quadratically

We start from the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** and take the square root to obtain RMSE.

**Step 1 — Define the empirical loss on a test set $T$:**

$$ L(\hat{r}) = \frac{1}{\vert T \vert}\sum_{(u,i) \in T} (\hat{r}_{ui} - r_{ui})^2 $$

**Step 2 — Substitute the definition of "error" $e_{ui} = \hat{r}_{ui} - r_{ui}$:**

$$ L = \frac{1}{\vert T \vert}\sum_{(u,i) \in T} e_{ui}^2 $$

**Step 3 — Take the square root to obtain RMSE in the original units of ratings (1–5 stars):**

$$ \text{RMSE} = \sqrt{L} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\vert T \vert}\sum_{(u,i) \in T} e_{ui}^2} $$

**Step 4 — Observe the *amplification* of large errors.** Suppose we have two predictions:
- Case A: 100 predictions, each with error 1.0.
- Case B: 99 predictions with error 0, and 1 prediction with error 10.

The **MAE** for both cases is the same: **1.0**.

The **RMSE** values are:
- Case A: $\sqrt{100 \times 1.0^2 / 100} = 1.000$
- Case B: $\sqrt{(99 \times 0 + 1 \times 100)/100} = \sqrt{1.00} = 1.000$

But if the single error in Case B grows to **5**:

$$ \text{RMSE}_B = \sqrt{(0 + 25)/100} = \sqrt{0.25} = 0.500 $$

Versus MAE of 0.05. The squared term inflates *large* mistakes — a property crucial for the Netflix Prize because a single 5-star-vs-1-star miss on a popular title is a *catastrophic* recommendation failure.

**Step 5 — The "Netflix RMSE baseline"** was **Cinematrix's** score of $\text{RMSE}_{\text{Netflix}} = 0.9514$. The grand prize required improving this by **10%**, i.e., reaching $\text{RMSE} \le 0.8563$.

## 3.2 Derivation: NDCG@K (Normalised Discounted Cumulative Gain)

**Step 1 — Define the relevance label.** For a user $u$, let $rel_i$ be the *relevance* of item $i$ (e.g., a binary 0/1, or a graded relevance from the rating).

**Step 2 — Compute the Discounted Cumulative Gain (DCG) at cut-off K:**

$$ \text{DCG@K} = \sum_{i=1}^{K}\frac{2^{rel_i} - 1}{\log_2(i + 1)} $$

The $\log_2(i+1)$ denominator is the *positional discount*: a relevant item at position 1 contributes its full value; at position 100 it is heavily demoted.

**Step 3 — Compute the Ideal DCG (IDCG).** Sort all items by *true* relevance in descending order, take the top K, and apply the same DCG formula.

**Step 4 — Normalise:**

$$ \text{NDCG@K} = \frac{\text{DCG@K}}{\text{IDCG@K}} $$

This bounds NDCG in $[0, 1]$, with $1$ being a *perfect* ranking.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: The Netflix Prize vs Modern Evaluation

| Dimension | Netflix Prize (2006) | Modern Industrial Practice (2024) | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Metric** | RMSE | NDCG@K, Recall@K, MAP | Ranking > Rating prediction for UI lists. |
| **Data Split** | Temporal (probe) | Temporal + user-stratified | Reflects deployment reality. |
| **Held-out Set Size** | ~1.4 M (probe) + 3 M (qualifying) | ~5–10% of interactions | Balance between statistical power and leakage. |
| **Bias Handling** | None (raw ratings) | Inverse-propensity scoring, IPS | Counteracts selection bias. |
| **User Study** | None | A/B testing, interleaving | Validates offline gains online. |
| **Cold-Start Eval** | Not separately evaluated | Separate cold-start benchmark set | Critical for new users/items. |
| **Long-Tail Coverage** | Not optimised | Explicit coverage/diversity metrics | Prevents "popularity filter bubble". |
| **Temporal Drift** | Model retrained once at end | Continuous learning, sliding window | User tastes change weekly. |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — RMSE on a Mini-Probe Set

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the exact KTU 2-mark calculation pattern.** Given 5 true ratings and 5 predictions, compute RMSE.

Predicted ratings $\hat{r} = \{4, 3, 5, 2, 4\}$, True ratings $r = \{5, 3, 4, 1, 4\}$.

**Step 1 — Compute per-pair squared errors:**

$$ e_1^2 = (4-5)^2 = 1,\quad e_2^2 = (3-3)^2 = 0,\quad e_3^2 = (5-4)^2 = 1,\quad e_4^2 = (2-1)^2 = 1,\quad e_5^2 = (4-4)^2 = 0 $$

**Step 2 — Sum the squared errors:**

$$ \sum e_i^2 = 1 + 0 + 1 + 1 + 0 = 3 $$

**Step 3 — Divide by N and take the square root:**

$$ \text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{3}{5}} = \sqrt{0.6} \approx 0.7746 $$

This rating-scale error is *below* Netflix's threshold — it represents a very strong predictor for this toy user.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Offline Evaluation Pipeline — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Interaction Log] --> B[Data Cleaning and Deduplication]
    B --> C[Temporal Sort by Timestamp]
    C --> D{Splitting Strategy}
    D --> E[Training Set 80 percent]
    D --> F[Validation Set 10 percent]
    D --> G[Test Probe Set 10 percent]
    E --> H[Model Training]
    H --> I[Hyperparameter Tuning on Validation]
    I --> J[Final Model Selection]
    J --> K[Offline Metric Computation on Test]
    K --> L[Metric Bundle RMSE MAE NDCG Coverage]
    L --> M{Acceptance Threshold Met}
    M -- Yes --> N[Promote to Online A B Test]
    M -- No --> O[Iterate or Reject]
    N --> P[Production Deployment]
    P --> Q[Live Business KPI Monitoring]
    Q --> R[Feedback Loop back to Data Log]
    R --> A
```

**Reading the Diagram:** This is the canonical **closed-loop offline-to-online evaluation pipeline** used by every major recommendation system team. The critical design choice happens at node **D** — the splitting strategy. A *random* split leaks future information into training; a *temporal* split (which the Netflix Prize used) is the *only* split that mirrors real deployment, where the model is trained on the past and must predict the future.

## 4.2 Netflix Prize Data Set — Internal Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Netflix Prize Data Set 100.48 M Ratings] --> B[Training Set 90 percent]
    A --> C[Probe Set 1.42 M Ratings]
    A --> D[Qualifying Set 2.82 M Ratings]
    D --> E[Quiz Half Score visible to leaderboard]
    D --> F[Test Half Final Grand Prize Determinant]
    B --> G[Each User ID integer 1 to 480189]
    B --> H[Each Movie ID integer 1 to 17770]
    B --> I[Each Rating integer 1 to 5]
    B --> J[Each Timestamp date of rating]
    C --> K[Leaderboard Visible RMSE]
    D --> L[Hidden Test Final Score]
    M[Probe Set Ground Truth given to Participants] --> C
    N[Qualifying Set Ground Truth withheld by Netflix] --> D
```

**Reading the Diagram:** Notice the deliberate **two-stage test design**. The *probe* set's true ratings were released so participants could validate their local RMSE calculations. The *qualifying* set's true ratings were *sealed* at Netflix — participants only saw the (user, movie, date) tuples and submitted predictions. This **anti-leakage design** is the single most important *design issue* the Netflix Prize introduced to the recommender systems community.

## 4.3 The Offline-Online Evaluation Gap

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Offline Metric RMSE improves by 1 percent] --> B{Does it improve online KPI}
    B -- Often No --> C[Causes Popularity Bias Amplification]
    B -- Often No --> D[Causes Filter Bubble Reinforcement]
    B -- Often No --> E[Causes Cold Start Degradation]
    B -- Sometimes Yes --> F[Real Generalisation]
    C --> G[Solution Inverse Propensity Scoring]
    D --> H[Solution Diversity and Serendipity Regularisation]
    E --> I[Solution Hybrid Content Collaborative Model]
    G --> J[Re-evaluate with IPS Adjusted Metric]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Validate via Interleaving or A B Test]
```

**Reading the Diagram:** This flow shows the **5 most common reasons offline improvements fail online** — and their corresponding **engineering remedies**. In a 14-mark KTU question, this is the *core diagram* to draw or describe.

## 4.4 The Netflix Prize Timeline

```mermaid
gantt
    title Netflix Prize Competition Milestones
    dateFormat YYYY-MM
    axisFormat %Y
    section Preparation
    Dataset Released                 :milestone, m1, 2006-10, 0d
    section Leaderboard
    First Submission Above Baseline  :milestone, m2, 2006-11, 0d
    BellKor 8.9 percent Improvement  :milestone, m3, 2007-10, 0d
    BigChaos 9.4 percent             :milestone, m4, 2008-05, 0d
    section Grand Prize
    BellKor Pragmatic Chaos          :crit, milestone, m5, 2009-06, 0d
    Prize Awarded 1M USD             :milestone, m6, 2009-09, 0d
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]`
**Q: Differentiate between offline and online evaluation of recommender systems. Which one did the Netflix Prize use, and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Offline Evaluation | Online Evaluation |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Source** | Historical, static snapshot | Live, real-time user interactions |
| **User Interaction** | None — replay of past data | Real users see real recommendations |
| **Metrics** | RMSE, MAE, NDCG, Precision@K | CTR, Conversion, Watch Time, Retention |
| **Cost & Risk** | Low — no user impact | High — bad recs hurt real users |
| **Speed** | Fast — runs in hours | Slow — needs weeks of traffic |
| **Scale of Comparison** | Thousands of candidates | Few (2–5) A/B variants |

The **Netflix Prize used offline evaluation** because (i) it allowed **global participation** from research teams without requiring user access, (ii) it provided a **standardised, reproducible benchmark** with a single primary metric (RMSE), and (iii) it was **legally and ethically safer** for Netflix, which could not expose live user data. *(3 marks — full allocation: 1 mark per concept + 1 mark for the why-Netflix-used-it justification.)*

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Jul 2023]`
**Q: List any three design issues encountered in offline recommender evaluation with reference to the Netflix Prize data set.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Data Sparsity** — Only **1.18%** of the user-item matrix is observed; this forces heavy regularisation and degrades neighbourhood-based methods.
2. **Temporal Drift** — User preferences shift over 7 years (1998–2005); a model trained on all data *equally* ignores that recent ratings better predict future ones.
3. **Selection Bias** — Users rate what they choose to watch; the observed ratings are not IID samples of the underlying preference distribution.
*(Optional 4th for extra credit: Popularity bias — the long tail is under-represented.)* *(3 marks — 1 mark per issue with the Netflix context.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — READ CAREFULLY:**
> 1. Do **not** write generic definitions of RMSE without linking them to the *Netflix Prize* context. Examiners explicitly test **contextual application**.
> 2. Do **not** state the dataset size from memory without units (e.g., "100 M" — always say "100.48 million ratings").
> 3. In *design issues* questions, you **must** discuss at least **one mitigation** for every issue you raise. A bare list of issues scores ≤ 7 out of 14.
> 4. **Do not** confuse *probe set* with *qualifying set*. Probe is *released*; Qualifying is *held back*.
> 5. Hand-drawn diagrams (or neatly-labelled Mermaid reproductions) earn 1–2 extra marks if the labels are **legible and contextual**.

### Question B-A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**Q: "The Netflix Prize is the most influential case study in offline recommender evaluation." Discuss this statement in detail, covering:**
**(a) The structure and statistics of the Netflix Prize data set, and why it became a benchmark.** *(7 marks)*
**(b) The design issues that surfaced during the competition, and the engineering lessons learned for modern recommender systems.** *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**1. Dataset Structure (3 marks):**
The Netflix Prize data set, released on **2 October 2006**, consisted of four files:
- `training_set.tar` — 17,770 files, one per movie, containing (user, rating, date) tuples. Total = **100,480,507 ratings**.
- `probe.txt` — 1,408,342 (user, movie) pairs whose true ratings were *released* to participants to help local validation.
- `qualifying.txt` — 2,817,131 (user, movie, date) tuples whose true ratings were *withheld*.
- `movie_titles.txt` — movie ID → title + year mapping.

**2. Dataset Statistics (2 marks):**

| Statistic | Value |
|---|---|
| Total ratings | 100,480,507 |
| Unique users | 480,189 |
| Unique movies | 17,770 |
| Rating scale | 1 to 5 (integer) |
| Time span | Oct 1998 — Dec 2005 (~7 years) |
| Mean rating | ~3.60 |
| Matrix density | 1.18% |
| RMSE baseline (Cinematrix) | 0.9514 |
| Grand-prize target RMSE | 0.8563 (10% improvement) |

**3. Why it Became the Benchmark (2 marks):**
- **Scale** — 100 M+ ratings was an unprecedented public dataset in 2006.
- **Realism** — collected from a *production* system with real users and real consumption patterns.
- **Standardised metric** — RMSE is reproducible and unambiguous.
- **Prize incentive** — $1M USD drove top-tier research participation.
- **Anonymisation** — preserved user privacy while retaining behavioural richness.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Design Issue 1 — Data Sparsity (1.5 marks):**
With 98.82% of the matrix unknown, pure memory-based methods (user-user, item-item KNN) failed. The winning approaches (BellKor's Pragmatic Chaos) used **latent-factor models** (SVD, time-SVD++) and **restricted Boltzmann machines (RBM)** to compress the sparse observations into a dense $k$-dimensional factor space. **Mitigation:** Hybrid models combining collaborative filtering with content/metadata features.

**Design Issue 2 — Temporal Dynamics (1.5 marks):**
User preferences drift; a 1998 rating has *less* predictive power for 2005 preferences than a 2004 rating. The winner's time-SVD++ model added **temporal bias terms** $b_u(t)$ and $b_i(t)$ that allowed user and item biases to evolve smoothly over time. **Mitigation:** Sliding-window training, time-decay weighting, continuous retraining.

**Design Issue 3 — Metric Overfitting to RMSE (1.5 marks):**
Once participants focused exclusively on RMSE, they discovered ways to *game* the metric (e.g., the famous "blending" trick where predictions on a probe set are slightly shifted to align with probe-distribution statistics). **Mitigation:** Use *multiple* metrics (RMSE + NDCG@K + coverage) and validate against a *fresh* temporal split.

**Design Issue 4 — Generalisation Gap (1.5 marks):**
The 10% RMSE improvement on the Netflix test set did not automatically translate to Netflix's own production gains. A model that predicts ratings more accurately does not necessarily *rank* movies better for the user's home page. **Mitigation:** A/B test before full deployment; use interleaving for fast online comparison.

**Design Issue 5 — Selection & Popularity Bias (1 mark):**
The dataset over-represents popular movies because users only rate what they have watched. Models that exploit this bias appear to score well on RMSE but suffer on long-tail catalogues. **Mitigation:** Inverse-propensity scoring, exposure-aware loss functions.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Jul 2024, Model]`

**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the offline evaluation pipeline for a recommender system. Then, derive the mathematical formulations of RMSE, Precision@K, and NDCG@K, and discuss why RMSE alone was an insufficient metric for the Netflix Prize competition.** *(14 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Pipeline Diagram (4 marks)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw User Item Interaction Log] --> B[Preprocessing: Deduplication, Filtering Bots]
    B --> C[Temporal Sort by Interaction Timestamp]
    C --> D[Train Validation Test Split: 80 10 10 Temporal]
    D --> E[Model Training: SVD, RBM, KNN, Hybrid]
    E --> F[Hyperparameter Tuning on Validation Grid Search]
    F --> G[Final Model Selection Lowest Validation RMSE]
    G --> H[Offline Test Set Evaluation]
    H --> I[Compute Metric Bundle: RMSE, MAE, NDCG, P at K, R at K, Coverage]
    I --> J{Improvement over Baseline Greater than Threshold}
    J -- Yes --> K[Candidate for Online A B Test]
    J -- No --> L[Iterate Model Architecture or Features]
    K --> M[Interleaving or A B Test with Live Users]
    M --> N[Compare Online CTR Retention Watch Time]
    N --> O{Statistically Significant Online Lift}
    O -- Yes --> P[Promote to Production]
    O -- No --> Q[Rollback and Investigate Offline Online Gap]
```

#### Model Solution — RMSE Derivation (3 marks)

**Definition:** The RMSE on a held-out test set $T$ is:

$$ \text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\vert T \vert}\sum_{(u,i) \in T}\left(\hat{r}_{ui} - r_{ui}\right)^2} $$

**Properties:**
- Penalises large errors *quadratically* (derivation already shown in Section 3.1).
- Sensitive to outliers; a single 5-vs-1 error outweighs twenty 4.0-vs-4.1 errors.
- Lower is better; perfect prediction → RMSE = 0.
- Bounded by rating range: $0 \le \text{RMSE} \le 4$ for the 1–5 scale.

**Numerical example:** Already worked in Section 3.4 (mini probe set gives RMSE ≈ 0.775).

#### Model Solution — Precision@K Derivation (2 marks)

**Definition:** For a user $u$, let $\text{TopK}(u)$ be the set of $K$ items recommended, and $\text{Rel}(u)$ the set of items the user actually found relevant (e.g., rated ≥ 4 in the test set).

$$ \text{Precision@K}(u) = \frac{\vert \text{TopK}(u) \cap \text{Rel}(u) \vert}{K} $$

Averaged across all users: $\text{P@K} = \frac{1}{\vert U \vert}\sum_{u} \text{Precision@K}(u)$.

**Why it matters for Netflix:** RMSE asks *"how close is your rating estimate?"* — Precision@K asks *"did you show the user a movie they would have loved?"* The latter aligns with the actual product: a *list* of recommendations, not a single rating prediction.

#### Model Solution — NDCG@K Derivation (2 marks)

Already derived in Section 3.2:

$$ \text{NDCG@K} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{K}\frac{2^{rel_i} - 1}{\log_2(i + 1)}}{\text{IDCG@K}} \in [0, 1] $$

**Position awareness:** Unlike Precision, NDCG rewards a relevant item being ranked at position 1 over position 50. This mirrors *real user behaviour* — most users never scroll past the first 2–3 rows.

#### Model Solution — Why RMSE Was Insufficient (3 marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the highest-weight sub-section. Examiners award full marks only to answers that articulate the *business* and *UX* consequences, not just the mathematical ones.**

1. **Rating prediction ≠ Ranking quality.** Netflix's UI shows a *list*, not a predicted star rating. A model with RMSE 0.85 can still rank popular items above niche but highly-relevant ones for a specific user.
2. **Ignores the long tail.** RMSE is dominated by users with thousands of ratings (heavy raters). RMSE improvements can come from serving heavy raters better while neglecting the majority who have < 20 ratings.
3. **No notion of *novelty, diversity, or serendipity*.** A model that recommends only the top 100 most-popular movies can achieve a low RMSE but produces a stale, uninteresting UX.
4. **Adversarial overfitting.** Because the leaderboard was a single scalar (RMSE), teams optimised it *hyper-aggressively* — including blending with probe-set-specific biases. This is **metric overfitting** — winning the metric, not the underlying task.
5. **No cold-start evaluation.** A new user with 3 ratings cannot be evaluated on RMSE meaningfully; the metric *assumes* a dense history that the product does not have for most users.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print and tape to your wall before the exam.**

- [ ] The Netflix Prize dataset has **100,480,507 ratings**, **480,189 users**, **17,770 movies**, span **1998–2005**, density **1.18%**.
- [ ] The **probe set** (1.4 M) is *released*; the **qualifying set** (2.8 M) is *withheld*. The grand prize is judged on the qualifying set.
- [ ] Baseline RMSE = **0.9514** (Cinematrix). Grand-prize target = **0.8563** (10% improvement).
- [ ] BellKor's Pragmatic Chaos won in **2009** with $1M prize. Their ensemble combined **time-SVD++** and **RBM** models.
- [ ] RMSE penalises large errors *quadratically*; MAE does so *linearly*.
- [ ] **Top-K metrics** (Precision@K, Recall@K, NDCG@K) align with real product UX; RMSE does not.
- [ ] Five canonical design issues: **Sparsity, Temporal drift, Selection bias, Metric overfitting, Generalisation gap**.
- [ ] The **offline-online gap** is real: offline RMSE gains do not guarantee online KPI lifts. Always A/B test.
- [ ] **Temporal splitting** is *correct*; random splitting *leaks* future information.
- [ ] Modern evaluation uses **multiple metrics** (RMSE + NDCG + Coverage + Diversity), **counterfactual methods** (IPS, doubly-robust), and **online interleaving**.
- [ ] Cold-start users and items need **separate evaluation** protocols; they cannot be fairly scored on RMSE.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Examiner's Note:** The single most common reason students lose 4–5 marks in the 14-mark question is that they *describe* the design issues but *do not propose mitigations*. Always pair every problem with a **practical engineering solution** — this is the difference between a 7/14 and a 12/14 answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
