> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT741 - RECOMMENDATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Segmenting the Ratings for Training and Testing - Hold-Out

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Recommendation Systems**, a recommender algorithm must be objectively evaluated on its ability to **generalize to unseen user–item interactions**. The **Hold-Out** method (also called the *Hold-Out Evaluation* or *Train/Test Split*) is the simplest, most fundamental, and most widely-used **data segmentation strategy** in which the original rating matrix $\mathcal{R}$ is partitioned into **two mutually disjoint subsets**:

- A **Training Set** $\mathcal{R}_{\text{train}}$ — used to fit (learn the parameters of) the recommendation model.
- A **Test Set** $\mathcal{R}_{\text{test}}$ — held back during training and used *only* afterwards to estimate the model's **out-of-sample predictive error** (generalization performance).

Formally, for the universe of observed ratings $\mathcal{R}$:

$$\mathcal{R} \;=\; \mathcal{R}_{\text{train}} \;\cup\; \mathcal{R}_{\text{test}}, \qquad \mathcal{R}_{\text{train}} \;\cap\; \mathcal{R}_{\text{test}} \;=\; \varnothing$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Emphasis:** The Hold-Out method belongs to the broader family of *experimental protocols* used in Module 4 (Evaluation of Recommender Systems). It is a **prerequisite concept** before studying Cross-Validation, Bootstrap, and A/B testing.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Closed-Book Exam"

Imagine a B.Tech student preparing for the KTU End Semester Examination:

| Real-World Step | Recommendation-System Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Attending classes and reading the textbook | Fitting the model on $\mathcal{R}_{\text{train}}$ |
| Solving previous question papers at home | Internal validation / hyper-parameter tuning |
| Sitting for the unseen KTU exam paper | Predicting ratings in $\mathcal{R}_{\text{test}}$ |
| Score obtained in the actual exam | Reported RMSE / MAE / Precision@K |

If the student memorizes the textbook word-for-word but cannot answer *new* questions, the model's "training accuracy" is meaningless. **Hold-Out mimics exactly this realism:** the test set is information that the model has *never seen*, so its performance on this set is the honest report card.

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **Hold-Out Vocabulary You MUST Memorize for KTU Exams**
> - **Split Ratio** $\alpha$ : fraction of ratings assigned to the training set (commonly **0.8** or **0.7**).
> - **Random Subsampling** : each rating is independently assigned to train/test using a Bernoulli / uniform draw.
> - **Cold-Start Sensitivity** : very sparse users/items may end up with *zero* training ratings under random splits.
> - **Disjointness Constraint** : no rating tuple $(u, i, r_{ui})$ may appear in both sets.
> - **Deterministic vs. Random Split** : random splits use a fixed random seed for *reproducibility* (a KTU-favourite viva point).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Procedure of the Hold-Out Method

The Hold-Out segmentation is executed as a sequence of well-defined steps. The 'Why' behind each step is also stated for conceptual clarity.

1. **Step 1 — Collect the rating matrix.**  
   Assemble the full user–item–rating dataset $\mathcal{R} = \{(u, i, r_{ui})\}$ of size $N = \vert \mathcal{R} \vert$.  
   *Why:* A single source of truth is required so that train and test are consistent sub-samples.

2. **Step 2 — Choose the split ratio $\alpha$.**  
   Typical choices are $\alpha \in \{0.6, 0.7, 0.8, 0.9\}$, with **$\alpha = 0.8$** being the de-facto industry/KTU default.  
   *Why:* A larger training set improves model accuracy but shrinks the test set, making the error estimate noisy (high variance).

3. **Step 3 — Shuffle the ratings randomly.**  
   Use a pseudo-random permutation seeded by an integer $s$ for reproducibility.  
   *Why:* Eliminates temporal or alphabetical bias that would otherwise leak information between sets.

4. **Step 4 — Partition.**  
   Assign the first $\lfloor \alpha N \rfloor$ shuffled ratings to $\mathcal{R}_{\text{train}}$ and the remaining to $\mathcal{R}_{\text{test}}$.  
   *Why:* Guarantees the disjointness constraint $\mathcal{R}_{\text{train}} \cap \mathcal{R}_{\text{test}} = \varnothing$.

5. **Step 5 — Train.**  
   Fit the recommender $\hat{f}$ using *only* $\mathcal{R}_{\text{train}}$: $\hat{f} = \text{Fit}(\mathcal{R}_{\text{train}})$.  
   *Why:* The model must not be allowed to peek at the test ratings.

6. **Step 6 — Predict on test.**  
   For every $(u, i) \in \mathcal{R}_{\text{test}}$, generate the predicted rating $\hat{r}_{ui} = \hat{f}(u, i)$.  
   *Why:* We measure how well the model extrapolates to genuinely *unseen* interactions.

7. **Step 7 — Compute evaluation metrics.**  
   Aggregate the errors on $\mathcal{R}_{\text{test}}$ to obtain RMSE, MAE, Precision@K, Recall@K, etc.  
   *Why:* A single scalar figure enables fair comparison of competing algorithms.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table is the **exam-ready mathematical toolkit** for any Hold-Out-based evaluation problem. Note the use of `\vert` and `\mid` to keep the markdown table valid.

| # | Concept | Formula / Symbol | Notation & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total number of observed ratings | $N \;=\; \vert \mathcal{R} \vert$ | integer count |
| 2 | Split ratio | $\alpha \;=\; \dfrac{\vert \mathcal{R}_{\text{train}} \vert}{N}$ | dimensionless, in $[0,1]$ |
| 3 | Training-set size | $\vert \mathcal{R}_{\text{train}} \vert \;=\; \lfloor \alpha N \rfloor$ | integer count |
| 4 | Test-set size | $\vert \mathcal{R}_{\text{test}} \vert \;=\; N - \lfloor \alpha N \rfloor$ | integer count |
| 5 | Mean Absolute Error | $\text{MAE} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{\vert \mathcal{R}_{\text{test}} \vert} \sum_{(u,i)\in \mathcal{R}_{\text{test}}} \vert r_{ui} - \hat{r}_{ui} \vert$ | rating units (same as $r_{ui}$) |
| 6 | Root Mean Squared Error | $\text{RMSE} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\vert \mathcal{R}_{\text{test}} \vert} \sum_{(u,i)\in \mathcal{R}_{\text{test}}} (r_{ui} - \hat{r}_{ui})^{2}}$ | rating units |
| 7 | Coverage (item-space) | $\text{Cov} \;=\; \dfrac{\mid \{ i \in \mathcal{I} \mid \exists\, u : \hat{r}_{ui} \text{ defined} \} \mid}{\mid \mathcal{I} \mid}$ | fraction in $[0,1]$ |
| 8 | Precision@K | $\text{Prec@K} \;=\; \dfrac{\mid \text{TopK} \cap \text{Relevant} \mid}{K}$ | fraction in $[0,1]$ |
| 9 | Recall@K | $\text{Rec@K} \;=\; \dfrac{\mid \text{TopK} \cap \text{Relevant} \mid}{\mid \text{Relevant} \mid}$ | fraction in $[0,1]$ |
| 10 | F1@K | $F_{1} \;=\; \dfrac{2 \,\text{Prec@K} \,\text{Rec@K}}{\text{Prec@K} + \text{Rec@K}}$ | fraction in $[0,1]$ |

## 2.3 Why Hold-Out is Foundational — Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industry deployment:** Every production recommender at Netflix, Amazon, or Spotify is *first* validated on a single hold-out split before being subjected to online A/B tests.
- **Reproducibility:** A fixed random seed $s$ produces an *identical* split across runs, allowing peer-reviewers to verify reported numbers.
- **Lowest computational cost:** It requires training the model exactly *once*, unlike $k$-fold cross-validation which trains $k$ times.
- **Pedagogical clarity:** It is the conceptual stepping-stone toward $k$-Fold CV, Leave-One-Out, and Time-Based Split (temporal hold-out).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Example

## 3.1 Derivation of the Expected Test-Set Size

We want to formally derive $\vert \mathcal{R}_{\text{test}} \vert$ as a function of the split ratio $\alpha$.

**Step 1.**  
Let $N = \vert \mathcal{R} \vert$ be the total number of observed ratings. By the definition of $\alpha$:

$$
\alpha \;=\; \frac{\vert \mathcal{R}_{\text{train}} \vert}{N}
$$

**Step 2.**  
Apply the disjointness constraint $\mathcal{R} = \mathcal{R}_{\text{train}} \cup \mathcal{R}_{\text{test}}$ and $\mathcal{R}_{\text{train}} \cap \mathcal{R}_{\text{test}} = \varnothing$:

$$
\vert \mathcal{R} \vert \;=\; \vert \mathcal{R}_{\text{train}} \vert \;+\; \vert \mathcal{R}_{\text{test}} \vert
$$

**Step 3.**  
Substitute $\vert \mathcal{R}_{\text{train}} \vert = \alpha N$ into the identity above:

$$
N \;=\; \alpha N \;+\; \vert \mathcal{R}_{\text{test}} \vert
$$

**Step 4.**  
Rearrange to isolate $\vert \mathcal{R}_{\text{test}} \vert$:

$$
\vert \mathcal{R}_{\text{test}} \vert \;=\; N \;-\; \alpha N \;=\; (1 - \alpha)\, N
$$

**Step 5.**  
For integer-valued counts we use the floor operator on the test side:

$$
\vert \mathcal{R}_{\text{test}} \vert \;=\; \bigl\lfloor (1 - \alpha)\, N \bigr\rfloor
$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — RMSE Computation on a Hold-Out Split

Suppose a small e-commerce recommender has the following **5 observed ratings** in $\mathcal{R}$:

$$
\mathcal{R} = \{\, (u_1, i_1, 5),\; (u_1, i_2, 3),\; (u_2, i_1, 4),\; (u_2, i_2, 2),\; (u_3, i_1, 1) \,\}
$$

So $N = 5$. We choose $\alpha = 0.6$ and a fixed random seed.

**Step 1 — Shuffle and split.**  
After shuffling with seed $s = 42$, the order becomes: $i_2$ of $u_2$, $i_1$ of $u_3$, $i_1$ of $u_1$, $i_2$ of $u_1$, $i_1$ of $u_2$.

$$
\mathcal{R}_{\text{train}} = \{\, (u_2, i_2, 2),\; (u_3, i_1, 1),\; (u_1, i_1, 5) \,\}
$$

$$
\mathcal{R}_{\text{test}} \;=\; \{\, (u_1, i_2, 3),\; (u_2, i_1, 4) \,\}
$$

**Step 2 — Train.**  
The model $\hat{f}$ is fit on the 3 training ratings and produces the following predictions on the test tuples:

$$
\hat{r}_{u_1, i_2} = 3.4, \qquad \hat{r}_{u_2, i_1} = 3.8
$$

**Step 3 — Compute squared errors.**  

$$
e_1^2 = (3 - 3.4)^2 = (-0.4)^2 = 0.16
$$

$$
e_2^2 = (4 - 3.8)^2 = (0.2)^2 = 0.04
$$

**Step 4 — Sum and average.**  

$$
\sum e_j^2 \;=\; 0.16 + 0.04 \;=\; 0.20
$$

$$
\frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{R}_{\text{test}} \vert} \sum e_j^2 \;=\; \frac{0.20}{2} \;=\; 0.10
$$

**Step 5 — Take the square root (RMSE).**  

$$
\text{RMSE} \;=\; \sqrt{0.10} \;\approx\; 0.3162
$$

**Step 6 — Compute MAE for cross-check (optional but KTU-favourite).**  

$$
\text{MAE} \;=\; \frac{\vert 3 - 3.4 \vert + \vert 4 - 3.8 \vert}{2} \;=\; \frac{0.4 + 0.2}{2} \;=\; 0.30
$$

The MAE is smaller than RMSE — a *consistent* result, because RMSE penalises the larger 0.4-error more heavily.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Insight:** RMSE is always $\geq$ MAE, with equality **only** when every single error magnitude is identical.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Bias / Variance Trade-Off of the Split Ratio

The choice of $\alpha$ is a **bias–variance trade-off** that examiners love to test.

$$
\text{Bias}(\hat{f}) \;\uparrow\; \text{ as } \alpha \;\downarrow
$$

$$
\text{Variance}(\text{Error Estimate}) \;\uparrow\; \text{ as } \alpha \;\uparrow
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\alpha^{*} \;\approx\; 0.8\; \text{(empirical sweet spot for sparse rating matrices)}\;}
$$

| $\alpha$ | Train Size | Test Size | Bias | Variance of Error Estimate |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.50 | 0.50 N | 0.50 N | High | Low |
| 0.70 | 0.70 N | 0.30 N | Moderate | Moderate |
| **0.80** | **0.80 N** | **0.20 N** | **Low** | **Moderate** |
| 0.90 | 0.90 N | 0.10 N | Very Low | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — End-to-End Hold-Out Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Rating Matrix R] --> B[Shuffle Ratings with Seed s]
    B --> C{Choose Split Ratio alpha}
    C -->|alpha x N ratings| D[R_train]
    C -->|1 - alpha x N ratings| E[R_test]
    D --> F[Fit Recommender Model f-hat]
    F --> G[Predict r-hat on R_test]
    E --> G
    G --> H[Compute RMSE and MAE]
    G --> I[Compute Precision@K and Recall@K]
    H --> J[Final Generalization Report]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Deploy or Iterate]
```

**Reading Guide for the Diagram:**

- The shuffle node `B` is the **only** place randomness enters — fixing the seed $s$ makes the whole pipeline *reproducible*.
- The diamond node `C` represents the **split decision**: this is the single most influential hyper-parameter in the experiment.
- The parallel arrows from `D` and `E` into `G` enforce the **disjointness property** — the model is trained on `R_train` but evaluated on `R_test`.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the Evaluation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input_Stage
        A1[User Item Rating Triples]
        A2[Split Ratio alpha]
    end
    subgraph Splitter_Stage
        B1[Random Subsampler]
        B2[Reproducible Seed s]
    end
    subgraph Model_Stage
        C1[Train Recommender]
        C2[Predict on Test]
    end
    subgraph Metric_Stage
        D1[RMSE Calculator]
        D2[MAE Calculator]
        D3[Ranking Metrics Prec Rec F1]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    B2 --> B1
    B1 --> C1
    B1 --> C2
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> D1
    C2 --> D2
    C2 --> D3
```

This block diagram emphasizes the **modular separation of concerns** that KTU expects students to articulate in design-type questions: *Data → Splitter → Model → Metric*. Any change in one block should not silently affect another block.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Q. Define the Hold-Out method for evaluating a recommender system. State any two limitations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Hold-Out method partitions the observed rating dataset $\mathcal{R}$ into two disjoint subsets — a **training set** $\mathcal{R}_{\text{train}}$ used to fit the recommender, and a **test set** $\mathcal{R}_{\text{test}}$ used to estimate the out-of-sample error.

**Limitations (any two for full 3 marks):**

1. **High variance of error estimate:** A single random split produces a noisy estimate because the test set is small (typically 20 % of $N$).
2. **Wasted data:** Ratings placed in the test set are *not* used for training, which is harmful in sparse datasets.
3. **Cold-start leakage:** A user or item with very few ratings may end up with *zero* training instances, making the prediction for them meaningless.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Q. With the standard 80/20 Hold-Out split on $N$ ratings, derive the size of the test set.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Given $\alpha = 0.8$ and total ratings $N$:

$$
\vert \mathcal{R}_{\text{train}} \vert \;=\; \alpha N \;=\; 0.8 N
$$

By the disjointness constraint $\vert \mathcal{R} \vert = \vert \mathcal{R}_{\text{train}} \vert + \vert \mathcal{R}_{\text{test}} \vert$:

$$
\vert \mathcal{R}_{\text{test}} \vert \;=\; N - 0.8 N \;=\; 0.2 N \;=\; \frac{N}{5}
$$

For an integer count:

$$
\boxed{\;\vert \mathcal{R}_{\text{test}} \vert \;=\; \bigl\lfloor 0.2\, N \bigr\rfloor\;}
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. You are expected to attempt **ONE full question** out of the two alternatives.

### Question B — Choice A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the **Hold-Out evaluation methodology** for a recommender system with a neat flowchart. Discuss why a *random* split with a *fixed seed* is mandatory. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Hold-Out method randomly partitions the rating matrix $\mathcal{R}$ into $\mathcal{R}_{\text{train}}$ and $\mathcal{R}_{\text{test}}$ such that $\mathcal{R} = \mathcal{R}_{\text{train}} \cup \mathcal{R}_{\text{test}}$ and $\mathcal{R}_{\text{train}} \cap \mathcal{R}_{\text{test}} = \varnothing$. The model is fit on $\mathcal{R}_{\text{train}}$ and then evaluated on the *unseen* ratings in $\mathcal{R}_{\text{test}}$.

**Why random?**  
If the split is *not* random, for example if all early-ratings go to training and late-ratings to test, the evaluation will be biased by the temporal ordering of clicks. Randomness guarantees that train and test have **statistically similar distributions** of users, items, and rating values.

**Why fixed seed?**  
A pseudo-random number generator seeded with the same integer $s$ produces the *same* permutation of ratings on every run. This **reproducibility** is a scientific and KTU-grading requirement: two students should obtain identical split files for the same input, enabling fair comparison of two different recommendation algorithms.

*Flowchart: Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 — full 1 mark is reserved for the labelled block diagram.*

**(b)** Consider a dataset with $N = 50\,000$ ratings. Using an 80/20 Hold-Out split, a collaborative-filtering model yields the following test predictions summarised in the table below:

| Predicted $\hat{r}_{ui}$ | Actual $r_{ui}$ | Frequency $n$ |
|---|---|---|
| 4.0 | 5.0 | 3000 |
| 3.0 | 4.0 | 4000 |
| 2.0 | 2.0 | 3000 |

Compute the **MAE** and **RMSE** for this evaluation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The test set size is $\vert \mathcal{R}_{\text{test}} \vert = 0.2 \times 50\,000 = 10\,000$ ratings.

**Step 1 — Absolute errors per group.**

Group 1: $\vert 4.0 - 5.0 \vert = 1.0$, contribution $= 3000 \times 1.0 = 3000$.  
Group 2: $\vert 3.0 - 4.0 \vert = 1.0$, contribution $= 4000 \times 1.0 = 4000$.  
Group 3: $\vert 2.0 - 2.0 \vert = 0.0$, contribution $= 3000 \times 0.0 = 0$.

**Step 2 — MAE.**

$$
\text{MAE} \;=\; \frac{3000 + 4000 + 0}{10\,000} \;=\; \frac{7000}{10\,000} \;=\; 0.70
$$

**Step 3 — Squared errors per group.**

Group 1: $(1.0)^2 = 1.0$, contribution $= 3000 \times 1.0 = 3000$.  
Group 2: $(1.0)^2 = 1.0$, contribution $= 4000 \times 1.0 = 4000$.  
Group 3: $(0.0)^2 = 0.0$, contribution $= 3000 \times 0.0 = 0$.

**Step 4 — Sum and divide.**

$$
\sum (r_{ui} - \hat{r}_{ui})^{2} \;=\; 3000 + 4000 + 0 \;=\; 7000
$$

$$
\text{MSE} \;=\; \frac{7000}{10\,000} \;=\; 0.70
$$

**Step 5 — RMSE.**

$$
\text{RMSE} \;=\; \sqrt{0.70} \;\approx\; 0.8367
$$

**Final Answer:** $\text{MAE} = 0.70$ and $\text{RMSE} \approx 0.8367$.

---

### Question B — Choice B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Compare **Hold-Out** and **$k$-Fold Cross-Validation** for evaluating a recommender system. Which is more suitable when $N$ is very large? Justify. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Criterion | Hold-Out | $k$-Fold Cross-Validation |
|---|---|---|
| Number of models trained | 1 | $k$ (typically 5 or 10) |
| Variance of error estimate | Higher | Lower (averaged over $k$ folds) |
| Computational cost | Low | $k$ times higher |
| Data utilization | Only $\alpha N$ used for training | $(k-1)N/k$ used per fold |
| Reproducibility | Seed-controlled | Seed-controlled per fold |
| Best for very large $N$ | ✅ Yes | ❌ Impractical (too slow) |

**Conclusion for large $N$:** When $N$ is in the millions, the **Hold-Out method is preferred** because the 20 % test set is still huge (e.g., 200 K ratings) and provides a *statistically stable* error estimate, while $k$-Fold would multiply the training cost by $k$ with negligible improvement in estimate quality.

**(b)** A recommender system is evaluated on a Hold-Out split of 100 K ratings. The split ratio is 90/10. The model produces the following confusion-style summary on the top-10 recommendations:

- **True Positives** TP = 600  
- **False Positives** FP = 400  
- **False Negatives** FN = 1400

Compute **Precision@10, Recall@10, and F1@10**. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The size of the test set is $0.10 \times 100\,000 = 10\,000$ ratings. The number of recommendations per user is $K = 10$, hence $TP + FP = 10 \times \text{users} = 1000$, which is consistent with $TP + FP = 1000$.

**Step 1 — Precision@10.**

$$
\text{Prec@10} \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \;=\; \frac{600}{600 + 400} \;=\; \frac{600}{1000} \;=\; 0.60
$$

**Step 2 — Recall@10.**

$$
\text{Rec@10} \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \;=\; \frac{600}{600 + 1400} \;=\; \frac{600}{2000} \;=\; 0.30
$$

**Step 3 — F1@10.**

$$
F_1 \;=\; \frac{2 \times 0.60 \times 0.30}{0.60 + 0.30} \;=\; \frac{0.36}{0.90} \;=\; 0.40
$$

**Comment (1 mark):** The model is moderately precise (60 %) but misses a large fraction of relevant items (only 30 % recall). This is the classic **precision-heavy** behaviour of short-list recommenders; improving recall would require increasing $K$ or adopting a two-stage retrieve-and-rerank pipeline.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in Hold-Out questions — avoid these!**
> 1. **Forgetting disjointness:** Several students write $\mathcal{R}_{\text{train}} \subset \mathcal{R}$ but forget to state $\mathcal{R}_{\text{train}} \cap \mathcal{R}_{\text{test}} = \varnothing$. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 2. **Wrong denominator in RMSE:** Using $N$ (the *total*) instead of $\vert \mathcal{R}_{\text{test}} \vert$ is the single most common arithmetic error. **Lose 2 marks.**
> 3. **Confusing $\alpha$ with $1-\alpha$:** The split ratio $\alpha$ is the *training fraction*, not the test fraction. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 4. **No seed mentioned:** Examiners explicitly test reproducibility. A Hold-Out answer without "with a fixed random seed $s$" loses a mark in viva.
> 5. **Mixing MAE and RMSE formulas:** The square root is applied *only* in RMSE, not in MAE. Double-check the final line.
> 6. **Skipping the flowchart:** A 1-mark allocation is reserved for a labelled block diagram in long answers — always include one.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Hold-Out** is the *first* evaluation protocol taught in Module 4: a single random split of $\mathcal{R}$ into $\mathcal{R}_{\text{train}}$ and $\mathcal{R}_{\text{test}}$.
- The disjointness identity $\mathcal{R} = \mathcal{R}_{\text{train}} \cup \mathcal{R}_{\text{test}}$ and the intersection $\mathcal{R}_{\text{train}} \cap \mathcal{R}_{\text{test}} = \varnothing$ are *non-negotiable*.
- The **split ratio** $\alpha = 0.8$ is the de-facto default; choosing it smaller raises bias, choosing it larger raises variance of the error estimate.
- The training set fits the model; the test set is the *only* legitimate source of reported performance numbers.
- **RMSE** penalises large errors quadratically; **MAE** penalises them linearly. RMSE $\geq$ MAE always, with equality only when all errors are equal in magnitude.
- A **fixed random seed** is mandatory for *reproducibility* — a frequent viva question.
- **Hold-Out is preferred over $k$-Fold CV** when the rating count $N$ is very large (e.g., millions).
- Limitations to recall: high variance of the estimate, data wastage, and cold-start leakage for sparse users/items.
- Always close with a **flowchart or block diagram** in long answers to claim the dedicated 1-mark allocation.
- One-line mantra for viva: *"Train on what is known, test on what is unknown — and never let the two sets overlap."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-Validation (CV)** is a **resampling-based statistical validation procedure** used in Recommendation Systems to estimate the **generalization performance** of a trained recommender model on **unseen user–item interactions**, while mitigating the risks of **overfitting, underfitting, and data leakage**.

In the context of **PECDT741 (Recommendation Systems)**, cross-validation is the standard mechanism for:

1. **Model Selection** — comparing collaborative filtering, matrix factorization, and deep models.
2. **Hyperparameter Tuning** — selecting optimal latent factors, regularization $\lambda$, learning rate $\eta$.
3. **Performance Estimation** — producing an unbiased estimate of metrics such as **RMSE, MAE, Precision@K, Recall@K, NDCG@K, MAP, and MRR**.

$$\text{Generalization Error} \approx \mathbb{E}_{(u,i) \sim \mathcal{D}_{\text{test}}} \big[ \mathcal{L}\big(\hat{r}_{ui}, r_{ui}\big) \big]$$

where $\mathcal{D}_{\text{test}}$ is the held-out test distribution, $\hat{r}_{ui}$ is the predicted rating, and $r_{ui}$ is the ground-truth rating.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Mock Test Before the Real Exam":**
>
> Imagine you are preparing for the **KTU End Semester Examination**. You do not write the actual exam 10 times to know your preparation level. Instead, you solve **10 mock test papers** under simulated conditions. Each mock paper is a *fold*. Your **average score across 10 mock papers** is a far more reliable estimate of your real exam performance than a single test.
>
> Similarly, in a **Recommender System**, instead of training on all data and testing on one held-out chunk (which gives a noisy, single-shot estimate), we **rotate the train/test split K times**, train K models, and **average their performance**. This is **K-Fold Cross-Validation** — the "10 mock papers" of machine learning.

## 1.3 Why Cross-Validation is Critical in Recommendation Systems

Unlike generic supervised learning, recommenders operate on **highly sparse, temporally-ordered, user-biased** data. This makes CV in RS uniquely challenging:

| Data Property | Impact on CV Design |
|---|---|
| **Extreme Sparsity** (often > 99% missing) | Standard random K-fold causes **user/item cold-start leakage** |
| **Temporal Ordering** | Random splits cause **temporal leakage** (training on the future to predict the past) |
| **User-Specific Distributions** | Need **user-stratified** or **leave-one-user-out** strategies |
| **Implicit Feedback** (clicks, views) | Requires **negative sampling** strategy inside CV |

## 1.4 The Bias–Variance Trade-off in CV

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High Bias** → too few folds (e.g., Hold-Out 50/50) — the model sees too little training data, performance estimate is pessimistic.
>
> **High Variance** → too many folds (e.g., Leave-One-Out) — models are nearly identical, but CV becomes **computationally expensive** and estimates have high variance for small datasets.

The optimal K balances these two — typically **K = 5 or K = 10** for medium-scale RS datasets (MovieLens 1M/10M, Amazon Reviews).

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Cross-Validation Techniques (KTU High-Yield)

The major CV strategies used in **PECDT741** are organized hierarchically below:

### A. **Non-Temporal Cross-Validation**

#### 2.1.1 K-Fold Cross-Validation

The dataset $\mathcal{D}$ of $N$ observed ratings is partitioned into **K equal-sized (or near-equal) disjoint folds** $F_1, F_2, \dots, F_K$ such that:

$$\bigcup_{k=1}^{K} F_k = \mathcal{D}, \quad F_i \cap F_j = \emptyset \;\; \forall\, i \neq j$$

For each fold $k$:
- **Training Set:** $\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}^{(k)} = \mathcal{D} \setminus F_k$
- **Test Set:** $\mathcal{D}_{\text{test}}^{(k)} = F_k$

The final performance estimate is the **arithmetic mean** of fold-wise metric values:

$$\text{CV Score} = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathcal{M}\big( f^{(k)}, \mathcal{D}_{\text{test}}^{(k)} \big)$$

where $f^{(k)}$ is the model trained on fold $k$'s training set, and $\mathcal{M}$ is the evaluation metric (RMSE, MAE, NDCG, etc.).

#### 2.1.2 Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation (LOOCV)

The extreme case where $K = N$. Each fold contains **exactly one observation**:

$$\text{LOOCV Score} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{M}\big( f^{(-i)}, \{(u_i, i_i, r_i)\} \big)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Pros:** Low bias (model trains on $N-1$ samples).
> **Cons:** High variance, **computationally infeasible** for large-scale recommenders (e.g., 100M ratings → 100M model trainings).

#### 2.1.3 Stratified K-Fold

Ensures each fold preserves the **rating distribution** (e.g., proportion of ratings $\{1,2,3,4,5\}$). Important for **imbalanced explicit feedback** datasets.

#### 2.1.4 User/Item-Stratified K-Fold

For each fold, ensure the **same set of users (or items)** appears in both train and test. Prevents **cold-start leakage**.

### B. **Temporal Cross-Validation (RS-Specific — KTU High-Yield)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In production recommender systems (Netflix, Spotify, Amazon), **time is the most important feature**. Random K-fold violates the **causal arrow of time**.

#### 2.1.5 Time-Based Split (Rolling Window / Forward Chaining)

Partition data by **timestamp** $t$. For window $k$:

$$\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}^{(k)} = \{ (u,i,r,t) \in \mathcal{D} \mid t \in [t_k^{\text{start}}, t_k^{\text{split}}] \}$$

$$\mathcal{D}_{\text{test}}^{(k)} = \{ (u,i,r,t) \in \mathcal{D} \mid t \in [t_k^{\text{split}}, t_k^{\text{end}}] \}$$

Sliding window moves forward: $(t_1, t_2, t_3, \dots)$ — each split simulates a real "predict the future" scenario.

#### 2.1.6 Global Temporal Split (Hold-Out-by-Time)

Single split: train on the **first 80% of interactions chronologically**, test on the **last 20%**. Common in industry leaderboards (Netflix Prize used a real temporal test set famously).

### C. **Group-Based CV (For Implicit Feedback & Sessions)**

#### 2.1.7 Leave-One-User-Out
For each user $u$, train on all other users' data, test on $u$'s held-out items. Used for evaluating **personalization quality**.

#### 2.1.8 Leave-One-Session-Out
Used in **session-based recommenders** (e.g., next-basket prediction). Each user session is a fold.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains all **mathematically verifiable** evaluation metrics that CV estimates. All pipes $\vert$ are rendered as $\mid$ to preserve markdown table integrity.

| Metric | Formula | Range | Best Value | Use Case in RS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **RMSE** | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{\mid \mathcal{T} \mid}\sum_{(u,i)\in \mathcal{T}}(\hat{r}_{ui} - r_{ui})^{2}}$ | $[0, +\infty)$ | $0$ | Explicit rating prediction |
| **MAE** | $\frac{1}{\mid \mathcal{T} \mid}\sum_{(u,i)\in \mathcal{T}} \mid \hat{r}_{ui} - r_{ui} \mid$ | $[0, +\infty)$ | $0$ | Explicit rating prediction (robust) |
| **Precision@K** | $\frac{\mid \text{Recommended}_K \cap \text{Relevant} \mid}{K}$ | $[0,1]$ | $1$ | Top-N ranking, ranking quality |
| **Recall@K** | $\frac{\mid \text{Recommended}_K \cap \text{Relevant} \mid}{\mid \text{Relevant} \mid}$ | $[0,1]$ | $1$ | Top-N ranking, coverage |
| **F1@K** | $\frac{2 \cdot P@K \cdot R@K}{P@K + R@K}$ | $[0,1]$ | $1$ | Balanced ranking |
| **NDCG@K** | $\frac{DCG@K}{IDCG@K}$, where $DCG@K = \sum_{k=1}^{K}\frac{2^{rel_k} - 1}{\log_2(k+1)}$ | $[0,1]$ | $1$ | Graded relevance ranking |
| **MAP** | $\frac{1}{\mid \mathcal{U} \mid}\sum_{u} AP(u)$ | $[0,1]$ | $1$ | Mean of per-user Average Precision |
| **MRR** | $\frac{1}{\mid \mathcal{U} \mid}\sum_{u} \frac{1}{\text{rank}_u^{\text{first relevant}}}$ | $[0,1]$ | $1$ | First-hit ranking |
| **Hit Rate@K** | $\frac{1}{\mid \mathcal{U} \mid}\sum_{u} \mathbb{1}[\text{hit}_u @ K]$ | $[0,1]$ | $1$ | Binary relevance |
| **Coverage** | $\frac{\mid \bigcup_u \text{Recommended}_K(u) \mid}{\mid \mathcal{I} \mid}$ | $[0,1]$ | $1$ | Catalog coverage |

**Notation Glossary:**
- $\mathcal{T}$ — test set of (user, item) pairs in current CV fold
- $\hat{r}_{ui}$ — predicted rating/score for user $u$ on item $i$
- $r_{ui}$ — ground-truth rating
- $rel_k$ — relevance label of item at rank $k$ (binary or graded)
- $\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}$ — set of users and items
- $\mathbb{1}[\cdot]$ — indicator function

## 2.3 The CV Procedure — Operational Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **operational pipeline** of any cross-validation run in an RS project is the following 6-step process:

1. **Data Partitioning:** Split $\mathcal{D}$ into $K$ folds (or temporal windows).
2. **Model Training:** For each fold $k$, train model $f^{(k)}$ on $\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}^{(k)}$.
3. **Prediction:** Generate predictions $\hat{r}_{ui}$ for all $(u,i) \in \mathcal{D}_{\text{test}}^{(k)}$.
4. **Metric Computation:** Compute $\mathcal{M}^{(k)}$ on fold $k$'s test predictions.
5. **Aggregation:** Compute mean $\mu_{\mathcal{M}}$ and standard deviation $\sigma_{\mathcal{M}}$ across folds.
6. **Statistical Comparison:** Use **paired t-test** or **Wilcoxon signed-rank test** to compare two CV score distributions.

$$\mu_{\mathcal{M}} = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K}\mathcal{M}^{(k)}, \quad \sigma_{\mathcal{M}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{K-1}\sum_{k=1}^{K}\big(\mathcal{M}^{(k)} - \mu_{\mathcal{M}}\big)^{2}}$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Netflix Prize (2006–2009):** Used a **temporal hold-out** (qualifying set + test set) — random CV would have been meaningless for movie recommendation.
- **Spotify Music Recommendation:** Uses **time-based rolling window CV** for Discover Weekly evaluation.
- **E-commerce (Amazon):** Uses **leave-one-session-out** for cart-abandonment and next-purchase prediction.
- **Academic Benchmarks (MovieLens, Amazon Reviews):** Default is **K = 5 stratified K-fold** for reproducible research.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: RMSE Estimator in K-Fold CV

### Step 1 — Define Per-Fold RMSE

For fold $k$, let $\mathcal{T}_k$ be its test set with cardinality $n_k = \mid \mathcal{T}_k \mid$. The squared-error loss for a single prediction is:

$$L^{(k)}_{ui} = \big(\hat{r}^{(k)}_{ui} - r_{ui}\big)^{2}$$

The Mean Squared Error on fold $k$ is:

$$\text{MSE}^{(k)} = \frac{1}{n_k} \sum_{(u,i) \in \mathcal{T}_k} L^{(k)}_{ui}$$

### Step 2 — Convert to RMSE

Taking the square root (the RMSE is in the **same units as the rating**, unlike MSE):

$$\text{RMSE}^{(k)} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}^{(k)}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n_k} \sum_{(u,i) \in \mathcal{T}_k} \big(\hat{r}^{(k)}_{ui} - r_{ui}\big)^{2}}$$

### Step 3 — CV Aggregated RMSE

The cross-validated RMSE is the **unweighted mean of fold-wise RMSEs** (most common) or the **pooled RMSE** (concatenate all predictions first):

$$\boxed{\;\text{RMSE}_{\text{CV}} = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \text{RMSE}^{(k)}\;}$$

**Alternative — Pooled RMSE** (when fold sizes are balanced):

$$\text{RMSE}_{\text{pooled}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N_{\text{total}}} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{(u,i)\in \mathcal{T}_k} \big(\hat{r}^{(k)}_{ui} - r_{ui}\big)^{2}}$$

where $N_{\text{total}} = \sum_k n_k$.

## 3.2 Derivation: NDCG@K from First Principles

### Step 1 — Cumulative Gain (CG@K)

The naive sum of relevances in the top-K list:

$$\text{CG@K} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} rel_k$$

**Problem:** Permutations of equally-relevant items produce the same CG. CG does not penalize placing highly relevant items at the bottom.

### Step 2 — Discounted Cumulative Gain (DCG@K)

Apply a **logarithmic discount** based on position:

$$\text{DCG@K} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{rel_k}{\log_2(k+1)}$$

The discount $\frac{1}{\log_2(k+1)}$ is $1$ at $k=1$ and decays smoothly — this is the standard formula (used in the original Burges et al. 2005 paper).

**Alternative DCG formula (used in many libraries like LightFM, Microsoft RankNet):**

$$\text{DCG@K} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{2^{rel_k} - 1}{\log_2(k+1)}$$

This **exponentially amplifies** highly graded relevant items (e.g., $rel=5$ gives $31$, $rel=1$ gives $1$).

### Step 3 — Ideal DCG (IDCG@K)

Sort all items by **true relevance descending** and compute DCG on that ideal ranking:

$$\text{IDCG@K} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{2^{rel^{\text{ideal}}_k} - 1}{\log_2(k+1)}$$

### Step 4 — Normalize

$$\text{NDCG@K} = \frac{\text{DCG@K}}{\text{IDCG@K}}$$

**Properties:** $\text{NDCG@K} \in [0, 1]$, equals $1$ iff the predicted ranking matches the ideal ranking.

## 3.3 Derivation: Average Precision (AP) and MAP

### Step 1 — Precision at Cutoff $k$

$$P@k = \frac{\mid \text{Recommended}_k \cap \text{Relevant} \mid}{k}$$

### Step 2 — Average Precision for User $u$

For a user $u$ with relevant items at positions $k_1, k_2, \dots, k_m$ in the ranked list:

$$\text{AP}(u) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{j=1}^{m} P@k_j$$

### Step 3 — Mean Average Precision (MAP)

Averaged over all users in the test fold:

$$\text{MAP} = \frac{1}{\mid \mathcal{U}_{\text{test}} \mid} \sum_{u \in \mathcal{U}_{\text{test}}} \text{AP}(u)$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — K-Fold RMSE

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A recommender produces the following test-set results across $K=4$ folds on a MovieLens-1M subset.

| Fold $k$ | $n_k$ | $\text{RMSE}^{(k)}$ | $\text{MAE}^{(k)}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 200,000 | 0.921 | 0.724 |
| 2 | 200,000 | 0.918 | 0.721 |
| 3 | 200,000 | 0.930 | 0.731 |
| 4 | 200,000 | 0.915 | 0.718 |

### Compute $\text{RMSE}_{\text{CV}}$:

$$\text{RMSE}_{\text{CV}} = \frac{1}{4}\big(0.921 + 0.918 + 0.930 + 0.915\big)$$

$$\text{RMSE}_{\text{CV}} = \frac{1}{4} \times 3.684 = 0.921$$

### Compute $\sigma_{\text{RMSE}}$:

$$\sigma_{\text{RMSE}} = \sqrt{\frac{(0.921-0.921)^{2} + (0.918-0.921)^{2} + (0.930-0.921)^{2} + (0.915-0.921)^{2}}{4-1}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{0 + 0.000009 + 0.000081 + 0.000036}{3}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.000126}{3}} = \sqrt{0.000042} \approx 0.0065$$

**Final Reported Result:** $\text{RMSE}_{\text{CV}} = 0.921 \pm 0.0065$.

## 3.5 Statistical Significance: Paired t-Test for Model Comparison

To determine if Model A ($\mu_A, \sigma_A$) is significantly better than Model B ($\mu_B, \sigma_B$) across K folds:

$$t = \frac{\mu_A - \mu_B}{\sigma_d / \sqrt{K}}$$

where $\sigma_d$ is the standard deviation of the **paired differences** $d_k = \text{score}_A^{(k)} - \text{score}_B^{(k)}$:

$$\sigma_d = \sqrt{\frac{1}{K-1}\sum_{k=1}^{K}(d_k - \bar{d})^{2}}, \quad \bar{d} = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K}d_k$$

Compare $\mid t \mid$ against the critical value $t_{\alpha/2, K-1}$ from the Student's t-distribution. If $\mid t \mid > t_{\text{critical}}$, the difference is **statistically significant at significance level $\alpha$** (typically $\alpha = 0.05$).
