<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Define Spearman's Rank Correlation Coefficient. State its range and the formula used in the absence of tied ranks.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Spearman's Rank Correlation Coefficient (denoted by the Greek letter $\rho$ or $r_s$) is a non-parametric statistical measure that quantifies the strength and direction of the *monotonic* relationship between two ranked variables. In recommendation systems, it is used to assess the degree to which the **predicted item ranking** produced by a model matches the **ground-truth item ranking** derived from user feedback.

> **Range (1 mark):** $\rho \in [-1, +1]$ where $+1$ indicates perfect positive agreement, $-1$ indicates perfect inverse agreement, and $0$ indicates no monotonic relationship.

> **Formula without ties (1 mark):**
> $$\rho = 1 - \dfrac{6 \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i^2}{n(n^2 - 1)}$$
> where $n$ is the number of items and $d_i$ is the difference between the predicted and true rank of item $i$.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Distinguish between Spearman's ρ and Kendall's τ in the context of recommendation system evaluation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Spearman's ρ | Kendall's τ |
|---|---|---|
| **Mathematical basis** | Sum of squared rank differences (1 mark) | Difference between concordant ($C$) and discordant ($D$) pair counts (1 mark) |
| **Interpretation** | Strength of monotonic relationship | Probability of pairwise agreement minus probability of pairwise disagreement |
| **Complexity** | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n^2)$ — enumerates all pairs (1 mark) |
| **Sensitivity to local swaps** | Lower (squared penalty smooths impact) | Higher (each pair contributes equally) |

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Format)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper]**
> **(a)** A recommender system generates the following top-6 ranking for a user, while the true relevance order is also given. Compute **Spearman's ρ** using the shortcut formula. Interpret the result. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the steps involved in evaluating a recommender's ranking quality using Kendall's τ-b. State two situations in which Kendall's τ is preferred over Spearman's ρ. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Spearman's ρ Computation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Tabulate ranks and compute $d_i$ (2 marks):**

| Item | Predicted Rank $p_i$ | True Rank $g_i$ | $d_i = p_i - g_i$ | $d_i^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | 1 | 3 | −2 | 4 |
| P2 | 2 | 1 | +1 | 1 |
| P3 | 3 | 2 | +1 | 1 |
| P4 | 4 | 6 | −2 | 4 |
| P5 | 5 | 4 | +1 | 1 |
| P6 | 6 | 5 | +1 | 1 |
| | | | **$\sum d_i^2$** | **12** |

**Step 2 — Apply the formula with $n = 6$ (3 marks):**
$$\rho = 1 - \dfrac{6 \sum d_i^2}{n(n^2 - 1)} = 1 - \dfrac{6 \times 12}{6(36 - 1)} = 1 - \dfrac{72}{210} = 1 - 0.3429 = 0.6571$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation (2 marks):**
$\rho \approx 0.657$ indicates a **moderately strong positive monotonic relationship** between predicted and true rankings. The recommender captures the general user preference ordering well, but with noticeable local inversions — particularly in the lower half of the list (P4 dropped from rank 4 to true rank 6).

---

**(b) Kendall's τ-b Procedure and Justification (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Procedure for Kendall's τ-b evaluation (4 marks):**
1. For each user $u$, obtain the predicted ranking $P_u$ and ground-truth ranking $G_u$ over the $n$ candidate items.
2. Enumerate all $\binom{n}{2}$ unordered pairs $(i, j)$ with $i < j$ in the predicted ranking.
3. For each pair, classify it as:
   - **Concordant** if $g_i < g_j$ (order preserved).
   - **Discordant** if $g_i > g_j$ (order reversed).
   - **Tied** in $G$ if $g_i = g_j$ — increment $T_Q$ counter.
   - **Tied in $P$** if $p_i = p_j$ — increment $T_P$ counter.
4. Apply the τ-b formula:
$$\tau_b = \dfrac{C - D}{\sqrt{(C + D + T_P)(C + D + T_Q)}}$$
5. Average over all users to get the system-level score.

**Step 2 — Two situations preferring Kendall's τ (3 marks):**
- **Top-K cut-off evaluation:** When only the top-$K$ recommended items matter (the realistic deployment scenario), Kendall's τ-c handles the *rectangular* table structure naturally, whereas Spearman's ρ assumes the full $n$ items are ranked.
- **Sparse implicit feedback:** When user interactions produce many ties (e.g., multiple items clicked an equal number of times), τ-b explicitly accounts for ties in both rankings, while the Spearman shortcut becomes invalid and must fall back to a more cumbersome computation.
- **Sample size efficiency:** For the same $\rho$ and $\tau$ magnitudes, Kendall's τ has a smaller asymptotic variance, making it a more *statistically efficient* estimator when the test set is small.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper]**
> **(a)** Derive Spearman's rank correlation formula from Pearson's correlation coefficient. Clearly state the assumptions under which the shortcut formula is valid. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A news aggregator uses a hybrid recommender. For a sample user, the predicted and true top-7 rankings are given below. Compute **Kendall's τ-a** and **Spearman's ρ**. Which one is more appropriate here and why? **(7 marks)**

**Data:**

| Article | Predicted Rank $p_i$ | True Rank $g_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| A1 | 1 | 2 |
| A2 | 2 | 1 |
| A3 | 3 | 3 |
| A4 | 4 | 5 |
| A5 | 5 | 4 |
| A6 | 6 | 7 |
| A7 | 7 | 6 |

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Starting from Pearson's formula (1 mark):**
$$r = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^2 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n}(Y_i - \bar{Y})^2}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $X_i = p_i$, $Y_i = g_i$ and use $\bar{p} = \bar{g} = (n+1)/2$ (2 marks):**

**Step 3 — Compute the rank variance using $\sum i^2 = n(n+1)(2n+1)/6$ (2 marks):**
$$\sum(p_i - \bar{p})^2 = \sum g_i^2 = \dfrac{n(n^2 - 1)}{12}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the identity $d_i = p_i - g_i$ and the rank-sums equality to simplify the numerator (1 mark):**

**Step 5 — Final assembled formula (1 mark):**
$$\rho = 1 - \dfrac{6 \sum d_i^2}{n(n^2 - 1)}$$

**Assumptions for validity (1 mark):** *No ties* in either ranking; rankings are a permutation of $\{1, 2, \dots, n\}$; both variables are at least ordinally measured.

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

**Spearman's ρ — Compute $d_i$ and $d_i^2$ (3 marks):**

| Article | $p_i$ | $g_i$ | $d_i$ | $d_i^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A1 | 1 | 2 | −1 | 1 |
| A2 | 2 | 1 | +1 | 1 |
| A3 | 3 | 3 | 0 | 0 |
| A4 | 4 | 5 | −1 | 1 |
| A5 | 5 | 4 | +1 | 1 |
| A6 | 6 | 7 | −1 | 1 |
| A7 | 7 | 6 | +1 | 1 |
| | | | **$\sum d_i^2$** | **6** |

With $n = 7$:
$$\rho = 1 - \dfrac{6 \times 6}{7(49 - 1)} = 1 - \dfrac{36}{336} = 1 - 0.1071 = 0.8929$$

**Kendall's τ-a — Enumerate all 21 pairs (3 marks):**
Counting carefully:
- Concordant: (A1,A4), (A1,A5), (A1,A6), (A1,A7), (A2,A4), (A2,A5), (A2,A6), (A2,A7), (A3,A4), (A3,A5), (A3,A6), (A3,A7), (A4,A6), (A5,A7) → $C = 14$
- Discordant: (A1,A2), (A4,A5), (A6,A7) → $D = 3$ (Note: A1↔A2 swap; A4↔A5 swap; A6↔A7 swap)
- Tied: (A3 vs others? — no ties since all ranks distinct)
- Verify: $C + D = 17 \ne 21$. **Recount:** With 7 items there are 21 pairs; here all pairs are distinct ranks so no ties. Let me recount discordant pairs: (A1,A2) discord, (A4,A5) discord, (A6,A7) discord → $D = 3$. Therefore $C = 21 - 3 = 18$.

**Recount verification:** $C = 18, D = 3$.
$$\tau_a = \dfrac{18 - 3}{21} = \dfrac{15}{21} = 0.7143$$

**Final selection and justification (1 mark):**

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Spearman's ρ | 0.8929 |
| Kendall's τ-a | 0.7143 |

For a **news aggregator**, only the **top few articles** drive user engagement (the *position bias* phenomenon). **Kendall's τ** is more appropriate because:
- It weighs every discordant pair equally, including those at the top of the list.
- It directly corresponds to the **probability that any two randomly chosen articles are in the correct relative order** — a directly meaningful user-experience quantity.
- Spearman's $\rho = 0.89$ may *overstate* quality because the squared-distance penalty is dominated by the consistent middle of the list.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the no-tie assumption:** Students use the Spearman shortcut on tied data and lose 2–3 marks. Always verify that all ranks are distinct integers from 1 to $n$ before applying $1 - 6\sum d_i^2 / [n(n^2-1)]$.
> 2. **Sign errors in $d_i$:** The squaring in $d_i^2$ masks the sign, but $\sum d_i$ must equal **zero** (a free sanity check). If it doesn't, you've transposed a table column.
> 3. **Kendall's denominator:** Mixing up $\tau_a$, $\tau_b$, and $\tau_c$ denominators is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction. The question stem will dictate which to use based on tie structure.
> 4. **Forgetting the unit of averaging:** Always state that the per-user coefficient is *averaged over the user population* to obtain a system-level score.
> 5. **Interpretation vague:** "The correlation is 0.8" gets 0 marks for interpretation. Always say: *"This indicates a strong positive monotonic relationship — the recommender's predicted ranking closely tracks the true user preference ordering."*

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Definition:** Correlation-based ranking evaluation quantifies the *agreement* between the **predicted item order** and the **true relevance order** using Spearman's ρ or Kendall's τ.
- **Two canonical metrics:** Spearman's ρ (monotonic strength) and Kendall's τ (pairwise agreement probability).
- **Spearman shortcut formula:** $\rho = 1 - \dfrac{6 \sum d_i^2}{n(n^2 - 1)}$ — valid **only** when there are no ties.
- **Tie-handling formula (Spearman):** Use Pearson's correlation *computed on the rank values* themselves; tied ranks get the **mid-rank** $r_i = (l + h)/2$.
- **Kendall's τ-a:** $\tau_a = \dfrac{C - D}{n(n-1)/2}$ — no ties allowed.
- **Kendall's τ-b:** Adjusts denominator for ties — $\tau_b = \dfrac{C - D}{\sqrt{(C+D+T_P)(C+D+T_Q)}}$.
- **Kendall's τ-c:** Designed for **rectangular** top-K evaluation tables.
- **Range of all metrics:** $\rho, \tau \in [-1, +1]$; perfect ranking → $+1$, reversed → $-1$, random → $0$.
- **Inequality:** $|\rho| \ge |\tau|$ generally holds; both should be reported in a thorough KTU answer.
- **Significance test:** $t = \rho \sqrt{(n-2)/(1-\rho^2)}$ with $n - 2$ degrees of freedom; report $p < 0.05$ for significance.
- **Why use correlation over RMSE:** Correlation **amplifies errors at the top of the list** — the user-visible region — whereas RMSE treats all positions equally.
- **Real-world applications:** A/B test comparison, cold-start validation, Learning-to-Rank (LTR) loss, search re-ranking, streaming content ordering.
- **Default unit of aggregation:** Average per-user correlation across the test population to get a single system-level score.
- **Default tie-breaking:** When in doubt, fall back to the **Pearson-on-ranks** formula — it is universally valid and reduces to Spearman's shortcut when there are no ties.
- **Board tip:** Always **define the metric, state its range, write the formula, and interpret the numerical value in a complete sentence** — KTU examiners award marks for each of these four elements.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluating Ranking via Utility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT741 - RECOMMENDATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Evaluating Ranking via Utility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Evaluating Ranking via Utility** is the quantitative assessment of a ranked list produced by a recommender (or information retrieval) system based on the **utility (usefulness / gain / reward)** that a user derives from each item, with a position-dependent discount applied because items placed higher in the list are exponentially more likely to be inspected and acted upon.

Mathematically, a ranking function is modelled as:

$$\mathcal{R}: U \times I \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$$

where every user–item pair $(u, i)$ is assigned a real-valued utility score $r_{u,i}$, and items are ordered by descending $r_{u,i}$. Evaluation then asks: *how much of the **true user utility** is captured by the top-$K$ positions of the predicted list?*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECDT741, Module 4):** Utility-based evaluation bridges two worlds — (a) the **system perspective** (how accurate is the ranking?), and (b) the **user perspective** (how much real value does the user get?). It is the bridge between offline metrics and online business value (clicks, purchases, dwell time).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Magazine Cover" Intuition

Imagine you are a guest editor for a fashion magazine. You have **20 garments** to feature, but the **cover (Rank 1)** will be seen by 10,000 readers, the **inner back page (Rank 2)** by 4,000, and a random page in between (Rank 18) by perhaps 200.

- If the cover item is *exactly* what the reader wants, they buy the magazine (huge utility).
- If the same item is buried on page 18, the reader may never even see it (zero utility, even though the item itself is perfect).

**Utility evaluation rewards systems that put the right item at the right position.** It is not enough to recommend good items — they must be **ranked high enough** that the user actually encounters them.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Utility-Based Ranking

| Term | Standard Notation | KTU Board Weight |
|------|------|------|
| Relevance / Gain | $rel_i \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots\}$ | High |
| Rank Position | $i \in \{1, 2, \dots, p\}$ | High |
| Position Discount | $\frac{1}{\log_2(i+1)}$ | Very High |
| Ideal Discount | $IDCG_p$ | Very High |
| Final Score | $NDCG_p$ | **Most frequent in KTU ESE** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention Used in KTU Boards:** The relevance grade scale is typically **binary** $\{0, 1\}$ in textbook problems, but the **graded** scale $\{0, 1, 2, 3\}$ is expected in advanced exam questions on DCG/NDCG.

## 1.4 Why Not Just Use Accuracy or RMSE?

- **RMSE** and **MAE** evaluate *rating prediction*, not *rank quality*.
- An item with predicted rating 4.8 might still be ranked below an item with rating 4.5 in a "Top-10" list due to a small rounding — RMSE would say *"fine"*, but the user may never see the 4.8 item.
- **Utility metrics** such as **NDCG**, **MAP**, and **MRR** directly penalize misplacements in the **ranking order**, which is what truly matters in recommendation and search.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Utility-Based Ranking Evaluation

The evaluation pipeline follows a **six-step** logical structure, almost identical in form to a software engineering data flow:

1. **Generate Predicted List** — Use a ranker $\mathcal{R}$ to score all unseen items for a user.
2. **Sort by Descending Score** — Produce the predicted ranking $\pi_{pred}$.
3. **Map to Ground-Truth Relevance** — Look up the *true* relevance $rel_i^{\ast}$ for each item in the test set.
4. **Compute Position-wise Gain** — Assign $gain_i = rel_i^{\ast}$ (or $2^{rel_i^{\ast}} - 1$ for the exponential variant).
5. **Apply Position Discount** — Divide by $\log_2(i+1)$ (the **logarithmic discount**), modelling the empirical fact that user attention drops logarithmically with rank.
6. **Aggregate & Normalize** — Sum the discounted gains, then divide by the **Ideal DCG (IDCG)** to obtain a value in $[0, 1]$.

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" of the $\log_2(i+1)$ Discount:** Eye-tracking studies by Joachims (2002) and later by Google search behaviour analysis showed that click probability vs. position follows a curve that is **very close to $1 / \log_2(rank+1)$**. The logarithmic decay is therefore not a heuristic — it is a *fitted empirical model* of human attention.

## 2.2 The Five KTU-Standard Utility Metrics

### (a) Cumulative Gain (CG)
The simplest baseline — sum of all relevance grades up to position $p$. *No discount* applied.

$$CG_p = \sum_{i=1}^{p} rel_i^{\ast}$$

**Why CG fails as a standalone metric:** It treats "relevant item at position 1" and "relevant item at position 100" as equal. Boards often ask why CG is insufficient — answer: **it ignores rank position**, the very heart of utility-based evaluation.

### (b) Discounted Cumulative Gain (DCG)

$$DCG_p = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \frac{rel_i^{\ast}}{\log_2(i+1)}$$

**Exponential DCG variant (used in industry, e.g., Microsoft Bing, Yahoo! Learning to Rank):**

$$DCG_p = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \frac{2^{rel_i^{\ast}} - 1}{\log_2(i+1)}$$

The exponential version amplifies the gap between low-grade and high-grade relevance, which is appropriate for graded scales (0–3 or 0–4).

### (c) Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (NDCG)

$$NDCG_p = \frac{DCG_p}{IDCG_p}$$

where $IDCG_p$ is the DCG of the **ideal ranking** (relevance grades sorted in descending order). NDCG $\in [0, 1]$ allows comparison **across users** and **across queries**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Normalize?** If a user has only 2 relevant items in the entire catalogue, their $IDCG_2$ is small. If another user has 200 relevant items, their $IDCG_{200}$ is huge. Without normalization, DCG scores are not comparable. **IDCG is the KTU favourite — be ready to compute it.**

### (d) Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR)
Best suited for **single-answer** queries (e.g., "what is the capital of France?").

$$MRR = \frac{1}{\vert Q \vert} \sum_{q=1}^{\vert Q \vert} \frac{1}{rank_q}$$

where $rank_q$ is the position of the **first correct answer** for query $q$.

### (e) Mean Average Precision (MAP)

$$MAP = \frac{1}{\vert Q \vert} \sum_{q=1}^{\vert Q \vert} AP(q)$$

$$AP(q) = \frac{1}{R_q} \sum_{i=1}^{n} Precision(i) \cdot rel_i^{\ast}$$

where $R_q$ is the total number of relevant items for query $q$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!CAUTION]
> **Table formatting rule:** All vertical bars in math expressions below use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| Metric | Formula | Output Range | Best For | Penalty for Bad Ranking |
|--------|---------|--------------|----------|-------------------------|
| **CG** | $CG_p = \sum_{i=1}^{p} rel_i^{\ast}$ | $[0, p \cdot rel_{max}]$ | Baseline, rarely used alone | **None** — position-blind |
| **DCG** | $DCG_p = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \dfrac{rel_i^{\ast}}{\log_2(i+1)}$ | $[0, +\infty)$ | Within-user comparison | Logarithmic |
| **NDCG** | $NDCG_p = \dfrac{DCG_p}{IDCG_p}$ | $[0, 1]$ | **Cross-user / cross-query** | Logarithmic, normalized |
| **MRR** | $MRR = \dfrac{1}{\vert Q \vert} \sum_{q=1}^{\vert Q \vert} \dfrac{1}{rank_q}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Single-answer queries | Inverse linear |
| **MAP** | $MAP = \dfrac{1}{\vert Q \vert} \sum_{q=1}^{\vert Q \vert} AP(q)$ | $[0, 1]$ | Multi-relevant queries | Position-weighted |
| **Precision@K** | $P@K = \dfrac{\vert \text{relevant} \cap \text{top-}K \vert}{K}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Top-K slot quality | Linear |
| **Recall@K** | $R@K = \dfrac{\vert \text{relevant} \cap \text{top-}K \vert}{\vert \text{relevant} \vert}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Coverage of true set | Linear |
| **F1@K** | $F1@K = \dfrac{2 \cdot P@K \cdot R@K}{P@K + R@K}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Balanced P/R | Harmonic mean |
| **ERR** | $ERR = \sum_{r=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{r} \prod_{i=1}^{r-1}(1 - R_i)$ | $[0, 1]$ | Cascade user model | Cascade probability |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Metric of Choice | Why |
|--------|------------------|-----|
| Web Search (Google, Bing) | **NDCG@10** | Multi-level relevance, position-critical, cross-query comparable |
| E-commerce (Amazon, Flipkart) | **NDCG, MAP, P@K** | Many products, binary "bought" or "did not buy" |
| Q&A Systems (Stack Overflow, Quora) | **MRR** | Usually one accepted answer |
| News Feed (Twitter, LinkedIn) | **NDCG, ERR** | Cascade model — users scroll until bored |
| Movie / Music (Netflix, Spotify) | **NDCG, MAP** | Long lists, graded engagement (watch %, skip %) |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — A Complete NDCG Computation

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** A 14-mark question on NDCG/MRR usually presents a small ranked list (4–6 items) with graded relevance and asks for full computation **including IDCG**. The model solution below mirrors the board valuation key.

### Problem Statement

A recommender system returns the top-5 items for user $U_1$ in the following order:

| Predicted Rank $i$ | Item ID | Predicted Score | True Relevance $rel_i^{\ast}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_7$ | 0.91 | **3** |
| 2 | $I_2$ | 0.84 | **2** |
| 3 | $I_{11}$ | 0.76 | **0** |
| 4 | $I_4$ | 0.65 | **2** |
| 5 | $I_9$ | 0.55 | **1** |

The full ground-truth relevance of all candidate items known to be relevant is: $I_7, I_2, I_4, I_9, I_{15}, I_3$.
Compute the **NDCG@5** for this user.

### Step 1 — Compute the Discount Factor for Each Position

We need $\log_2(i+1)$ for $i = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$.

| $i$ | $i+1$ | $\log_2(i+1)$ | $\dfrac{1}{\log_2(i+1)}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | 1.0000 | **1.0000** |
| 2 | 3 | 1.5849 | **0.6309** |
| 3 | 4 | 2.0000 | **0.5000** |
| 4 | 5 | 2.3219 | **0.4307** |
| 5 | 6 | 2.5849 | **0.3868** |

### Step 2 — Compute Discounted Gain (DG) at Each Position

For the **linear DCG** form: $DG_i = \dfrac{rel_i^{\ast}}{\log_2(i+1)}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
DG_1 &= \frac{3}{1.0000} = 3.0000 \\[4pt]
DG_2 &= \frac{2}{1.5849} = 1.2618 \\[4pt]
DG_3 &= \frac{0}{2.0000} = 0.0000 \\[4pt]
DG_4 &= \frac{2}{2.3219} = 0.8614 \\[4pt]
DG_5 &= \frac{1}{2.5849} = 0.3868
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Sum the Discounted Gains → DCG@5

$$
\begin{aligned}
DCG@5 &= \sum_{i=1}^{5} DG_i \\[4pt]
&= 3.0000 + 1.2618 + 0.0000 + 0.8614 + 0.3868 \\[4pt]
&= 5.5100
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Construct the Ideal Ranking

Take the **6 truly relevant items** with their relevance grades and sort in **descending** order. The top 5 of the ideal list are:

| Ideal Rank $i$ | Item | $rel_i^{\ast}$ (Ideal) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_7$ | 3 |
| 2 | $I_2$ | 2 |
| 3 | $I_4$ | 2 |
| 4 | $I_9$ | 1 |
| 5 | $I_3$ | 0 |

### Step 5 — Compute the IDCG@5

Using the same discount factors (since they depend only on position, not on which item):

$$
\begin{aligned}
IDCG@5 &= \frac{3}{1.0000} + \frac{2}{1.5849} + \frac{2}{2.3219} + \frac{1}{2.5849} + \frac{0}{2.8073} \\[4pt]
&= 3.0000 + 1.2618 + 0.8614 + 0.3868 + 0.0000 \\[4pt]
&= 5.5100
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Curious observation:** Here $IDCG@5 = DCG@5 = 5.51$ because the predicted ranking **happened to be optimal** (except for $I_3$ being swapped with $I_9$, both having low relevance). NDCG will therefore equal **1.0**, indicating a perfect top-5 utility alignment.

### Step 6 — Final NDCG@5

$$
\begin{aligned}
NDCG@5 &= \frac{DCG@5}{IDCG@5} = \frac{5.5100}{5.5100} = 1.0000
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Counter-Example — When NDCG < 1

To clearly demonstrate the **penalty mechanism**, consider a perturbed ranking where the model **swaps** $I_7$ (rel = 3) and $I_4$ (rel = 2):

| Predicted Rank $i$ | Item | $rel_i^{\ast}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_2$ | 2 |
| 2 | $I_7$ | 3 |
| 3 | $I_4$ | 2 |
| 4 | $I_9$ | 1 |
| 5 | $I_{11}$ | 0 |

Recompute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
DCG@5 &= \frac{2}{1.0000} + \frac{3}{1.5849} + \frac{2}{2.3219} + \frac{1}{2.5849} + \frac{0}{2.8073} \\[4pt]
&= 2.0000 + 1.8928 + 0.8614 + 0.3868 + 0.0000 \\[4pt]
&= 5.1410
\end{aligned}
$$

Now $IDCG@5$ remains **5.51** (because the ideal order is unchanged), so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
NDCG@5 = \frac{5.1410}{5.5100} \approx 0.9330
\end{aligned}
$$

**Drop from 1.0000 → 0.9330** = **6.7 % utility loss** for a single swap at the top. This is the **NDCG penalty mechanism** in action.

## 3.3 MRR — Step-by-Step Derivation

Suppose we have **3 users** $U_1, U_2, U_3$ with the position of the first relevant item in their respective top-10 lists being **2, 1, 5**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
MRR &= \frac{1}{3} \left( \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{1} + \frac{1}{5} \right) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{3} (0.5000 + 1.0000 + 0.2000) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{3} \times 1.7000 = 0.5667
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 MAP — Step-by-Step Derivation

For a single user, the top-6 ranked items and their relevance are: `[1, 0, 1, 1, 0, 0]`.

Precision at each position where a relevant item appears (i.e., at $i = 1, 3, 4$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(1) &= \frac{1}{1} = 1.0000 \\[4pt]
P(3) &= \frac{2}{3} = 0.6667 \\[4pt]
P(4) &= \frac{3}{4} = 0.7500
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
AP &= \frac{1}{R} \sum_{P @ relevant} P(i) = \frac{1}{3}(1.0000 + 0.6667 + 0.7500) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{3}(2.4167) = 0.8056
\end{aligned}
$$

For 3 users with AP = 0.8056, 0.6667, 0.5000:

$$
\begin{aligned}
MAP = \frac{0.8056 + 0.6667 + 0.5000}{3} = 0.6574
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Why the Logarithmic Discount? — A Behavioural Derivation

The discount factor $1 / \log_2(i+1)$ can be derived from a **cascade user model**:

- Let $R_i$ be the probability that the user finds the item at position $i$ relevant.
- The probability of **continuing to inspect** position $i+1$ given the user is at $i$ is $(1 - R_i)$.
- The probability of **stopping** at position $i$ is $R_i \cdot \prod_{j=1}^{i-1} (1 - R_j)$.
- The expected utility of the **ranked list** is then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[U] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i \cdot \prod_{j=1}^{i-1} (1 - R_j) \cdot \phi(i)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\phi(i)$ is a strictly decreasing position utility (e.g., $\phi(i) = 1 / \log_2(i+1)$). The **NDCG** can be shown to be a **first-order approximation** of this expected utility when relevance probabilities are independent and uniform — a fact board examiners occasionally mention in the **"Justify the choice of discount"** 7-mark sub-question.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Utility-Based Ranking Evaluation Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **data flow** and **algorithmic interactions** within a typical utility-based ranking evaluation module, as a substitute for physical drawings of metric curves.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User u and Item Pool I] --> B[Train Recommender Model R]
    B --> C[Generate Predicted Score r subscript ui]
    C --> D[Sort Items by Descending Score]
    D --> E[Predicted Top-K List pi subscript pred]
    E --> F[Lookup Ground Truth Relevance rel superscript asterisk]
    F --> G{Choose Metric}
    G --> H1[Compute CG p]
    G --> H2[Compute DCG p with Log Discount]
    G --> H3[Compute NDCG p with IDCG Normalization]
    G --> H4[Compute MRR Reciprocal of First Hit]
    G --> H5[Compute MAP Average Precision]
    H1 --> I[Aggregate over all Users U]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    H5 --> I
    I --> J[Final Utility Report NDCG MRR MAP]
    J --> K{Decision Threshold}
    K -- Pass --> L[Deploy Model to Production]
    K -- Fail --> M[Retrain with New Features or Hyperparameters]
    M --> B
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Discount Function Comparison

This second diagram visually contrasts the **logarithmic discount** of NDCG with the **inverse-rank discount** of MRR. It is a high-yield schematic for the "Compare and Contrast" type of KTU questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Rank Position i] --> B1[Log Discount 1 over log2 i plus 1]
    A --> B2[Inverse Rank 1 over i]
    A --> B3[Step Function 1 if i less than K else 0]
    B1 --> C1[Used in NDCG smooth decay]
    B2 --> C2[Used in MRR sharp penalty]
    B3 --> C3[Used in Precision at K hard cutoff]
    C1 --> D[Final Score in 0 to 1]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
```

## 4.3 Schematic Explanation of the Architecture

| Block | Function | Engineering Counterpart |
|------|----------|-------------------------|
| Train Recommender Model $R$ | Builds the ranker; could be Matrix Factorization, BPR, LambdaMART, etc. | ML training pipeline (e.g., TensorFlow, PyTorch) |
| Sort by Descending Score | The actual ranking step that the user perceives | Elasticsearch / Solr sort, Spark MLlib |
| Ground-Truth Lookup | A/B test logs, click logs, purchase logs, expert ratings | Offline label store (BigQuery, Snowflake) |
| Metric Computation | NDCG, MAP, MRR computation | `sklearn.metrics`, `pytrec_eval`, `rankeval` |
| Decision Threshold | CI/CD gate; if NDCG < 0.80, block deployment | Model registry (MLflow, Kubeflow) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** When the question asks *"How would you deploy a ranker in production?"*, the correct answer mentions: **(i) offline NDCG validation, (ii) online A/B test, (iii) interleaving, (iv) guard-rail metrics like coverage and diversity**. Boards award 2 marks for each of these four points.
