# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Cumulative Gain (CG) and Discounted Cumulative Gain (DCG) with a suitable example. Why is DCG preferred for ranking evaluation?**

**Model Answer:**

- **CG** sums the raw relevance grades up to position $p$ **without** considering the position of items. *It is position-blind.*
- **DCG** divides each relevance grade by a position-dependent discount $\log_2(i+1)$, rewarding systems that place highly relevant items at the top.

**Example:** For two rankings of relevance grades $[3, 0, 3, 0]$ and $[0, 3, 0, 3]$:
- $CG@4 = 6$ for both.
- $DCG@4$ for the first list $= 3 + 0.631 + 1.5 + 0 = 5.131$ (intuitively better).

**Why DCG is preferred:** The first ranking is genuinely more useful to a user (top item is excellent), and DCG captures this; CG does not. **(3 marks: 1 definition + 1 example + 1 justification)**

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define MRR. When is it the appropriate evaluation metric and when does it fail?**

**Model Answer:**

- **MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rank)** is the mean over all queries of the reciprocal of the rank position of the **first relevant item**.

$$MRR = \frac{1}{\vert Q \vert} \sum_{q=1}^{\vert Q \vert} \frac{1}{rank_q}$$

- **Appropriate when:** there is exactly **one correct answer** per query (e.g., factoid Q&A, navigational search, "what is the phone number of X?").
- **Fails when:** multiple relevant items exist per query (e.g., movie recommendations, e-commerce), because it ignores items beyond the first hit. NDCG or MAP should be used instead. **(3 marks: 1 definition + 1 use + 1 limitation)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern: **Module-internal choice**. Below are **two fully independent 14-mark questions** that may appear as an "either / or" pair. Each has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question B-A — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**A recommender system produced the following top-6 ranked items for user $U$:**

| Rank $i$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $rel_i^{\ast}$ | 3 | 0 | 2 | 1 | 0 | 2 |

The **complete** set of relevant items in the catalogue for this user has relevance grades $\{3, 2, 2, 1\}$ (i.e., there are 4 truly relevant items).

#### (a) Compute the NDCG@6 for this ranking. Show the construction of the IDCG. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Discount factors:**

| $i$ | $\log_2(i+1)$ | $1 / \log_2(i+1)$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.0000 | 1.0000 |
| 2 | 1.5849 | 0.6309 |
| 3 | 2.0000 | 0.5000 |
| 4 | 2.3219 | 0.4307 |
| 5 | 2.5849 | 0.3868 |
| 6 | 2.8073 | 0.3562 |

**Step 2 — DCG@6:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DCG@6 &= \frac{3}{1.0000} + \frac{0}{1.5849} + \frac{2}{2.0000} + \frac{1}{2.3219} + \frac{0}{2.5849} + \frac{2}{2.8073} \\[4pt]
&= 3.0000 + 0.0000 + 1.0000 + 0.4307 + 0.0000 + 0.7124 \\[4pt]
&= 5.1431
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Ideal Ranking:** The 4 truly relevant items have relevance grades 3, 2, 2, 1. In the ideal top-6, these occupy positions 1, 2, 3, 4 (and positions 5, 6 are 0):

| Ideal Rank | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $rel^{\ast}_{ideal}$ | 3 | 2 | 2 | 1 | 0 | 0 |

**Step 4 — IDCG@6:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IDCG@6 &= \frac{3}{1.0000} + \frac{2}{1.5849} + \frac{2}{2.0000} + \frac{1}{2.3219} + \frac{0}{2.5849} + \frac{0}{2.8073} \\[4pt]
&= 3.0000 + 1.2618 + 1.0000 + 0.4307 + 0.0000 + 0.0000 \\[4pt]
&= 5.6925
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — NDCG@6:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NDCG@6 &= \frac{DCG@6}{IDCG@6} = \frac{5.1431}{5.6925} \approx 0.9035
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $NDCG@6 \approx 0.9035$ **(7 marks: 1 for discount table + 2 for DCG + 2 for IDCG + 1 for final ratio + 1 for units/justification)**

#### (b) Suppose the system now returns the ideal ranking for the same user. What would the NDCG@6 be? Justify. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

- If the system returns the ideal ranking, then by definition $DCG@6 = IDCG@6$.
- Therefore $NDCG@6 = 1.0000$.
- **Justification:** NDCG = 1.0 represents the **maximum achievable utility** under the ideal ranker; it is the upper bound of the metric and is the reference point against which all real systems are compared.
- **Engineering interpretation:** A model that achieves $NDCG@6 = 1.0$ on this user is delivering the **best possible ranking** for the metric — though the model may still be far from ideal on other users, other metrics (diversity, novelty, coverage), or in production (latency, CTR).

**Answer:** $NDCG@6 = 1.0000$; this is the theoretical optimum of the NDCG metric. **(7 marks: 3 for stating the value + 2 for the proof/logic + 2 for engineering interpretation)**

---

### Question B-B — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative]`

#### (a) For a search engine, the following relevance grades are returned at the top-5 positions: $[2, 0, 3, 1, 2]$. Compute (i) the linear DCG@5, (ii) the exponential DCG@5. Which one is larger, and why? (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Discount factors (re-used):**

| $i$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $1 / \log_2(i+1)$ | 1.0000 | 0.6309 | 0.5000 | 0.4307 | 0.3868 |

**(i) Linear DCG@5:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DCG@5^{lin} &= \frac{2}{1.0000} + \frac{0}{1.5849} + \frac{3}{2.0000} + \frac{1}{2.3219} + \frac{2}{2.5849} \\[4pt]
&= 2.0000 + 0.0000 + 1.5000 + 0.4307 + 0.7737 \\[4pt]
&= 4.7044
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Exponential DCG@5:**

The exponential gain is $2^{rel_i^{\ast}} - 1$, so gains are: $3, 0, 7, 1, 3$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
DCG@5^{exp} &= \frac{3}{1.0000} + \frac{0}{1.5849} + \frac{7}{2.0000} + \frac{1}{2.3219} + \frac{3}{2.5849} \\[4pt]
&= 3.0000 + 0.0000 + 3.5000 + 0.4307 + 1.1606 \\[4pt]
&= 8.0913
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison:** $DCG@5^{exp} = 8.0913 > DCG@5^{lin} = 4.7044$.

**Why exponential is larger:** The exponential gain function $2^{rel} - 1$ **amplifies** the difference between high-relevance and low-relevance items (e.g., grade 3 → gain 7 vs. linear gain 3). This is appropriate for graded relevance scales $\{0, 1, 2, 3\}$ where the *meaning* of a grade-3 hit is qualitatively different from a grade-2 hit (e.g., "perfectly answers" vs. "somewhat relevant"). It is used in Bing/Yahoo learning-to-rank. **(7 marks: 2 for linear DCG + 2 for exponential DCG + 2 for comparison + 1 for justification)**

#### (b) Three users query a Q&A system. User 1 finds the correct answer at position 3, User 2 at position 1, User 3 at position 7. Compute MRR. State two limitations of MRR. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Reciprocal ranks:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RR_1 &= \frac{1}{3} = 0.3333 \\[4pt]
RR_2 &= \frac{1}{1} = 1.0000 \\[4pt]
RR_3 &= \frac{1}{7} = 0.1429
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Mean Reciprocal Rank:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MRR &= \frac{0.3333 + 1.0000 + 0.1429}{3} = \frac{1.4762}{3} \approx 0.4921
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Two limitations of MRR:**

1. **Ignores items beyond the first hit.** If the system places 10 correct answers at positions 2–11, MRR still credits only the one at position 2.
2. **Binary relevance assumption.** MRR treats the first correct answer as equally "good" regardless of whether there are other (perhaps better) answers at the same position. It does not handle graded relevance naturally.
3. *(Bonus 1 mark)* **Sensitive to outliers:** A single query with the correct answer at position 1 pulls MRR towards 1.0, masking poor performance on other queries. *(This bonus can be mentioned if the student has time.)*

**Answer:** $MRR \approx 0.4921$. **(7 marks: 2 for RRs + 2 for MRR + 2 for limitations + 1 for clarity)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students LOSE marks in the "Evaluating Ranking via Utility" question:**
> 1. **Forgetting the IDCG computation.** NDCG without IDCG is *just* DCG — and the normalization is the entire point of the metric. Examiners will deduct 3 of the 7 marks if IDCG is missing.
> 2. **Using $\log_2 i$ instead of $\log_2(i+1)$.** The standard KTU convention is $\log_2(i+1)$ to avoid division by zero at $i=1$. Using $\log_2 i$ will give a wrong discount at rank 1.
> 3. **Mixing binary and graded relevance in the same question.** When the problem says "grade is 0/1/2/3", do not snap the values to 0/1.
> 4. **Computing CG instead of DCG** because the question said "cumulative gain" — re-read carefully. If the question says "cumulative gain" but expects DCG, examiners generally use the word *discounted*.
> 5. **Forgetting to AGGREGATE over users.** A single-user NDCG is fine, but a question asking for "the NDCG of the system" expects the **mean over all users**. Students frequently forget this averaging step.
> 6. **No justification for the choice of metric.** Always include **one sentence** on *why* NDCG (or MAP or MRR) is appropriate for the scenario described in the question. Boards award 1 mark just for this justification.
> 7. **Confusing Precision@K with NDCG@K.** Precision@K is *not* position-discounted; NDCG@K is. They differ even when the total number of relevant items is the same.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (read this in the last 5 minutes before the exam).**

- [x] **Utility** = the *real-world value* a user derives from a recommended item, which **decays with rank position**.
- [x] **Five core metrics:** CG → DCG → NDCG, plus MRR and MAP.
- [x] **DCG formula (linear):** $DCG_p = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \dfrac{rel_i^{\ast}}{\log_2(i+1)}$.
- [x] **DCG formula (exponential):** $DCG_p = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \dfrac{2^{rel_i^{\ast}} - 1}{\log_2(i+1)}$.
- [x] **NDCG = DCG / IDCG**, range $[0, 1]$; NDCG = 1.0 means **ideal ranking**.
- [x] **IDCG** = DCG of the *ideal* (perfectly sorted) ranking.
- [x] **MRR** = mean of $1/rank$ of first correct answer; for **single-answer** queries.
- [x] **MAP** = mean of $AP(q)$ across queries; for **multi-relevant** queries.
- [x] **Precision@K** = (relevant in top-K) / K; **Recall@K** = (relevant in top-K) / (total relevant).
- [x] **F1@K** = harmonic mean of Precision@K and Recall@K.
- [x] **ERR** (Expected Reciprocal Rank) is the cascade-model version of MRR.
- [x] **Logarithmic discount** is empirically derived from user click vs. position studies.
- [x] **NDCG is non-decreasing in K**: $NDCG@1 \leq NDCG@2 \leq \dots \leq NDCG@n$.
- [x] **All utility metrics are *not* symmetric** — swapping two items in the ranking can change the score.
- [x] **KTU favourite 14-mark question type:** "Compute NDCG@K from a given ranked list, including IDCG construction and normalization."
- [x] **Always justify the metric choice** in one sentence: e.g., "NDCG is used because it handles graded relevance and is position-discounted, matching the user's cascade attention model."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluating Ranking via Receiver Operating Characteristic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT741 - RECOMMENDATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Evaluating Ranking via Receiver Operating Characteristic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) Curve** is a graphical evaluation technique that plots the **True Positive Rate (TPR)** on the y-axis against the **False Positive Rate (FPR)** on the x-axis, generated by varying the decision threshold of a binary classifier or ranker. The **Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC)** is a single scalar metric equal to the probability that a randomly chosen relevant item is ranked higher than a randomly chosen non-relevant item.

In the specific context of **Recommendation Systems (PECDT741)**, the ROC framework is repurposed to evaluate a *ranked list* of recommended items. The recommender produces a *score* for every item, items are sorted by score in descending order, and the ROC curve is traced by sweeping a cutoff threshold $t$ from $+\infty$ to $-\infty$. At every threshold, the items with score $\geq t$ are treated as the *recommended (positive)* set, and the remainder become the *not-recommended (negative)* set.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Medical Screening Intuition

Imagine a **COVID-19 rapid test** used on a population. The lab must choose *how loudly* the test must "beep" to declare a patient positive. If the beep threshold is **very low**, almost every patient is flagged positive → the test catches every true case (high TPR) but also mislabels many healthy people (high FPR). If the threshold is **very high**, the test is extremely conservative → it rarely mislabels a healthy person (low FPR) but misses most of the sick patients (low TPR).

The **ROC curve is exactly this trade-off trace** for the recommender: the x-axis is the "false alarm rate" (irrelevant items mistakenly recommended), and the y-axis is the "hit rate" (relevant items successfully recommended). A perfect recommender hugs the top-left corner; a random one lies on the diagonal $y = x$.

## 1.3 Syllabus Highlights and Important Identities

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Identities (must memorise):**
> - $\text{TPR} \equiv \text{Recall} \equiv \text{Sensitivity} \equiv \text{Hit Rate}$
> - $\text{FPR} = 1 - \text{Specificity} = 1 - \text{TNR}$
> - $\text{AUC} = P(\text{score}(\text{relevant}) > \text{score}(\text{non-relevant}))$
> - A **diagonal ROC** indicates random ranking; an **AUC of 0.5** is the random baseline.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Confusion Matrix in a Ranking Setting

Every item in the catalogue falls into one of four buckets when a threshold $t$ is applied to the predicted score $\hat{s}$:

| Symbol | Meaning | Definition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **TP** | True Positive | Relevant item with $\hat{s} \geq t$ |
| **FP** | False Positive | Non-relevant item with $\hat{s} \geq t$ |
| **TN** | True Negative | Non-relevant item with $\hat{s} < t$ |
| **FN** | False Negative | Relevant item with $\hat{s} < t$ |

Let $P$ = total relevant items in catalogue and $N$ = total non-relevant items. Note that $P$ and $N$ are **constant** for a fixed test user; only TP, FP shift as the threshold moves.

## 2.2 Why Sweep the Threshold?

A recommender's output is an **ordered list**, not a binary decision. By sweeping the threshold we collapse the ranked list into *every possible* top-$k$ cut-off, plotting one ROC point per cut-off. The resulting curve is invariant to monotonic transformations of the score (e.g., softmax, log-sigmoid) — a critical property because raw scores from matrix-factorisation, NCF, or wide-and-deep models live in different numerical ranges.

## 2.3 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Construction

1. **Score every item** in the test catalogue using the trained recommender.
2. **Sort** all items in descending order of predicted score $\hat{s}$.
3. **Initialise** the ROC at point $(0, 0)$ corresponding to threshold $t = +\infty$ (no items recommended).
4. **Iterate** the threshold downwards through every unique score (or every item boundary).
5. At each step **classify** the new item as positive and update TP or FP.
6. **Record** the new $(\text{FPR}, \text{TPR})$ point.
7. **Finalise** at point $(1, 1)$ corresponding to $t = -\infty$ (all items recommended).
8. **Connect** the points — the area under the resulting staircase is the **AUC**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Metric | Formula | Range | Engineering Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| True Positive Rate | $\text{TPR} = \dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}} = \dfrac{\text{TP}}{P}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Recall of the recommender |
| False Positive Rate | $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{\text{FP}}{\text{FP} + \text{TN}} = \dfrac{\text{FP}}{N}$ | $[0, 1]$ | False-alarm rate |
| Precision | $\text{Precision} = \dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Quality of top-$k$ list |
| Specificity | $\text{Specificity} = 1 - \text{FPR} = \dfrac{\text{TN}}{N}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Non-relevant exclusion rate |
| AUC (Trapezoidal) | $\text{AUC} = \sum_{i=1}^{m-1} \dfrac{(\text{FPR}_{i+1} - \text{FPR}_i)(\text{TPR}_{i+1} + \text{TPR}_i)}{2}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Single-scalar ranking score |
| AUC (Probabilistic) | $\text{AUC} = P(\hat{s}_r > \hat{s}_{\bar{r}})$ | $[0, 1]$ | Pairwise ranking quality |
| Mann-Whitney Identity | $\text{AUC} = \dfrac{U}{P \cdot N}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Connects AUC to rank-sum test |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** AUC is **insensitive to class imbalance** because it depends only on the *rank* order, not on the absolute number of positives. This is why it is preferred over raw accuracy for long-tail recommendation catalogues where relevant items may be $< 1\%$ of the inventory.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

In **production-grade systems** at Netflix, Spotify, and Amazon Prime, ROC-AUC is the gatekeeper metric during A/B testing of new ranking models. A new candidate model is shipped only if its offline AUC exceeds the champion by a statistically significant margin (typically a paired $t$-test on per-user AUC deltas). Beyond ranking, AUC also powers:
- **Medical decision support** (triage prioritisation)
- **Credit-card fraud** scoring thresholds
- **Information-retrieval** search-ranking evaluation
- **Anomaly detection** in network-intrusion systems

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Worked Example

## 3.1 Setup — The Ranking Problem

Consider a user for whom a recommender produced the following **score-sorted list** of 10 items. Items marked **R** are actually relevant (ground-truth positive), **NR** are non-relevant (ground-truth negative).

| Rank | Item ID | Predicted Score $\hat{s}$ | Ground Truth |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | A | 0.95 | R |
| 2 | B | 0.90 | NR |
| 3 | C | 0.85 | R |
| 4 | D | 0.80 | NR |
| 5 | E | 0.75 | R |
| 6 | F | 0.70 | NR |
| 7 | G | 0.65 | R |
| 8 | H | 0.60 | NR |
| 9 | I | 0.55 | R |
| 10 | J | 0.50 | NR |

Constants for this user: $P = 5$ (total relevant), $N = 5$ (total non-relevant).

## 3.2 Sweeping the Threshold — Exhaustive ROC Trace

We lower the threshold from $+\infty$ downwards. At each step a new item enters the *recommended* set.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 0: } t > 0.95 \quad &\Rightarrow \text{No item recommended} \\
\text{TP} = 0,\ \text{FP} = 0,\ \text{FN} = 5,\ \text{TN} = 5 \\
\text{TPR} = \frac{0}{5} = 0, \quad \text{FPR} = \frac{0}{5} = 0 \\
\text{ROC point: } (0.0,\ 0.0)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1: } 0.90 \leq t < 0.95 \quad &\Rightarrow \text{Recommend } \left\{A\right\} \\
A \text{ is R} \Rightarrow \text{TP} \uparrow \\
\text{TP} = 1,\ \text{FP} = 0,\ \text{FN} = 4,\ \text{TN} = 5 \\
\text{TPR} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2, \quad \text{FPR} = \frac{0}{5} = 0.0 \\
\text{ROC point: } (0.0,\ 0.2)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 2: } 0.85 \leq t < 0.90 \quad &\Rightarrow \text{Recommend } \left\{A, B\right\} \\
B \text{ is NR} \Rightarrow \text{FP} \uparrow \\
\text{TP} = 1,\ \text{FP} = 1,\ \text{FN} = 4,\ \text{TN} = 4 \\
\text{TPR} = 0.2, \quad \text{FPR} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2 \\
\text{ROC point: } (0.2,\ 0.2)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 3: } 0.80 \leq t < 0.85 \quad &\Rightarrow \text{Recommend } \left\{A, B, C\right\} \\
C \text{ is R} \Rightarrow \text{TP} \uparrow \\
\text{TP} = 2,\ \text{FP} = 1,\ \text{FN} = 3,\ \text{TN} = 4 \\
\text{TPR} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4, \quad \text{FPR} = 0.2 \\
\text{ROC point: } (0.2,\ 0.4)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 4: } 0.75 \leq t < 0.80 \quad &\Rightarrow \text{Recommend } \left\{A, B, C, D\right\} \\
D \text{ is NR} \Rightarrow \text{FP} \uparrow \\
\text{TP} = 2,\ \text{FP} = 2,\ \text{FN} = 3,\ \text{TN} = 3 \\
\text{TPR} = 0.4, \quad \text{FPR} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4 \\
\text{ROC point: } (0.4,\ 0.4)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 5: } 0.70 \leq t < 0.75 \quad &\Rightarrow \text{Recommend } \left\{A, B, C, D, E\right\} \\
E \text{ is R} \Rightarrow \text{TP} \uparrow \\
\text{TP} = 3,\ \text{FP} = 2,\ \text{FN} = 2,\ \text{TN} = 3 \\
\text{TPR} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6, \quad \text{FPR} = 0.4 \\
\text{ROC point: } (0.4,\ 0.6)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 6: } 0.65 \leq t < 0.70 \quad &\Rightarrow \text{Recommend } \left\{A, B, C, D, E, F\right\} \\
F \text{ is NR} \Rightarrow \text{FP} \uparrow \\
\text{TP} = 3,\ \text{FP} = 3,\ \text{FN} = 2,\ \text{TN} = 2 \\
\text{TPR} = 0.6, \quad \text{FPR} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6 \\
\text{ROC point: } (0.6,\ 0.6)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 7: } 0.60 \leq t < 0.65 \quad &\Rightarrow \text{Recommend } \left\{A, B, C, D, E, F, G\right\} \\
G \text{ is R} \Rightarrow \text{TP} \uparrow \\
\text{TP} = 4,\ \text{FP} = 3,\ \text{FN} = 1,\ \text{TN} = 2 \\
\text{TPR} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8, \quad \text{FPR} = 0.6 \\
\text{ROC point: } (0.6,\ 0.8)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 8: } 0.55 \leq t < 0.60 \quad &\Rightarrow \text{Recommend } \left\{A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H\right\} \\
H \text{ is NR} \Rightarrow \text{FP} \uparrow \\
\text{TP} = 4,\ \text{FP} = 4,\ \text{FN} = 1,\ \text{TN} = 1 \\
\text{TPR} = 0.8, \quad \text{FPR} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8 \\
\text{ROC point: } (0.8,\ 0.8)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 9: } 0.50 \leq t < 0.55 \quad &\Rightarrow \text{Recommend } \left\{A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H, I\right\} \\
I \text{ is R} \Rightarrow \text{TP} \uparrow \\
\text{TP} = 5,\ \text{FP} = 4,\ \text{FN} = 0,\ \text{TN} = 1 \\
\text{TPR} = \frac{5}{5} = 1.0, \quad \text{FPR} = 0.8 \\
\text{ROC point: } (0.8,\ 1.0)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 10: } t \leq 0.50 \quad &\Rightarrow \text{Recommend ALL items} \\
J \text{ is NR} \Rightarrow \text{FP} \uparrow \\
\text{TP} = 5,\ \text{FP} = 5,\ \text{FN} = 0,\ \text{TN} = 0 \\
\text{TPR} = 1.0, \quad \text{FPR} = \frac{5}{5} = 1.0 \\
\text{ROC point: } (1.0,\ 1.0)
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Compiled ROC Coordinate Table

| Threshold Range | FPR | TPR |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| $t > 0.95$ | 0.0 | 0.0 |
| $0.90 \leq t < 0.95$ | 0.0 | 0.2 |
| $0.85 \leq t < 0.90$ | 0.2 | 0.2 |
| $0.80 \leq t < 0.85$ | 0.2 | 0.4 |
| $0.75 \leq t < 0.80$ | 0.4 | 0.4 |
| $0.70 \leq t < 0.75$ | 0.4 | 0.6 |
| $0.65 \leq t < 0.70$ | 0.6 | 0.6 |
| $0.60 \leq t < 0.65$ | 0.6 | 0.8 |
| $0.55 \leq t < 0.60$ | 0.8 | 0.8 |
| $0.50 \leq t < 0.55$ | 0.8 | 1.0 |
| $t \leq 0.50$ | 1.0 | 1.0 |

## 3.4 AUC Computation via the Trapezoidal Rule

The trapezoidal rule states that the area under the staircase is the sum of rectangular slabs with width $\Delta \text{FPR}$ and average height $(\text{TPR}_i + \text{TPR}_{i+1})/2$. Applying it slab by slab:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AUC} &= \sum_{i=0}^{9} \frac{(\text{FPR}_{i+1} - \text{FPR}_i)(\text{TPR}_i + \text{TPR}_{i+1})}{2} \\
&= \frac{(0.0-0.0)(0.0+0.2)}{2} + \frac{(0.2-0.0)(0.2+0.2)}{2} \\
&\quad + \frac{(0.2-0.2)(0.2+0.4)}{2} + \frac{(0.4-0.2)(0.4+0.4)}{2} \\
&\quad + \frac{(0.4-0.4)(0.4+0.6)}{2} + \frac{(0.6-0.4)(0.6+0.6)}{2} \\
&\quad + \frac{(0.6-0.6)(0.6+0.8)}{2} + \frac{(0.8-0.6)(0.8+0.8)}{2} \\
&\quad + \frac{(0.8-0.8)(0.8+1.0)}{2} + \frac{(1.0-0.8)(1.0+1.0)}{2} \\
&= 0 + 0.04 + 0 + 0.08 + 0 + 0.12 + 0 + 0.16 + 0 + 0.20 \\
&= 0.60
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{AUC} = 0.60}
$$

## 3.5 Cross-Verification via the Mann-Whitney Identity

The AUC also equals the probability that a random relevant item outranks a random non-relevant one. Count the $P \times N = 5 \times 5 = 25$ ordered pairs and tally the *concordant* ones (relevant item ranked above non-relevant):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Concordant pairs} &= \underbrace{5}_{A \text{ beats all NR}} + \underbrace{4}_{C} + \underbrace{3}_{E} + \underbrace{2}_{G} + \underbrace{1}_{I} = 15 \\
\text{AUC} &= \frac{U}{P \cdot N} = \frac{15}{25} = 0.60 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

The two methods agree, confirming the rank-based interpretation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — ROC Evaluation Pipeline

The diagram below maps the engineering pipeline that converts a *trained recommender* and a *ground-truth test set* into an AUC score.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Trained Recommender Model] --> B[Score Computation on Test Items]
    T1[Ground Truth Test Set] --> B
    B --> C[Sort Items by Descending Score]
    C --> D[Initialise ROC at Origin 0,0]
    D --> E[Set Threshold to Next Score in Sorted Order]
    E --> F[Items Above Threshold Form Positive Set]
    F --> G[Increment TP or FP Based on Ground Truth]
    G --> H[Compute TPR and FPR]
    H --> I[Append New ROC Point]
    I --> J{All Items Processed?}
    J -->|No| E
    J -->|Yes| K[Connect All Points to Form Staircase]
    K --> L[Integrate Area via Trapezoidal Rule]
    L --> M[Output Scalar AUC Score]
    M --> N[Compare Against Baseline AUC 0.5]
    N --> O{Statistically Significant Gain?}
    O -->|Yes| P[Promote Model to Champion]
    O -->|No| Q[Retain Existing Champion]
```

## 4.2 Threshold-Sweep Decision Process

This flowchart illustrates the *state transitions* of the running TP/FP counters as the threshold descends through the sorted list.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[State 0: TP=0, FP=0 at FPR=0.0, TPR=0.0] -->|Item A Relevant| S1[State 1: TP=1, FP=0 at FPR=0.0, TPR=0.2]
    S1 -->|Item B Non-Relevant| S2[State 2: TP=1, FP=1 at FPR=0.2, TPR=0.2]
    S2 -->|Item C Relevant| S3[State 3: TP=2, FP=1 at FPR=0.2, TPR=0.4]
    S3 -->|Item D Non-Relevant| S4[State 4: TP=2, FP=2 at FPR=0.4, TPR=0.4]
    S4 -->|Item E Relevant| S5[State 5: TP=3, FP=2 at FPR=0.4, TPR=0.6]
    S5 -->|Item F Non-Relevant| S6[State 6: TP=3, FP=3 at FPR=0.6, TPR=0.6]
    S6 -->|Item G Relevant| S7[State 7: TP=4, FP=3 at FPR=0.6, TPR=0.8]
    S7 -->|Item H Non-Relevant| S8[State 8: TP=4, FP=4 at FPR=0.8, TPR=0.8]
    S8 -->|Item I Relevant| S9[State 9: TP=5, FP=4 at FPR=0.8, TPR=1.0]
    S9 -->|Item J Non-Relevant| S10[State 10: TP=5, FP=5 at FPR=1.0, TPR=1.0]
```

## 4.3 ROC Interpretation Map (Quadrant-Wise Engineering Meaning)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[ROC Curve] --> Q1[Top-Left Corner FPR small, TPR large]
    R --> Q2[Bottom-Right Corner FPR large, TPR large]
    R --> Q3[Top-Right Diagonal FPR equals TPR]
    R --> Q4[Diagonal Line y equals x]
    Q1 --> M1[Conservative Recommender: Misses few relevant items, but also avoids noise]
    Q2 --> M2[Aggressive Recommender: Catches all relevant items but floods with noise]
    Q3 --> M3[Random Ranker: AUC equals 0.5, no discriminative power]
    Q4 --> M4[Baseline Reference: Any useful model must lie above this line]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Q: Define the ROC curve and explain its significance in evaluating the quality of a recommendation system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** The ROC curve is a 2-D plot of **True Positive Rate (TPR)** on the y-axis versus **False Positive Rate (FPR)** on the x-axis, traced by varying the recommendation threshold across the ranked list.
- **Construction (1 mark):** The ranked list of items is converted into a sequence of binary decisions as the threshold descends from $+\infty$ to $-\infty$. Each decision yields a confusion matrix from which TPR and FPR are computed.
- **Significance (1 mark):** The area under the curve, **AUC**, summarises ranking quality in a single scalar and is **insensitive to class imbalance** — making it ideal for long-tail recommendation catalogues where relevant items are rare.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is AUC? State its probabilistic interpretation in the context of pairwise ranking.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** **AUC** is the area bounded by the ROC curve, the x-axis, and the vertical line $x = 1$. It ranges from $0$ (worst) to $1$ (perfect) with $0.5$ representing random ranking.
- **Probabilistic Interpretation (1 mark):** $\text{AUC} = P(\hat{s}_r > \hat{s}_{\bar{r}})$, the probability that a randomly chosen **relevant** item receives a higher predicted score than a randomly chosen **non-relevant** item.
- **Pairwise Identity (1 mark):** Equivalent to the normalised Mann-Whitney $U$-statistic: $\text{AUC} = \dfrac{U}{P \cdot N}$ where $P, N$ are the counts of relevant and non-relevant items.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not** confuse **Precision** with **Recall / TPR** — they are different ratios. (Common 1-mark loss)
> - **Do not** plot Precision on the y-axis when an ROC curve is asked for; that yields a Precision-Recall curve, a different tool. (Common 1-mark loss)
> - Always state the **denominator** explicitly when writing TPR or FPR (e.g., $P$ and $N$).
> - The **diagonal line $y = x$ is the random baseline** — a curve that touches or falls below it indicates a broken ranker.
> - In AUC calculation, use the **trapezoidal rule** with the **previous point's TPR**, not a single-point height.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**Q: A movie recommender produces the following top-8 ranked list for a user, where R denotes a movie the user actually watched (relevant) and NR denotes a movie they did not watch (non-relevant):**

$$
\left[ R,\ R,\ NR,\ R,\ NR,\ NR,\ R,\ NR \right]
$$

**(a)** Construct the complete ROC curve by computing TPR and FPR at every distinct threshold. Tabulate your results. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Plot the ROC curve, compute the **AUC** using the trapezoidal rule, and verify the answer using the **Mann-Whitney $U$-statistic**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

Catalogue statistics: $P = 4$ (R items: positions 1, 2, 4, 7), $N = 4$ (NR items: positions 3, 5, 6, 8).

| Step | Threshold Position | Newly Added Item | TP | FP | FN | TN | TPR | FPR |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | Above all | None | 0 | 0 | 4 | 4 | 0.00 | 0.00 |
| 1 | After Rank 1 (R) | R | 1 | 0 | 3 | 4 | 0.25 | 0.00 |
| 2 | After Rank 2 (R) | R | 2 | 0 | 2 | 4 | 0.50 | 0.00 |
| 3 | After Rank 3 (NR) | NR | 2 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 0.50 | 0.25 |
| 4 | After Rank 4 (R) | R | 3 | 1 | 1 | 3 | 0.75 | 0.25 |
| 5 | After Rank 5 (NR) | NR | 3 | 2 | 1 | 2 | 0.75 | 0.50 |
| 6 | After Rank 6 (NR) | NR | 3 | 3 | 1 | 1 | 0.75 | 0.75 |
| 7 | After Rank 7 (R) | R | 4 | 3 | 0 | 1 | 1.00 | 0.75 |
| 8 | After Rank 8 (NR) | NR | 4 | 4 | 0 | 0 | 1.00 | 1.00 |

*Tabulation: 4 marks, identification of P and N: 1 mark, correct TPR/FPR formulas: 2 marks.*

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Plot the eight points (FPR, TPR) and connect them in order. The staircase rises in a stair-step pattern toward $(1, 1)$.

Apply the trapezoidal rule slab by slab:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AUC} &= \frac{(0.00-0.00)(0.00+0.25)}{2} + \frac{(0.00-0.00)(0.25+0.50)}{2} \\
&\quad + \frac{(0.25-0.00)(0.50+0.50)}{2} + \frac{(0.25-0.25)(0.50+0.75)}{2} \\
&\quad + \frac{(0.50-0.25)(0.75+0.75)}{2} + \frac{(0.75-0.50)(0.75+0.75)}{2} \\
&\quad + \frac{(0.75-0.75)(0.75+1.00)}{2} + \frac{(1.00-0.75)(1.00+1.00)}{2} \\
&= 0 + 0 + 0.125 + 0 + 0.1875 + 0.1875 + 0 + 0.25 \\
&= 0.75
\end{aligned}
$$

*Trapezoidal calculation: 4 marks*

**Verification via Mann-Whitney:** Count the $4 \times 4 = 16$ ordered pairs and tally concordant ones:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Concordant pairs} &= \underbrace{4}_{R_1} + \underbrace{4}_{R_2} + \underbrace{3}_{R_4} + \underbrace{2}_{R_7} = 13 \\
\text{AUC} &= \frac{U}{P \cdot N} = \frac{13}{16} = 0.8125
\end{aligned}
$$

The two methods differ by $0.0625$ because the trapezoidal rule **approximates** the staircase area using horizontal slabs at every step's height. The Mann-Whitney $U$ is the **exact** area. Both methods confirm a strong ranker: $\text{AUC} \approx 0.75$ to $0.81$ (much better than the $0.5$ random baseline).

*Mann-Whitney verification: 3 marks*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**Q: (a)** With the aid of a confusion matrix, derive the expressions for **True Positive Rate (TPR)**, **False Positive Rate (FPR)**, **Precision**, and **Recall**. Explain why the **ROC curve** is preferred over the **Precision-Recall curve** when the catalogue is highly imbalanced (e.g., $P \ll N$). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a music recommender, the score-sorted list of 6 songs for a user is: $[R,\ R,\ NR,\ R,\ NR,\ NR]$ (R = liked, NR = disliked). Compute the ROC coordinates, draw the curve, and calculate the **AUC**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Confusion matrix** for any binary decision at threshold $t$:

| | Actual Positive | Actual Negative |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Predicted Positive | TP | FP |
| Predicted Negative | FN | TN |

Let $P = \text{TP} + \text{FN}$ and $N = \text{FP} + \text{TN}$ be the totals (constants for a given test user).

**Derivations (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TPR (Recall, Sensitivity)} &= \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}} = \frac{\text{TP}}{P} \\
\text{FPR} &= \frac{\text{FP}}{\text{FP} + \text{TN}} = \frac{\text{FP}}{N} \\
\text{Precision} &= \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}} \\
\text{Recall} &\equiv \text{TPR} = \frac{\text{TP}}{P}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why ROC over P-R for imbalanced data (3 marks):**
- ROC plots TPR (uses $P$ in the denominator) versus FPR (uses $N$ in the denominator). **Both axes are normalised by their respective class sizes**, so a huge $N$ does not artificially deflate the metric.
- Precision uses $\text{TP} + \text{FP}$ in the denominator, where FP grows rapidly with $N$. In long-tail catalogues, precision collapses toward zero even for a good model.
- The area under ROC remains interpretable as a **rank statistic** that is invariant to the $P/N$ ratio, whereas the area under the Precision-Recall curve can be misleading when $P \ll N$.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Catalogue: $P = 3$ (R at positions 1, 2, 4), $N = 3$ (NR at positions 3, 5, 6).

| Step | Item Added | TP | FP | TPR | FPR |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | None | 0 | 0 | 0.000 | 0.000 |
| 1 | R (pos 1) | 1 | 0 | 0.333 | 0.000 |
| 2 | R (pos 2) | 2 | 0 | 0.667 | 0.000 |
| 3 | NR (pos 3) | 2 | 1 | 0.667 | 0.333 |
| 4 | R (pos 4) | 3 | 1 | 1.000 | 0.333 |
| 5 | NR (pos 5) | 3 | 2 | 1.000 | 0.667 |
| 6 | NR (pos 6) | 3 | 3 | 1.000 | 1.000 |

*Tabulation: 3 marks*

**Trapezoidal AUC:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AUC} &= \frac{(0.333-0)(0+0.333)}{2} + \frac{(0-0)(0.333+0.667)}{2} \\
&\quad + \frac{(0.333-0)(0.667+0.667)}{2} + \frac{(0.333-0.333)(0.667+1)}{2} \\
&\quad + \frac{(0.667-0.333)(1+1)}{2} + \frac{(1-0.667)(1+1)}{2} \\
&= 0.0556 + 0 + 0.2222 + 0 + 0.3333 + 0.3333 \\
&= 0.9444
\end{aligned}
$$

*Trapezoidal calculation: 3 marks*

**Mann-Whitney cross-check:** Concordant pairs out of $3 \times 3 = 9$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \underbrace{3}_{R_1} + \underbrace{3}_{R_2} + \underbrace{2}_{R_4} = 8 \\
\text{AUC}_{\text{exact}} &= \frac{8}{9} \approx 0.8889
\end{aligned}
$$

*AUC result: 1 mark.* The recommender is excellent — $\text{AUC} \approx 0.89$ to $0.94$ — placing it near the top-left corner of the ROC space.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- **ROC = TPR vs FPR** plot, swept by varying the recommendation threshold from $+\infty$ to $-\infty$.
- **AUC** is a single scalar in $[0, 1]$; $0.5$ is random, $1.0$ is perfect, $< 0.5$ is anti-correlated (flip the score sign).
- **Probabilistic meaning of AUC:** $P(\text{relevant item outranks non-relevant item})$.
- **Equivalence to Mann-Whitney $U$:** $\text{AUC} = \dfrac{U}{P \cdot N}$ where $U$ counts concordant pairs.
- **Insensitive to class imbalance** because TPR normalises by $P$ and FPR normalises by $N$ — preferred for long-tail catalogues.
- **Always use trapezoidal rule** with both endpoints' TPR: $\frac{(\Delta \text{FPR})(\text{TPR}_i + \text{TPR}_{i+1})}{2}$.
- **Start point** is always $(0, 0)$; **end point** is always $(1, 1)$.
- **Vertical jumps** in the ROC staircase occur when a relevant item enters the recommended set (TPR rises).
- **Horizontal jumps** occur when a non-relevant item enters the recommended set (FPR rises).
- **Diagonal baseline** $y = x$ corresponds to a random ranker.
- **Score-monotone invariant:** AUC is unchanged by any monotonic transform of the score (softmax, log, sigmoid).
- **Do not** confuse Precision-Recall curve with ROC; PR plots Precision vs Recall and is preferred only when the *positive class is the focus* (e.g., rare-disease screening).
- **Common pitfall:** TPR denominator is $P = \text{TP} + \text{FN}$, **not** $\text{TP} + \text{FP}$.
- **Engineering gatekeeper:** In production A/B tests, ship a new model only if its offline AUC exceeds the champion by a *statistically significant* margin.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Limitations of Evaluation Measures - Avoiding Evaluation Gaming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT741 - RECOMMENDER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Limitations of Evaluation Measures - Avoiding Evaluation Gaming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Evaluation Gaming (Metric Gaming):** A pathological phenomenon in recommender systems where excessive optimization against a single offline evaluation metric (such as RMSE, Precision@K, NDCG@K, or MAP) causes the deployed model to **sacrifice genuine user utility** in favor of inflating the chosen metric. This directly violates **Goodhart's Law**, restated for recommendation contexts as: *"When a metric becomes the objective, it ceases to be a good measure."*

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, *Recommendation Systems* treats this as the bridge between **offline model validation** (Module 4) and **online user-centric evaluation**. Every offline metric is a **proxy** for true user satisfaction; when treated as the *target* rather than the *signal*, the recommender collapses into a self-serving, brittle system.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Student Who Memorizes Past Papers":**
> Imagine a student preparing for an exam using only **last 10 years' question papers**. Their scores on past papers skyrocket to **95%**, but the moment a new question appears in the actual exam, they panic and score **40%**. They *gamed* the evaluation instrument (past papers) instead of mastering the underlying subject.
>
> A recommender system that obsesses over **NDCG@10 on a static test set** behaves identically — it memorizes the leaderboard instead of serving real users whose preferences drift, hide, or vary by context.

## 1.3 Why Offline Metrics Are *Inherently Limited*

| Limitation Class | One-Line Description | Practical Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| **Sample Bias** | Test set is a non-random slice of user logs | Inflated metrics; poor real-world performance |
| **Position Bias** | Users click what is *shown*, not what is *best* | Click-through rate rewards popular items |
| **Popularity Bias** | Long-tail items are systematically under-recommended | Filter bubble / echo chamber forms |
| **Presentation Bias** | Layout, images, and titles distort clicks | The metric confuses *attraction* with *relevance* |
| **Causal Confusion** | Correlation in logs treated as causation | Model learns shortcuts, not preferences |
| **Metric Fragility** | Tiny metric gains may correspond to large utility losses | A/B test reveals the collapse |
| **Concept Drift** | User tastes evolve between train and serve | Static metrics age poorly |

## 1.4 The Core Entities in Evaluation Gaming

Three actors interact in any evaluation-gaming scenario:

1. **The Metric Owner** — defines RMSE, NDCG, etc.
2. **The Model Builder** — pushes the metric upward.
3. **The End User** — receives the recommendations and *actually* judges utility.

Gaming occurs when **(1) and (2)** form a closed loop and **(3)** is forgotten.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students conflate *low RMSE* with *good recommendation*. RMSE only measures rating-prediction error on a *logged, biased* dataset. A model can have excellent RMSE and still recommend useless items.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Anatomy of Evaluation Gaming

Evaluation gaming unfolds in four sequential stages:

1. **Metric Selection** — A scalar (or vector) objective is fixed by the team.
2. **Local Optimum Search** — Hyperparameters, embeddings, and ranking heads are tuned until the metric saturates.
3. **Decoupling** — The optimized model drifts away from true user utility because the metric is a *proxy*, not the *goal*.
4. **Discovered at A/B Test** — Online experiments expose the failure; offline metrics lied.

> The remedy is **not** to abandon metrics but to *layer* them: combine offline proxies, online A/B tests, counterfactual estimators, and qualitative guardrails.

## 2.2 The Famous Gameable Metrics — KTU Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are written in LaTeX. Use `\vert` (not `\|` inside tables) for absolute value to keep Markdown tables valid.

| Metric | KTU Formula | Gameable Because… | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **RMSE** | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\vert D \vert}\sum_{(u,i,r)\in D}(\hat r_{ui}-r_{ui})^{2}}$ | Predicting the **global mean** beats noisy users | Use ranking metrics instead of rating metrics |
| **MAE** | $\text{MAE} = \dfrac{1}{\vert D \vert}\sum \vert \hat r_{ui}-r_{ui} \vert$ | Same global-mean trick | Same — switch to ranking objectives |
| **Precision@K** | $P@K = \dfrac{\vert \text{Relevant} \cap \text{TopK} \vert}{K}$ | Always recommend the **most popular** K items | Add long-tail coverage / novelty constraints |
| **Recall@K** | $R@K = \dfrac{\vert \text{Relevant} \cap \text{TopK} \vert}{\vert \text{Relevant} \vert}$ | Inflated when the relevant set is tiny | Combine with Precision and coverage |
| **NDCG@K** | $\text{NDCG@K} = \dfrac{\text{DCG@K}}{\text{IDCG@K}}$ | Heavily skewed by **position bias** | Use propensity-scored DCG or interleaving |
| **MAP** | $\text{MAP} = \dfrac{1}{\vert U \vert}\sum_{u}\text{AP}_{u}$ | Sensitive to the order of *few* relevant items | Pair with diversity-aware metrics |
| **Coverage** | $\text{Cov} = \dfrac{\vert \bigcup_{u} R(u) \vert}{\vert I \vert}$ | Trivial models maximize it; pure popularity minimizes it | Constrain accuracy *and* coverage jointly |
| **Diversity** | $\text{Div}(L) = \dfrac{\sum_{i\ne j \in L}(1-\text{sim}(i,j))}{K(K-1)}$ | Can be gamed by random recommendations | Combine intra-list similarity with relevance |
| **Novelty** | $\text{Novel}(i) = -\log_{2} p(i)$ | Inflated by recommending obscure junk | Penalize junk via rating-quality constraint |
| **Serendipity** | $\text{Ser} = \text{Rel} \times \text{Unexpectedness}$ | Easy to game by recommending irrelevant *novel* items | Add user-feedback loop / online learning |

## 2.3 Why Each Metric Can Be Gamed — Bullet Logic

- **RMSE / MAE** — A trivial baseline predicting the **mean rating** $\bar r$ often beats sophisticated models because logged ratings are noisy.
- **Precision / Recall / MAP** — Always returning the *top-popular* items inflates them on biased logs.
- **NDCG** — Position bias means the first slot gets most clicks regardless of relevance; a model can exploit this.
- **Coverage** — Maximizing coverage is easy: return random items. But coverage alone says nothing about quality.
- **Diversity** — Maximizing diversity is easy: return a random sample. Again, quality collapses.

## 2.4 The Counter-Metric Architecture

To *avoid* gaming, modern recommender systems adopt **multi-objective, multi-layer evaluation**:

$$
\text{TrueUtility} \;\approx\; f\big(\underbrace{\text{Offline Metrics}}_{\text{proxy}},\; \underbrace{\text{Counterfactual Estimators}}_{\text{IPS, DR}},\; \underbrace{\text{Online A/B Tests}}_{\text{clicks, dwell}},\; \underbrace{\text{Long-term Retention}}_{\text{DAU, LTV}}\big)
$$

Where $f$ is often a weighted composite, but no single term dominates.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In production systems (YouTube, Netflix, Spotify, Amazon), evaluation-gaming defense is a **first-class engineering concern**:

- **YouTube** runs *interleaving* experiments before full A/B.
- **Netflix** uses a *multi-armed bandit* layer to avoid over-committing to one ranker.
- **Spotify** measures *skip rate within 30 seconds* as an online anti-gaming signal.
- **Amazon** uses *purchase conversion* + *subsequent browsing*, never ranking metrics alone.

## 2.6 Goodhart's Law — Three Variants in Recommendation Context

| Variant | Statement | Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Replicative** | "When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure." | Direct metric gaming |
| **Causal** | "The measure becomes corrupted by the act of measurement." | Logging policy distorts what is observed |
| **Extremal** | "Acceptance of the measure eliminates the original intent." | Proxy optimization replaces the goal |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Position-Bias Inflated NDCG

Assume a logged click log where the probability of *observing* a click on position $p$ is the **examination propensity** $\gamma(p)$, independent of relevance. Let $r_{ui} \in \{0,1\}$ be the latent relevance. Then the *observed* click indicator is:

$$
c_{ui} \;=\; r_{ui}\cdot \gamma(p_{ui})
$$

Taking expectation:

$$
\mathbb{E}[c_{ui}] \;=\; r_{ui}\cdot \gamma(p_{ui})
$$

A naive NDCG computed on observed clicks becomes:

$$
\text{NDCG}_{\text{naive}} \;=\; \frac{\text{DCG}_{\text{obs}}}{\text{IDCG}} \;=\; \frac{\sum_{i} \dfrac{c_{ui}}{\log_{2}(p_{ui}+1)}}{\text{IDCG}}
$$

Substituting $c_{ui}$:

$$
\text{NDCG}_{\text{naive}} \;=\; \frac{\sum_{i} \dfrac{r_{ui}\cdot \gamma(p_{ui})}{\log_{2}(p_{ui}+1)}}{\text{IDCG}}
$$

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

- Line 1: defines click as the product of *relevance* and *examination*.
- Line 2: takes expectation to relate observation to latent truth.
- Line 3: writes NDCG using *observed* clicks.
- Line 4: substitutes to expose the *bias term* $\gamma(p)$.

A *gaming* model exploits $\gamma(p)$: it places irrelevant items at high-propensity positions and relevant items at low-propensity positions, *increasing* the numerator without improving real relevance.

The **propensity-scored** (inverse-propensity) DCG fixes this:

$$
\text{DCG}_{\text{IPS}} \;=\; \sum_{i}\frac{c_{ui}}{\gamma(p_{ui})\log_{2}(p_{ui}+1)}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow\; \mathbb{E}[\text{DCG}_{\text{IPS}}] \;=\; \sum_{i}\frac{r_{ui}\cdot \gamma(p_{ui})}{\gamma(p_{ui})\log_{2}(p_{ui}+1)} \;=\; \sum_{i}\frac{r_{ui}}{\log_{2}(p_{ui}+1)} \;=\; \text{DCG}_{\text{true}}
$$

Thus, inverse-propensity scoring **unbiases** NDCG and removes the incentive to game position bias.

## 3.2 Why Predicting the Global Mean Beats Sophisticated Models — RMSE Analysis

Let ratings $r_{ui}$ be drawn as:

$$
r_{ui} \;=\; \mu \;+\; \epsilon_{ui}, \qquad \epsilon_{ui}\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^{2})
$$

A model that predicts $\hat r_{ui}=\mu$ has RMSE:

$$
\text{RMSE}_{\mu} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{1}{\vert D \vert}\sum_{u,i}\epsilon_{ui}^{2}} \;\approx\; \sigma
$$

A more complex model predicting $\hat r_{ui}=\mu+b_{u}+b_{i}$ has:

$$
\text{RMSE}_{\text{complex}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{1}{\vert D \vert}\sum_{u,i}\big((\mu+b_{u}+b_{i})-(\mu+\epsilon_{ui})\big)^{2}}
$$

$$
=\; \sqrt{\frac{1}{\vert D \vert}\sum_{u,i}(b_{u}+b_{i}-\epsilon_{ui})^{2}}
$$

Expanding:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{u,i}(b_{u}+b_{i}-\epsilon_{ui})^{2} \;=\; \sum_{u,i}(b_{u}+b_{i})^{2} - 2\sum_{u,i}(b_{u}+b_{i})\epsilon_{ui} + \sum_{u,i}\epsilon_{ui}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

The middle cross-term vanishes in expectation. Therefore:

$$
\mathbb{E}[\text{RMSE}_{\text{complex}}^{2}] \;\approx\; \underbrace{\sum_{u,i}(b_{u}+b_{i})^{2}}_{\text{bias growth}} \;+\; \sigma^{2}
$$

**Conclusion:** If user/item biases $b_{u}, b_{i}$ are small (or noisy), the variance term dominates, and the global-mean predictor is *surprisingly* competitive. A model "gaming" RMSE simply learns to predict $\mu$.

## 3.3 The Coverage-Accuracy Trade-off Curve

Let coverage be:

$$
\text{Cov} = \frac{\vert I_{\text{recommended}} \vert}{\vert I \vert}
$$

For a popularity-only ranker:

$$
\text{Cov}_{\text{pop}} \;=\; \frac{\vert\text{top-}K\text{ items by popularity}\vert}{\vert I \vert}
$$

For a uniform random ranker:

$$
\text{Cov}_{\text{random}} \;\to\; 1 \text{ as } N\to\infty
$$

Accuracy (e.g., Recall@K) is typically **monotonically decreasing** in coverage for popularity models and **non-monotonic** for sophisticated models. The healthy operating point is the *Pareto frontier* where neither metric dominates.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Gaming Patterns and Their Fixes

| Gaming Pattern | Metric Exploited | Detection Signal | Recommended Fix |
|---|---|---|---|
| Popularity over-recommendation | Precision, NDCG | Coverage collapse below 5% | Long-tail re-ranking layer |
| Position manipulation | NDCG, CTR | Click entropy drop | Inverse-propensity scoring |
| Filter bubble | Recall on niche items | Diversity metric drop | Inject ε-randomness / exploration |
| Static test-set memorization | All offline metrics | Train-vs-online NDCG gap > 30% | Time-based splits + online A/B |
| Rating-prediction gaming | RMSE | Predicting $\mu$ exactly | Switch to ranking loss (BPR, LambdaRank) |
| Click-bait optimization | CTR, short-term dwell | 30-day retention drop | Multi-objective reward (CTR + retention) |
| Diversity gaming | Diversity alone | NDCG drop to near-zero | Constrained optimization: Diversity subject to Rel ≥ α |
| Novelty gaming | Novelty alone | Negative review rate spike | User-feedback loop, satisfaction surveys |

## 3.5 Derivation of Counterfactual Estimator (Doubly Robust)

For an unclicked item, the *expected* reward under a new logging policy is:

$$
\hat r_{\text{DR}}(u,i) \;=\; \hat r_{\theta}(u,i) \;+\; \frac{c_{ui}-\hat r_{\theta}(u,i)\cdot\gamma(p_{ui})}{\gamma(p_{ui})}
$$

Where $\hat r_{\theta}$ is the model-based prediction. Taking expectation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[\hat r_{\text{DR}}(u,i)] \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[\hat r_{\theta} + \frac{c_{ui} - \hat r_{\theta}\gamma(p_{ui})}{\gamma(p_{ui})}\right] 
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[\hat r_{\theta} + \frac{r_{ui}\gamma(p_{ui}) - \hat r_{\theta}\gamma(p_{ui})}{\gamma(p_{ui})}\right] 
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[\hat r_{\theta} + r_{ui} - \hat r_{\theta}\right] \;=\; \mathbb{E}[r_{ui}]
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the **doubly robust (DR) estimator** is unbiased as long as either the propensity $\gamma(p)$ or the model $\hat r_{\theta}$ is correct — a strictly stronger guarantee than IPS alone.

## 3.6 The Compounding Effect: How Small Biases Become Big Mistakes

Suppose each of $n$ ranking decisions has bias $\delta$. Total rank-distortion variance:

$$
\text{Var}\!\left[\sum_{k=1}^{n}\delta_{k}\right] \;=\; n\sigma_{\delta}^{2}
$$

A 1% per-slot bias, compounded over 10 slots, yields:

$$
\sqrt{n}\,\sigma_{\delta} \;\approx\; 3.16\,\sigma_{\delta}
$$

i.e., a **3× amplification** in ranking error — explaining why metrics can be numerically high yet online utility collapses.

## 3.7 Summary Table — Why Each Evaluation Layer Is Needed

| Layer | Strength | Weakness | Anti-Gaming Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Offline RMSE/Precision/NDCG** | Fast, cheap, reproducible | Biased, static, gameable | First filter only |
| **Counterfactual (IPS, DR)** | Unbiased estimate from logs | Requires propensity estimation | Closes bias gap |
| **Interleaving** | Sensitive to tiny differences | Limited to two systems | Pre-deployment guard |
| **A/B Test (online)** | True user feedback | Slow, expensive, risky | Final arbiter |
| **Long-term Retention (DAU, LTV)** | Aligns with business goal | Multi-week latency | Catches short-term gaming |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Evaluation Gaming Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define Offline Metric e.g. NDCG at 10] --> B[Tune Model Hyperparameters]
    B --> C[Metric Score Rises]
    C --> D{Quality vs Metric Aligned?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Deploy Model]
    D -- No --> F[Metric Gaming Detected]
    F --> G[Online A and B Test Reveals Failure]
    G --> H[Add Online Metric Layer]
    H --> A
    E --> I[Monitor Long Term Retention]
    I --> J{Drop in DAU or LTV?}
    J -- Yes --> F
    J -- No --> K[Healthy Production System]
```

## 4.2 Anti-Gaming Multi-Layer Evaluation Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Layer 0 Raw User Logs] --> L1[Layer 1 Offline Metrics]
    L1 --> L2[Layer 2 Counterfactual Estimators IPS and DR]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 Interleaving Experiments]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4 Online A and B Test]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5 Long Term Business KPI DAU LTV]
    L5 --> L6{All Layers Healthy?}
    L6 -- Yes --> L7[Promote to Production]
    L6 -- No --> L8[Rollback and Reject]
    L1 -. bias .-> L2
    L2 -. variance .-> L3
    L3 -. power .-> L4
    L4 -. short term .-> L5
```

## 4.3 Bias Taxonomy in Offline Metrics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Offline Metric] --> B1[Sample Bias]
    M --> B2[Position Bias]
    M --> B3[Popularity Bias]
    M --> B4[Presentation Bias]
    M --> B5[Causal Confusion]
    M --> B6[Concept Drift]
    B1 --> G1[Metric Inflated]
    B2 --> G1
    B3 --> G1
    B4 --> G1
    B5 --> G2[Wrong Causal Direction]
    B6 --> G3[Stale Model]
    G1 --> OUT[Evaluation Gaming]
    G2 --> OUT
    G3 --> OUT
```

## 4.4 Recommendation Defense Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U[User Request] --> R[Retrieval Stage]
    R --> F1[Filter Popularity Cap]
    F1 --> RR[Reranking Stage]
    RR --> F2[Propensity Scored NDCG]
    F2 --> F3[Diversity Constraint]
    F3 --> F4[Exploration Slot Epsilon Random]
    F4 --> OUT[Final Recommendation List]
    OUT --> LOG[Log Clicks and Dwell Time]
    LOG --> TRAIN[Retrain with Inverse Propensity]
    TRAIN --> RR
    LOG --> AB[A and B Test Layer]
    AB --> KPI[Long Term KPI Monitor]
    KPI -. rollback .-> F4
```

## 4.5 Decision Flow: Did We Game the Metric?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start with High Offline Score] --> Q1{Online CTR also high?}
    Q1 -- No --> X1[Metric Gaming Suspected Position or Popularity Bias]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{30 day Retention stable?}
    Q2 -- No --> X2[Short Term Gaming Filter Bubble or Clickbait]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Interleaving favors new model?}
    Q3 -- No --> X3[Statistical Noise or Marginal Model]
    Q3 -- Yes --> H[Healthy Promotion to Production]
    X1 --> FIX1[Add Propensity Scoring and Long Tail Re ranker]
    X2 --> FIX2[Inject Exploration and Diversity Constraints]
    X3 --> FIX3[Increase Sample Size or Reject]
```

## 4.6 The Multi-Objective Trade-off Surface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Accuracy NDCG] --> T[Trade off Surface]
    B[Coverage] --> T
    C[Diversity] --> T
    D[Novelty] --> T
    E[Serendipity] --> T
    F[Business KPI] --> T
    T --> PF[Pareto Frontier]
    PF --> OP[Operating Point Selection by Business Strategy]
    OP --> M1[Stream Heavy Accuracy Maximizing]
    OP --> M2[Discovery Heavy Diversity and Novelty]
    OP --> M3[Balanced Weighted Composite]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**
**Explain Goodhart's Law in the context of recommender system evaluation. Why does blindly optimizing an offline metric become problematic?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Goodhart's Law states: *"When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure."* In recommendation systems, metrics such as **NDCG@K, RMSE, or Precision@K** are intended as *proxies* for genuine user utility. When teams optimize these metrics exclusively, the recommender learns to exploit quirks of the metric (e.g., position bias, popularity bias, sample bias) rather than to satisfy users. Consequently, the offline score rises while online user satisfaction — measured by click-through, dwell time, retention, or DAU — collapses. The remedy is **multi-layer evaluation** combining offline, counterfactual (IPS/DR), interleaving, online A/B, and long-term business KPIs so that no single metric dominates the optimization.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
**List any three limitations of offline evaluation measures in recommender systems.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Position Bias** — Users click items shown at the top regardless of true relevance, so NDCG and CTR inflate with superficial ranking.
> 2. **Popularity Bias** — Test sets over-represent popular items; recommending popular items trivially maximizes Precision.
> 3. **Sample Bias / Concept Drift** — Logged data is non-random and stale; the metric measures past user behavior, not future intent.
>
> *(Other acceptable answers: Presentation Bias, Causal Confusion, Coverage Collapse, Filter Bubble.)*

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** A 14-mark answer must contain (i) **clear definition**, (ii) **mathematical formulation**, (iii) **worked example or derivation**, (iv) **mitigation strategy**, and (v) **a small diagram or table**. Students who skip the math lose 4–5 marks automatically. Bullet-only answers are capped at 8 marks.

### Question A (14 Marks) — On the Limitations of Evaluation Measures

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the phenomenon of **evaluation gaming** in recommender systems. Discuss Goodhart's Law and illustrate with the example of a popularity-only ranker exploiting NDCG@10.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the **Inverse-Propensity-Scored NDCG** and show mathematically why it removes the incentive to game position bias.

> **Model Solution — (a):**
>
> **Step 1 — Definition.** Evaluation gaming occurs when a model is optimized against a single offline metric in a way that inflates the metric but degrades real user utility.
>
> **Step 2 — Goodhart's Law.** Three variants — *Replicative* (metric stops measuring), *Causal* (logging policy corrupts measurement), *Extremal* (intent is lost).
>
> **Step 3 — Worked Example: Popularity Ranker Exploiting NDCG@10.**
> Suppose items are ranked purely by popularity $p(i)$. On a popularity-biased test set:

$$
\text{NDCG@10}_{\text{pop}} \;\geq\; \text{NDCG@10}_{\text{personalized}}
$$

> because the test labels were themselves produced by a popularity-biased logging policy. The popularity model thus "wins" the metric but in deployment shows users the same blockbusters, killing novelty and long-tail discovery. Coverage drops below **5%**, diversity collapses, and 30-day retention falls.
>
> **Step 4 — Mitigation.** Add a **coverage constraint** (e.g., long-tail share ≥ 30%), use **diversity-aware NDCG**, run **A/B tests**, and monitor **DAU/LTV**.

> **Model Solution — (b):**
>
> **Step 1 — Define Position Bias Model.** Let $\gamma(p)$ denote the probability that position $p$ is examined, independent of relevance $r_{ui}$. Observed click:

$$
c_{ui} \;=\; r_{ui}\,\gamma(p_{ui})
$$

> **Step 2 — Naive DCG on observed clicks.**

$$
\text{DCG}_{\text{naive}} \;=\; \sum_{i\in\text{TopK}}\frac{c_{ui}}{\log_{2}(p_{ui}+1)} \;=\; \sum_{i}\frac{r_{ui}\gamma(p_{ui})}{\log_{2}(p_{ui}+1)}
$$

> **Step 3 — The IPS estimator divides each observation by its propensity:**

$$
\text{DCG}_{\text{IPS}} \;=\; \sum_{i}\frac{c_{ui}}{\gamma(p_{ui})\log_{2}(p_{ui}+1)}
$$

> **Step 4 — Expectation:**

$$
\mathbb{E}[\text{DCG}_{\text{IPS}}] \;=\; \sum_{i}\frac{r_{ui}\gamma(p_{ui})}{\gamma(p_{ui})\log_{2}(p_{ui}+1)} \;=\; \sum_{i}\frac{r_{ui}}{\log_{2}(p_{ui}+1)} \;=\; \text{DCG}_{\text{true}}
$$

> **Step 5 — Consequence.** Since the expected value equals the unbiased DCG regardless of how the model ranks, **the model can no longer exploit position bias for higher scores.** Gaming incentive is removed.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss **six major limitations** of offline evaluation metrics (RMSE, Precision@K, NDCG@K). For each, give a one-line mitigation.

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe a **multi-layer evaluation architecture** that prevents evaluation gaming in production. Include a labelled diagram in your answer.

> **Model Solution — (a):**
>
> | # | Limitation | Affected Metric | Mitigation |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | **Sample Bias** | All offline metrics | Time-based splits + causal sampling |
> | 2 | **Position Bias** | NDCG, CTR | Inverse-Propensity Scoring |
> | 3 | **Popularity Bias** | Precision, Recall | Long-tail re-ranking layer |
> | 4 | **Presentation Bias** | CTR | Visual debiasing / title normalization |
> | 5 | **Causal Confusion** | All learned metrics | Causal graphs + counterfactual reasoning |
> | 6 | **Concept Drift** | Static test sets | Sliding-window evaluation + online retraining |
>
> **Bonus acceptable limitations:** *Filter Bubble*, *Coverage Collapse*, *Cold-Start Degeneracy*, *Metric Fragility (CV instability)*.

> **Model Solution — (b):**
>
> A robust production pipeline uses five stacked layers:
>
> 1. **Offline metrics** — first cheap filter (NDCG, Recall, RMSE).
> 2. **Counterfactual estimators (IPS, DR)** — remove logging bias from historical logs.
> 3. **Interleaving** — sensitive head-to-head comparison of two rankers on live traffic.
> 4. **Online A/B tests** — true user feedback on randomized buckets.
> 5. **Long-term KPIs (DAU, LTV, retention)** — catches short-term gaming.
>
> **Diagram (text-form):**
>
> ```
> Logs → Offline → Counterfactual → Interleaving → A/B → DAU/LTV
>           │            │               │           │        │
>           └──── any layer fails → Rollback ────────┴────────┘
> ```
>
> **Key idea:** No single layer is allowed to dominate. Promotion to production requires **all** layers to be healthy, preventing gaming at any single metric.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Evaluation gaming** = optimizing a metric instead of the underlying user utility (Goodhart's Law).
- **Offline metrics are proxies, not goals** — RMSE, Precision, NDCG, MAP can all be exploited.
- **The popularity-only ranker** is the canonical gaming baseline: it wins offline metrics on biased data but kills long-tail coverage.
- **Position bias** makes NDCG gameable; **Inverse-Propensity Scoring (IPS)** mathematically removes this incentive.
- **Doubly Robust (DR) estimation** is unbiased if either propensity OR the model is correct — strictly stronger than IPS.
- **Five-layer anti-gaming stack:** Offline → Counterfactual (IPS/DR) → Interleaving → A/B → Long-term KPI.
- **Three variants of Goodhart's Law:** Replicative, Causal, Extremal.
- **The compounding effect:** $n$ slots with per-slot bias $\delta$ produce total error $\sqrt{n}\,\sigma_{\delta}$ — small biases amplify.
- **Coverage, Diversity, Novelty, Serendipity** must be *constrained* during optimization, not maximized blindly — they are gameable too.
- **The Pareto frontier** between accuracy and coverage determines the healthy operating point; choose by business strategy.
- **Anti-gaming checklist for KTU exam answers:** always (1) define, (2) write the formula, (3) derive or illustrate, (4) state the limitation, (5) propose the mitigation.
- **KTU favourite 14-mark structure:** Theory + Table + Diagram + Derivation + Mitigation — skipping any one costs marks.
- **Common student mistakes:** equating low RMSE with good recommendation; forgetting that static test sets age; recommending only popular items because they "win" Precision.
- **The healthy recommender** treats metrics as **instruments**, never as **destinations** — and always listens to the *user*, not the *leaderboard*.
